title: Circular Planar Electrical Networks I: The Electrical Poset EPn
overall_summary: The paper defines and studies the electrical poset EP_n, whose elements are equivalence classes of circular planar electrical networks with n boundary vertices, ordered by edge deletion and contraction. Its central combinatorial tool is the medial graph or wiring-diagram model: critical circular planar graphs correspond to full lensless wiring diagrams, and edge deletion or contraction corresponds to one of the two local smoothings of a crossing. The paper proves that EP_n is the closure poset of the cells in the response-matrix stratification and that EP_n is graded by the number of edges of critical representatives, equivalently by crossings in the associated full lensless wiring diagrams. It also proves a rank-two diamond property for EP_n and conjectures stronger topological properties such as lexicographic shellability and Eulerianness. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the precise crossing-resolution poset technology: medial matchings, motions, lens elimination, legal crossing breakings, closure-by-resolution, and diamond/graded arguments for cubical or uncrossing complexes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1.5 and graph analogue
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (Γ_1, γ_1) and (Γ_2, γ_2) be circular planar electrical networks, where Γ_i is a graph embedded in a disk with specified boundary vertices and γ_i assigns positive conductances to edges. The two networks have the same response matrix if and only if they are related by a sequence of local equivalences: removal or insertion of self-loops and spikes at non-boundary degree-one vertices, replacement of parallel edges by a single edge with summed conductance, replacement of series edges through a non-boundary degree-two vertex by a single edge with reciprocal conductance sum, and Y-Δ transformations with the usual conductance formulas. After forgetting conductances, two circular planar graphs are equivalent in the paper's sense if and only if they are related by the corresponding conductance-free local equivalences.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The network statement is quoted from de Verdiere-Gitler-Vertigan. Each listed local move preserves the boundary response by Ohm's and Kirchhoff's laws, and the cited theorem says these moves generate all response-matrix equivalences. The graph-level analogue follows by choosing conductances realizing equality of response matrices and then forgetting the conductance values along the local-equivalence sequence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be an n by n real matrix indexed by boundary vertices of a disk. For disjoint ordered boundary subsets P = {p_1, ..., p_k} and Q = {q_1, ..., q_k} forming a circular pair, meaning p_1, ..., p_k, q_k, ..., q_1 occur in clockwise cyclic order, let M(P; Q) be the determinant of the submatrix with rows P and columns Q. Then M is the response matrix of some circular planar electrical network if and only if M is symmetric, all row and column sums are zero, and every circular minor M(P; Q) is nonnegative. Moreover, if M is the response matrix of a network (Γ, γ), then the circular minor M(P; Q) is positive exactly when Γ contains vertex-disjoint paths from p_i to q_i for all i, with no path using any boundary vertex other than its two endpoints.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is quoted from Curtis-Ingerman-Morrow. The paper notes that its convention for current direction removes the sign factors that appear in that source. Part (a) characterizes response matrices by positivity of all circular minors; part (b) identifies strict positivity with the existence of the corresponding disjoint boundary connection. This connectivity criterion is later used to identify equivalence classes of graphs with sets of connected circular pairs.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorems 2.3.2 and 2.3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A circular planar graph G is called critical if every allowable single edge deletion or contraction destroys at least one circular-pair connection, i.e. for every such edge removal there is a circular pair (P; Q) connected in G but not after the removal. Every equivalence class of circular planar graphs has a critical representative. If G_1 and G_2 are critical circular planar graphs with the same boundary vertices, then G_1 and G_2 are related by Y-Δ transformations if and only if π(G_1) = π(G_2), where π(G) is the set of circular pairs connected in G by vertex-disjoint paths avoiding other boundary vertices.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Both assertions are quoted from de Verdiere-Gitler-Vertigan and Curtis-Ingerman-Morrow. The first supplies a minimal or reduced representative in every response-equivalence class. The second says that, once criticality is imposed, the full equivalence class is combinatorially classified by the set of positive circular minors or, equivalently, by the set of connected circular pairs.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For arbitrary circular planar graphs G_1 and G_2 with the same boundary vertices, G_1 and G_2 are equivalent in the paper's sense if and only if π(G_1) = π(G_2), where π(G) denotes the set of circular pairs connected in G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If G_1 and G_2 are equivalent, Theorem 2.1.5 gives a sequence of local equivalences. Y-Δ transformations preserve π by Theorem 2.3.3 in the critical setting, and the other local equivalences are directly checked to preserve the existence of disjoint boundary connections; hence π(G_1) = π(G_2). Conversely, choose critical representatives H_1 equivalent to G_1 and H_2 equivalent to G_2 using Theorem 2.3.2. Local equivalence preserves π, so π(H_1) = π(G_1) = π(G_2) = π(H_2). Since H_1 and H_2 are critical, Theorem 2.3.3 implies they are Y-Δ equivalent, hence G_1 and G_2 are equivalent.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a critical circular planar graph with N edges and let π = π(G). Let Ω(π) be the set of response matrices whose positive circular minors are exactly those indexed by π. The conductance-response map r_G: R_{>0}^N -> Ω(π), sending a positive conductance assignment on the N edges of G to the resulting response matrix, is a diffeomorphism.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is quoted from Curtis-Ingerman-Morrow. Criticality is essential: it is exactly the condition under which the edge conductances can be recovered uniquely and smoothly from the response matrix. The result gives each equivalence class of critical graphs an open cell of response matrices, parametrized by one positive coordinate per edge.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a circular planar electrical network (Γ, γ), the following conditions on the underlying circular planar graph Γ are equivalent: (1) Γ is critical; (2) given the response matrix of (Γ, γ) and the graph Γ, the conductance map γ can be uniquely recovered; (3) Γ has the minimal possible number of edges among all circular planar graphs equivalent to Γ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence of (1) and (2) is quoted from Curtis-Ingerman-Morrow. To prove that minimal edge count implies criticality, suppose Γ is not critical; then some edge deletion or contraction yields Γ' with π(Γ') = π(Γ), so by Proposition 2.3.4 the graph Γ' is equivalent to Γ but has fewer edges, contradicting minimality. Conversely, if Γ is critical and there were an equivalent graph with fewer edges, choose among equivalent graphs one with minimal edge count. By the previous implication that minimal representative is critical, this representative is critical. Two equivalent critical graphs have the same π and hence are Y-Δ equivalent by Theorem 2.3.3, but Y-Δ transformations preserve the number of edges, contradicting the assumed smaller edge count.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a circular planar graph and M(G) its medial graph. The graph G is critical if and only if M(G) is lensless. Here the medial graph has degree-one medial boundary vertices and degree-four interior vertices; its geodesics are obtained by going straight through degree-four vertices. A lens is a pair of geodesics that meet in two distinct points and do not meet between those two points along the two geodesic segments, and lensless means that every geodesic connects two medial boundary vertices and no two geodesics form a lens.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is quoted from Curtis-Ingerman-Morrow. The intuition used throughout the paper is that lenses in the medial graph encode reducible series or parallel configurations in the primal network, while closed geodesics or lenses indicate noncriticality. Thus criticality of circular planar graphs is translated into a purely topological condition on crossing curves.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If M(G) and M(H) are two lensless medial graphs whose geodesics match the same pairs of medial boundary vertices, then M(G) and M(H) are related by motions, equivalently G and H are related by Y-Δ transformations. A motion is the local move on a medial graph corresponding to a Y-Δ move in the primal graph.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper says this is implicit in Curtis-Ingerman-Morrow. Since lensless medial graphs have no lenses and no closed geodesics, their motion-equivalence class is determined by the induced perfect matching of the 2n medial boundary vertices. The result is used to replace detailed embedded curve arrangements by boundary matchings, provided lenslessness is maintained.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.4.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There is a bijection between equivalence classes of critical circular planar graphs with n boundary vertices and motion-equivalence classes of full lensless wiring diagrams of order n. A wiring diagram is a collection of n smooth wires in a disk connecting 2n medial boundary vertices in pairs, with no triple crossings or self-loops. It is full if there is no dividing line V_i V_j between two boundary vertices such that no wire connects points on opposite sides of that line.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Starting from a critical graph G, its medial graph M(G) is lensless by Theorem 2.4.2, and the image consists of full diagrams; Proposition 2.4.3 shows that the motion-equivalence class is well-defined on graph equivalence classes. Conversely, given a full lensless wiring diagram W, checkerboard-color the faces of the disk cut by W. Fullness implies each face contains at most one boundary vertex, and all boundary vertices lie in faces of the same color, say black. Build a circular planar graph E(W) by placing a vertex in every black face not already containing a boundary vertex, retaining the given boundary vertices, and drawing an edge through each crossing of two wires where the corresponding black faces meet. This construction gives a critical graph whose medial graph is W, and the two constructions G -> M(G) and W -> E(W) are inverse up to the stated equivalences.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.4 key observation: deletion and contraction as crossing breakings
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the medial graph of a circular planar graph, deleting or contracting a primal edge corresponds locally to breaking, or smoothing, the corresponding crossing of two medial geodesics in one of the two possible ways. Breaking outward from the primal edge corresponds to contraction, while breaking along the primal edge corresponds to deletion. A breaking is called legal if it does not create a dividing line; creating a dividing line is exactly the medial manifestation of attempting to contract a boundary edge, which the paper disallows.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The statement is explained by the local pictures in Figure 7. A primal edge corresponds to a degree-four medial vertex where two geodesics cross. Removing that primal edge by deletion or contraction changes the adjacency of the four local sectors, and the two possible sector pairings are precisely the two smoothings of the crossing. The paper then tracks legality through fullness: if a smoothing creates a line between boundary vertices separating the diagram with no wire crossing it, the operation corresponds to an impermissible boundary-edge contraction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a circular planar graph and suppose H can be obtained from G by a sequence of allowable edge contractions and deletions. If G' is any circular planar graph equivalent to G, then there is a sequence of allowable edge contractions and deletions starting from G' whose result is some graph H' equivalent to H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction, first reducing to the case where H is obtained from G by one edge removal and G' differs from G by one local equivalence. If the local equivalence deletes a self-loop or boundary spike, the claim is immediate. For a Y-Δ move with outer vertices A, B, C and central Y-vertex P, edge removals away from the local move commute with the move; edge removals inside the local configuration are checked case by case. For example, contracting a leg AP of a Y corresponds on the Δ side to deleting the opposite edge BC; deleting a leg AP of a Y corresponds on the Δ side to deleting the two adjacent Δ edges AB and AC; deleting an edge AB of a Δ corresponds on the Y side to contracting the opposite leg CP; contracting an edge AB of a Δ is simulated on the Y side by contracting the two Y-legs incident to A and B. Parallel-edge replacement is handled similarly: if G' is obtained by removing one of two parallel edges, then an edge removal outside the pair is performed on G' and the extra parallel edge is deleted as needed; removals of edges in the pair are checked directly. The reverse operation of adding a parallel edge is handled by performing the desired removal and then deleting the added edge. The series case is analogous. This proves that the deletion/contraction order descends to equivalence classes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1.2 and Theorem 3.1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a circular planar graph G, let Ω(G) be the open cell of response matrices arising from positive conductances on G, and let the closure be taken in the ambient space of symmetric n by n matrices. Then Ω(G) closure is the disjoint union of Ω(H) over all equivalence classes H with H ≤ G in EP_n, where H ≤ G means H can be obtained from G up to equivalence by a sequence of edge deletions and contractions. Consequently, for equivalence classes [H] and [G], one has [H] ≤ [G] in EP_n if and only if Ω(H) is contained in the closure of Ω(G).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: One may assume G critical. By Theorem 2.3.6, positive conductances on the N edges of G give coordinates r_G: R_{>0}^N -> Ω(G). Let γ_i be the conductance of an edge e_i. Sending γ_i to 0 has the physical effect that no current flows through e_i, so the limiting response matrix is that of the graph obtained by deleting e_i. Sending γ_i to infinity identifies the two endpoints electrically, so the limiting response matrix is that of the graph obtained by contracting e_i. Iterating these limits shows Ω(H) is contained in the closure of Ω(G) for every H ≤ G. Conversely, if M is in the closure of Ω(G), take a convergent sequence M_k = r_G(C_k) of response matrices from positive conductances. Circular minors are continuous and nonnegative in the limit, so M is the response matrix of some network H. For each edge e, the conductance sequence C_k(e), after passing to limiting behavior in [0, infinity], tends to a nonnegative finite value or to infinity. A boundary edge cannot have conductance tending to infinity, because applying positive voltage at one endpoint and zero at the others would force an unbounded boundary current and prevent convergence of M_k. Therefore one obtains H by contracting exactly the non-boundary edges whose conductances tend to infinity, deleting the edges whose conductances tend to zero, and retaining positive finite limiting conductances on the remaining edges. Thus H ≤ G. Disjointness of the cells follows from the circular-minor classification, giving the closure-poset characterization.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be critical and consider a crossing p = ab ∩ cd in the lensless medial graph M(G), where a, c, b, d are medial boundary vertices in clockwise order and breaking p in the chosen way produces geodesics ad and cb and a medial graph equivalent to M(H). Let F be the set of geodesics f_i in M(G) that intersect the segment of ab between a and p and also intersect the segment of cd between d and p. Then there exists a lensless medial graph K equivalent to M(G) such that the same geodesics ab and cd still cross at p, breaking p still gives a medial graph equivalent to M(H), and whenever f_i, f_j in F cross each other, their crossing lies outside the sector bounded by the boundary arc from d to a and the geodesic segments a-p and p-d.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each f_i in F, let v_i be its intersection with ab between a and p. For any f_i that crosses another f_j in the sector a-p-d, let D_i be the first such crossing encountered along f_i after v_i. If no such crossings exist, there is nothing to do. Otherwise, restrict attention to the subgraph formed by the geodesics in F together with ab and cd, and choose a point D_i for which the number of regions in the configuration bounded by f_i, f_j, and the segment a-p is minimal. This number must be 1: if it were larger, another geodesic f_k would cross f_j earlier and cut off a smaller such region, contradicting minimality. Hence f_i, f_j, and a-p form an empty triangle, and a motion moves the crossing D_i out of the sector. Repeating finitely many times removes all such F-F crossings from the sector. None of these motions uses the crossing p, so the subsequent breaking of p commutes with the motions and still yields a diagram equivalent to M(H).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting after Lemma 3.2.2, let f_1 be the geodesic in F whose intersection v_1 with ab is closest to p, and let w_1 be the intersection f_1 ∩ cd. Then there exists a lensless medial graph K' equivalent to K such that ab and cd still cross at p, breaking p still yields a medial graph equivalent to M(H), and no other geodesic of K' enters the triangle with vertices v_1, p, and w_1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First consider geodesics that enter the triangle by intersecting only cd and f_1. Using the same minimal empty-triangle argument as in Lemma 3.2.2, apply motions so that intersections among these geodesics occur outside the triangle. Then apply motions at w_1 to move these geodesics out of the triangle so that they instead intersect f_1 in the sector b-p-d. Perform the analogous operation for geodesics intersecting ab and f_1. The motions avoid the crossing p, so the equivalence type of the diagram obtained by breaking p is unchanged.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let [G] and [H] be elements of EP_n. Then [G] covers [H] in EP_n if and only if, for a critical representative G in [G], a single edge of G can be contracted or deleted to obtain a critical graph belonging to [H].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If a critical G becomes a critical H by one edge removal and there were an intermediate [X] with [G] > [X] > [H], then some sequence of at least two edge removals from G would produce a graph equivalent to H. That graph would have fewer edges than the critical graph H, contradicting the minimal-edge characterization of critical graphs in Theorem 2.3.8. Conversely, suppose removing an edge e from a critical G gives a noncritical graph H. In the medial graph this is a breaking of a crossing p. Apply Lemmas 3.2.2 and 3.2.3 to replace M(G) by an equivalent lensless diagram in which the bad breaking is localized in an empty triangle, and let T be the corresponding critical primal graph. In the contraction case, contracting e in T gives H' equivalent to H with a pair of parallel edges; replacing that pair by one edge gives H'' equivalent to H. If e has endpoints B and C and the parallel pair arises from a triangle A B C, criticality gives a circular pair (P; Q) connected in T but not in H'. Any such connection must use both B and C, cannot use the edge B C, and cannot use both A B and A C, since then replacing A B ∪ A C by B C would give a forbidden connection in H'. Thus, say, some connection avoids A B. Deleting A B from T gives an intermediate graph G' not equivalent to H, because the chosen connection remains, but deleting or contracting the original edge from G' yields H''; hence [G] > [G'] > [H]. In the deletion case, deleting e = Z C leaves two edges A Z and Z B in series in a graph H' equivalent to H, so T contains a Y with center Z and outer vertices A, B, C. Replacing the series pair by A B gives H'' equivalent to H. Choose a circular pair connected in T but not in H; its connection uses Z C and exactly one of A Z, B Z. If it uses A Z, contract B Z to get an intermediate G' that still has the connection, while deleting Z C from G' gives H''. Thus any noncritical one-edge removal factors through a genuine intermediate element, proving the covering criterion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The electrical poset EP_n is graded by the number of edges in a critical representative. Equivalently, if [G] is an element of EP_n and G_c is any critical graph in [G], then rank([G]) = number of edges of G_c is well-defined, and every cover relation decreases this number by exactly one.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 2.3.8 implies all critical representatives in the same equivalence class have the same number of edges: any two critical equivalent graphs are Y-Δ equivalent and Y-Δ transformations preserve edge count. If [G] covers [H], Proposition 3.2.1 says that a critical representative of [G] has a single edge deletion or contraction yielding a critical representative of [H]. Therefore the critical edge count drops by exactly one along every cover, which is precisely gradedness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let x be an element of EP_n of rank r - 1 and z an element of EP_n of rank r + 1 with x < z. Then there are exactly two elements y of rank r such that x < y < z. Equivalently, every closed interval of length two in EP_n has four elements.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By gradedness and the medial-graph model, x is obtained from z by two legal crossing breakings in a lensless wiring diagram. If the second breaking involves one of the wires created by the first, the local possibilities reduce up to motion to two cyclic configurations on six boundary endpoints. In the representative notation used in the paper, one path is {ab, cd, ef} -> {ac, bd, ef} -> {ac, bf, de}. In one subcase there are two apparent alternative middle diagrams, {ab, cf, de} and {ae, cd, bf}, but the latter necessarily contains a lens, so only {ab, cf, de} is valid; in the other subcase the same valid alternative is the only possibility. Legality follows from the legality of the original two breakings. If the two breakings involve disjoint pairs of wires, the local form is {ab, cd, ef, gh} -> {ac, bd, ef, gh} -> {ac, bd, eg, fh}. The only possible alternative order is through {ab, cd, eg, fh}. The paper enumerates the essentially different cyclic configurations of the eight endpoints and checks in each case that this alternative order remains legal and lensless whenever the original order is. Thus exactly the two orders of performing the two breakings give rank-r elements between z and x.

proof_techniques.name: Medial graph translation of edge operations into crossing resolutions
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically replaces circular planar graphs by medial graphs or wiring diagrams. In this translation, criticality becomes lenslessness, Y-Δ transformations become local motions of three wires, series and parallel reductions become lens resolutions, and the two primal edge removals, deletion and contraction, become the two smoothings of a crossing. This converts an electrical-network poset into an uncrossing or crossing-breaking poset of curves in a disk, with fullness imposing a boundary nonseparation condition.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The complex F_w is built directly from matchings with crossings and facets given by local crossing resolutions. The medial-graph dictionary suggests studying F_w via an associated resolution poset, with special attention to whether the word-label condition rules out lens-like reducible configurations or supplies a replacement for fullness/legal breakings.

proof_techniques.name: Use lensless representatives to ignore internal isotopy complexity
proof_techniques.description: A crucial fact in the paper is that two lensless medial graphs inducing the same pairing of medial boundary vertices are related by motions. Thus, once lenslessness is achieved, the internal ordering of crossings can be altered by local moves without changing the equivalence class. Many arguments choose a convenient representative by performing motions before applying a crossing breaking.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a useful subclass or normal form of quasi-reduced matchings in F_w can be shown to be lensless in an analogous sense, then cells might be indexed largely by endpoint pairings and labels rather than by complicated embedded representatives. This could simplify links and attaching maps in a contractibility proof.

proof_techniques.name: Closure via positive coordinates and boundary limits
proof_techniques.description: For a critical graph G, positive edge conductances give coordinates on the response-matrix cell Ω(G). Sending a coordinate to 0 deletes the corresponding edge, while sending it to infinity contracts the edge. The paper proves the entire cell closure is obtained by iterating exactly these limits, giving a geometric realization of the deletion/contraction poset as a closure poset.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: F_w already assigns cubes to crossing sets, with facets corresponding to crossing resolutions. The electrical proof suggests trying to realize F_w or its face poset as a compactification of a space with one positive parameter per crossing, where contractibility might follow from an ambient contractible parameter space or from controlled closure strata.

proof_techniques.name: Well-defined resolution order under local equivalence
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.1.1 proves that deletion/contraction order is independent of the chosen representative in an equivalence class. The proof reduces to a finite case analysis showing that an edge removal before a local equivalence can be simulated by a sequence of edge removals after the local equivalence, producing an equivalent result.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one introduces moves on matchings in F_w analogous to motions or Reidemeister-type transformations, this lemma gives a template for proving that the resolution poset and cubical attaching maps do not depend on the chosen diagrammatic representative.

proof_techniques.name: Innermost-triangle normalization before resolving a bad crossing
proof_techniques.description: In the proof of Proposition 3.2.1, the paper handles a crossing whose breaking produces a noncritical diagram by first using motions to push interfering crossings out of a sector. It chooses a nearest intersection minimizing the number of enclosed regions, proves the minimal region is an empty triangle, applies a motion to remove it, and iterates. A second normalization isolates a triangle into which no other geodesic enters, making the primal obstruction visible as a series or parallel pair.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This innermost-triangle or empty-digon strategy could be used to analyze local obstructions in F_w, especially if quasi-reducedness prevents certain small lenses. It provides a way to replace global crossing complexity by a controlled local configuration before constructing collapses or proving link properties.

proof_techniques.name: Cover criterion by excluding hidden noncritical one-step removals
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves that a cover in EP_n is exactly a single edge removal that remains critical. If a one-step removal is noncritical, the localized medial analysis exposes a parallel or series pair, and a circular-pair connection is used to find an intermediate graph strictly between the original and the result. This prevents noncritical smoothings from being covers.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, a similar criterion might distinguish genuine codimension-one faces from resolutions that factor through additional forced simplifications. Such a criterion can be useful when identifying the face poset of the cubical complex or constructing a discrete Morse matching.

proof_techniques.name: Rank-two diamond verification by commuting crossing breakings
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.3.2 proves that every rank-two interval of EP_n is a diamond. The proof analyzes whether two crossing breakings share a wire or are disjoint; in each case, the two legal orders of performing the breakings give the two middle elements, while other apparent intermediates are ruled out by lens formation or illegality.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Cubical complexes require compatible square faces for pairs of independent resolutions. An analogous diamond property for the resolution poset underlying F_w would support proving that F_w is a regular cubical complex, analyzing vertex links, or applying CAT(0)/collapsibility criteria.

proof_techniques.name: Bruhat-order analogy for wiring diagrams
proof_techniques.description: The paper observes that EP_n resembles the strong Bruhat order on the symmetric group: a permutation can be drawn as a lensless wiring diagram between two parallel boundary lines, and the Bruhat cover relation corresponds to breaking a crossing, except that in the Bruhat setting each crossing has only one legal breaking. This analogy motivates shellability questions for EP_n.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Known EL-shellings and subword-complex techniques for Bruhat-type uncrossing orders may provide templates for attacking F_w, especially if the word-label constraints select intervals, ideals, or subcomplexes inside a larger crossing-resolution order.

proof_techniques.name: Inductive expansion and refinement of wiring diagrams
proof_techniques.description: In the enumerative section, the paper defines expansion at a wire by inserting a new boundary vertex and replacing one wire by either two crossed or two uncrossed wires, and refinement at a boundary vertex by deleting two incident wires and joining their other endpoints. Lemmas 4.1.3 to 4.1.5 control when fullness is preserved or how failure of fullness splits the diagram into smaller full diagrams.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although used for counting in the paper, expansion/refinement resembles inductively adding or removing a matched pair. For F_w, a comparable operation at a cancellable inverse-letter pair could support induction on word length or define deformation retractions to smaller words.

proof_techniques.name: Noncrossing-partition decomposition of boundary separations
proof_techniques.description: For rank enumeration, the paper treats dividing lines as noncrossing partitions of the boundary vertices. Drawing all dividing lines of a noncrossing partition decomposes the disk into independent wiring regions, and Möbius inversion over the noncrossing partition lattice extracts full diagrams from arbitrary diagrams.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If label-compatible matchings in F_w decompose along noncrossing cuts or subwords, a noncrossing-partition decomposition could organize an induction, an inclusion-exclusion for Euler characteristics, or a cover by simpler contractible subcomplexes.

other_useful_info: After proving gradedness, the paper defines W_n as the poset of equivalence classes of lensless wiring diagrams not necessarily full, ordered by crossing breakings without worrying about dividing lines, and states that EP_n is an interval in W_n. The paper conjectures that EP_n is Eulerian and more strongly lexicographically shellable, hence Cohen-Macaulay, spherical, and Eulerian; it reports computational verification of Eulerianness for n ≤ 7 and of the expected sphere homology for EP_n minus its minimum and maximum for n ≤ 4. It also conjectures that W_n with an added minimum element is Eulerian. The paper explicitly compares EP_n with strong Bruhat order and cites Bjorner-Wachs on Bruhat shellability as background. The enumerative sections give sanity-check data for the uncrossing poset: |EP_n| satisfies X_1 = 1 and X_n = 2(n - 1)X_{n-1} + sum_{j=2}^{n-2} (j - 1)X_j X_{n-j}; near the top rank, |EP_{n, binom(n,2)-c}| = binom(n - 1 + c, c) for 0 ≤ c ≤ n - 2, and |EP_{n, binom(n,2)-(n-1)}| = binom(2n - 2, n - 1) - n.
why_relevant: Introduces and studies the electrical poset, one origin of the uncrossing order on medial matchings. It supplies combinatorial and topological descriptions of crossing-resolution posets that may model the unlabeled version of F_w.

title: Electroid Varieties and a Compactification of the Space of Electrical Networks
overall_summary: Lam constructs a compactification En of the space of circular planar electrical networks by allowing cactus networks, i.e. networks whose boundary vertices may be identified according to a noncrossing partition. The compactified space has a cell decomposition indexed by all perfect matchings on [2n], with cells Eτ isomorphic to positive orthants and closure order given by the uncrossing partial order on matchings. A central technique is to embed En into the totally nonnegative Grassmannian Gr(n−1,2n)≥0 as a linear slice X≥0, using a Temperley-type correspondence between groves and bipartite-network matchings. The uncrossing order on matchings is identified with the dual of an induced subposet of affine Bruhat order via a bounded affine permutation fτ associated to each matching τ. For the original problem, the most useful content is the rigorous relationship among crossing resolutions, cell closures, uncrossing posets, affine Bruhat order, and positive-geometric compactifications indexed by matchings.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 2.2 and 2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let σ be a noncrossing partition of boundary vertices [n] placed around a circle, and let σ~ be the dual noncrossing partition on interleaved dual boundary vertices [~n], with ~i lying between i and i+1. Then the number of parts satisfies |σ|+|σ~|=n+1. If τ(σ) is the noncrossing matching on [2n] whose arcs separate the blocks of σ from the blocks of σ~, with vertex 2i−1 placed between ~(i−1) and i and vertex 2i placed between i and ~i, then σ ↦ τ(σ) is a bijection from noncrossing partitions of [n] to noncrossing matchings on [2n]. In particular the number of noncrossing partitions of [n] is the Catalan number (1/(n+1)) binom(2n,n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The dual partition σ~ is obtained by taking the complementary regions cut out by the convex hulls or planar arcs representing σ. The equation |σ|+|σ~|=n+1 is the Euler characteristic count for the planar subdivision formed by σ and its dual. Given σ and σ~, the boundary between the primal and dual regions is a collection of nonintersecting arcs pairing the 2n interleaved boundary points, giving τ(σ). Conversely, a noncrossing matching on [2n] divides the disk into regions that can be two-colored according to whether their boundary contains original or dual vertices; the original-colored regions give a noncrossing partition σ and the dual-colored regions give σ~. These constructions are inverse.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.12 and Theorem 2.13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a circular planar electrical network Γ, let G(Γ) be its medial graph. Local electrical transformations have medial-graph counterparts: a Y−Δ or star-triangle move on Γ corresponds to a Yang-Baxter move on G(Γ), and series-parallel reductions, loop removals, and pendant removals correspond to lens removals or loop removals in the medial graph. Moreover, by results of Curtis-Ingerman-Morrow and Colin de Verdière-Gitler-Vertigan: every circular planar electrical network is electrically equivalent to a critical network, meaning one whose medial graph is lensless; two critical networks with the same response matrix are related by Y−Δ moves; the conductances of a critical network are uniquely recoverable from its response matrix; and the response-matrix space decomposes into positive cells obtained by fixing a critical network and varying its edge weights.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The local correspondence is verified by drawing the medial graph near each electrical move: the three-legged star and triangle produce the two sides of the Yang-Baxter move, while deleting loops, removing pendant edges, and series-parallel simplifications remove closed medial loops or two-strand lenses. The classification and cell-decomposition statements are cited from CIM and CGV rather than reproved in this paper. They rely on repeatedly eliminating medial lenses and loops to reach a lensless representative, showing that critical representatives differ only by star-triangle moves, and proving that response matrices determine the edge conductances in a fixed critical graph.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let N be a weighted planar bipartite graph embedded in a disk with n boundary vertices, all boundary vertices of degree 1, and suppose almost perfect matchings exist. If k is the number of white boundary vertices plus the number of interior white vertices minus the number of interior black vertices, then for each k-subset I of [n], the boundary measurement ΔI(N), defined as the weighted sum of almost perfect matchings with boundary subset I, gives homogeneous Plücker coordinates of a point M(N) in the totally nonnegative Grassmannian Gr(k,n)≥0 when all weights are nonnegative. Conversely, every point of Gr(k,n)≥0 is realized as M(N) for some planar bipartite graph N with nonnegative weights.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The forward direction is the planar-bipartite/dimer realization of Plücker coordinates; the paper cites Kuo-type graphical condensation as the mechanism ensuring that the boundary measurement sums satisfy the Plücker relations and are nonnegative. The converse is Postnikov's network parametrization of the totally nonnegative Grassmannian: every totally nonnegative point lies in some positroid cell and can be represented by a reduced planar bipartite network whose positive edge weights parametrize that cell.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the Grassmannian Gr(k,n), let Bound(k,n) denote bounded affine permutations of type (k,n). For X in Gr(k,n), define fX by fX(i)=min{j≥i : vi lies in span(vi+1,...,vj)}, using periodic columns vi+n=vi of a matrix representative. The open positroid stratum ˚Πf is the set of X with fX=f. Then the closed positroid variety Πf is the closure of ˚Πf and decomposes as Πf = disjoint union over f′≥f of ˚Πf′, where ≥ is affine Bruhat order. The totally nonnegative part (˚Πf)>0 = ˚Πf ∩ Gr(k,n)≥0 is a cell isomorphic to R_{>0}^{k(n−k)−ℓ(f)}. All points in this positive cell have the same positroid M(f), equivalently described as the intersection of cyclically shifted Schubert matroids determined by the Grassmann necklace I(f).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is cited from Postnikov and Knutson-Lam-Speyer. The affine permutation fX records the rank jumps among cyclic intervals of columns; its equivalent Grassmann necklace records lexicographically minimal nonzero Plücker coordinates in each cyclic order. Positroid cells are obtained from reduced planar bipartite graphs whose trips encode f, and network parameters identify the positive part with a positive orthant. The closure relation is affine Bruhat order, proved by comparing rank conditions or equivalently vanishing patterns of Plücker coordinates.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.10 and Lemma 3.12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X lie in the open positroid stratum ˚Πf of Gr(k,n). Suppose i < f(i) < f(i+1) < i+n+1. Let I(f)=(I1,...,In) be the Grassmann necklace of f, and assume a = Δ_{Ii+1}(X)/Δ_{Ii+1∪{i}−{i+1}}(X) is defined. Then X·xi(−a) lies in ˚Π_{f si}, where f si > f in Bruhat order. If X is totally nonnegative, the denominator is always nonzero and X·xi(−a) remains totally nonnegative. There is a dual statement for yi(−a) using the dual Grassmann necklace J(f), producing si f > f. On the network side, adding a bridge between adjacent boundary vertices i and i+1 realizes the action of xi(a) or yi(a) and changes Plücker coordinates by ΔI ↦ ΔI + a Δ_{I−{i+1}∪{i}} or ΔI ↦ ΔI + a Δ_{I−{i}∪{i+1}}, respectively, in the appropriate cases.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Right multiplication by xi(−a) subtracts a times column i from column i+1. Thus Plücker coordinates transform by ΔJ(X·xi(−a)) = ΔJ(X) − a Δ_{J−{i+1}∪{i}}(X) when i+1∈J and i∉J, and remain unchanged otherwise. The chosen ratio makes the necklace coordinate Δ_{Ii+1} vanish and therefore changes the affine permutation by the adjacent transposition si. For total nonnegativity, the proof uses a Plücker relation and a positivity lemma to show the denominator is positive and all transformed Plücker coordinates remain nonnegative. The bridge formula is proved by splitting almost perfect matchings according to whether they use the new bridge edge; if they use it, the boundary subset changes by exchanging i and i+1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A cactus network is an electrical network embedded in a cactus obtained by identifying boundary vertices of a disk according to a noncrossing partition. For cactus networks: (1) every cactus network is electrically equivalent to one whose medial graph is lensless, called critical or reduced; (2) any two electrically equivalent reduced cactus networks are related by Y−Δ transformations; and (3) every perfect matching on [2n] occurs as the medial pairing of some critical cactus network with n original boundary vertices.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Parts (1) and (2) are obtained by applying the usual circular-planar critical-network theory separately on each disk component of the cactus. For (3), start with any lensless medial graph G in a disk whose strands realize the prescribed matching τ on [2n]. The strands cut the disk into regions. Glue together original boundary vertices that lie in the same region; this produces the cactus shape. On each disk component of the resulting cactus, the restricted medial pairing is one arising from a critical circular planar network. Combining these components yields a critical cactus network with medial pairing τ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E′n be the space of circular planar electrical networks modulo electrical equivalence, embedded in projective noncrossing-partition coordinate space PNC_n by grove measurements Lσ. Let En be the Hausdorff closure of E′n in PNC_n. Then En is exactly the set of grove-measurement points L(Γ) of cactus networks Γ. Moreover, a cactus network Γ is determined up to electrical equivalence by its projective grove-measurement point L(Γ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A point of En is a limit of ordinary circular planar networks. Since the top cell of E′n is dense, the sequence may be taken in a fixed underlying critical graph with varying conductances. The conductance coordinates depend continuously on the grove point, so a limit is obtained by allowing degenerations such as infinite conductances, which identify boundary vertices according to a noncrossing partition and hence produce a cactus network. Conversely, any cactus network arises as such a degeneration. For uniqueness, decompose a cactus network Γ into circular planar components Γ(r) on the disk components of the cactus. The cactus shape is determined by L(Γ). If i and j are distinct boundary vertices in the same component Γ(r), and σij is obtained from the cactus shape σ by gluing the parts containing i and j, then Proposition 2.4 gives Λ(Γ(r))ij = − L_{σij}(Γ)/L_σ(Γ). Contributions from other disk components cancel in this ratio. Thus the response matrix of every component Γ(r) is recovered, and ordinary electrical-network uniqueness determines Γ up to electrical equivalence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 4.10 and 4.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a perfect matching τ on [2n], let Eτ be the subset of En represented by critical cactus networks with medial pairing τ. Let c(τ) be the number of crossings in any lensless medial graph representing τ. Then Eτ is parametrized by choosing one critical cactus network with medial pairing τ and allowing its edge conductances to vary positively; hence Eτ is isomorphic to R_{>0}^{c(τ)}. The compactification decomposes as a disjoint union En = ⨆_{τ∈Pn} Eτ over all matchings τ on [2n]. The zero-dimensional cells are precisely the points pσ with one nonzero grove coordinate Lσ, indexed by noncrossing partitions σ; pσ is represented by the hollow cactus whose boundary vertices are identified according to σ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A cactus network decomposes into ordinary circular planar networks on the disk components of the cactus. For a fixed critical representative, Theorem 2.13 gives positive conductance coordinates, one for each edge, and the number of edges equals the number of crossings of the lensless medial pairing. Proposition 4.2 ensures every matching occurs as a medial pairing of some critical cactus network, and critical equivalence does not change the cell. When there are no crossings, there are no edges; the cactus is hollow, and its grove measurement has a single nonzero coordinate corresponding to the boundary identification partition σ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a lensless medial graph with medial pairing τ. Suppose boundary points a,b,c,d occur in cyclic order, and τ has one strand from a to c and another from b to d. Let G′ be obtained by uncrossing one intersection of these two strands so that in G′ the endpoints are paired as a with d and b with c. Then G′ is lensless if and only if no other medial strand of G goes from the boundary arc (a,b) to the boundary arc (c,d). Consequently, whether a given local uncrossing gives a cover relation below τ depends only on τ, not on the chosen lensless medial representative G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If a third strand connects (a,b) to (c,d), then after the chosen uncrossing it is forced, by planar separation, to form a lens with one of the newly routed strands, so G′ cannot be lensless. Conversely, if no such third strand exists, the local uncrossing cannot create a self-intersection or a two-strand lens; any putative new lens would have to trap a strand crossing from (a,b) to (c,d), contradicting the hypothesis. Since the criterion is stated entirely in terms of endpoints of τ, cover relations are independent of the chosen lensless drawing.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Pn be the uncrossing partial order on matchings of [2n], where τ′ ⋖ τ when τ′ is obtained from a lensless medial graph for τ by resolving one crossing in one of the two possible ways and the result remains lensless. Then Pn is graded by the crossing number c(τ): each cover relation lowers c by exactly one.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A cover relation is defined by resolving exactly one crossing in a lensless medial graph and requiring the result to remain lensless. Thus the resulting matching has one fewer crossing in any lensless representative. Since the partial order is generated by such covers, c(τ) gives a rank function. The lensless condition and Lemma 4.12 ensure that no hidden lens-removal operation is included in a cover, so the rank drop is exactly one.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.15 and Theorem 4.16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a matching τ on [2n], regard τ as a fixed-point-free involution on [2n] and define a bounded affine permutation gτ of type (n,2n) by gτ(i)=τ(i) if i<τ(i), and gτ(i)=τ(i)+2n if i>τ(i), extended periodically. Let τtop be the matching i ↦ i+n mod 2n, and let g0=g_{τtop}. Then gτ can be written as w g0 w^{-1} for some affine permutation w with ℓ(w)=binom(n,2)−c(τ), and ℓ(gτ)=2ℓ(w). The map τ ↦ gτ identifies the uncrossing poset Pn with an induced subposet of the dual affine Bruhat order: for matchings τ and τ′, one has τ′≤τ in Pn if and only if gτ≤gτ′ in affine Bruhat order. Equivalently, larger cells in the uncrossing order correspond to smaller affine permutations gτ. In particular ℓ(gτ)=2(binom(n,2)−c(τ)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The factorization gτ=w g0 w^{-1} is proved by induction from the top matching. If τ is not top, choose an adjacent inversion gτ(i)>gτ(i+1), swap the adjacent boundary positions and their paired partners to obtain τ′ with τ′ ⋗ τ, and observe that gτ = si gτ′ si while the length changes by two. For the order comparison, first suppose τ′ ⋖ τ arises by uncrossing strands with endpoints a<b<c<d, with no forbidden strand as in Lemma 4.12. Then gτ′ is obtained from gτ by the corresponding affine transpositions, e.g. gτ′=t_{a,b} t_{c,d} gτ = t_{a,b} gτ t_{a,b}, and a direct Bruhat comparison shows gτ′>gτ. Conversely, if gτ′>gτ, choose a Bruhat cover t_{a,b} gτ above gτ. Let c=gτ(a) and d=gτ(b). The proof uses affine rank matrices r_f(i,j)=#{a′≤i : f(a′)≥j} and the rank-matrix Bruhat criterion. The rectangular regions where right and left transpositions change the rank matrix are disjoint because a<b<c<d<a+2n. Therefore any gτ′ above both one-sided covers is above the two-sided element t_{a,b} gτ t_{a,b}, which corresponds to a valid single uncrossing. Induction on Bruhat length then gives τ′≤τ. The word 'induced' follows because no extra Bruhat comparabilities occur among the image elements beyond uncrossing relations.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.18
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Define fτ(i)=gτ(i)−1 for a matching τ on [2n]. Then fτ is an electrical affine permutation of type (n−1,2n). For τ,τ′ in Pn, the following are equivalent: (1) τ′≤τ in the uncrossing order; (2) fτ≤fτ′ in affine Bruhat order; and (3) the Grassmann necklace I(fτ) is componentwise below I(fτ′) in cyclic dominance order, i.e. I_a(fτ)≤_a I_a(fτ′) for every cyclic order starting at a.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Grassmann necklace of gτ always contains the current index a in its a-th term. Passing from gτ to fτ removes that element and shifts the remaining elements by one, so dominance comparisons for I(gτ) and I(fτ) are equivalent. Theorem 3.5 identifies affine Bruhat order on bounded affine permutations with componentwise cyclic dominance order on Grassmann necklaces. Combining this with Theorem 4.16 gives the equivalence with the uncrossing order.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 4.19, 4.20, and 4.22
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A Catalan subset is an (n−1)-subset I of [2n] such that {1}∪{a+1 : a∈I} is the set of up-step positions of a Dyck path of length 2n; cyclically shifted versions are defined with respect to a cyclic order ≤_a. If I and J are Catalan subsets, then I≤J in dominance order if and only if the Dyck path P(I) never goes below P(J). For every matching τ, its Grassmann necklace I(τ)=I(fτ) consists of cyclically shifted Catalan subsets: walking clockwise from boundary point a, mark U the first time a strand endpoint is encountered and D the second time, and the U positions determine the corresponding necklace term. For every a∈[2n], the map σ ↦ I_a(σ) from noncrossing partitions σ to a-shifted Catalan subsets is a bijection. Explicitly, if σ has parts σ1,...,σr and dual σ~ has parts σ~1,...,σ~s, then [2n]\I_a(σ) is the union of the ≤_a-maximal elements of all parts of σ and all parts of σ~.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Dyck-path criterion for dominance is the standard translation between partial sums of U/D sequences and coordinatewise comparison of up-step positions. For a matching τ, the first/second encounter rule always gives n U's and n D's, and in the noncrossing case gives a Dyck path; for general τ, the necklace terms associated to fτ are obtained by this same first-encounter bookkeeping. Formula (4.3) follows because a boundary or dual part contributes exactly the unique endpoint at which the corresponding region closes in cyclic order. Since |σ|+|σ~|=n+1, the complement has size n+1, so I_a(σ) has size n−1. The construction is reversible from the Catalan path, giving the bijection.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For an electrical network Γ, construct a planar bipartite graph N(Γ) by placing black vertices for vertices and faces of Γ, white vertices for edges of Γ, and boundary vertices 1,2,...,2n interleaved with the original and dual boundary vertices. For an (n−1)-subset I of [2n], let E(I) be the set of noncrossing partitions σ such that every part of σ and every part of the dual partition σ~ contains exactly one element not in I; such σ are called concordant with I. Then there is a weight-preserving bijection between almost perfect matchings of N(Γ) with boundary subset I and pairs consisting of a grove F of Γ with boundary partition σ plus a compatible rooting, where σ is concordant with I. Consequently the Plücker boundary measurements of N(Γ) satisfy ΔI(N(Γ)) = Σ_{σ∈E(I)} Lσ(Γ), where Lσ(Γ) is the grove measurement of Γ. In particular M(N(Γ)) lies in the linear subspace H defined by these concordance sums.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Given a grove F in Γ, let F~ be the dual spanning forest in the planar dual Γ~, determined by including the dual edge exactly when the primal edge is absent from F. If σ and σ~ are their boundary partitions, choose one root boundary vertex in each component of σ and σ~; this rooting is equivalent to choosing the complement of I, with one non-I element in every primal and dual block. Orient each tree component of F and F~ toward its root. For each edge e of Γ, the corresponding white vertex we of N(Γ) is matched to the black vertex at the source of e in the oriented primal or dual forest, depending on whether e lies in F or its dual lies in F~. Remaining unmatched boundary-adjacent black vertices are matched to the selected root boundary vertices. This gives an almost perfect matching with boundary subset I. Conversely, from an almost perfect matching Π of N(Γ), put e in F if the white vertex we is matched to a primal vertex of Γ, and put the dual edge in F~ otherwise. A cycle in F would enclose a region with an odd number of bipartite vertices left to be matched internally, impossible; similarly for F~. Thus F and F~ are dual forests, and the boundary vertices matched to the boundary determine the rooting. Summing weights over this bijection gives the displayed linear formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 5.13 and 5.15
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let xi(a) and yi(a) be the elementary one-parameter subgroups of GL_{2n} acting on Gr(n−1,2n) by adjacent column operations, with indices cyclic. Define ui(a)=xi(a) yi_{i−1}(a)=yi_{i−1}(a) xi(a). Then the ui(a) satisfy the electrical braid relations: ui(a)ui(b)=ui(a+b); ui(a)uj(b)=uj(b)ui(a) for |i−j|≥2; and ui(a)ui±1(b)ui(c)=ui±1(bc/(a+c+abc)) ui(a+c+abc) ui±1(ab/(a+c+abc)). Moreover the linear slice Xn = Gr(n−1,2n)∩H is stable under every ui(a).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The braid relations are verified directly inside GL_{2n} using the elementary-matrix relations for xi and yi and the commutation xi(a)yi−1(a)=yi−1(a)xi(a). Stability of Xn is proved in grove coordinates. If X∈Xn is represented by noncrossing-partition coordinates Lσ with ΔI=Σ a_{Iσ}Lσ, then applying ui(a) corresponds to adding a boundary spike or boundary edge in the electrical-network model. The resulting grove coordinates L′σ are explicit linear combinations of the Lσ: for instance, in the odd boundary-spike case, if the relevant boundary vertex is isolated in σ then L′σ=Lσ+a times the sum of Lκ over partitions κ obtained by merging that vertex with another part, while otherwise L′σ=Lσ. A direct check with the concordance matrix a_{Iσ} shows that the transformed Plücker coordinates are again of the form Σ a_{Iσ}L′σ, so ui(a) preserves H and hence Xn.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.17
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be an electrical or cactus network such that its grove point L(Γ) lies in the electrical cell Eτ, where τ is its medial pairing. Then the associated planar bipartite graph N(Γ) represents a point M(N(Γ)) in the open positroid stratum ˚Π_{fτ} of Gr(n−1,2n), where fτ is the electrical affine permutation associated to τ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By electrical equivalence, reduce to Γ critical. The proof is by induction on n and then on the number of edges. If Γ has no edges, it is a hollow cactus; one checks directly from the bipartite graph N(Γ) and its trips that f_{N(Γ)}=fτ. If Γ has an isolated boundary vertex or two glued adjacent boundary vertices, remove that feature, apply induction to the smaller network, and check directly how the affine permutation changes. Otherwise a critical representative has either a boundary spike or a boundary edge. Removing it gives Γ′ with known stratum by induction. Adding the spike or edge corresponds, by Proposition 5.12 and Lemma 3.12, to adding two adjacent bridges in N(Γ′), i.e. applying xi and yi operations, and this changes the bounded affine permutation exactly from f_{τ′} to fτ. Thus M(N(Γ)) lies in ˚Π_{fτ}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.18
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a noncrossing partition σ, let M(σ) be the collection of (n−1)-subsets I of [2n] concordant with σ. Then M(σ) is the positroid M(f_{τ(σ)}) associated to the electrical affine permutation of the noncrossing matching τ(σ). Equivalently, M(σ) = {I : I≥_a I_a(σ) for every a∈[2n]}, where I_a(σ) is the Catalan Grassmann-necklace term associated to σ and ≥_a is cyclic dominance order. In particular, each I_a(σ) is concordant with σ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The zero-dimensional cactus-network point pσ has exactly one nonzero grove coordinate Lσ. By Proposition 5.17, its image under the bipartite construction lies in the open positroid stratum ˚Π_{f_{τ(σ)}}. Theorem 5.10 says that the nonzero Plücker coordinates of this image are exactly those I concordant with σ. But every point in a positive positroid stratum has positroid M(f), and Theorem 3.9 describes M(f) as the intersection of cyclic Schubert matroids determined by the Grassmann necklace. Therefore the concordance collection equals M(f_{τ(σ)}) and has the stated dominance characterization.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.19
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The map ι: En → Gr(n−1,2n)≥0 induced by Γ ↦ N(Γ), equivalently by the linear formulas ΔI = Σ_{σ∈E(I)} Lσ, is injective.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Restrict the linear system ΔI=Σ Lσ to Catalan subsets I of the form I=I1(σ0), one for each noncrossing partition σ0. Proposition 5.18 implies σ0 is concordant with I1(σ0). If another σ′ is concordant with I1(σ0), then I1(σ′)≤I1(σ0) in dominance order. Therefore, after ordering noncrossing partitions by this dominance order, the restricted transition matrix from the Lσ to these Catalan Plücker coordinates is triangular with nonzero diagonal entries. Hence the Lσ are recovered uniquely from the ΔI, proving injectivity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.25
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f be an electrical affine permutation in Elec(n), and suppose i < f(i) < f(i+1). Let I=I_{i+1}(f) be the corresponding Grassmann-necklace term, let J=J_i(f) be the dual Grassmann-necklace term recording starting positions of balls, and set I′=I−{i+1}∪{i} and J′=J−{i−1}∪{i}. Then si−1 f si is again electrical and lies above f in the precise sense that si−1 f si > si−1 f and f si > f. For every X∈X∩˚Πf, one has ΔI(X)=ΔJ(X) and ΔI′(X)=ΔJ′(X). If a=ΔI(X)/ΔI′(X)=ΔJ(X)/ΔJ′(X) is defined, then ui(−a)·X lies in X∩˚Π_{si−1 f si}. If X is totally nonnegative, this ratio is always defined and ui(−a)·X remains totally nonnegative.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The electrical condition on f implies a complementarity relation between the shifted set {a+1 mod 2n : a∈I_{i+1}(f)}, the dual set J_i(f), and {i,i+1}; this is Lemma 5.22. Lemma 5.23 shows that i+1∈I and i−1∈J, so I′ and J′ are valid. The equalities ΔI=ΔJ and ΔI′=ΔJ′ are proved by comparing concordance sets inside the electroid E(f): Lemma 5.24 shows E(I)∩E(f)=E(J)∩E(f) and likewise for I′,J′, using uniqueness of the noncrossing partition with a given Catalan necklace term and a local grove argument involving an edge corresponding to the crossing between strands i and i+1. Since X lies in the slice X, Plücker coordinates are sums over the same Lσ and hence are equal. Applying Proposition 3.10 first to xi(−a) and then to yi−1(−a), together with Proposition 5.15 preserving X, moves X into the stratum ˚Π_{si−1 f si}. Positivity and nonvanishing of the denominator in the totally nonnegative case are exactly the positivity conclusions of Proposition 3.10.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be the projective linear subspace of Plücker space whose coordinates satisfy ΔI=Σ_{σ concordant with I} Lσ for some noncrossing-partition coordinates Lσ, and let X = Gr(n−1,2n)∩H. For a bounded affine permutation f of type (n−1,2n), define ˚Xf = X∩˚Πf. Then ˚Xf is nonempty if and only if f is an electrical affine permutation, equivalently f=fτ for some matching τ on [2n]. Moreover, for every τ, the closed electroid variety X_{fτ}=X∩Π_{fτ} decomposes as X_{fτ} = disjoint union over τ′≤τ of ˚X_{fτ′}, where ≤ is the uncrossing order on matchings. The statement holds over C as well as over R.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on the affine length of f. If ℓ(f)=0, the stratum is the top positroid cell and f is the top electrical permutation. For ℓ(f)>0, choose a descent i with f(i)>f(i+1). For a generic parameter a, apply ui(a) to X∈X∩˚Πf. By Proposition 5.15 the result remains in X, and by the behavior of adjacent column operations it lies in a strictly lower positroid stratum ˚Π_{f′}, where f′ is either f si or si−1 f si depending on the Bruhat comparison. By induction, f′ is electrical. One checks that f′ has i < f′(i) < f′(i+1), so Proposition 5.25 applies in reverse: the original point X can occur only from the electrical permutation si−1 f′ si. Hence f itself is electrical. The closure decomposition then follows by intersecting the positroid closure relation Πf = ⋃_{f′≥f} ˚Πf′ with X and translating Bruhat comparisons among electrical permutations into uncrossing order via Corollary 4.18.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The construction Γ ↦ N(Γ) gives an injection ι: En → Gr(n−1,2n) whose image is exactly X≥0 = X∩Gr(n−1,2n)≥0. Thus every totally nonnegative point in the linear slice X is represented by a cactus network. Furthermore, ι maps each electrical cell Eτ onto (˚X_{fτ})≥0 = ˚X_{fτ}∩Gr(n−1,2n)≥0, and the Hausdorff closures satisfy ˚X_{fτ} closure = disjoint union over τ′≤τ of ˚X_{fτ′} and Eτ closure = disjoint union over τ′≤τ of Eτ′.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 5.10 shows that ι(En) is contained in X≥0, because Plücker coordinates of N(Γ) are nonnegative concordance sums of grove coordinates. Proposition 5.17 identifies the stratum: if L(Γ)∈Eτ, then ι(Γ)∈˚Π_{fτ}, hence ι(Eτ)⊂(˚X_{fτ})≥0. Proposition 5.19 proves injectivity by triangular inversion on Catalan Plücker coordinates. For surjectivity, take X∈X≥0∩˚Π_f; by Theorem 5.6, f=fτ is electrical. Induct first on n and then on the codimension of the positroid stratum. If f has a fixed point f(i)=i with f(i+1)=i+2n−1, Proposition 5.21 deletes the corresponding isolated boundary vertex and produces a point Y∈X_{n−1}≥0; by induction Y is represented by a smaller cactus network, and adding back an isolated boundary vertex represents X. If f has no fixed point, choose i with i<f(i)<f(i+1). Proposition 5.25 gives X′=ui(−a)X in a larger electrical stratum and still totally nonnegative. By induction X′ is represented by a cactus network Γ′; applying the corresponding boundary spike or boundary edge operation vi(a) to Γ′ gives a network representing X. The closure assertions combine the positroid closure order with Theorem 5.6 and the electrical closure order of Proposition 5.26.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.26
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the cell decomposition En=⨆_{τ∈Pn}Eτ, the closure order is exactly the uncrossing order: Eτ closure = disjoint union over τ′≤τ of Eτ′.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If τ′≤τ, then τ′ is obtained from τ by a sequence of allowed uncrossings. In a medial graph, resolving a crossing corresponds in the electrical network to either deleting the corresponding edge, i.e. setting its conductance to 0, or contracting it, i.e. sending its conductance to infinity. Thus Eτ′ lies in the closure of Eτ. Conversely, if L∈closure(Eτ), injectivity and continuity of ι imply ι(L) lies in the closure of ι(Eτ). Proposition 5.17 places ι(Eτ) in ˚X_{fτ}, so the positroid closure relation gives f_{τ′}≥fτ for the stratum containing ι(L). Corollary 4.18 translates this Bruhat inequality back to τ′≤τ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.27
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a lensless medial graph. Let G′ be obtained from G by uncrossing any number of crossings in arbitrary choices of resolutions, and then removing any lenses or loops by lens removals and loop removals. Then the medial pairing τ(G′) is below τ(G) in the uncrossing poset Pn.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Uncrossing crossings in G corresponds to sending conductances of selected edges of the associated critical network to 0 or infinity. Lens and loop removals correspond to electrical equivalences such as series-parallel reduction, loop removal, or pendant removal, which do not change the grove point. Therefore the cell for τ(G′) lies in the closure of the cell for τ(G). Proposition 5.26 identifies this closure relation with the uncrossing order, giving τ(G′)≤τ(G).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.28
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any matching η on [2n], let E(η) be the electroid of the cell Eη, i.e. the set of noncrossing partitions σ such that the grove coordinate Lσ is nonzero for any point in Eη. Then E(η) = {σ∈NC_n : τ(σ)≤η}, where τ(σ) is the noncrossing matching associated to σ and ≤ is the uncrossing order on matchings.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If Lσ(Γ)≠0 for Γ∈Eη, choose a grove F in Γ with boundary partition σ. Delete all edges not in F and contract the edges of F. The resulting cactus network has no edges and represents the zero-dimensional point pσ, so pσ lies in the closure of Eη. By Proposition 5.26 this means τ(σ)≤η. Conversely, if τ(σ)≤η, the definition of the uncrossing order gives a sequence of edge deletions and contractions from a critical representative of η to a hollow cactus representing pσ. The contracted edges can be chosen to contain a grove with boundary partition σ, so in the original network the coordinate Lσ is nonzero. Hence σ belongs to E(η).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.32 and Corollary 5.33
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a matching τ, let I(τ)=(I1(τ),...,I_{2n}(τ)) be its Catalan Grassmann necklace. For each s, let σ_s(τ) be the unique noncrossing partition whose s-shifted Catalan subset I_s(σ_s(τ)) equals I_s(τ). Then the electroid of τ is E(τ) = {σ∈NC_n : σ≥_s σ_s(τ) for every s∈[2n]}, where σ≥_s κ means I_s(σ)≥_s I_s(κ) in cyclic dominance order. Consequently, for matchings τ and τ′, the following are equivalent: τ′≤τ; E(τ′)⊆E(τ); and the associated partition-necklace data satisfy Σ(τ′)≥Σ(τ) componentwise in the shifted dominance orders.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key point is to show that if I_s(σ)=I_s(τ), then τ(σ)≤τ. By Corollary 4.18, this is equivalent to showing I_t(τ)≤_t I_t(σ) for every t. The paper translates these inequalities into interval counts: for a circular interval [t,r], let τ[t,r] be the number of strands of τ with both endpoints in [t,r]. The dominance inequalities are equivalent to τ[t,r]≤τ(σ)[t,r] for all intervals. Taking s outside the interval, view τ(σ) as a U/D sequence starting at s; strands inside [t,r] correspond to matched U-D pairs in a consecutive subsequence. Adding extra U/D letters to a sequence cannot decrease the number of matched pairs, giving the interval-count inequality. The formula for E(τ) then follows from Theorem 5.28 and the necklace criterion in Corollary 4.18. The equivalences in Corollary 5.33 are immediate from this dominance description and the order-ideal description of E(τ).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 5.35 and 5.36
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The grove coordinates Lσ of a cactus network satisfy quadratic relations induced by the Plücker relations under the linear map ΔI=Σ_{σ∈E(I)}Lσ. Explicitly, for I={i1<...<i_{n−1}}, J={j1<...<j_{n−1}}, and 1≤k<n−1, the Plücker relation obtained by swapping the first k indices of J with k indices of I becomes Σ_{σ∈E(I), κ∈E(J)} Lσ Lκ = Σ_{I′,J′} (−1)^{a(I′,J′)} Σ_{σ∈E(I′), κ∈E(J′)} Lσ Lκ, where the sum is over the standard Plücker swapped pairs I′,J′ and E(I) is empty if I has repeated elements. Conversely, a point p in projective noncrossing-partition coordinate space PNC_n lies in En if and only if all Lσ(p) are nonnegative and these quadratic relations hold; it is enough to impose the k=1 Plücker relations.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If p=L(Γ), Theorem 5.10 expresses every Plücker coordinate ΔI of N(Γ) as a concordance sum of grove coordinates. Substituting these sums into the Plücker relations gives the displayed quadratic identities. Conversely, start from nonnegative coordinates Lσ satisfying the k=1 quadratic identities and define ΔI by the concordance sums. The k=1 Plücker relations cut out the Grassmannian inside Plücker projective space, so the ΔI define a point X in X. Nonnegativity of the Lσ implies X is totally nonnegative. By Theorem 5.8, X is represented by a cactus network, and by the injectivity argument of Proposition 5.19 the original Lσ are exactly its grove coordinates. Hence p∈En.

proof_techniques.name: Cactus compactification to include all matchings
proof_techniques.description: The paper enlarges ordinary circular planar electrical networks by allowing boundary vertices to be identified according to a noncrossing partition, producing cactus networks. This removes the restriction that only some medial pairings occur for ordinary disk networks: Proposition 4.2 shows that every perfect matching on [2n] is the medial pairing of some critical cactus network. The compactification En is then the closure of ordinary electrical networks in grove-coordinate projective space, and Theorem 4.9 identifies it exactly with grove points of cactus networks.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem already considers arbitrary crossing matchings with boundary endpoints and local resolutions. A useful analogue is to realize the relevant matchings as cells of a compactified positive space after adding suitable boundary identifications or constraints, so that all resolutions live in one closure-stratified object.

proof_techniques.name: Medial graphs convert crossing resolutions into edge deletion/contraction
proof_techniques.description: In the electrical-network setting, crossings of medial strands correspond to edges of the network. Resolving a crossing in one way corresponds to deleting that edge, while resolving it in the other way corresponds to contracting it, i.e. sending its conductance to 0 or infinity. This is the mechanism behind Proposition 5.26, which identifies topological closure of cells with the combinatorial uncrossing order.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The CW complex F_w is built by replacing crossings with intervals whose endpoints are the two local resolutions. Modeling each crossing as an edge parameter with endpoints 0 and infinity could turn F_w or a related subcomplex into a parameter-space closure, making contractibility approachable through cell closure geometry.

proof_techniques.name: Lensless representatives and local uncrossing criterion
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses lensless medial graphs as canonical reduced representatives of matchings. Lemma 4.12 gives a purely endpoint-based criterion for when uncrossing a crossing of two strands in a lensless graph remains lensless: no third strand may connect the two opposite boundary arcs. This makes the uncrossing cover relation independent of the particular drawing.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, isotopy classes of crossing arcs may have redundant bigons or multiple crossings. A reduction to lensless or minimal representatives, together with an endpoint criterion for valid resolutions, could simplify the attaching combinatorics and prevent dependence on choices of drawings.

proof_techniques.name: Affine Bruhat encoding of matchings
proof_techniques.description: Each matching τ is encoded by a bounded affine permutation gτ, or equivalently fτ=gτ−1. Theorem 4.16 and Corollary 4.18 identify the uncrossing order with the dual of an induced affine Bruhat subposet and also with componentwise cyclic dominance of Grassmann necklaces. This replaces recursive crossing-resolution definitions with rank-matrix or necklace inequalities.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the word-label constraints in F_w can be translated into conditions on endpoints or intervals, one can try to describe the allowed cells as an induced subposet, interval, or order ideal in affine Bruhat order. Known structural tools for Bruhat-type posets, such as shellability or Eulerian properties, may then become available.

proof_techniques.name: Embedding into a totally nonnegative Grassmannian slice
proof_techniques.description: Lam embeds En into Gr(n−1,2n)≥0 as X≥0, where X is a linear subspace cut out by concordance sums between Plücker coordinates and grove coordinates. Positroid strata intersect X only in the electrical strata indexed by matchings. This imports the positroid stratification, affine Bruhat closure order, and bridge parametrizations into the electrical-network compactification.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A possible route for F_w is to construct a positive or semialgebraic model whose cells are the allowed matchings and whose coordinate hypercube directions are crossing resolutions. If such a model is a nonnegative slice or a deformation retract of one, contractibility could follow from positive-geometric methods rather than direct combinatorial collapsing.

proof_techniques.name: Temperley-type grove-to-dimer bijection
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 5.10 gives a concrete bijection from rooted groves in an electrical network and its dual to almost perfect matchings in an associated planar bipartite graph. Boundary partitions σ contribute to Plücker coordinates ΔI exactly when σ is concordant with I, yielding linear coordinate formulas ΔI=Σ_{σ∈E(I)}Lσ.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem imposes compatibility conditions on regions adjacent to matching arcs via inverse letters. A similar dual or rooted-forest encoding might transform those regional constraints into linear or matching constraints in an auxiliary planar bipartite graph.

proof_techniques.name: Induction by boundary bridges and electrical generators
proof_techniques.description: The proof of realizability in Theorem 5.8 uses an induction that either removes a fixed point of an electrical affine permutation, corresponding to an isolated boundary vertex, or applies a positivity-preserving generator ui(−a) to move to a larger stratum. Proposition 5.25 provides the key reduction step and ensures the operation remains inside the linear slice and inside the totally nonnegative part.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For contractibility of F_w, one could seek an induction that removes an outermost or forced pair, or else chooses a crossing whose resolution defines a deformation or collapse. The quasi-reduced hypothesis may be useful for guaranteeing such reduction steps do not create forbidden local configurations.

proof_techniques.name: Triangular recovery from Catalan coordinates
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 5.19 proves injectivity of the grove-to-Plücker map by restricting to Catalan subsets I_a(σ). Concordance with a Catalan subset is triangular with respect to dominance order on Dyck paths, so the noncrossing-partition coordinates Lσ can be recovered one by one from selected Plücker coordinates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If F_w is represented by coordinates indexed by fully resolved noncrossing matchings, a triangular order on resolutions may allow one to prove uniqueness, acyclicity of an incidence matrix, or construct explicit collapses ordered by Dyck-path dominance.

proof_techniques.name: Electroids as lower ideals of noncrossing matchings
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 5.28 shows that for a matching η, the set of nonzero grove coordinates in the cell Eη is exactly the set of noncrossing partitions σ whose associated noncrossing matching τ(σ) lies below η in the uncrossing order. Theorem 5.32 refines this by describing the same set as an intersection of cyclic dominance order filters determined by the Catalan necklace of η.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The vertices of F_w are fully resolved matchings satisfying label constraints. If the allowed vertices below a crossing matching form an order ideal or intersection of dominance conditions, this can be exploited to analyze links, stars, and possible collapsibility.

proof_techniques.name: Quadratic coordinate characterization via Plücker relations
proof_techniques.description: Propositions 5.35 and 5.36 characterize En inside projective noncrossing-partition space by nonnegativity plus quadratic relations obtained from Plücker relations after substituting concordance sums. Thus a combinatorially defined matching compactification is equivalently a semialgebraic positive variety.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: One might try to encode the word-constrained matching complex as a real nonnegative solution set of local quadratic or Plücker-type relations. Such an algebraic realization could provide global topological information or allow deformation arguments.

other_useful_info: The introduction notes that Alman-Lian-Tran, Kenyon, and Huang-Wen-Xie studied the uncrossing partial order on matchings, and that Lam's later paper [Lam15] uses Theorem 4.16 to prove that the poset obtained from Pn by adjoining a minimum element is Eulerian.
