The paper repeatedly uses standard positroid facts from Postnikov and Knutson-Lam-Speyer: positroid cell closures are controlled by affine Bruhat order, and positive cells are parametrized by reduced planar bipartite graphs. The unique top matching τtop is i ↦ i+n mod 2n, while the zero-dimensional cells of En are Catalan-number many and correspond to noncrossing partitions.
why_relevant: Relates matchings, uncrossing order, noncrossing partitions, affine Bruhat order, and a compactified electrical-network space. This gives a geometric route for proving contractibility or ball-like behavior of crossing-resolution complexes.

title: The Uncrossing Partial Order on Matchings is Eulerian
overall_summary: The paper proves that the augmented uncrossing poset hat P_n of perfect matchings of 2n cyclically ordered boundary points, ordered by resolving crossings of lensless strand diagrams and with a new bottom element adjoined, is Eulerian. The proof uses an embedding of P_n into the dual of an induced subposet of the affine symmetric-group Bruhat order, translating local adjacent-strand operations into simple-reflection descent manipulations. The main combinatorial mechanism is to choose adjacent boundary labels i,i+1, classify their strands as noncrossing, crossing, or matched, and then use the operation sigma -> s_i·sigma either as a parity-reversing involution on intervals or as part of an inductive interval decomposition. The paper also records useful facts about medial graphs with lenses: lensless reductions are well-defined, and resolving arbitrary crossings in a graph with lenses can only move downward in the uncrossing order. For the original CW complex built from crossing matchings and local resolutions, the most relevant contributions are the Eulerian signed-rank cancellation, the adjacent-toggle involutions, and the lens/loop handling needed when resolutions create non-lensless diagrams or closed components.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 1 setup: the uncrossing poset P_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix n. Let P_n be the set of perfect matchings of the cyclically ordered boundary points 1,2,...,2n on a circle. A matching tau in P_n may be represented by a medial graph or strand diagram; such a diagram is lensless if any two strands intersect at most once. For tau in P_n, let c(tau) be the number of crossings in any lensless medial graph representing tau. Define a cover relation tau' ⋖ tau if there is a lensless medial graph G representing tau such that resolving one crossing of G gives a lensless medial graph G' representing tau'. The partial order P_n is the transitive closure of these cover relations. The paper states that P_n is graded with rank function c, has a unique maximum element, and has Catalan number C_n minimal elements. Let hat P_n be obtained from P_n by adjoining a new minimum element hat0 with c(hat0) = -1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These are setup facts stated in the introduction, with references to alternative descriptions in [ALT, Ken, Lam]. The cover relation is designed so that resolving one crossing lowers c by one, and the cited earlier work supplies well-definedness and the graded-poset structure. The unique maximum and Catalan many minima are also stated as known structural properties of this uncrossing poset.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a medial graph or strand diagram on the 2n boundary points, possibly with lenses, where a lens means a pair of strands that intersect two or more times. Suppose G' and G'' are two lensless medial graphs obtained from G by repeatedly applying the three local moves displayed in the paper: the Yang-Baxter move, lens removal, and loop removal. If G' and G'' are interpreted as lensless representatives of matchings in P_n, then they represent the same matching tau in P_n. Consequently, the matching represented by a medial graph with lenses is well-defined by reducing it to any lensless graph using these moves.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper gives only a short justification: the result follows from the interpretation of medial graphs in terms of circular planar electrical networks, as developed in [ALT, Ken, Lam]. The role of the lemma is to ensure that although a graph with lenses may have many possible reduction sequences using Yang-Baxter, lens-removal, and loop-removal moves, all lensless outcomes define the same element of P_n. This well-definedness is later used when resolving crossings produces diagrams that are not initially lensless or that contain removable closed loops.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a medial graph, possibly with lenses, that represents a matching tau in P_n in the sense of Lemma 1. Let G' be obtained from G by resolving any number of crossings, in arbitrary choices of the two local resolutions, and by removing all closed interior loops that result. If G' represents a matching tau' in P_n, then tau >= tau' in the uncrossing partial order P_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof translates the operation to circular planar electrical networks. In the language of [Lam], G is the medial graph of an electrical network Gamma with response point L(Gamma) lying in the electroid stratum E_tau. Resolving a crossing in the medial graph corresponds to either deleting an edge of Gamma or contracting an edge of Gamma; closed interior loops correspond to isolated interior components after these operations and can be discarded. Thus G' is the medial graph of a network Gamma' obtained from Gamma by edge deletions and contractions. The closure relation for electroid strata, cited as [Lam, Theorem 5.7], says that the closure of E_tau is the disjoint union of E_eta over eta <= tau. Therefore the stratum of Gamma', namely E_tau', must satisfy tau' <= tau.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let tilde S_{2n} be the affine symmetric group with Bruhat order, and let s_i be the simple reflection indexed by i in Z/2nZ. For f in tilde S_{2n}, define the left descent set D_L(f) = {i in Z/2nZ : s_i f < f} and the right descent set D_R(f) = {i in Z/2nZ : f s_i < f}. If f <= g in Bruhat order and i belongs to D_L(g) but not D_L(f), then f <= s_i g and s_i f <= g. If f <= g and i belongs to D_R(g) but not D_R(f), then f <= g s_i and f s_i <= g.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the standard lifting property for Bruhat order, cited from Björner--Brenti, Proposition 2.2.7. The proof is not reproduced in the paper. Its use here is to turn a descent-set difference between the affine permutations corresponding to two matchings into two new Bruhat inequalities, which then translate back into inequalities in the uncrossing poset because P_n is embedded as the dual of an induced Bruhat subposet.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Adjacent-strand classification and the operation s_i·tau
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For tau in P_n and i in Z/2nZ, classify the two strands beginning at adjacent boundary labels i and i+1 as follows: i is in A(tau) if the strands labeled i and i+1 do not cross; i is in B(tau) if those strands cross; and i is in C(tau) if i is matched with i+1, so the two boundary labels lie on the same strand. These three sets A(tau), B(tau), C(tau) form a disjoint union of Z/2nZ. Let iota: P_n -> tilde S_{2n} be Lam's injection into the affine symmetric group, write g_tau = iota(tau), and define s_i·tau by g_{s_i·tau} = s_i g_tau s_i. Then i in A(tau) exactly when s_i g_tau s_i < g_tau, equivalently s_i·tau covers tau in P_n; i in B(tau) exactly when s_i g_tau s_i > g_tau, equivalently s_i·tau is covered by tau; and i in C(tau) exactly when s_i g_tau s_i = g_tau, equivalently s_i·tau = tau.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a key package of definitions and equivalences stated before Lemma 4. The paper explains the geometric meaning in one direction: if i lies in A(tau), then s_i·tau is obtained by adding a crossing between the strands labeled i and i+1 close to the boundary at i and i+1. The inequalities involving g_tau come from the embedding of P_n into the dual Bruhat order; adding a crossing in P_n corresponds to moving downward in Bruhat order for the associated affine permutation, while removing a crossing corresponds to moving upward in Bruhat order.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let tau <= eta in P_n. Let i in Z/2nZ. If i belongs to A(tau) and to B(eta), meaning the adjacent strands i,i+1 do not cross in tau but do cross in eta, then tau <= s_i·eta and s_i·tau <= eta in P_n, where s_i·rho is defined by g_{s_i·rho} = s_i g_rho s_i under Lam's affine-Bruhat embedding.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because the embedding iota realizes P_n as dual to an induced Bruhat subposet, tau <= eta in P_n translates to g_eta <= g_tau in affine Bruhat order. The condition i in A(tau) implies i lies in both D_L(g_tau) and D_R(g_tau). The condition i in B(eta) implies i lies in neither D_L(g_eta) nor D_R(g_eta). Apply the Bruhat lifting property of Lemma 3 to f = g_eta and g = g_tau using the left descent difference: this gives s_i g_eta <= g_tau. Since g_{s_i·eta} = s_i g_eta s_i is greater than s_i g_eta in Bruhat order, i is not a right descent of s_i g_eta. Applying Lemma 3 again on the right gives s_i g_eta s_i <= g_tau, i.e. g_{s_i·eta} <= g_tau, which is equivalent by duality to tau <= s_i·eta. The proof of s_i·tau <= eta is obtained by the symmetric argument interchanging left and right descent sets.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let tau <= eta in P_n. If i belongs to A(tau), meaning the strands beginning at adjacent boundary labels i and i+1 are distinct and do not cross in tau, then i does not belong to C(eta); equivalently, eta cannot match i directly with i+1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper says this follows easily from the uncrossing definition. The underlying point is that in a downward sequence of crossing resolutions from eta to tau, the configuration in which the adjacent boundary labels i and i+1 form one strand in eta cannot become, at the lower element tau, two distinct adjacent strands that do not cross. This lemma is used to rule out fixed points of the operation sigma -> s_i·sigma when constructing parity-reversing involutions on intervals.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P_n be the uncrossing poset of matchings of 2n cyclically ordered boundary points, ranked by c(tau), the number of crossings in a lensless medial graph. Let hat P_n be P_n with a new minimum element hat0 adjoined and ranked by c(hat0) = -1. A finite graded poset with unique minimum and maximum is Eulerian if, for every interval [x,y] with x < y, the number of elements of even rank in [x,y] equals the number of elements of odd rank in [x,y]. Then hat P_n is Eulerian.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For any subset S of hat P_n, write chi(S) = sum_{rho in S} (-1)^{c(rho)}. It suffices to prove chi([tau,eta]) = 0 for every proper interval. First consider intervals tau < eta entirely inside P_n. The proof is by descending induction on c(tau)+c(eta), with rank-one intervals and the top-base case clear. Since tau is not maximal, A(tau) is nonempty; choose i in A(tau). Lemma 5 rules out i in C(eta), leaving two cases. Case 1: i in A(eta). Then s_i·eta covers eta. One has [tau,eta] = [tau,s_i·eta] minus T, where T = {sigma : tau <= sigma <= s_i·eta and sigma not <= eta}. The key claim is T = {sigma : s_i·tau <= sigma <= s_i·eta and sigma not <= eta}. To prove it, take sigma in T. If i in A(sigma), then since i in B(s_i·eta), Lemma 4 applied to sigma < s_i·eta gives sigma <= eta, contradiction. If i in C(sigma), this contradicts Lemma 5 applied to tau <= sigma. Therefore i in B(sigma), and Lemma 4 applied to tau <= sigma gives s_i·tau <= sigma. The reverse inclusion is immediate. Hence T = [s_i·tau,s_i·eta] minus [s_i·tau,eta]. By the induction hypothesis, the signed chi of each interval appearing in this difference, and also of [tau,s_i·eta], is zero; hence chi([tau,eta]) = 0. Case 2: i in B(eta). For every sigma in [tau,eta], Lemma 5 rules out i in C(sigma). If i in A(sigma), Lemma 4 applied to sigma <= eta shows s_i·sigma <= eta, and tau <= sigma <= s_i·sigma gives s_i·sigma in the interval. If i in B(sigma), Lemma 4 applied to tau <= sigma shows tau <= s_i·sigma, and s_i·sigma <= sigma <= eta. Thus sigma -> s_i·sigma is a well-defined involution of [tau,eta] with no fixed points, and it changes c by 1, so it cancels even and odd ranks. Case 3, i in C(eta), is impossible by Lemma 5. This proves all proper intervals inside P_n. Now consider intervals [hat0,eta]. The case c(eta)=0 is immediate, so assume c(eta) >= 1 and choose i in B(eta), which is nonempty. The same adjacent-toggle argument pairs, with opposite parity, all nonzero sigma <= eta for which i is not in C(sigma); the remaining set is S = {hat0} union {sigma in P_n : sigma < eta and i in C(sigma)}. The paper then proves S has a unique maximal element kappa. Choose a medial graph G for eta such that the strands starting at i and i+1 cross at a point q before either meets any other strand. Resolve q in the direction that matches i directly with i+1, and let kappa be the matching represented by the resulting graph G'. For any sigma in S, a lensless graph G'' for sigma can be obtained from G by uncrossing a subset of crossings. Since i is in C(sigma), q must be among the uncrossed crossings. If q is resolved in G'' in the same direction as in G', then G'' is obtained from G' by further uncrossings, so Lemma 2 gives sigma <= kappa. If q is resolved in the other direction, changing that local resolution to the G' direction produces a graph representing the same sigma after a closed interior loop is removed; Lemma 2 again gives sigma <= kappa. Thus S = [hat0,kappa]. By induction chi(S)=0, and together with the parity cancellation outside S this gives chi([hat0,eta])=0. Therefore every proper interval in hat P_n has equal numbers of even- and odd-rank elements.

proof_techniques.name: Translate uncrossing questions to affine Bruhat lifting
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses Lam's embedding iota: P_n -> tilde S_{2n}, writing g_tau = iota(tau), which realizes P_n as dual to an induced subposet of affine Bruhat order. Local operations on adjacent boundary strands are encoded by conjugation g_tau -> s_i g_tau s_i. Whether this operation adds a crossing, removes a crossing, or fixes the matching is detected by the Bruhat comparison between s_i g_tau s_i and g_tau, equivalently by the left and right descent sets of g_tau. The standard Bruhat lifting property then gives inequalities between matchings after toggling an adjacent pair.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the matchings allowed by the word w can be embedded into, or compared with, the full uncrossing poset P_n, Bruhat-style lifting may supply order inequalities for local resolutions. Even without a literal affine-Bruhat embedding, the pattern suggests proving analogues of Lemma 4 for adjacent endpoints or for local word-compatible pairs.

proof_techniques.name: Parity-reversing adjacent-toggle involution
proof_techniques.description: When an interval [tau,eta] has an adjacent index i such that the i,i+1 strands do not cross in tau but do cross in eta, Lemma 4 shows that the operation sigma -> s_i·sigma stays inside the interval. Lemma 5 eliminates the fixed case i in C(sigma), so the map is a fixed-point-free involution. Since s_i·sigma differs from sigma by adding or removing exactly one crossing, it reverses the parity of the rank c and forces the signed rank sum of the interval to vanish.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, links or face posets may have signed Euler characteristic controlled by pairing cells that differ by a local crossing toggle. A solver could look for a word-compatible version of the A/B/C trichotomy and prove that the toggle preserves the set of admissible quasi-reduced cancellation matchings.

proof_techniques.name: Inductive interval decomposition by adding a crossing to the top
proof_techniques.description: When the chosen adjacent index i lies in A(tau) and also A(eta), the paper enlarges the interval [tau,eta] to [tau,s_i·eta], where s_i·eta is obtained by adding a crossing near the boundary. The original interval is the enlarged interval minus the elements not below eta. A key claim identifies that exceptional set as [s_i·tau,s_i·eta] minus [s_i·tau,eta], using Lemmas 4 and 5. Since all these new intervals have larger c(lower)+c(upper), descending induction applies to their signed rank sums.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a template for recursive proofs about resolution complexes: enlarge a cell-poset interval by allowing one extra local crossing, identify the complement by forcing the corresponding lower endpoint also to be toggled, and use induction. It may help analyze stars or links in F_w if adding a crossing preserves admissibility or can be confined to a controlled subcomplex.

proof_techniques.name: Residual subset with a unique maximum for intervals from the adjoined bottom
proof_techniques.description: For intervals [hat0,eta], a parity-reversing toggle cancels most nonzero elements. The uncancelled residual consists of hat0 together with matchings sigma < eta in which a chosen adjacent pair i,i+1 is directly matched. The paper constructs a unique maximal residual element kappa by taking a medial graph for eta, resolving the first crossing q between the i and i+1 strands in the direction that directly matches i to i+1, and then showing every residual sigma lies below kappa. The proof uses the fact that any sigma below eta can be represented by uncrossing a subset of crossings of eta, plus Lemma 2 to handle the possibility that resolving q in the other direction creates a removable closed loop.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This resembles a coning or recursive collapse argument: after pairing most cells, the unpaired cells form a smaller interval with a cone point or unique maximal element. For F_w, one could try to choose a cancellable adjacent inverse pair in the word and show the subcomplex of matchings that pair it directly has a unique maximal or contractible core.

proof_techniques.name: Lens and loop normalization
proof_techniques.description: The paper explicitly allows medial graphs with lenses during intermediate operations. Lemma 1 says reducing such a graph to lensless form by Yang-Baxter, lens-removal, and loop-removal moves gives a well-defined matching. Lemma 2 says that resolving crossings in any order and removing closed loops always moves downward in the uncrossing order, even if the intermediate graph has lenses. This lets the proof use convenient non-lensless diagrams without losing control of the poset relation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original definition of F_w permits embedded intervals with multiple pairwise intersections, so resolving one crossing can interact with lenses or create closed components in intermediate pictures. These lemmas suggest proving a normalization theorem ensuring that isotopy classes and face attachments are independent of lens-removal choices.

proof_techniques.name: Signed-rank Euler characteristic instead of explicit topology
proof_techniques.description: Eulerianity is proved by showing chi([x,y]) = sum (-1)^{rank} = 0 for every proper interval, not by constructing spheres or shellings. The argument repeatedly decomposes finite sets of matchings into parity-cancelling pairs plus smaller intervals. This gives a purely combinatorial route to the Eulerian property of the face-like poset.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Contractibility of F_w is stronger than an Euler characteristic computation, but signed-rank cancellation can still test whether proposed local links have the Euler characteristic of spheres or balls. It may also guide discrete Morse matchings on the cells of F_w.

proof_techniques.name: Use of first crossing near the boundary
proof_techniques.description: In the proof for intervals involving hat0, after choosing i in B(eta), the paper chooses a representative medial graph in which the strands starting at i and i+1 cross at a point q before either strand meets any other strand. Resolving this first crossing in the appropriate direction forces i and i+1 to become directly matched and defines the maximal residual element kappa. The choice of a first boundary crossing isolates the local operation from the rest of the diagram.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a word-labeled matching, isolating the first interaction of two boundary arcs may allow one to perform a local cancellation or contraction while preserving the label constraints. This is especially relevant when attempting induction on the number of letters or crossings.

other_useful_info: The paper notes that P_n has a topological interpretation as the closure poset of electroid cells E_tau in a compactification E_n of the space of circular planar electrical networks, with closure relation overline{E_tau} = union_{tau' <= tau} E_tau'. It also notes that the same partial order appears in the positive orthogonal Grassmannian and ABJM scattering-amplitude literature. The introduction states that [ALT] proves P_n is thin, meaning intervals of length two are diamonds; this is relevant because thin plus shellable would imply strong sphere-like interval topology. The paper conjectures that hat P_n is lexicographically shellable and gives an explicit EL-shelling only for hat P_3; shellability is not proved in this paper. A key external input used in the final proof is a comment after [Lam, Lemma 4.11]: if sigma <= eta, then a lensless medial graph representing sigma can be obtained from a graph representing eta by uncrossing a subset of its crossings. In the last paragraph of the printed proof, the displayed conditions involving C(eta) appear to be typographical: since i was chosen in B(eta), the involution and residual set must be separated according to whether i lies in C(sigma) for the varying element sigma.
why_relevant: Directly studies matchings of 2n boundary points ordered by resolving crossings. Its Eulerian and rank arguments are central for understanding any CW complex whose cells are built from crossing matchings and uncrossing maps.

title: CW Posets After the Poincare Conjecture
overall_summary: The paper reviews Björner's characterization of finite graded posets that occur as closure posets of finite regular CW complexes: precisely those whose lower open intervals have order complexes homeomorphic to spheres. Its main new result is that the classical Danaraj-Klee sufficient condition thin plus shellable can be weakened to thin plus homotopy Cohen-Macaulay, using the Poincare Conjecture to upgrade homotopy-sphere manifolds to actual spheres. The proof inducts on rank: links in interval order complexes decompose into smaller interval pieces, thinness supplies a pseudomanifold/no-boundary condition, and homotopy Cohen-Macaulayness restricts the possible homotopy types to contractible or sphere-like. For the original matching-complex problem, the useful content is not a direct contractibility criterion, but a toolkit for replacing geometric regular-CW verification by combinatorial poset checks, and for proving sphere-link/regularity properties via thinness plus homotopy-Cohen-Macaulay methods such as discrete Morse theory or Quillen-type arguments.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Order-complex/closure-poset observation from the introduction
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a finite regular CW complex, and let F(K) be its closure poset, whose elements are the cells of K together with a unique minimal element hat0 corresponding to the empty cell, ordered by closure containment: σ ≤ τ when σ is contained in the closure of τ. Let Δ(P) denote the order complex of a finite poset P, whose simplices are finite chains in P. Then Δ(F(K) minus {hat0}) is the first barycentric subdivision of K, hence is homeomorphic to K. Consequently, if τ is a cell of K, the order complex Δ(F(closure of τ) minus {hat0}) is homeomorphic to a ball, and the open intervals in the closure poset have order complexes homeomorphic to spheres of the dimensions dictated by the rank differences, equivalently S^{rk(τ)-rk(σ)-2} for an interval (σ, τ) in the usual rank convention rk(cell)=dim(cell)+1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The vertices of the first barycentric subdivision of a regular CW complex are the nonempty cells of K, and a simplex in the barycentric subdivision is exactly a chain of cells ordered by incidence/closure containment. This is precisely the definition of the order complex of F(K) with hat0 removed. Since barycentric subdivision does not change homeomorphism type, Δ(F(K) minus {hat0}) is homeomorphic to K. For a single cell τ in a regular CW complex, the closure of τ is a closed ball and its boundary is a sphere assembled from lower-dimensional cells. Passing to the corresponding subposets and intervals identifies the relevant order complexes with barycentric subdivisions of these balls, boundaries, or links, giving the required ball/sphere topology.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2 (Björner)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A finite graded poset P is called a CW-poset if: (1) P has a unique minimal element hat0; (2) P has at least one element other than hat0; and (3) for every y in P minus {hat0}, the order complex Δ((hat0, y)) of the open interval {z in P : hat0 < z < y} is homeomorphic to the sphere S^{rk(y)-2}, where rk(y) is the rank of y and hat0 has rank 0. Björner's theorem states that a finite graded poset P is the closure poset of a finite regular CW complex if and only if P is a CW-poset.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this theorem from Björner and does not reproduce the full proof. The necessity direction is motivated by the preceding barycentric-subdivision observation: in a regular CW complex, the closure of each cell is a ball and its boundary/link data give spherical order complexes for the appropriate lower intervals. The sufficiency direction is Björner's construction/recognition theorem: the interval-sphere condition gives enough topological control over the would-be cell boundaries to build a finite regular CW complex whose closure relation is exactly P.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Möbius/Eulerian necessary condition and thinness observation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a finite poset P and elements x < y, Philip Hall's relation gives μ_P(x, y) = ~tildeχ(Δ((x, y))), where μ_P is the Möbius function of P and ~tildeχ is reduced Euler characteristic. Therefore, if the open interval (x, y) has order complex homeomorphic to a sphere of dimension rk(y)-rk(x)-2, then μ_P(x, y) = (-1)^{rk(y)-rk(x)-2}. In particular, a necessary condition for closed intervals of a CW-poset is the Eulerian condition that μ_P(x, y) have this value for every x < y. The rank-2 part of this Eulerian condition is thinness: whenever rk(y)-rk(x)=2, the open interval (x, y) has exactly two elements. The paper also notes that, for graded posets, thinness implies that the order complex is a pseudomanifold.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key identity is Hall's formula μ_P(x, y)=~tildeχ(Δ((x, y))). If Δ((x, y)) is a d-sphere, then its reduced Euler characteristic is (-1)^d; substituting d=rk(y)-rk(x)-2 gives the Eulerian sign condition. In rank difference 2, the order complex of (x, y) is a discrete set with m vertices, where m is the number of elements strictly between x and y; its reduced Euler characteristic is m-1. The Eulerian value is 1, hence m=2, which is exactly thinness. The pseudomanifold assertion comes from the same rank-2 local condition: codimension-one faces of the order complex correspond to gaps of rank 2 in maximal chains, and thinness gives exactly two ways to fill such a gap.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.7 (Danaraj-Klee)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a finite graded poset in the setting of checking the CW-poset conditions, in particular with the required unique minimal element when one wants to conclude that P is a CW-poset. Suppose P is thin, meaning every open interval (x, y) with rk(y)-rk(x)=2 has exactly two elements. Suppose also that P is shellable, where a pure simplicial complex K is shellable if its facets can be ordered F_1, ..., F_k so that for every j ≥ 2 the intersection F_j ∩ (union_{i<j} F_i) is a pure codimension-one subcomplex of F_j, and a poset is called shellable when its order complex is shellable. Then P is a CW-poset.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this result from Danaraj and Klee as a practical sufficient condition and does not provide its proof. Its role in the paper is as the classical route to Björner's interval-sphere condition: shellability gives strong topological control over order complexes, while thinness supplies the no-boundary/pseudomanifold condition needed for the relevant shellable complexes to be spheres rather than arbitrary wedges or balls. The main theorem of the paper replaces the shellability hypothesis by the weaker homotopy Cohen-Macaulay hypothesis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Standard fact mentioned before Theorem 2.2: pure shellable implies homotopy Cohen-Macaulay
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A pure d-dimensional simplicial complex K is homotopy Cohen-Macaulay if, for every face σ of K including the empty face, the link lk_K(σ) is homotopy equivalent to a wedge of spheres of dimension d - dim(σ) - 1. The paper recalls the standard fact that every pure shellable simplicial complex is homotopy Cohen-Macaulay.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as well known and refers to Björner's handbook article for background. The usual proof is that shellability is inherited by links, and a pure shellable complex has the homotopy type of a wedge of spheres all in top dimension, with the ball case corresponding to a zero wedge/contractible case. Applying this to every link gives the homotopy Cohen-Macaulay link condition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1 (Poincare Conjecture, used as an input)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If K is a manifold that is a simply connected homology sphere, then K is homeomorphic to a sphere. Equivalently, if K is a manifold that is homotopy equivalent to a sphere, then K is homeomorphic to a sphere.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper uses this theorem as an external input and gives no proof. It notes the historical proof sources: dimension 2 follows from the classification of surfaces, dimensions at least 5 from Smale, dimension 4 from Freedman, and dimension 3 from Perelman. In the paper's main argument, this theorem is invoked after showing that an interval order complex is both a manifold and homotopy equivalent to a sphere, to conclude it is actually homeomorphic to a sphere as required in the definition of CW-poset.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2 (Hersh main result)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a finite graded poset with a unique minimal element hat0 and at least one element other than hat0. Suppose P is thin: for every x < y in P with rk(y)-rk(x)=2, the open interval (x, y) has exactly two elements. Suppose P is homotopy Cohen-Macaulay in the poset/order-complex sense used in the paper: the relevant order complexes and their links have the homotopy type of wedges of spheres in the prescribed top dimensions, so in particular each interval order complex Δ((x, y)) is homotopy equivalent to a wedge of spheres of dimension rk(y)-rk(x)-2. Then P is a CW-poset; that is, for every y > hat0, the order complex Δ((hat0, y)) is homeomorphic to S^{rk(y)-2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on rank, applied to intervals. In rank 2, thinness says that the open interval between the two endpoints contains exactly two elements, so its order complex is two isolated vertices, i.e. S^0; lower-rank cases are vacuous or give S^{-1} for the empty interval. For the induction step, fix an open interval (x, y). For a vertex z of Δ((x, y)), the link of z in Δ((x, y)) decomposes as the join Δ((x, z)) * Δ((z, y)); more generally, the link of a chain factors as a join of order complexes of smaller open intervals. By the induction hypothesis these smaller interval complexes are homeomorphic to spheres, and joins of spheres are spheres. Hence the vertex links, and therefore all local links, are spheres; Δ((x, y)) is a manifold. Homotopy Cohen-Macaulayness implies that Δ((x, y)) is homotopy equivalent to a wedge of spheres in the top dimension. For intervals of rank difference greater than 2, thinness lets one assume this complex is connected. The top integral homology of a connected manifold is either Z or 0, depending on orientability/boundary behavior, so a wedge of top-dimensional spheres compatible with being a manifold is either a single sphere up to homotopy or is contractible. The contractible alternative would make Δ((x, y)) a manifold with boundary, whose boundary would be pure codimension one. This contradicts thinness, because thinness implies every codimension-one face of the order complex lies in exactly two facets, i.e. there is no boundary. Therefore Δ((x, y)) is homotopy equivalent to a sphere. Since it is already a manifold, the Poincare Conjecture implies it is homeomorphic to a sphere. Taking x=hat0 proves the defining interval-sphere condition for P to be a CW-poset.

proof_techniques.name: Replace a regular CW complex by its closure poset and barycentric subdivision
proof_techniques.description: The paper emphasizes that the topology of a finite regular CW complex K is captured by its closure poset F(K): the order complex of F(K) with the minimal empty-cell element removed is the first barycentric subdivision of K. Thus topological questions about K can often be transferred to combinatorial/topological questions about the order complex of a poset of cells. This is especially useful because order complexes admit standard poset-topology tools such as shellability, Cohen-Macaulayness, discrete Morse matchings, and Quillen-type fiber arguments.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, one can try to define a closure poset whose elements are crossing matchings and their resolutions, ordered by face incidence. If F_w is regular or can be certified as regular, contractibility of F_w is equivalent to contractibility of the order complex of this poset with the bottom element removed.

proof_techniques.name: Certify regular CW structure via CW-poset interval spheres
proof_techniques.description: Björner's theorem, as used in the paper, gives a recognition principle: a finite graded poset with a unique minimum is the closure poset of a regular CW complex exactly when every lower interval (hat0, y) has order complex homeomorphic to a sphere of the appropriate dimension. This avoids having to analyze attaching maps directly; instead one proves local sphericality of intervals in the incidence poset.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the crossing-resolution cells of F_w produce a poset whose face incidences are combinatorially clear but whose attaching maps are hard to control, proving the CW-poset interval condition could justify treating the construction as a regular CW complex and allow barycentric-subdivision/poset methods.

proof_techniques.name: Thin plus homotopy Cohen-Macaulay as a substitute for shellability
proof_techniques.description: The main technique of the paper is to replace the strong and often difficult condition of shellability by homotopy Cohen-Macaulayness, provided the poset is thin. Thinness gives local rank-2 control and a pseudomanifold/no-boundary property, while homotopy Cohen-Macaulayness says intervals and links have the homotopy type of wedges of spheres in the correct dimensions. The Poincare Conjecture then upgrades the resulting manifold homotopy spheres to actual spheres.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In a crossing-resolution poset, thinness may correspond to the fact that a single unresolved crossing has exactly two local resolutions. If shellability of the matching poset is hard, it may be enough to prove homotopy Cohen-Macaulayness by discrete Morse theory or a fiber-lemma argument.

proof_techniques.name: Induction on rank using link decompositions into joins of smaller intervals
proof_techniques.description: In the proof of Theorem 2.2, the link of a vertex z in an interval order complex Δ((x, y)) decomposes as a join of smaller interval order complexes, namely Δ((x, z)) * Δ((z, y)); links of longer chains decompose similarly into joins over the successive subintervals. Inductively knowing that smaller intervals are spheres implies their joins are spheres, and this proves that the larger interval order complex is a manifold.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For matchings, resolving a set of crossings may split the remaining choices into independent subproblems. If intervals in the crossing-resolution poset factor similarly, their links may be expressible as joins/products of smaller matching complexes, enabling induction.

proof_techniques.name: Use thinness to rule out boundary and force sphere rather than ball/contractible manifold
proof_techniques.description: After homotopy Cohen-Macaulayness reduces an interval order complex to a wedge of top-dimensional spheres, the manifold condition restricts the top homology to either Z or 0. The Z case gives a homotopy sphere; the 0 case gives a contractible manifold. The paper rules out the contractible case by observing that a contractible manifold would have boundary, while thinness forces every codimension-one face to lie in exactly two facets, so the complex has no boundary.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a local complex arising from F_w is known to be a manifold-like pseudomanifold, the two-resolution property at codimension-one faces may prevent boundary phenomena. This can be used to prove local links are spheres, a key step in regularity and poset-topology arguments.

proof_techniques.name: Use top homology of manifolds to collapse wedge possibilities
proof_techniques.description: A homotopy Cohen-Macaulay complex is allowed to be a wedge of many top-dimensional spheres. The paper exploits the additional fact that the complex is a connected manifold: its top integral homology is either Z or 0. Therefore the wedge has either exactly one top sphere or none, reducing the possible homotopy types to homotopy sphere or contractible.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If links or intervals in the matching complex can be shown to be manifolds and homotopy Cohen-Macaulay, then one may not need to compute their full homology ranks; manifold top-homology constraints can force the desired spherical local topology.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly says that replacing shellability by homotopy Cohen-Macaulayness broadens the usable toolkit, naming discrete Morse theory, especially Forman and Babson-Hersh, and the Quillen Fiber Lemma as methods for proving the needed homotopy Cohen-Macaulay property. It also notes that pure shellable simplicial complexes are homotopy Cohen-Macaulay, so shellability remains a sufficient route when available. Thinness is highlighted as both the rank-2 part of the Eulerian condition and as a condition implying pseudomanifold behavior for graded posets. Examples and related references mentioned include Björner-Wachs proving Bruhat order thin and shellable, hence a CW-poset, and the observation that nontrivial simplicial posets with unique minimal element are CW-posets. Anders Björner is noted as having independently obtained related Poincare-Conjecture-based results.
why_relevant: Gives criteria for posets to be face posets of regular CW complexes, especially using Eulerian and shellability hypotheses. This is useful for converting combinatorial information about crossing-resolution posets into topological conclusions.

title: The Space of Circular Planar Electrical Networks
overall_summary: The paper studies circular planar electrical networks through medial strand diagrams, response matrices, and explicit coordinate systems on the cells of the response-matrix space. Its central constructions assign to every strand matching a standard minimal network via Dyck tilings, then recover the conductances of that network from Pfaffian expressions in the response matrix using tripod grove variables. It also constructs B variables on the cells of a minimal strand diagram; conductances are biratios of adjacent B variables and the B variables mutate by the cube recurrence under Y-Delta moves. For the original matching-complex problem, the most relevant material is the paper’s use of strand matchings, crossing resolutions, canonical rectilinear representatives, biratio coordinates, and boundary-compatible degeneration of positive coordinates when local network pieces are resolved or transformed.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Background facts recalled in Sections 2.2 and 2.3: equivalence, minimality, and strand matchings
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a circular planar electrical network, i.e. a finite graph embedded in the disk or plane with a distinguished cyclically ordered set of boundary vertices called nodes, and with positive conductances on edges. Electrical transformations are the local moves shown in Figure 1 of the paper: deleting a dead branch, deleting a self-loop, combining series edges, combining parallel edges, and applying a Y-Delta transformation. Two networks are electrically equivalent, meaning they have the same response matrix, if and only if they can be obtained from one another by electrical transformations. A network is minimal, or reduced, if it has the fewest edges in its topological equivalence class; any two minimal equivalent networks are related using only Y-Delta moves. The medial graph of a network has one degree-4 vertex for each network edge, stubs at boundary nodes, and strands obtained by going straight through every medial vertex. A circular planar network is minimal if and only if its medial graph has no closed strands, no strand crossing itself, and no pair of strands crossing more than once. For a minimal network with n nodes, labeling the two stubs at node i by 2i-1 and 2i gives a fixed-point-free involution pi of {1,...,2n}, called the strand matching. Y-Delta moves preserve pi, so pi depends only on the topological equivalence class of a minimal network. Conversely, every fixed-point-free involution of {1,...,2n} is the strand matching of some circular planar electrical network with n nodes, possibly a cactus network in which boundary nodes may be glued or disconnected.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence and minimality assertions are recalled from Colin de Verdiere, Gitler, and Vertigan. The paper does not reprove the full theorem, but uses the following mechanisms. Electrical transformations preserve the response matrix, and the cited theorem says they generate all response-matrix equivalences. Minimality can be read from the medial graph because closed strands, self-crossings, and repeated crossings correspond to reducible local configurations; absence of these configurations is exactly reducedness. A Y-Delta move changes only a small triangular/star patch in the network and does not alter how strands connect boundary stubs, hence the strand matching is invariant. To realize an arbitrary fixed-point-free involution, put 2n points in generic position on a circle and connect paired points by chords; the chord diagram is used as the medial strand diagram, and coloring complementary regions alternately produces dual circular planar networks realizing the matching.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3 construction: standard networks from Dyck tilings and strand matchings
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each fixed-point-free involution, or strand matching, on {1,...,2n}, the paper constructs a distinguished rectilinear strand diagram and an associated circular planar network called a standard network. The construction uses Dyck tilings. A Dyck path of order n is a simple random-walk path on Z starting at 0, staying nonnegative, and returning to 0 after 2n steps. If lambda and mu are Dyck paths with lambda below mu, the skew region lambda/mu may be tiled by fattened Dyck paths, called Dyck tiles, subject to a cover-inclusive condition: whenever two tiles are vertically adjacent, the horizontal span of the upper tile is contained in the horizontal span of the lower tile. Given such a Dyck tiling, the associated strand diagram is drawn by placing strand endpoints along the upper Dyck path, letting each tile contain two strands crossing once, and letting possible additional strands pass horizontally through the tile. Coloring the complementary regions alternately gives a primal network and a dual network. The strand-matching-to-Dyck-tiling construction starts from the empty matching and uses two inverse elementary operations: inserting a new strand with adjacent paired stubs, which corresponds to splitting the Dyck tiling along a column and inserting a tent into every tile crossing that column; and crossing a pair of adjacent previously uncrossed strands, replacing pairs (a,i) and (i+1,b), with a<i<i+1<b, by (a,i+1) and (i,b), which corresponds to adding a square Dyck tile. In the special case where the lower Dyck path is the minimal zigzag path, the upper path is maximal, and all tiles are single boxes, the construction gives the usual standard well-connected network.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction is inductive. The base case n=0 is the empty matching and empty Dyck tiling. Any nonempty matching can be deconstructed by finding a peak in the Dyck-path representation of the matching. Such a peak corresponds either to an adjacent matched pair, which is removed by deleting the pair and merging the surrounding columns, or to adjacent endpoints belonging to two crossing-capable strands, which is uncrossed by replacing (a,i+1),(i,b) with (a,i),(i+1,b). Reversing these deconstruction steps gives the two construction moves. The local rules inside a Dyck tile ensure that when a square is added, exactly one new strand crossing is introduced, and when a tent is inserted, all affected strands are extended without changing their relative crossing data except as prescribed. Coloring the cells of the resulting rectilinear strand diagram produces the primal and dual networks.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1: Pfaffian formula for tripod partition functions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a circular planar network with cyclically ordered nodes, response matrix L, and grove partition sums Z_tau. For a partition tau of the nodes, Z_tau is the weighted sum over groves whose connected components induce tau, with weight the product of edge conductances. Let Z_uncrossing denote the grove partition sum for the partition in which every node is in a separate tree. Suppose tau is a tripod partition: the boundary can be divided into red, green, and blue arcs so that tau has one triple part and all remaining nonsingleton parts are parallel doubletons connecting nodes of different colors, with possible outer singleton parts at the color interfaces. Then Z_tau/Z_uncrossing is the Pfaffian of an explicitly defined skew-symmetric matrix constructed from L. In the color order red, then green, then blue, the ordinary rows and columns are indexed by the fully colored nodes and one additional row/column is appended. For two ordinary nodes i and j, the entry is 0 if they have the same color; it is L_ij for red-green and green-blue pairs; and, with the paper’s cyclic sign convention, it is -L_ij for red-blue pairs, extended skew-symmetrically below the diagonal. The last column is obtained, for each ordinary row i, by summing the positive ordinary entries in that row and adding the response-matrix term involving the singleton node, if present, whose two colors have no color in common with the color of i. With this convention, Pf of this matrix equals Z_tau/Z_uncrossing.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof reduces the statement to an earlier Pfaffian formula from Kenyon-Wilson for dual-tripod partitions in the dual network, expressed in terms of pairwise effective resistances. First assume tau has three singleton boundary nodes, one at each color interface. Then the dual partition tau* is a pairing of dual nodes, and planar duality gives Z_tau/Z_uncrossing = Z^*_tau*/Z^*_tree, where Z^*_tree is the weighted tree sum in the dual network. Apply the earlier dual-tripod formula in the dual network, where a formal variable t appears and the desired quantity is the coefficient of t in a Pfaffian. Index primal and dual nodes so that dual node i lies between primal nodes i and i+1. Perform Pfaffian-preserving operations subtracting each row from the preceding row and each column from the preceding column. Away from block boundaries, entries of the form -1/2 R^*_{i,j} + 1/2 R^*_{i+1,j} + 1/2 R^*_{i,j+1} - 1/2 R^*_{i+1,j+1} occur. These simplify to -L_ij using the dual-resistance/primal-response identity R^*_{i,j} = sum L_{i',j'} over cyclically ordered indices i'<=i<j'<=j. After these operations, t appears in only two positions. Extracting the linear coefficient in t expresses the answer as a signed sum of two Pfaffians. Moving one row/column to the last position and combining the two Pfaffians gives the stated single Pfaffian, and further row/column sign changes put it into the symmetric red-green-blue form described. If tau lacks one or more singleton interface nodes, add isolated boundary nodes with zero response entries; neither Z_tau nor Z_uncrossing changes, and the same Pfaffian formula applies.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2: uniqueness of comb groves in standard networks and their extensions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a standard minimal network obtained from the Dyck-tiling construction, and let chi be a crossing in its rectilinear strand diagram. Define the comb at chi as follows: take the strand segment going upward-left from chi as the spine, and at every crossing on this spine take the upward-right strand segment as a tooth. Resolve every crossing in this comb vertically and every crossing outside the comb horizontally; this determines a partition tau_chi of the nodes of G and a dual partition tau^*_chi. Also let tau_- be the all-horizontal partition. Let G~_chi be the enlarged network obtained by extending the strand diagram and inserting the notch above the top tooth of the comb so that the corresponding partition tau~_chi is a tripod or dual-tripod partition; let tau~_- be the all-horizontal partition in G~_chi. Then in G~_chi there is a unique grove of type tau~_chi and a unique grove of type tau~_-. In G there is a unique grove of type tau_chi and a unique grove of type tau_-, and these groves extend uniquely to the corresponding groves in G~_chi.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If tau~_chi is not a tripod partition in the primal network, pass to the dual network, where it is a tripod partition. Consider the grove drawn by taking vertical edges at comb crossings and horizontal edges elsewhere. The triple part of the tripod separates the disk into three regions. In each region, the partition pairs boundary nodes in nested parallel fashion. Starting from the outermost pair and moving inward toward the triple part, each connection in the displayed grove is forced to be as far from the triple part as possible; planarity leaves no room to reroute any of these pairings without changing the partition. Thus the grove of type tau~_chi is unique. The same forcing argument applies to the all-horizontal partition tau~_-. Finally, if G itself had two distinct groves inducing tau_chi or tau_-, either one could be spliced into the fixed outside part of G~_chi, producing two distinct groves of type tau~_chi or tau~_-, contradicting uniqueness in the extension.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3: tripod variables are monomials and conductances are biratios
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a standard minimal circular planar network, with rectilinear strand diagram coming from a Dyck tiling. For every crossing chi of the strand diagram, let tau_chi be the comb partition defined by resolving the comb at chi vertically and all other crossings horizontally, and include also the exterior all-horizontal partition tau_-. The associated tripod variables are the partition sums Z_tau for tau in {tau_-} union {tau_chi : chi a crossing}. Then the number of tripod variables is one more than the number of edges of G. Each tripod variable is a monomial, namely the product of the conductances of the edges in the unique grove of its partition type. For any crossing e, let c_e be its vertical conductance, meaning the conductance of the primal edge or dual edge that is vertical at that crossing. Let a be the first crossing on the strand going upward-left from e, let b be the first crossing on the strand going upward-right from e, and let f be the first crossing on the strand going upward-left from b. If a, b, or f does not exist, replace the corresponding tau_a, tau_b, or tau_f by tau_-. Then c_e = (Z_tau_e Z_tau_f)/(Z_tau_a Z_tau_b). Equivalently, using the corresponding dual partition sums, c_e = (Z^*_{tau^*_e} Z^*_{tau^*_f})/(Z^*_{tau^*_a} Z^*_{tau^*_b}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Each edge of a standard minimal network corresponds to exactly one strand crossing, so the variables consist of one variable per edge plus the exterior variable. Lemma 2 gives uniqueness of the grove of every comb partition and of the exterior partition; hence each partition sum is the product of edge conductances in that unique grove. Compare the comb based at e with the comb based at a. The comb at e has exactly one additional tooth, starting at e, relative to the comb at a; therefore Z_tau_e/Z_tau_a is the product of all vertical conductances along the upward-right strand segment starting at e. Similarly, Z_tau_b/Z_tau_f is the product of all vertical conductances along the same upward-right strand segment after the first crossing b. Dividing these two ratios cancels every factor except the vertical conductance at e, giving the biratio formula. The dual formula follows because for every relevant partition tau, the dual grove weight satisfies Z^*_{tau^*} = Z_tau divided by the product of all primal edge weights, so the global factors cancel in the same biratio.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3.1 reconstruction consequence: conductances as Pfaffian rational functions of the response matrix
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a standard minimal network G, the conductance of every edge can be computed explicitly as a rational function of the response matrix L. For each crossing chi, enlarge G to the network G~_chi used in Lemma 2, in which the comb partition becomes a tripod or dual-tripod partition. The response matrix of such an enlargement can be obtained from L by a sequence of elementary response-matrix operations: adjoining an edge of conductance c between adjacent boundary nodes i and i+1 adds c to the off-diagonal entries L_{i,i+1}=L_{i+1,i} and subtracts c from L_{i,i} and L_{i+1,i+1}; inserting an isolated new node inserts a zero row and zero column; and internalizing a boundary node is performed by Schur reduction. Then the ratios Z_tau_e/Z_tau_a and Z_tau_f/Z_tau_b appearing in Theorem 3 equal ratios of normalized tripod Pfaffians in the corresponding enlarged networks, so the biratio formula of Theorem 3 reconstructs all conductances from Pfaffians of matrices built from L.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix crossings e,a,b,f as in Theorem 3. The paper chooses the extensions so that G~_a = G~_e and G~_f = G~_b. By Lemma 2, for any choice of conductances on the new edges in the extension, the new-edge contributions to the unique grove weights cancel in the ratio, and the normalizing factor Z_uncrossing cancels as well. Concretely, Z_tau_e/Z_tau_a = (Z_{tau~_e}(G~_e)/Z_unc(G~_e))/(Z_{tau~_a}(G~_a)/Z_unc(G~_a)), and similarly Z_tau_f/Z_tau_b = (Z_{tau~_f}(G~_f)/Z_unc(G~_f))/(Z_{tau~_b}(G~_b)/Z_unc(G~_b)). Each normalized extended partition function is a tripod or dual-tripod quantity, so Proposition 1 and its dual-tripod counterpart compute it as a Pfaffian in the response matrix of the extension. The extension response matrices are obtained from L by the three explicit operations above. Substituting these Pfaffian ratios into c_e = (Z_tau_e Z_tau_f)/(Z_tau_a Z_tau_b) gives the desired rational formula. The paper notes that computations are often simplified by taking the limiting case in which the newly added edges have infinite conductance.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4: B variables satisfy a conductance biratio formula
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be any minimal circular planar network, and draw its minimal strand diagram so that every strand (i,j), with i<j, moves monotonically from left to right. For a crossing between strands (i1,j1) and (i2,j2) with i1<i2<j1<j2, call the region between boundary nodes i1 and i2 the left region of that crossing. The horizontal conductance of the crossing is the conductance of the primal edge or dual edge whose endpoints lie in the left and right regions of the crossing. For each cell C of the strand diagram, corresponding to either a primal vertex or a primal face, define B_C to be the product of the horizontal conductances of all crossings whose left region contains C. Then for every primal edge e with endpoints v and v' and adjacent faces f and f', its conductance satisfies c(e) = B_v B_v'/(B_f B_f').
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix a crossing e and examine how its horizontal conductance contributes to the biratio B_v B_v'/(B_f B_f') at another crossing e'. If e' lies strictly inside the shadow, i.e. the left region, of e, then all four adjacent cells at e' contain the factor from e, so it cancels. If e' lies outside the shadow, none of the four B variables contains that factor. If e' lies on the boundary of the shadow but is not e itself, exactly two adjacent cells contain the factor, and those two factors again cancel between numerator and denominator. The only non-canceling contribution occurs when e'=e; there the biratio contributes exactly the conductance of the edge at e. Multiplying over all crossings gives the formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3.3 observation: B variables transform by the cube recurrence under Y-Delta moves
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a minimal network equipped with B variables as in Proposition 4, a local Y-Delta transformation changes the B variables by the cube recurrence. In the local notation of Figure 11, where B0 is the central B variable on one side of the move, B7 is the new central B variable after the move, and B1,...,B6 are the six surrounding B variables, the relation is B0 B7 = B1 B4 + B2 B5 + B3 B6. The surrounding variables are unchanged by the local move.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper records this as a property of B variables and points to the cube recurrence. The derivation is local: express the three conductances in the Y configuration as biratios of the adjacent B variables using Proposition 4, apply the standard Y-Delta conductance transformation from Figure 1, then require the three conductances in the Delta configuration to be expressible by the same biratio rule with one unknown central variable B7. Solving the resulting equations gives exactly B0 B7 = B1 B4 + B2 B5 + B3 B6.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5: contiguous minors as Laurent polynomials from truncated Aztec diamonds
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be an n by n matrix. For cyclic row and column indices, define a contiguous minor CMa,b,y(M) to be det M^{b+y-1,...,b}_{a,a+1,...,a+y-1}, i.e. the determinant with y consecutive rows and y consecutive columns, with the columns listed in reverse cyclic order as in the paper. Define the central minor CMx,y(M) by choosing a = floor((x-y)/2) and b = floor((x-y+n-(n-1 mod 2))/2), with indices interpreted modulo n. Let TADx,y,ell,n be the order-ell Aztec diamond centered at (x,y), truncated to use only squares with vertical coordinate between 0 and n. Assign to each horizontal domino centered at (p,q), with p integer and q half-integer, weight 1/(v_{p,q-1/2} v_{p,q+1/2}), and to each vertical domino centered at (p,q), with p half-integer and q integer, weight 1/(v_{p-1/2,q} v_{p+1/2,q}). Let P(TADx,y,ell,n) be the weighted sum of domino tilings multiplied by the monomial product of the variables v_{p,q} in the truncated diamond. If every variable v_{p,q} is specialized to the central minor CMp,q(M), then P(TADx,y,ell,n) equals a contiguous minor of M. More precisely, along the horizontal span of the truncated Aztec diamond, the rightmost central minor has either a row set or a column set different from the corresponding set of the adjacent central minor to its left; the leftmost central minor has the opposite type of changed set. The resulting contiguous minor uses the row set from the rightmost central minor and the column set from the leftmost central minor, or with row and column interchanged in the opposite orientation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof combines Kuo graphical condensation for domino tilings with the Desnanot-Jacobi determinant identity. For determinants, Desnanot-Jacobi gives a three-term recurrence relating a contiguous minor, the corresponding smaller minor, and four neighboring minors obtained by shifting row or column intervals. For weighted Aztec diamonds, Kuo condensation gives W(AD_{x,y,ell}) W(AD_{x,y,ell-2}) equal to the sum of two products: the up/down pair multiplied by the two horizontal boundary domino weights, plus the left/right pair multiplied by the two vertical boundary domino weights. Truncation is handled by setting weights of forbidden vertical boundary dominos to 0 and appropriate horizontal boundary weights to 1. With the special weights 1/(v v) and after multiplying by the monomial factors defining P, the explicit boundary weights cancel, leaving the clean recurrence P(TAD_{x,y,ell,n}) P(TAD_{x,y,ell-2,n}) = P(TAD_{x,y+1,ell-1,n}) P(TAD_{x,y-1,ell-1,n}) + P(TAD_{x-1,y,ell-1,n}) P(TAD_{x+1,y,ell-1,n}). The Laurent polynomials P and the claimed contiguous minors therefore satisfy the same recurrence. The base case ell=0 is tautological because P is just one central minor, and the case ell=1 is checked directly by Desnanot-Jacobi. Induction on ell proves the theorem.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 6: matrix entries are positive Laurent polynomials in central minors
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any n by n matrix M, each individual entry M_{i,j} can be expressed as a Laurent polynomial in the central minors CMx,y(M), and all coefficients in this Laurent polynomial are positive.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Theorem 5 with y=1 and choose x and ell so that the resulting contiguous minor has the single row i and single column j. The associated truncated Aztec-diamond Laurent polynomial is a sum over domino tilings with positive coefficients and monomial weights, so the expression has positive coefficients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 7: disjoint contiguous minors are positive Laurent polynomials in small central minors
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be an n by n matrix. The small central minors are CMx,y(M) with 1 <= y < n/2, together with those with y = n/2 and x+y odd; in the nonsymmetric case x ranges over 1,...,2n. If a contiguous minor of M has disjoint row and column index sets, then it can be expressed as a Laurent polynomial with positive coefficients in the small central minors of M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use Theorem 5 to express the given contiguous minor as P(TADx,y,ell,n) for a suitable truncated Aztec diamond. If the row and column intervals are disjoint, one can take ell at most floor(n/2)-y, with the extreme case occurring when the intervals are adjacent. Thus the variables occurring in the truncated diamond have height at most floor(n/2). For odd n all central minors up to that height are small. For even n, the central minors at height n/2 that are not classified as small correspond to overlapping row and column sets, and hence do not appear in the disjoint contiguous-minor situation. The domino-tiling expansion supplies positivity of coefficients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 8: arbitrary disjoint minors are Laurent polynomials in small central minors
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any n by n matrix M, any minor whose row index set and column index set are disjoint can be expressed as a Laurent polynomial in the small central minors of M. Positivity of coefficients is not asserted for arbitrary noncontiguous minors in this corollary.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A determinant is a polynomial in its matrix entries. If the row and column sets of the minor are disjoint, every matrix entry appearing in the determinant is off-diagonal. By Corollary 6, and in the required disjoint cases by the small-minor control of Corollary 7, these off-diagonal entries are Laurent polynomials in the small central minors. Substituting these Laurent expressions into the determinant expansion gives a Laurent polynomial in the small central minors.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 9: noninterlaced minors are positive rational functions of small central minors
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be an n by n matrix, with row and column indices arranged cyclically. A minor is noninterlaced if its row set and column set have the same cardinality and lie in noninterlacing cyclic intervals. Then every noninterlaced minor of M can be written as a positive rational function of the small central minors of M, meaning an expression formed from small central minors using addition, multiplication, and division with positive coefficients.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key step is a determinant identity called the jaw move. Starting from the elementary identity for a matrix A with two equal rows, 0 = det of a 3 by 3 matrix whose first two rows are both indexed by z and whose third row is indexed by d, and columns are a,b,c, one applies Jacobi’s complementary minor identity after setting A generically equal to M^{-1}. This yields an identity of the form det M_b^b det M_{...}^{...,a,c} = det M_a^b det M_{...}^{...,b,c} + det M_c^b det M_{...}^{...,a,b}, with the exact deleted-row/deleted-column notation as in the paper. The identity expresses one noninterlaced determinant as a positive rational combination of simpler noninterlaced determinants. For a noninterlaced minor with at least one isolated node interspersed between the two sides, choose b to be such an isolated node, choose a and c to be the first and last nodes on the same side as b, and choose d to be an endpoint on the other side. With this choice, the jaw move either decreases the number of strands in the minor or keeps that number fixed while decreasing the number of interspersed isolated nodes. Repeatedly applying the move reduces any noninterlaced minor to noninterlaced contiguous minors. Corollary 7 expresses those contiguous minors as positive Laurent polynomials in small central minors, so the original minor is a positive rational function of small central minors.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 10: symmetric noninterlaced minors need only half the central minors
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a symmetric n by n matrix. Then every noninterlaced minor of M is a positive rational function of the small central minors CMx,y(M) with 1 <= x <= n, using the symmetric convention that identifies central minors related by reversing row and column roles.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For odd n, symmetry gives CMx+n,y = CMx,y, so Theorem 9 immediately reduces the needed variables to representatives with 1 <= x <= n. For even n, the only issue is that the minimally off-center contiguous minors of height n/2 occur in opposite pairs, and only one member of each pair is included among the chosen small central minors. A condensation identity expresses the omitted off-center contiguous minor as a positive rational expression involving its opposite included off-center minor and central contiguous minors. After this replacement, the proof of Theorem 9 uses only the chosen half-set of small central minors.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 11: positivity of small central response minors implies well-connectedness
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a circular planar network with n nodes and response matrix L. If each of the binomial(n,2) small central minors of L, in the symmetric sense of Theorem 10, is positive, then G is well-connected. Here well-connected means equivalently that for every pair of noninterlaced node subsets A and B of the same size, there are pairwise vertex-disjoint paths connecting A to B in pairs, or equivalently that all noninterlaced minors of L are strictly positive.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Theorem 10, every noninterlaced minor of the symmetric response matrix L is a positive rational function of the small central minors. If all small central minors are positive, all noninterlaced minors are therefore positive. The Colin de Verdiere criterion recalled in the paper says that positivity of all noninterlaced response minors is equivalent to well-connectedness of the circular planar network.

proof_techniques.name: Medial strand matching as a combinatorial invariant
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically replaces a circular planar network by its medial strand diagram. Minimality becomes a purely diagrammatic condition: no closed strands, no self-crossing strands, and no pair of strands crossing twice. The boundary behavior of the strands is encoded by a fixed-point-free involution of boundary stubs, and Y-Delta transformations preserve this involution. Thus a large geometric/electrical equivalence problem is reduced to manipulating planar matchings of strand endpoints.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original CW complex is built from crossing matchings and their local resolutions. The medial-strand viewpoint suggests trying to identify invariants of matchings under local moves and to characterize which crossing diagrams are reduced or canonical using only forbidden local strand configurations.

proof_techniques.name: Canonical rectilinear representative via Dyck tilings
proof_techniques.description: For every strand matching, the paper constructs a standard rectilinear strand diagram using Dyck tilings. The construction is recursive and uses two elementary operations: adding a local crossing of adjacent strands and inserting a new adjacent matched pair by splitting the tiling. This gives a controlled representative in which directions such as upward-left and upward-right are meaningful and local combs can be defined.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A proof of contractibility for a matching complex may benefit from assigning a canonical representative or normal form to every isotopy class. The Dyck-tiling recursion is a candidate template for inducting on boundary pairs or crossings while preserving a planar matching structure.

proof_techniques.name: Comb resolutions and unique monomial witnesses
proof_techniques.description: In a standard network, a crossing determines a comb-shaped subset of the strand diagram. Resolving comb crossings vertically and all other crossings horizontally gives a boundary partition whose grove is unique. Therefore the corresponding partition function is not a complicated sum but a single monomial in conductances. Comparing neighboring combs makes almost all factors cancel and leaves one local conductance as a biratio.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original cells are cubes whose faces are crossing resolutions. The comb construction suggests looking for distinguished resolution patterns with uniqueness or forcing properties, then using cancellation between adjacent patterns to build inductive contractions or coordinates on cells.

proof_techniques.name: Biratio coordinates on vertices and faces
proof_techniques.description: The B-variable construction assigns a product of horizontal conductances to every cell of a minimal strand diagram. Each conductance is recovered as the cross ratio B_v B_v'/(B_f B_f') of the two endpoint-vertex variables and two adjacent-face variables. The proof is purely combinatorial: for any other crossing, its contribution to the cross ratio cancels unless it is the crossing being measured.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The cancellation-by-regions argument is potentially portable to any planar crossing complex with local face/region data. It may help define a height function, potential, or discrete Morse matching by assigning multiplicative or additive weights to regions and showing local crossing changes affect only one factor.

proof_techniques.name: Cube recurrence for local mutations
proof_techniques.description: Under a Y-Delta move, B variables transform by the cube recurrence B0 B7 = B1 B4 + B2 B5 + B3 B6. This packages a local planar transformation into a positive algebraic mutation, while keeping the surrounding variables fixed. It is a compatibility statement between local cell changes and global coordinates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The cells of F_w are products of intervals indexed by crossings, and facets are local resolutions. The cube recurrence is a model for proving that different orders of local transformations are compatible, which is often the key obstruction in showing that a cubical or CW complex is a ball or contractible.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary-compatible conductance degeneration
proof_techniques.description: The paper’s coordinates are designed so that moving from one cell of the response-matrix space to a boundary cell corresponds to a conductance tending to 0 or infinity. Although the regularity of the full Lam-Pylyavskyy cell complex is stated as a conjectural/topological context in the introduction, the explicit coordinate formulas show how local network degenerations are reflected by vanishing or diverging coordinate factors.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original attaching maps are precisely given by resolving one crossing at a time. A plausible strategy is to realize F_w, or a closely related complex, as a boundary stratification of a contractible positive parameter space where facets are reached by one coordinate going to an endpoint.

proof_techniques.name: Pfaffian evaluation of planar boundary partition functions
proof_techniques.description: Tripod and dual-tripod grove partition functions are computed as Pfaffians of matrices made from the response matrix. The proof uses planar duality, an effective-resistance formula in the dual network, Pfaffian-preserving row/column operations, and extraction of a coefficient in an auxiliary variable. The result turns certain planar connectivity sums into explicit algebraic coordinates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem has inverse-letter pairing constraints around complementary regions. Pfaffian formulas are a natural tool for planar pairing enumerations and may help encode allowed resolution states or count/weight them in a way that behaves well under local uncrossing.

proof_techniques.name: Condensation and positive Laurent expansions
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 5 proves Laurent expansions for contiguous minors by matching the Desnanot-Jacobi recurrence for determinants with Kuo condensation for domino tilings of truncated Aztec diamonds. The result is a positive Laurent phenomenon: complicated minors are represented by sums over tilings, and the recurrence proves consistency.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the matching complex admits recursive decompositions by resolving local crossings, an analogous condensation recurrence could prove that different recursive reductions are compatible or that certain intervals in the face poset have positive tiling models, supporting shellability or contractibility arguments.

proof_techniques.name: Jaw-move induction
proof_techniques.description: The proof of positivity for noninterlaced minors uses a local determinant identity, the jaw move, to reduce a noncontiguous noninterlaced configuration to simpler ones. The complexity measure is lexicographic: first the number of strands, then the number of interspersed isolated nodes. Repetition reduces to contiguous cases already controlled by Laurent expansions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The quasi-reduced word condition in the original problem may allow a similar complexity-reducing local move: choose an isolated or extremal boundary feature and express the global complex in terms of simpler matching complexes, aiming for an inductive contractibility proof.

other_useful_info: The introduction recalls Lam and Pylyavskyy’s cell complex on the space Omega_n of response matrices: cells are parametrized by equivalence classes of minimal networks, and the cell dimension is the number of edges of a minimal representative. They conjectured that this is a regular CW complex whose attaching maps arise by sending a conductance to 0 or infinity; Lam later showed the cell complex is Eulerian. This is closely related to uncrossing partial orders on matchings; the paper cites Lam, 'The uncrossing partial order on matchings is Eulerian' [29]. After Theorem 3, the paper notes that the tripod variables satisfy one monomial relation: choosing a lowermost crossing chi, the tripod variable Z_{tau_chi} can be expressed as a Laurent monomial in the other tripod variables because the corresponding edge conductance appears in only one biratio. The paper states several conjectures that may be relevant as analogies: Conjecture 1 says that for a minimal network with m edges, positivity should be testable using m noninterlaced minors; Conjecture 2 predicts a base set S of m noninterlaced minors such that every nonzero noninterlaced minor is both Laurent and positive in S; Conjecture 3, later proved by Tri Lai according to the paper’s note, predicts domino-tiling Laurent formulas for semicontiguous minors. The final question asks for a decision procedure that, using at most binomial(n,2) positivity tests, determines the strand matching whose standard-network conductances are positive.
why_relevant: Develops coordinates and cell-structure compatibility for circular planar electrical networks. The degeneration of conductances to boundary cells parallels resolving crossings in a matching complex.

title: Valid Plane Trees: Combinatorial Models for RNA Secondary Structures with Watson-Crick Base Pairs
overall_summary: The paper studies rooted plane trees whose boundary half-edges are labeled by a word P in a complementary alphabet, with the constraint that every tree edge pairs complementary letters; equivalently, these are noncrossing perfect matchings of the letters of P. Its main structural result is that the graph of P-valid plane trees is connected under valid local moves of Condon-Heitsch-Hoos, and after orienting local moves of type 2 this graph is acyclic with a unique sink. The unique sink is produced by an explicit greedy stack algorithm and is also characterized by having no outgoing type-2 move and by minimizing total distance from the root.
