The core techniques are the plane-tree/noncrossing-matching dictionary, interval decomposition along an edge, a leaf argument that creates a common edge by one valid local move, and a Lyapunov function for directed local moves. For the original problem, the paper is most useful for understanding the 0- and 1-dimensional combinatorics: noncrossing inverse-letter matchings are exactly valid plane trees, and the boundary of a one-crossing matching corresponds closely to the paper's two local resolutions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 3.1 (bijection between plane trees and noncrossing matchings)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let a rooted plane tree with n edges have its 2n half-edges labeled 1,2,...,2n by walking counterclockwise from the root, and write e(i,j) for the edge whose left and right half-edge labels are i and j. There is a classical bijection between rooted plane trees with n edges and noncrossing perfect matchings on the set {1,...,2n}, sending each edge e(i,j) to the matched pair (i,j). This bijection respects inclusion: subtrees of a plane tree correspond to submatchings of the noncrossing matching, and conversely. If P=p1p2...p2n is a word in a complementary alphabet and a plane tree is P-valid, meaning that p_i and p_j are complements whenever e(i,j) is an edge, then the associated noncrossing perfect matching pairs only complementary letters of P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Reading the two half-edge labels of every edge in a rooted plane tree gives n pairs on {1,...,2n}; planarity of the embedded rooted tree prevents pairs i< i' < j < j' from occurring, so the matching is noncrossing. Conversely, a noncrossing perfect matching can be thickened or nested in the standard Catalan way to recover a rooted plane tree whose counterclockwise boundary labels are the original endpoints. The inclusion statement follows because an edge e(i,j) cuts out a contiguous interval of boundary labels inside it, and the pairs entirely in that interval are exactly the corresponding subtree or submatching. The validity assertion is immediate from the definition of P-validity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1 (basic structure of plane trees)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S be a rooted plane tree with n edges, with half-edges labeled 1,2,...,2n counterclockwise from the root, and let e(i,j) denote an edge with left half-edge label i and right half-edge label j. Then: (1) S satisfies the noncrossing condition: no two edges e(i,j) and e(i',j') have i < i' < j < j'. (2) For each edge e(i,j), the half-edges whose labels lie strictly between i and j form a subtree, and the half-edges whose labels lie outside the interval [i,j] form the complementary subtree. (3) For every edge e(i,j), the labels i and j have opposite parity.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The noncrossing condition is the usual planar/noncrossing property under the standard bijection between plane trees and noncrossing matchings. Once noncrossing is known, an edge e(i,j) separates the boundary labels into the interval i+1,...,j-1 and its complement; no edge can have one endpoint in the interval and one endpoint outside without crossing e(i,j), so these labels form subtrees. Since the interval between i and j is filled by complete edges, it contains an even number of half-edge labels; hence j-i-1 is even, so j-i is odd and i,j have opposite parity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.2 and Propositions 2.6-2.7 (valid local moves and endpoint-label criterion)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet, meaning a set with a fixed-point-free involution B ↦ bar(B), and let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A. A plane tree S is P-valid if every edge e(i,j) of S has p_j=bar(p_i). For indices i<j<i'<j', a type-1 local move replaces two adjacent edges e(i,j) and e(i',j') of a plane tree by e(i,j') and e(j,i'); a type-2 local move is the inverse operation, replacing adjacent edges e(i,j') and e(j,i') by e(i,j) and e(i',j'). A local move is valid with respect to P if both the initial and final plane trees are P-valid. A type-1 local move is valid if and only if its inverse type-2 local move is valid. More explicitly, a type-1 move on adjacent edges e(i,j) and e(i',j') is valid if and only if there is a letter B in A such that p_i=p_i'=B and p_j=p_j'=bar(B). Similarly, a type-2 move on adjacent edges e(i,j') and e(j,i') is valid if and only if there is a letter B in A such that p_i=p_i'=B and p_j=p_j'=bar(B). Consequently the graph G_P whose vertices are P-valid plane trees and whose edges are valid local moves is a well-defined undirected graph.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The two local moves are inverse as operations on plane trees, so validity of one is equivalent to validity of the inverse between the same two P-valid trees. For the endpoint-label criterion, consider type 1. Since the initial tree is P-valid, p_i is complementary to p_j and p_i' is complementary to p_j'. The final tree is P-valid exactly when p_i is complementary to p_j' and p_j is complementary to p_i'. Complements are unique in A, so p_j and p_j' must be the same complement of p_i, and p_i and p_i' must be the same complement of p_j. Thus p_i=p_i'=B and p_j=p_j'=bar(B) for some B. Conversely, those equalities make all four possible pairings complementary, so both initial and final trees are valid. The type-2 statement is the same argument applied to the inverse move. Since every valid move has a valid inverse, the graph with edges given by valid local moves is undirected.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.8 (validity inherited by interval subtrees)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet, let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A, and let T be a P-valid rooted plane tree. Suppose T contains an edge e(i,j), and let v be the endpoint of e(i,j) farthest from the root. Let T_in be the subgraph consisting of e(i,j) together with all descendants of v; its half-edge labels are exactly i,i+1,...,j. Let P_in be the subsequence of P in those positions. Let T_out be the subgraph obtained by erasing T_in from T, and let P_out be the subsequence of P in the complementary positions. Then T_in is P_in-valid and T_out is P_out-valid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By the convention for labeling half-edges of a rooted plane tree, the descendants of the far endpoint v of e(i,j) are exactly the half-edges with labels i+1,...,j-1. These descendants form a subtree, and adding e(i,j) gives T_in. Every edge of T_in is an edge of T, so its endpoint labels are complementary in P and hence also in the restricted sequence P_in. Removing T_in leaves the connected outside subgraph T_out with the outside half-edge labels; its edges are again inherited from the P-valid tree T, so they remain complementary with respect to P_out.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.9 (one valid local move can create a common edge)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet, let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A, and let T and T' be two P-valid plane trees. Then either T and T' share an edge, or there exists a valid local move on one of the trees, say T' to T'', such that T and T'' share an edge.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume T and T' have no common edge. Choose a leaf edge of T. A leaf edge has consecutive half-edge labels, so write it as e(i,i+1). Since T is P-valid, the letters p_i and p_{i+1} are complementary. In T', the half-edges i and i+1 cannot be paired to each other, because T and T' share no edge. The noncrossing/plane-tree structure forces the two edges incident to the half-edges i and i+1 in T' to occur in one of three adjacent local configurations: both i and i+1 are left half-edges, both are right half-edges, or they form a peak-shaped nested configuration. Let the other endpoints in these two edges be j and k. Because T' is P-valid and p_i,p_{i+1} are complementary, the labels at j and k have exactly the complementary pattern required by the endpoint-label criterion for a valid local move. In each of the three configurations, performing the appropriate type-1 or type-2 local move replaces the two adjacent edges by a configuration containing the leaf e(i,i+1). The resulting tree T'' is P-valid and shares the edge e(i,i+1) with T.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.2 and Lemma 3.3 (greedy stack algorithm and interval deletion)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet and let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A. The greedy algorithm reads the letters from left to right using a stack of indexed letters: push p1; for each subsequent letter p_j, inspect the top indexed letter p_i on the stack, and if p_i=bar(p_j), pop p_i and record the ordered pair (i,j), while otherwise pushing p_j. Let E(P) be the set of recorded pairs. If the algorithm, when run on P, pops p_i at the jth step, and if P' is obtained from P by deleting the contiguous block p_i p_{i+1} ... p_j while retaining the original indices outside that block, then running the greedy algorithm on P' gives the same final stack as running it on P. Moreover E(P) is the disjoint union of E(P') and a perfect matching M on the set of indices {i,i+1,...,j}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If p_i is popped at step j, then p_i must still be on top of the stack immediately before reading p_j. Therefore every letter among p_{i+1},...,p_{j-1} that was ever pushed onto the stack must have been popped before step j; otherwise it would lie above p_i and prevent p_i from being popped. Also no letter among p_1,...,p_{i-1} can have been popped during the intervening steps i+1,...,j-1, because p_i remains above them until step j. Thus the operations between i and j match all indices in the interval {i,...,j} among themselves, and after step j the stack is exactly what it was just before p_i was read. Deleting the interval therefore does not affect the subsequent stack evolution outside the interval, and the recorded pairs split into the outside pairs E(P') and the perfect matching M inside the deleted interval.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.4 (the greedy algorithm finds a valid plane tree whenever one exists)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet and let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A. If there exists at least one P-valid plane tree, then the greedy stack algorithm terminates with an empty stack, and its recorded pairs E(P) form a P-valid plane tree T0(P).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The interval-deletion lemma implies that whenever the greedy algorithm records a pair (i,j), all indices between i and j are matched among themselves before p_i is popped; hence the recorded pairs are nested/noncrossing. Thus an empty final stack gives a P-valid noncrossing perfect matching, equivalently a P-valid plane tree. It remains to prove the stack is empty if any valid tree exists. The paper argues by minimal counterexample. Suppose Q=q1...q2k is a shortest word for which a Q-valid plane tree exists but the greedy algorithm ends with a nonempty stack. Any valid plane tree has a leaf e(i,i+1), so q_i and q_{i+1} are complementary; if no pop has occurred before step i+1, then the algorithm pops at that step, and otherwise it already popped earlier. Hence the greedy algorithm pops at least once. Let the first pop occur at step j+1, necessarily popping q_j, so q_j and q_{j+1} are complementary. By Lemma 3.3, deleting q_j q_{j+1} gives a shorter word Q' on which the greedy algorithm has the same final nonempty stack. If some Q-valid tree has the leaf e(j,j+1), remove that leaf to obtain a Q'-valid tree. If not, take any Q-valid tree; the two consecutive half-edges j and j+1 lie in one of the adjacent local configurations from Proposition 2.9, and because q_j and q_{j+1} are complementary, a valid local move produces a Q-valid tree containing the leaf e(j,j+1), which can then be removed. Thus Q' is a shorter word with a valid tree but nonempty greedy stack, contradicting minimality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.5 (the greedy tree has no valid type-2 move)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet, let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A with at least one P-valid plane tree, and let T0(P) be the P-valid tree produced by the greedy algorithm. Then T0(P) has no valid local move of type 2; equivalently, it has no adjacent nested pair of edges e(i,j') and e(j,i') with i<j<i'<j' that can be validly replaced by e(i,j) and e(i',j').
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Suppose such a valid type-2 move existed in T0(P). By the endpoint-label criterion, for some letter B one has p_i=p_i'=B and p_j=p_j'=bar(B), after possibly naming B appropriately. In the greedy construction of T0(P), when the algorithm reaches position j, the letter p_i is still on the stack because it is paired in T0(P) with the later position j'. Proposition 2.8, applied to the relevant subtrees inside the edge e(i,j'), ensures that all letters pushed after i and before j have already been popped, so p_i is actually on top of the stack at step j. Since p_j is its complement, the greedy algorithm would pop p_i at step j and create the edge e(i,j), contradicting the assumption that T0(P) contains e(i,j') instead. Therefore no valid type-2 move exists in T0(P).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.6 (every non-greedy valid tree has an outgoing type-2 move)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet, let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A with at least one P-valid plane tree, and let T0(P) be the greedy P-valid tree. If T is a P-valid plane tree with T ≠ T0(P), then T admits a valid local move of type 2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since a noncrossing matching is determined by which half-edges are left endpoints and which are right endpoints, T and T0 differ at some first half-edge label j where one has j as a left half-edge and the other has j as a right half-edge. First show that j must be a right half-edge in T0. If instead j were a left half-edge in T0 and a right half-edge in T, let e(i',j) be the edge of T ending at j. The interval i'+1,...,j-1 forms a subtree of T, and by the choice of j all earlier left/right endpoint statuses agree with T0, so the same interval structure occurs in T0. Thus p_i' would be on top of the greedy stack at step j, and since T is P-valid, p_j is its complement; the greedy algorithm would pair i' with j, making j a right half-edge in T0 after all, a contradiction. Hence j is a right half-edge in T0, say in the edge e(i,j). Then i and j are both left half-edges in T: i is before the first difference and is left in T0, while j differs. The labels p_i and p_j are complementary because e(i,j) is an edge of T0. The interval between i and j is a subtree in T0 and, again by the first-difference choice, is also present in T. This gives exactly the local nested configuration in T that supports a type-2 move, and the endpoint-label criterion shows that the move is valid.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.7 (connectedness of the graph of valid plane trees)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet and let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A. Let G_P be the graph whose vertices are the P-valid plane trees and whose edges are valid local moves of type 1 or type 2. If V(P), the set of P-valid plane trees, is nonempty, then G_P is connected; equivalently, any two P-valid plane trees can be joined by a finite sequence of valid local moves.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on n, the number of edges. The case n=1 is immediate. Let S and T be two P-valid plane trees with n edges. If they share no edge, Proposition 2.9 gives a valid local move on one of them to a tree that shares an edge with the other; it is therefore enough to handle the case in which S and T share an edge e(i,j). Cut both trees along e(i,j). The portion inside the interval [i,j] and the complementary outside portion are P-valid trees for the corresponding shorter subsequences by Proposition 2.8. By the induction hypothesis, the inside subtree of S can be changed into the inside subtree of T by valid local moves supported entirely inside the interval, and the outside subtree of S can be changed into the outside subtree of T by valid local moves supported entirely outside the interval. Performing those same moves in the original tree, while keeping the common edge e(i,j) fixed, gives a path from S to T in G_P. Prepending the one move supplied by Proposition 2.9 handles the no-common-edge case.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.8 (unique sink of the type-2 orientation)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet, let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A with at least one P-valid plane tree, and let T0(P) be the greedy P-valid plane tree. Define a directed graph G_P^+ on the P-valid plane trees by directing an edge T → T' when T' is obtained from T by a valid local move of type 2. Then T0(P) is the unique sink of G_P^+.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Corollary 3.5 says that T0(P) has no valid type-2 move, so no edge of G_P^+ leaves T0(P); hence T0(P) is a sink. Theorem 3.6 says that every other P-valid tree has at least one valid type-2 move, so every other vertex has an outgoing directed edge. Therefore no other sink exists.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.10 (acyclicity and directed paths to the greedy tree)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet, let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A with at least one P-valid plane tree, let T0(P) be the greedy tree, and let G_P^+ be the directed graph whose edges are valid type-2 moves. Then G_P^+ has no directed cycles. Moreover, for every P-valid plane tree T, there is a directed path in G_P^+ from T to T0(P), consisting only of valid type-2 moves.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a rooted plane tree T with root v0, define d_T = sum over vertices v of dist(v,v0), the total distance of all vertices from the root. A direct comparison of the local type-2 move shows that d_T drops by at least one under every valid type-2 move, and drops by more if one of the intervening subtrees in the schematic is nonempty. Since d_T strictly decreases along directed edges, G_P^+ has no directed cycle. The undirected graph G_P is connected by Theorem 3.7 and finite. Starting from any T, repeatedly follow an outgoing edge if one exists. Strict decrease of d_T prevents infinite repetition, so the process terminates at a sink. By Corollary 3.8 the only sink is T0(P), giving a directed path from T to T0(P).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.11 (characterizations of the greedy tree)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet and let P=p1p2...p2n be a word in A with at least one P-valid plane tree. For a P-valid plane tree T, define d_T = sum over vertices v of dist(v,v0), where v0 is the root. The distinguished tree T0(P) may be characterized equivalently as the tree produced by the greedy stack algorithm, as the unique sink in the type-2 oriented graph G_P^+, and as a P-valid plane tree with minimum total distance d_T among all P-valid plane trees.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence between the greedy construction and the unique sink statement is Corollary 3.8 together with the definition of T0(P). For the distance characterization, Corollary 3.10 supplies a directed path of type-2 moves from any P-valid tree T to T0(P), and each type-2 move strictly decreases d_T. Hence no tree reached before T0(P) on such a path can have smaller distance than T0(P), so T0(P) realizes the minimum of d_T over V(P).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1 (every plane tree can occur as a greedy tree for some word)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a complementary alphabet with 2m letters, m≥1. For every rooted plane tree T with n edges, there exists a word P=p1p2...p2n in A such that T is P-valid and T is the greedy tree T0(P) produced by the greedy algorithm.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Label each edge incident to the root by any complementary pair of letters from A. Every non-root edge has a parent edge. Proceed outward from the root, and label each non-root edge by a complementary pair chosen so that this edge and its parent do not form the endpoint-label pattern that would allow a valid type-2 local move. At each step, only one choice of the left half-edge label is forbidden by the parent, so at least 2m-1 choices remain. After all edges are labeled, the resulting labeled tree is P-valid for the word P read around the half-edges, and it has no valid type-2 local move because any type-2 move would occur between a parent edge and a child edge. By Theorem 3.6 or Corollary 3.11, a P-valid tree with no type-2 move must be the greedy tree T0(P).

proof_techniques.name: Plane-tree/noncrossing-matching dictionary
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically replaces rooted plane trees by noncrossing perfect matchings on the boundary labels 1,...,2n. Each edge e(i,j) is a matched pair, and planarity is encoded by the absence of interleavings i<i'<j<j'. The dictionary also turns an edge e(i,j) into an interval decomposition: labels between i and j belong to the descendant subtree, while labels outside form the complementary part.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The vertices of F_w are 0-crossing matchings, i.e. noncrossing perfect matchings pairing inverse letters. This dictionary allows one to import rooted-tree language, interval subproblems, and local-move arguments directly at the vertex level.

proof_techniques.name: Stack-greedy normal form
proof_techniques.description: The greedy algorithm reads the word left to right, pushing a letter unless it is complementary to the stack top, in which case the stack top is popped and paired with the current letter. The key lemma says that if a pop pairs i with j, then the entire interval i,...,j is internally matched and can be deleted without changing the final outside stack. A minimal-counterexample argument then proves that if any valid plane tree exists, the greedy algorithm produces one.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This supplies a canonical noncrossing inverse-letter matching whenever such matchings exist. A contraction or discrete Morse strategy for F_w could try to flow all cells toward the greedy vertex, using the interval-deletion property to define recursive choices.

proof_techniques.name: Endpoint-label criterion for valid local resolutions
proof_techniques.description: For two adjacent edges with boundary labels i<j<i'<j', the paper identifies exactly when both local resolutions are valid: the two left endpoints must carry the same letter B and the two right endpoints must carry the complement bar(B). This reduces validity of a local move to a four-letter test independent of the surrounding subtrees.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A 1-crossing cell of F_w has two local resolutions. This criterion is the precise combinatorial test for when the two resolutions remain inverse-letter matchings, so it can identify the 1-skeleton moves induced by cell attachments.

proof_techniques.name: Create a common edge by moving a leaf
proof_techniques.description: To compare two valid plane trees T and T', the paper chooses a leaf e(i,i+1) of T. If T' does not contain this leaf, the consecutive half-edges i and i+1 occur in T' in one of three possible adjacent configurations. Since the leaf in T proves p_i and p_{i+1} are complementary, the endpoint-label criterion makes the appropriate local move in T' valid, and that move creates the desired leaf.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a way to force agreement with a target noncrossing matching after at most one local resolution move. It is a natural ingredient for inductive connectivity or contractibility arguments that cut along a shared arc.

proof_techniques.name: Cut along a common edge and recurse
proof_techniques.description: Once two valid trees share an edge e(i,j), Proposition 2.8 splits each tree into inside and outside valid subtrees for shorter subsequences. Local moves supported inside the interval and outside the interval can then be performed independently while keeping e(i,j) fixed. This is the induction step in the connectedness proof.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a subcomplex of F_w consisting of matchings containing a fixed noncrossing arc decomposes into smaller word-complexes, this technique suggests a recursive proof of contractibility or collapsibility.

proof_techniques.name: Acyclic orientation via total root distance
proof_techniques.description: The paper orients every valid local move according to type 2 and defines d_T as the sum of distances from the root to all vertices of a plane tree T. A direct local check shows every type-2 move strictly lowers d_T, so the directed graph has no cycles. Together with connectedness and the no-type-2 characterization of the greedy tree, this gives a unique sink and directed paths from every valid tree to that sink.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a candidate Lyapunov function for a discrete Morse or gradient-flow approach to F_w. One could try to extend the orientation from vertices/edges to higher-dimensional crossing cells by resolving crossings in the direction that decreases an analogue of d_T.

proof_techniques.name: Labeling to avoid type-2 moves
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 5.1 labels any prescribed plane tree so that no parent-child pair of edges satisfies the endpoint-label pattern for a type-2 move. The resulting tree is valid for the word read around it and, because it has no type-2 move, is the greedy tree for that word.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is useful for constructing controlled examples: one can prescribe the canonical noncrossing matching and then choose labels so that it is the unique sink of the valid local-move orientation.

other_useful_info: The paper emphasizes that local moves need not involve successively labeled half-edges: there may be many other edges or subtrees between the four displayed labels, so locality is in the plane tree rather than in the boundary word. Remark 3.9 notes that the type-2 oriented graph can have multiple sources even though it has a unique sink; the example P=AABBAABB has two sources and one sink. The local moves used are those of Condon, Heitsch, and Hoos, and the paper cites Kemp-Mahlburg-Rattan-Smyth and Schumacher-Yan for related enumeration of noncrossing pairings on words.
why_relevant: Introduces foldings of complementary words around plane trees, equivalently noncrossing perfect matchings of inverse/complementary letters. This is directly relevant to the vertices of F_w and to greedy or inductive constructions of valid matchings.

title: Hyperplanes of Squier’s cube complexes
overall_summary: The paper studies Squier cube complexes S(P,w) associated to semigroup presentations P and base words w, focusing on their hyperplanes. Its central contributions are explicit descriptions of when two Squier hyperplanes coincide or cross, an ordered relation ≺ on crossing hyperplanes whose chains exactly correspond to cubes, criteria for specialness, and decompositions of Squier complexes by cutting along well-chosen clean disjoint hyperplanes. The most transferable material for the matching complex F_w is the hyperplane technology: classify local moves up to context, order transverse hyperplanes by planar/linear position, prove chain-to-cube and transitivity statements, and then use CAT(0) cubulation, product-of-trees embeddings, or graph-of-spaces decompositions. The paper does not discuss the free-group matching complex directly, but it provides a detailed model for proving contractibility of a cubical complex built from local resolutions by showing it is CAT(0), or by inductively cutting along product-neighborhood hyperplanes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.2 and Theorem 2.4 (cubical CAT(0) facts recalled)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A cube complex is nonpositively curved if the link of every vertex is a simplicial flag complex; in the cubical setting recalled in the paper, a simply connected nonpositively curved cube complex is CAT(0). If X is a CAT(0) cube complex, then X is special in the sense of Haglund and Wise, and every hyperplane of X separates X into two connected components called halfspaces.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes these as standard facts, citing Bridson-Haefliger for the nonpositive curvature/CAT(0) criterion, Haglund-Wise for specialness of CAT(0) cube complexes, and Sageev for hyperplane separation. The operational content used later is that CAT(0) rules out hyperplane pathologies and gives a halfspace pocset: each hyperplane has exactly two complementary sides, so vertices can be encoded by choices of sides.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.12 (cubulating a pocset)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (Sigma,<,*) be a pocset: a partially ordered set with an involution * such that A and A* are never comparable, and A < B if and only if B* < A*. An ultrafilter alpha chooses exactly one of A and A* for each A and is upward closed in the sense that B < A and B in alpha imply A in alpha. A DCC ultrafilter is one in which every infinite descending chain is eventually constant. Construct a cube complex X(Sigma) whose vertices are DCC ultrafilters, whose edges join ultrafilters differing on exactly one complementary pair, and whose higher cubes are filled whenever their 1-skeletons appear. Then every connected component of X(Sigma) is a CAT(0) cube complex.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper records this as Sageev-Roller cubulation and does not reprove it. The construction makes a cube whenever a finite set of choices can be flipped independently. The DCC condition selects the vertices in the intended component. The flag condition in links is built into the rule that every compatible family of pairwise independent flips spans a cube, and the standard cubulation theorem gives simply connectedness of components and hence CAT(0).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.14 (distance after cubulating selected hyperplanes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a CAT(0) cube complex, let H be a collection of hyperplanes of X, and let X(H) be the cube complex obtained by cubulating the pocset of halfspaces delimited by hyperplanes in H. For a vertex v of X, let phi(v) be the principal ultrafilter consisting of all halfspaces in this pocset that contain v. Then for any vertices x,y of X, the combinatorial distance in X(H) between phi(x) and phi(y) is exactly the number of hyperplanes in H that separate x from y in X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In a CAT(0) cube complex, combinatorial distance between two vertices equals the number of hyperplanes separating them. The two principal ultrafilters phi(x) and phi(y) differ precisely for those hyperplanes of H whose two halfspaces contain x and y on opposite sides. In the cubulated complex, crossing one edge flips exactly one such choice, so the graph distance is the number of differing choices, i.e. the number of selected hyperplanes separating x and y.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.15, Theorem 2.18, and Lemma 2.20 (Squier complexes, Farley complexes, and universal covers)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P = <Sigma | R> be a semigroup presentation and w a base word in Sigma+. The Squier complex S(P,w) is the connected component of the Squier complex whose vertices are words equal to w modulo P, edges apply one defining relation inside a context, and n-cubes apply n relations in disjoint contexts. The diagram group D(P,w) is naturally isomorphic to pi_1(S(P),w). The Farley complex X(P,w) has vertices reduced semigroup diagrams Delta with top(Delta)=w, and its n-cubes are spanned by diagrams Delta·P where P ranges over prefixes of a thin diagram with n cells. Farley's theorem states that X(P,w) is a complete CAT(0) cube complex. The map Delta ↦ bot(Delta) induces the universal covering X(P,w) → S(P,w), and the quotient X(P,w)/D(P,w) is cellularly isomorphic to S(P,w).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A loop in S(P) based at w is a finite sequence of relations that starts and ends at w; stacking the corresponding atomic semigroup diagrams gives a spherical diagram. The squares and higher cubes of S(P) encode commutations of independent relations, so the homotopy class of the loop matches the reduced spherical diagram, giving D(P,w) ≃ pi_1(S(P),w). Farley's complex is built by allowing independent atomic diagrams to be attached simultaneously; its cube structure records the Boolean lattice of prefixes of a thin diagram. The CAT(0) property is quoted from Farley. Left multiplication by spherical diagrams gives a free action of D(P,w) on X(P,w), and taking bottom labels forgets the chosen lift but records exactly the Squier vertex and cube, producing the quotient and universal cover.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1 (equality of Squier hyperplanes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (p,a→b,q) and (r,c→d,s) be oriented edges in the Squier complex S(P), where a=b and c=d are defining relations in the displayed orientations. Denote by [p,a→b,q] and [r,c→d,s] the oriented hyperplanes dual to these edges. Then [p,a→b,q] = [r,c→d,s] if and only if a=c and b=d as words in Sigma+, and p=r and q=s modulo P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the forward implication, take a path of parallel edges in the equivalence class defining the hyperplane. Induct on the length of this path. Passing from one edge to a parallel edge across a square only changes the context on the left or the right by applying a relation in P; the distinguished relation a→b itself is unchanged. Thus after any number of square moves the relation word is still a→b and the left and right contexts are only changed modulo P. For the converse, choose derivation sequences from p to r and from q to s modulo P. Applying these derivations in the left and right contexts of the fixed central edge (a→b) produces a grid of squares showing that the two displayed edges are connected by parallelism, hence dual to the same oriented hyperplane.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2 and Remark 3.3 (transversality and the order ≺)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (a,u→v,b) and (c,p→q,d) be edges of S(P). The hyperplanes [a,u→v,b] and [c,p→q,d] are transverse if and only if there exists a word y in Sigma+ satisfying either c = a u y and b = y p d modulo P, or d = y u b and a = c p y modulo P. Moreover, in the first alternative, the paper's relation [a,u→v,b] ≺ [c,p→q,d] holds: the first hyperplane is the one corresponding to the relation applied to the left subword in a square.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If the displayed congruences hold, one writes down the corresponding square: in the first case the two relations occur as disjoint subwords in the word a u y p d, with u→v on the left and p→q on the right; in the second case the roles are reversed. Lemma 3.1 identifies the hyperplanes dual to the sides of this square with the originally specified hyperplanes. Conversely, if two hyperplanes cross, they cross in some square (x,p→q,y,u→v,z) or (x,u→v,y,p→q,z). Applying Lemma 3.1 to compare the sides of this square with the given edges gives exactly one of the two systems of congruences.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.5 (product form of a Squier hyperplane neighborhood)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For words a,u,v,b with u=v a defining relation, let J = [a,u→v,b] be an oriented hyperplane in S(P). Let S(P,a) u S(P,b) denote the image of S(P,a) × S(P,b) under the map (alpha,beta) ↦ alpha u beta, and similarly define S(P,a) v S(P,b). With the orientation induced by u→v, the negative boundary component of the hyperplane neighborhood is ∂−J = S(P,a) u S(P,b), and the positive boundary component is ∂+J = S(P,a) v S(P,b). If J is clean, then J is naturally isometric to S(P,a) × S(P,b).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lift J to a hyperplane in the Farley complex. A cube in the carrier of the lift consists of the distinguished cell changing u to v, together with any collection of independent cells occurring strictly in the prefix a or suffix b. Therefore the two boundary components of the carrier are obtained by allowing arbitrary Squier moves in the prefix and suffix while keeping the middle word u or v fixed. Passing down to S(P) via the quotient X(P,w)/D(P,w) gives the stated descriptions. If J is clean, the quotient does not identify the two sides or fold the carrier along itself, so the mid-hyperplane is exactly the product of the prefix and suffix Squier complexes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 3.8, 3.9, and 3.10 (local hyperplane pathologies in Squier complexes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In any Squier complex S(P), every hyperplane is 2-sided. A hyperplane J is self-intersecting if and only if J = [a,p→q,c] for some words a,p,q,b,c with p=q a defining relation and with a = a p b and c = b p c modulo P. A hyperplane J is self-osculating if and only if J = [a,(k h)^n k → p,b] for some n ≥ 1 and words a,k,h,p,b, where the displayed arrow is a defining relation, such that a = a k h and b = h k b modulo P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For 2-sidedness, if a hyperplane were 1-sided, one would have [a,x→y,b] = [a,y→x,b], contradicting Lemma 3.1 because defining relations are not of the form x=x and the orientation data would have to agree. For self-intersection, one direction is explicit: if a = a p b and c = b p c modulo P, then Lemma 3.1 makes J dual to two nonparallel sides of the square (a,p→q,b,p→q,c). Conversely, any self-intersection occurs in such a square, and Lemma 3.1 forces the same relation to label both crossing directions and gives the displayed congruences. For self-osculation, two distinct edges dual to the same oriented hyperplane share an endpoint but do not span a square. The two occurrences of the same relation overlap; writing the overlap equation as u v = v w and choosing the maximal power of u that prefixes v yields u = k h, v = (k h)^n k, and w = h k. Lemma 3.1 then converts equality of hyperplanes into the congruences a = a k h and b = h k b. The converse builds the two osculating edges with common endpoint from these congruences.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.6 and Corollary 3.7 (criteria for clean and special Squier complexes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P = <Sigma | R> be a semigroup presentation and w0 in Sigma+ a base word. The following are equivalent: (i) every hyperplane of S(P,w0) is clean; (ii) S(P,w0) has no self-intersecting hyperplanes; (iii) there do not exist words a,b,p in Sigma+ such that w0 = a b, a = a p, and b = p b modulo P, with the congruence class [p]_P not equal to the singleton {p}. Moreover, S(P,w0) is special if and only if these equivalent conditions hold and, in addition, there do not exist words a,u,v,w,b,p,q,xi in Sigma+ such that w0 = a u v w b, au = au(v xi), wb = (xi v)wb modulo P, and uv=p and vw=q are defining relations in R. Corollary 3.7 states that if no words a,b,p satisfy w0=ab, a=ap, and b=pb modulo P, then S(P,w0) is special; in particular, if the congruence class [w0]_P is finite, then S(P,w0) is special.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implication clean implies no self-intersection is immediate. If condition (iii) fails, choose a nontrivial relation inside p, say p=x r y with r=s in R; then the hyperplane [a x,r→s,y b] self-intersects inside a square with two copies of the same relation, using a=ap and b=pb to place both copies in the component of w0. Conversely, if there is a self-intersection, Lemma 3.9 gives words with a repeated insertable block, yielding a violation of (iii); if there is self-osculation, Lemma 3.10 similarly yields an insertable block (k h)^{n+1} whose class is nontrivial, again violating (iii). Since Lemma 3.8 already gives 2-sidedness, this proves the equivalence. For specialness, after cleanliness only inter-osculation remains. The proof analyzes the two possible overlapping configurations of two edges with common endpoint: edges of the form (a,uv→p,wb) and (au,vw→q,b), or edges of the form (au,v→p,wb) and (a,uvw→q,b). Applying the transversality criterion Lemma 3.2 shows that each inter-osculation either violates condition (iii) or gives exactly the displayed obstruction involving xi. Conversely, if the displayed obstruction exists, the edges (a,uv→p,wb) and (au,vw→q,b) share the endpoint auvwb, do not lie in a common square, but their hyperplanes cross in the square (a,uv→p,xi,vw→q,b), so the hyperplanes inter-osculate. For the finiteness corollary, if a=ap and b=pb then w0 is congruent to a p^n b for all n, giving infinitely many representatives unless the insertion obstruction is absent.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.2 (chains in ≺ are realized by cubes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let J1,...,Jn be hyperplanes of S(P,w) such that Ji ≺ J_{i+1} for every i=1,...,n−1. Then there exists an n-cube (a1,u1→v1,a2,u2→v2,...,an,un→vn,a_{n+1}) in S(P,w) such that, for each k, the hyperplane dual to the kth direction is Jk; explicitly Jk = [a1 u1 ... a_{k−1} u_{k−1} a_k, u_k→v_k, a_{k+1} u_{k+1} ... a_n u_n a_{n+1}].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on n. The case n=2 is exactly the definition of J1 ≺ J2 as crossing in a square with J1 on the left. For the induction step, apply the hypothesis to J1,...,Jn to get an n-cube with Jn as its last direction. Since Jn ≺ J_{n+1}, there is a square (a,un→vn,b,p→q,c) in which Jn is the left hyperplane and J_{n+1} the right one. Lemma 3.1 identifies the prefix a with the prefix of the last direction in the existing n-cube and identifies b p c with the final suffix a_{n+1} modulo P. Replacing that suffix by b,p→q,c produces an (n+1)-cube. Lemma 3.1 again verifies that the first n directions still represent J1,...,Jn and that the new last direction represents J_{n+1}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.3 / Proposition 1.3 (ordered intersections of Squier hyperplanes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For hyperplanes of S(P,w), the relation ≺ has the following properties. First, if J1 ≺ J2 and J2 ≺ J3, then J1 ≺ J3. Second, two hyperplanes J1 and J2 are comparable with respect to ≺ if and only if they intersect. Third, the maximum length n of a chain J1 ≺ ... ≺ Jn is equal to dim S(P,w).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For transitivity, apply Lemma 4.2 to J1,J2,J3 to obtain a 3-cube with these as ordered coordinate directions; the square using only the first and third directions witnesses J1 ≺ J3. Comparability is built into the definition: whenever two hyperplanes meet in a square, one of the two possible left-right orders holds; and if one is ≺ the other, they meet in the defining square. For the dimension statement, any chain of length n gives an n-cube by Lemma 4.2, so dim S(P,w) ≥ n. Conversely, every n-cube has n ordered coordinate directions from left to right, and these hyperplanes form a chain J1 ≺ ... ≺ Jn, so the maximum chain length is at least the dimension.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.5 (restriction on the transversality graph)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The transversality graph of S(P,w), whose vertices are hyperplanes and whose edges join transverse hyperplanes, contains no induced cycle of odd length greater than five.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume an induced odd cycle J1,...,Jn with n≥5 and n odd. Choose an orientation of the first edge, say J1 ≺ J2. Since J2 crosses J3, either J2 ≺ J3 or J3 ≺ J2. The first option would imply J1 ≺ J3 by transitivity, hence J1 crosses J3, contradicting inducedness; therefore J3 ≺ J2. Continuing around the cycle forces the inequalities to alternate. Because n is odd, the final forced inequality is incompatible with closing the cycle: whichever orientation the crossing between Jn and J1 has, transitivity forces an extra crossing Jn with J2 or J1 with J_{n−1}, again contradicting that the cycle is induced.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.8 (equal-rank hyperplanes are disjoint)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a hyperplane J in S(P,w), define rank(J) = sup{n ≥ 0 such that there exist hyperplanes J1,...,Jn with J1 ≺ ... ≺ Jn ≺ J}. The rank of a hyperplane in the Farley complex X(P,w) is defined as the rank of its image in S(P,w). If J1 and J2 are hyperplanes of X(P,w) with finite equal rank, then J1 and J2 are disjoint.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If J1 and J2 were transverse, their images in S(P,w) would be comparable by Corollary 4.3; suppose J1 ≺ J2. Let r=rank(J1) and choose a chain H1 ≺ ... ≺ Hr ≺ J1. Then H1 ≺ ... ≺ Hr ≺ J1 ≺ J2 is a chain of length r+1 below J2, so rank(J2)>rank(J1), contradicting equality. The other orientation is symmetric.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.16 (isometric embedding of the Farley complex into a product of rank trees)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume that S(P,w) has no infinite descending chain of hyperplanes with respect to ≺. Let J_k be the set of hyperplanes of the Farley complex X(P,w) having rank k. Since hyperplanes of the same rank are pairwise disjoint, cubulating the pocset of halfspaces determined by J_k gives a tree Lambda_k. Let Phi_k: X(P,w) → Lambda_k be the natural map sending a vertex to the principal ultrafilter it defines, and let Phi = Phi_0 × Phi_1 × ... . Then Phi embeds X(P,w) isometrically into the product Lambda_0 × Lambda_1 × ... with respect to combinatorial metrics.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 4.8, each rank class J_k is a family of pairwise disjoint hyperplanes, so the dual cube complex to its halfspace pocset is 1-dimensional; because it is CAT(0), it is a tree. For vertices x,y in X(P,w), Proposition 2.14 gives d_{Lambda_k}(Phi_k(x),Phi_k(y)) equal to the number of rank-k hyperplanes separating x and y. In the product of trees, the combinatorial distance is the sum over k of these numbers. The no-infinite-descending-chain hypothesis ensures every hyperplane has a finite rank, so the sum counts all hyperplanes separating x and y in X(P,w). Since combinatorial distance in a CAT(0) cube complex is the number of separating hyperplanes, d(Phi(x),Phi(y)) = d(x,y).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.21 (dimension criterion for Squier complexes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n ≥ 1. The Squier complex S(P,w) has dimension at least n if and only if there exist words u1,...,un in Sigma+ such that w = u1 ... un modulo P and each congruence class [ui]_P is nontrivial, i.e. [ui]_P ≠ {ui}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If S(P,w) contains an n-cube (a1,p1→q1,...,an,pn→qn,a_{n+1}), then w is congruent to a product of n pieces, namely (a1 p1), ..., (a_{n−1} p_{n−1}), and (a_n p_n a_{n+1}), each of which has a nontrivial congruence class because the corresponding relation can be applied. Conversely, if w = u1 ... un modulo P and each [ui]_P is nontrivial, then inside each ui choose a subword pi on which a defining relation pi=qi applies, writing ui = xi pi yi. Applying all these n relations in the disjoint factors u1,...,un gives an n-cube (x1,p1→q1,y1 x2,p2→q2,...,y_{n−1} x_n,p_n→q_n,y_n) in S(P,w).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 5.5 and 5.6 (left hyperplanes are clean and mutually disjoint)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A hyperplane J=[a,u→v,b] of S(P,w) is called left if D(P,a) is trivial but D(P,a u) is nontrivial. Every left hyperplane is clean: it is 2-sided, non-self-intersecting, and non-self-osculating. Moreover, two left hyperplanes do not intersect each other.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If a left hyperplane self-intersected, Lemma 3.9 and Lemma 3.1 would give a congruence a = a u c for some c, so D(P,a u) embeds into D(P,a u c) ≃ D(P,a), contradicting D(P,a u) nontrivial and D(P,a) trivial. If it self-osculated, Lemma 3.10 gives u=(k h)^n k and a=a k h modulo P, so D(P,a u) embeds into D(P,a(k h)^{n+1}) ≃ D(P,a), again a contradiction. For two left hyperplanes crossing in a square (a,u→v,b,p→q,c), the right hyperplane would be [a u b,p→q,c] while the left one is [a,u→v,b p c]; leftness of the right hyperplane gives D(P,a u b)=trivial, but D(P,a u) embeds into D(P,a u b), contradicting leftness of the left hyperplane.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.7 (which edges remain after cutting along left hyperplanes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let J=[a,u→v,b] be a left hyperplane. Write u = p_J ell_J s_J, where ell_J is a letter, p_J and s_J are words, D(P,a p_J) is trivial, and D(P,a p_J ell_J) is nontrivial; p_J is the maximal prefix of u with this property. Let x,y,p,q be words with a u b = x p y as words and p=q a defining relation. Then the hyperplane H=[x,p→q,y] is not left if and only if the occurrence of p as a subword of a u b is contained entirely in a p_J or entirely in s_J b.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If p lies inside a p_J, then D(P,x p) embeds into D(P,a p_J)=1, so H cannot be left. If p lies inside s_J b, then a p_J ell_J lies inside x, so D(P,a p_J ell_J) embeds into D(P,x), making D(P,x) nontrivial; again H is not left. Conversely, if p is contained in neither a p_J nor s_J b, then its occurrence must contain the distinguished letter ell_J. Thus x lies inside a p_J, giving D(P,x)=1, while a p_J ell_J embeds into x p, giving D(P,x p) nontrivial. This is exactly the definition that H is left.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.8 (graph-of-spaces decomposition by left hyperplanes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let J be the set of left hyperplanes of S(P,w). For each left hyperplane J=[a,u→v,b], write u=p_J ell_J s_J and v=q_J m_J r_J as in Lemma 5.7, with the analogous maximal-prefix property on the v side. Define a graph of spaces whose vertex-spaces are the subcomplexes S(P,a p_J) ell_J S(P,s_J b) and S(P,a q_J) m_J S(P,r_J b), for all J=[a,u→v,b] in J, identifying equal vertex-spaces as one vertex. To each left hyperplane [a,u→v,b] attach the edge-space S(P,a) × S(P,b), with edge maps the canonical maps to S(P,a) u S(P,b) and S(P,a) v S(P,b). Then this graph of spaces has geometric realization S(P,w).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Remove from S(P,w) the open carriers N(J)\∂J of all left hyperplanes J. Lemmas 5.5 and 5.6 imply these hyperplanes are clean and mutually disjoint, so the original Squier complex is recovered by gluing back each carrier, which by Theorem 3.5 is S(P,a)×S(P,b)×[0,1] with boundary faces S(P,a)uS(P,b) and S(P,a)vS(P,b). It remains to identify the connected components of the cut complex. For the component C_u containing a u b, prove C_u = S(P,a p_J) ell_J S(P,s_J b). One inclusion is by induction on the combinatorial distance from a u b to the initial point of an edge e in C_u. At distance zero, Lemma 5.7 says any edge not dual to a left hyperplane must apply its relation entirely inside a p_J or inside s_J b, hence lies in the product subcomplex. For the induction step, the previous edge gives a vertex alpha ell_J beta with alpha congruent to a p_J and beta congruent to s_J b; if the next relation crossed ell_J, the same embedding argument as in Lemma 5.7 would make it a left hyperplane, impossible in the cut component. Thus it again lies entirely on one side. The reverse inclusion follows from Lemma 5.7 because no edge of S(P,a p_J) ell_J S(P,s_J b) is dual to a left hyperplane. The v side is symmetric. This identifies all vertices and edge gluings of the graph of spaces.

proof_techniques.name: Mark a local move and classify its hyperplane by context
proof_techniques.description: In a Squier complex, an edge is a local rewriting u→v inside a left and right context, written (a,u→v,b). Lemma 3.1 shows that the associated oriented hyperplane is determined exactly by the central relation u→v and by the two contexts a,b modulo the semigroup presentation. The proof works by commuting all other independent rewrites past the marked rewrite through squares, so that moving within the hyperplane changes only the contexts modulo P and never the marked relation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, one could try to mark a single crossing or local resolution and define hyperplanes as equivalence classes under commuting resolutions of other crossings. A useful first target is an analogue of Lemma 3.1: identify precisely when two marked crossings in different matchings represent the same hyperplane.

proof_techniques.name: Order transverse hyperplanes by left-right position
proof_techniques.description: The paper defines J1 ≺ J2 when two hyperplanes meet in a square and J1 corresponds to the rewrite in the left subword while J2 corresponds to the rewrite in the right subword. Lemma 4.2 proves that any chain J1≺...≺Jn is realized simultaneously as the n ordered coordinate directions of a single cube. Corollary 4.3 then gives transitivity, comparability exactly when hyperplanes intersect, and equality between maximum chain length and dimension.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The matching complex has an ambient linear order on boundary points and planar arcs, so crossings or resolution events may admit a natural left-right or nesting order. Proving a chain-to-cell lemma analogous to Lemma 4.2 would be a strong route to flag links, CAT(0)-type structure, or an inductive dimension control.

proof_techniques.name: Use hyperplane pathologies as explicit forbidden local configurations
proof_techniques.description: Rather than checking specialness abstractly, the paper translates self-intersection, self-osculation, and inter-osculation of Squier hyperplanes into concrete algebraic patterns: repeated insertable blocks for self-intersection, overlapping periodic words for self-osculation, and a specific two-relation overlap for inter-osculation. Theorem 3.6 then proves clean/special criteria by showing that all possible pathologies are exactly these patterns.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The quasi-reduced hypothesis in the original problem forbids triples ell ell^{-1} ell. A promising transfer is to classify possible self-osculations or non-flag link configurations in F_w and check whether quasi-reducedness is precisely the local condition excluding them.

proof_techniques.name: Product neighborhoods of hyperplanes
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3.5 describes the carrier boundary of a Squier hyperplane [a,u→v,b] as S(P,a)uS(P,b) on one side and S(P,a)vS(P,b) on the other; when the hyperplane is clean, the hyperplane itself is isometric to S(P,a)×S(P,b). This product structure comes from the fact that all moves independent of the marked rewrite occur either in the left context or in the right context.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Resolving or fixing an arc/crossing in a matching often separates the word into subintervals or regions. If the subcomplex around such a feature factors into independent inside/outside matching complexes, this can support an induction on word length or number of pairs.

proof_techniques.name: Cubulate a halfspace pocset to prove CAT(0) and contractibility
proof_techniques.description: The paper recalls Sageev-Roller cubulation: from a pocset of halfspaces one builds a CAT(0) cube complex whose vertices are DCC ultrafilters. Principal ultrafilters from a CAT(0) cube complex map into this cubulation, and Proposition 2.14 says distances are counted by the selected separating hyperplanes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If vertices of F_w can be encoded as consistent choices of sides of hyperplanes or choices of resolutions satisfying a DCC/consistency condition, cubulation could identify F_w or its universal cover with a CAT(0) cube complex. CAT(0) would immediately imply contractibility.

proof_techniques.name: Rank-color hyperplanes and embed into products of trees
proof_techniques.description: Using the order ≺, the paper defines the rank of a hyperplane as the length of the longest descending chain below it. Lemma 4.8 shows that hyperplanes of the same finite rank are pairwise disjoint. Each rank class therefore dualizes to a tree, and Theorem 4.16 embeds the Farley complex isometrically into the product of these trees by recording the side of every rank-k hyperplane.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If F_w has an ordered hyperplane structure with no infinite descending chains, ranks could provide a coloring by noncrossing families. This may help prove median or CAT(0) behavior, or at least give a controlled normal form for paths by counting separating hyperplanes.

proof_techniques.name: Cut along a clean disjoint family and reconstruct a graph of spaces
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 5.8 selects left hyperplanes, proves they are clean and mutually disjoint, removes their open carriers, identifies the components of the complement via a maximal-prefix argument, and then reconstructs the whole Squier complex as a graph of spaces. The edge spaces are product hyperplanes, and the vertex spaces are smaller Squier-type subcomplexes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, one might choose a family of outermost or leftmost arcs/crossings that are pairwise disjoint in the hyperplane sense. Cutting along them could reduce contractibility to smaller matching complexes glued along product subcomplexes, with the nerve possibly a tree.

proof_techniques.name: Induction by maximal trivial prefix
proof_techniques.description: In Lemma 5.7 and Theorem 5.8, the paper finds the first letter in a word prefix where the diagram group changes from trivial to nontrivial. This distinguished letter acts as a separator: any edge crossing it is necessarily dual to a left hyperplane, while edges not crossing it remain in one of two product factors. This gives an exact description of components after cutting.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A similar separator could be the first boundary point or first matched pair at which a reducible subword becomes nontrivial. Such a separator may allow an inductive decomposition of F_w into subcomplexes corresponding to the left and right parts of the word.

proof_techniques.name: Use transversality graph restrictions as diagnostics
proof_techniques.description: Because the Squier hyperplane intersection relation is ordered, Corollary 4.5 rules out induced odd cycles of length greater than five in the transversality graph. The proof is purely order-theoretic: an induced cycle forces alternating inequalities, and odd length makes the alternation inconsistent with transitivity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proposed hyperplane structure on F_w has a transversality graph with forbidden induced odd cycles, this supports an ordered-Squier-like model. Conversely, proving such an order may simplify link-flag checks by reducing possible obstruction cycles.

other_useful_info: Corollary 3.7 is especially concise: if the congruence class [w]_P is finite, then S(P,w) is special; the introduction phrases this as every compact Squier complex is special. Remark 4.6 notes that the no-induced-odd-cycle restriction is sharp only for odd cycles: induced even cycles can occur in Squier transversality graphs. Theorem 5.13 constructs right-angled Artin groups from complements of finite interval graphs as diagram groups, and Theorem 5.16 cites the characterization that a graph is the complement of an interval graph iff it has no induced C4 and is transitively orientable; this may be relevant only if a hyperplane crossing graph arising from matchings turns out to be interval-graph-like. The paper repeatedly uses the standard fact that combinatorial distance in a CAT(0) cube complex equals the number of separating hyperplanes, a useful metric criterion for any attempted cubical model of F_w.
why_relevant: Analyzes hyperplanes and decompositions of Squier cube complexes. If F_w admits a cubical or median structure, these hyperplane methods could help prove nonpositive curvature or contractibility.

title: The Totally Nonnegative Grassmannian is a Ball
overall_summary: The paper proves that several positive spaces in total positivity and electrical network theory are homeomorphic to closed balls: the totally nonnegative Grassmannian Gr_{>=0}(k,n), a link V_{>=0} in the totally nonnegative upper unipotent group, the cyclically symmetric amplituhedron, and Lam's compactification E_n of planar electrical networks. The central method is a general topological lemma: if a bounded smooth embedded submanifold has compact closure and admits a contractive flow that sends the whole closure instantly into the open submanifold, then the closure is a closed ball and its boundary is a sphere. For the electrical-network compactification, the paper embeds E_n into Gr_{>=0}(n-1,2n), realizes it as a linear slice indexed by noncrossing partitions, and checks that the same cyclic-shift contractive flow preserves this slice and improves nonnegativity to positivity. This is potentially useful for the original matching complex problem if F_w can be realized as a closure, slice, face, or quotient of an electrical-network or totally nonnegative object, or if an analogous crossing-reduction flow can be built directly on matchings.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.1 and Lemma 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R^N be a real normed vector space with norm ||·||. A map f: R × R^N -> R^N is a contractive flow if it is continuous, satisfies f(0,p)=p and f(t1+t2,p)=f(t1,f(t2,p)) for all p and all real t1,t2, and satisfies ||f(t,p)|| < ||p|| for every p != 0 and every t>0. For any contractive flow: (i) f(t,0)=0 for all real t; (ii) if p != 0, then the function t -> ||f(t,p)|| is strictly decreasing on all of R; (iii) if p != 0, then lim_{t->infinity} ||f(t,p)|| = 0 and lim_{t->-infinity} ||f(t,p)|| = infinity.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (i), continuity of s -> ||f(s,0)|| and strict contraction give a contradiction if f(t,0) != 0 for some t>0: some earlier point f(s,0), 0<s<t, would be nonzero and would have to contract to f(t,0), while also lying closer to 0 by continuity. The group law then gives f(-t,0)=0 as well. For (ii), if f(t,p)=0 for some p != 0, applying f(-t,·) and using (i) gives p=0, impossible; hence all orbit points are nonzero, and the strict contraction axiom applied to time differences gives strict monotonicity. For (iii), monotonicity gives limits r1 in [0,infinity) as t->infinity and r2 in (0,infinity] as t->-infinity. If r1>0, compactness of the sphere/closed ball gives a subsequential limit q with ||q||=r1, and then continuity plus contraction gives r1=||q||>||f(1,q)||=lim ||f(1+ti,p)||=r1, contradiction. The backward limit is handled similarly, giving r2=infinity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Q be a bounded smooth embedded submanifold of R^N of dimension d, and let cl(Q) denote its closure in R^N. Let f: R × R^N -> R^N be a contractive flow. Assume the inward-flow condition f(t,cl(Q)) ⊂ Q for every t>0. Then cl(Q) is homeomorphic to a closed d-dimensional ball, and cl(Q) minus Q is homeomorphic to a (d-1)-sphere.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because Q is bounded, cl(Q) is compact. Taking any point of cl(Q) and flowing forward, Lemma 2.2 forces orbit norms to tend to 0; hence 0 lies in cl(Q). Since f(t,cl(Q)) ⊂ Q and f(t,0)=0, one obtains 0 in Q. Choose r>0 small enough that B := closed ball B_r^N intersect Q is a closed d-ball; its boundary ∂B is sphere ∂B_r^N intersect Q. For any nonzero p, the orbit t -> f(t,p) intersects the norm sphere ∂B_r^N in a unique time t_r(p), because the norm along the orbit is strictly decreasing from infinity to 0; a direct open-set argument proves t_r is continuous. For p in cl(Q) minus {0}, define T(p)={t in R : f(t,p) in cl(Q)}. Boundedness and Lemma 2.2 make T(p) bounded below; the inward-flow condition makes T(p) an interval closed to the right; closedness of cl(Q) gives T(p) closed, hence T(p)=[t_∂(p),infinity). The paper proves t_∂ is continuous by considering the union R_Q of all flow lines through Q, showing Q is relatively open in R_Q, and using the same open-set argument as for t_r. Define alpha: cl(Q)->B by alpha(p)=f(t_r(p)-t_∂(p),p) for p != 0 and alpha(0)=0, and beta: B->cl(Q) by beta(p)=f(t_∂(p)-t_r(p),p) for p != 0 and beta(0)=0. The definitions use the orbit time at which the point enters cl(Q) and the time at which the same orbit meets the small sphere. The group law gives alpha(beta(p))=p and beta(alpha(p))=p. Continuity away from 0 follows from continuity of t_r and t_∂; continuity at 0 follows from contraction. Thus alpha is a homeomorphism from cl(Q) to the small closed ball B. Finally, if p lies in cl(Q) minus Q then t_∂(p)=0, so alpha(p) lies in ∂B; conversely beta maps ∂B into cl(Q) minus Q by maximality of t_∂. Thus the boundary is a sphere.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix integers 0 <= k <= n. Let S in End(R^n) be the signed left cyclic shift S(v1,...,vn)=(v2,...,vn,(-1)^{k-1}v1), and let tau=S+S^T, where S^T is the transpose with respect to the standard inner product. Let lambda_1 >= ... >= lambda_n be the eigenvalues of tau, with orthogonal eigenvectors u_1,...,u_n. If k is even, the eigenvalues begin lambda_1=lambda_2=2 cos(pi/n), lambda_3=lambda_4=2 cos(3pi/n), and so on. If k is odd, they begin lambda_1=2, lambda_2=lambda_3=2 cos(2pi/n), lambda_4=lambda_5=2 cos(4pi/n), and so on. In either case lambda_k = 2 cos((k-1)pi/n) > 2 cos((k+1)pi/n) = lambda_{k+1}. Let X_0 in Gr(k,n) be the span of u_1,...,u_k. Then X_0 is totally positive, with Pluecker coordinates Delta_I(X_0)= product over i<j in I of sin((j-i)pi/n), which are all positive for I a k-element subset of {1,...,n}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof works over C. If zeta is an nth root of (-1)^{k-1}, write z_m=(1,zeta,zeta^2,...,zeta^{n-1}) with zeta=e^{pi i m/n}, where m has the parity of k-1. Then S z_m = zeta z_m and S^T z_m = zeta^{-1} z_m, so tau z_m = (zeta+zeta^{-1}) z_m = 2 cos(pi m/n) z_m. The resulting z_m form a Vandermonde basis, giving the full eigenvalue list and the gap lambda_k>lambda_{k+1}. The top k eigenspace is the span of z_{-k+1}, z_{-k+3}, ..., z_{k-1}. Taking these vectors as rows, each k by k minor is a Vandermonde determinant after column rescaling. The determinant simplifies to the stated product of sines, which is positive for 1 <= i<j <= n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Using the eigenbasis u_1,...,u_n of tau from Lemma 3.1, define phi: Mat(k,n-k) -> Gr(k,n) by phi(A)=span(u_i + sum_{j=1}^{n-k} A_{i,j} u_{k+j} : 1 <= i <= k). This is the usual coordinate chart on the big Schubert cell relative to the decomposition R^n=span(u_1,...,u_k) direct sum span(u_{k+1},...,u_n). The proposition states that the image of phi contains the entire totally nonnegative Grassmannian Gr_{>=0}(k,n). Equivalently, every X in Gr_{>=0}(k,n) intersects span(u_{k+1},...,u_n) trivially.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume for contradiction that X in Gr_{>=0}(k,n) contains a nonzero vector v in span(u_{k+1},...,u_n). Extend v to a basis of X and put this basis as the rows of a k by n matrix M. Let M_0 be the k by n matrix with rows u_1,...,u_k. Since v is orthogonal to u_1,...,u_k, the first column of M_0 M^T is zero, so det(M_0 M^T)=0. By Cauchy-Binet, det(M_0 M^T)=sum_I det((M_0)_I) det(M_I), where I runs over k-subsets of {1,...,n}. Lemma 3.1 says all maximal minors of M_0 have the same nonzero sign. Total nonnegativity of X says all nonzero maximal minors of M have the same sign, and at least one is nonzero because M has rank k. Therefore every summand has the same sign and at least one summand is nonzero, contradicting det(M_0 M^T)=0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.5 and Corollary 3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S be the signed cyclic shift on R^n associated to Gr(k,n), and let tau=S+S^T. For every X in Gr_{>=0}(k,n) and every t>0, Lemma 3.5 states that exp(tS)·X lies in Gr_{>0}(k,n). By symmetry the same holds for exp(tS^T). Since S and S^T commute, Corollary 3.8 states that exp(t tau)·X lies in Gr_{>0}(k,n) for every X in Gr_{>=0}(k,n) and t>0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First prove nonnegativity for exp(tS). For |t|<1, apply 1+tS to a matrix representing X. If I={i_1,...,i_k}, multilinearity gives Delta_I((1+tS)·X)=sum_{epsilon in {0,1}^k} t^{epsilon_1+...+epsilon_k} Delta_{ {i_1+epsilon_1,...,i_k+epsilon_k} }(X), with indices read cyclically and the sign convention in S making the coefficients nonnegative for t>=0. Hence 1+tS preserves Gr_{>=0}. Since exp(tS)=lim_{j->infinity} (1+(t/j)S)^j and Gr_{>=0} is closed, exp(tS) preserves Gr_{>=0} for t>=0. To upgrade to strict positivity for t>0, the proof uses the derivative Delta_I(exp(tS)·X)=Delta_I(X)+t sum_{I'} Delta_{I'}(X)+O(t^2), where I' ranges over k-subsets obtained from I by increasing one element by 1 modulo n. The directed graph on k-subsets with these moves is strongly connected. If a nonnegative point is not totally positive, choose a zero Pluecker coordinate and a positive one, then a directed path from the zero to the positive gives an edge I -> I' with Delta_I=0 and Delta_{I'}>0. For small negative time, Delta_I(exp(tS)·X)<0. If exp(TS)·X for some T>0 were still on the boundary rather than positive, applying this small negative-time argument to that boundary point would contradict preservation of nonnegativity on the interval back to time 0. This proves Lemma 3.5. The corollary follows because the same argument applies to S^T and exp(t tau)=exp(tS) exp(tS^T).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For all k,n, the totally nonnegative Grassmannian Gr_{>=0}(k,n) is homeomorphic to a closed ball of dimension k(n-k).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the coordinate chart phi: Mat(k,n-k)->Gr(k,n) from Proposition 3.4, so Gr_{>=0}(k,n) embeds into the Euclidean vector space Mat(k,n-k). In these coordinates, the flow exp(t tau) acts diagonally: if A is a k by (n-k) matrix, then f(t,A)_{i,j}=exp(t(lambda_{k+j}-lambda_i)) A_{i,j}. With the Euclidean norm on matrices this is a contractive flow, because Lemma 3.1 gives lambda_i >= lambda_k > lambda_{k+1} >= lambda_{k+j}. Let Q=phi^{-1}(Gr_{>0}(k,n)). The closure of Q is phi^{-1}(Gr_{>=0}(k,n)); density of Gr_{>0} in Gr_{>=0} follows either from Postnikov's result or directly from Corollary 3.8 by writing X=lim_{t->0+} exp(t tau)·X. The closure is compact, hence bounded, and Corollary 3.8 gives the inward-flow condition f(t,cl(Q))⊂Q for t>0. Lemma 2.3 applies and gives that cl(Q), hence Gr_{>=0}(k,n), is a closed k(n-k)-ball.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 4.3 and 4.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let U be the group of upper triangular unipotent n by n real matrices, U_{>=0} its totally nonnegative part, and V={x in U : x_{1,2}+x_{2,3}+...+x_{n-1,n}=n-1}. Let e be the upper triangular principal nilpotent matrix with 1's on the superdiagonal, and let rho(t)=diag(t^{n-1},t^{n-2},...,1). For t>0 define a(t)·x = rho(1/t) exp((t-1)e) x rho(t). Lemma 4.3 states that a(t) defines an action of the multiplicative group R_{>0} on V. Fix c>1 and define coordinates b_{i,j}(x)=c^{i-j}((j-i)! x_{i,j}-1) for 1<=i<j<=n, with norm ||x||_infinity=max |b_{i,j}(x)|. Let V_{>0}=V∩U_{>0}, where U_{>0} is the totally positive part of U in the sense of the paper. Lemma 4.6 states: (i) if x in V_{>=0} and t>1, then a(t)·x in V_{>0}; (ii) if x != exp(e), then t -> ||a(t)·x||_infinity is strictly decreasing on (0,infinity).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 4.3, the paper verifies the action property directly using the entry formula (rho(1/t) exp((s-1)e) x rho(t))_{i,j} = t^{-(j-i)} sum_{l=i}^j ((s-1)^{l-i}/(l-i)!) x_{l,j}. This formula also shows that V is preserved and that a(s)a(t)=a(st). For Lemma 4.6(i), exp((t-1)e) is totally positive for t>1, and Cauchy-Binet applied to exp((t-1)e)x with x totally nonnegative shows the required minors become positive. For Lemma 4.6(ii), the action property reduces strict monotonicity to checking decrease at t=1. Expanding the b-coordinates gives b_{i,j}(a(t)·x)=b_{i,j}(x)+(t-1)(j-i)(b_{i+1,j}(x)/c - b_{i,j}(x))+O((t-1)^2), with b_{i+1,j}=0 when i+1=j. If |b_{i,j}(x)| achieves the maximum norm, then |b_{i+1,j}(x)/c|<|b_{i,j}(x)| because c>1, so this coordinate moves strictly toward zero at first order. Hence the maximum norm decreases at t=1, and by the multiplicative action property it decreases strictly for all forward times unless x=exp(e).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let U_{>=0} be the totally nonnegative part of the upper triangular unipotent group U in GL_n(R), and let V_{>=0}=U_{>=0}∩V where V is the affine slice x_{1,2}+...+x_{n-1,n}=n-1. Then V_{>=0} is homeomorphic to a closed ball of dimension binom(n,2)-1. Moreover U_{>=0} is homeomorphic to a closed half-space in R^{binom(n,2)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the b-coordinates from Lemma 4.6 to identify V with the vector space W of arrays (w_{i,j}) satisfying w_{1,2}+...+w_{n-1,n}=0, with the L_infinity norm. Define f(s,b(x))=b(a(exp(s))·x). Lemma 4.3 gives the flow property, and Lemma 4.6(ii) gives contraction. Let Q=b(V_{>0}). Lemma 4.6(i) gives f(s,cl(Q))⊂Q for s>0, with cl(Q)=b(V_{>=0}). Boundedness of V_{>=0} is shown by induction on j-i, giving 0 <= x_{i,j} <= (n-1)^{j-i} for x in V_{>=0}. Lemma 2.3 then gives that V_{>=0} is a closed ball. Finally U_{>=0} has a dilation action of R_{>0} multiplying entries on the (j-i)-th superdiagonal by t^{j-i}; this identifies U_{>=0} with the open cone on the compact link V_{>=0}, with the identity matrix as cone point. The cone on a closed ball is homeomorphic to a closed half-space.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let k,m,n be nonnegative integers with k+m <= n and m even. Let Z_0 be the (k+m) by n matrix whose rows are the first k+m orthonormal eigenvectors u_1,...,u_{k+m} of the cyclic operator tau from Section 3, chosen so that its maximal minors are positive. The cyclically symmetric amplituhedron A_{n,k,m}(Z_0) is the image of Gr_{>=0}(k,n) under the map induced by the linear map Z_0: R^n -> R^{k+m}. The theorem states that A_{n,k,m}(Z_0) is homeomorphic to a closed ball of dimension km.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the global coordinates phi: Mat(k,n-k)->Gr(k,n) from Section 3. Write A in Mat(k,n-k) as [A' | A''], where A' has k by m entries, and let pi(A)=A'. For X=phi(A) in Gr_{>=0}(k,n), applying Z_0 sends X to the row span of [Id_k | A'] in Gr(k,k+m). Hence the amplituhedron embeds into Mat(k,m) by a map gamma making the diagram gamma∘(Z_0)_{Gr}∘phi = pi commute on the nonnegative Grassmannian. Let Q=pi(phi^{-1}(Gr_{>0}(k,n))). Because pi is an open projection, Q is open in Mat(k,m), and its closure is pi(phi^{-1}(Gr_{>=0}(k,n))), which is compact and homeomorphic to A_{n,k,m}(Z_0). The diagonal contractive flow f on Mat(k,n-k) from Theorem 1.1 descends to f_0(t,A')_{i,j}=exp(t(lambda_{k+j}-lambda_i))A'_{i,j} on Mat(k,m), and f_0 is contractive for the same eigenvalue-gap reason. Since f(t,phi^{-1}(Gr_{>=0}))⊂phi^{-1}(Gr_{>0}) for t>0, applying pi gives f_0(t,cl(Q))⊂Q. Lemma 2.3 gives that cl(Q), and therefore the amplituhedron, is a closed km-ball.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Recalled embedding (6.1), from Lam18 Theorem 5.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let NC_n be the set of noncrossing partitions of the odd vertices [2n]_odd={1,3,...,2n-1}. For sigma in NC_n, let tilde sigma be its dual noncrossing partition of the even vertices [2n]_even={2,4,...,2n}, defined as the coarsest noncrossing partition of the even vertices such that sigma union tilde sigma is noncrossing on [2n]. A subset I of [2n] of size n-1 is concordant with sigma if every part of sigma and every part of tilde sigma contains exactly one element not in I. Let A_sigma be the sum of basis vectors e_I over all I concordant with sigma in R^{binom(2n,n-1)}, and let H be the projective linear subspace spanned by the images of all A_sigma. Let X_n=Gr(n-1,2n)∩H under the Pluecker embedding. The paper recalls from Lam18 that Lam's compactification E_n of the space of planar electrical networks with n boundary vertices embeds as E_n ≅ X_n ∩ Gr_{>=0}(n-1,2n) inside Gr_{>=0}(n-1,2n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The present paper does not reprove this embedding; it cites Lam18, Theorem 5.8. The recalled construction is used as the starting point for applying the ambient totally nonnegative Grassmannian flow to E_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (E_n)_{>0}=X_n∩Gr_{>0}(n-1,2n), where X_n is the electrical-network linear slice described in the recalled embedding. Then (E_n)_{>0} is diffeomorphic to R_{>0}^{binom(n,2)}, and the inclusion (E_n)_{>0} -> Gr_{>0}(n-1,2n) is a smooth embedding.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses known electrical-network parametrizations. By Curtis-Ingerman-Morrow, every point of the positive electrical-network space Omega_n^+=(E_n)_{>0} is uniquely represented by assigning a positive conductance to each edge of a well-connected circular planar electrical network Gamma with binom(n,2) edges. Lam's construction sends Gamma to a weighted bipartite graph N(Gamma) embedded in a disk, compatibly with the embedding into Gr_{>=0}(n-1,2n). The edge weights of N(Gamma) are monomials in the conductances of Gamma. The underlying bipartite graph parametrizes the full positive Grassmannian Gr_{>0}(n-1,2n): after choosing (n-1)(n+1) edges, arbitrary positive weights on those edges and weight 1 on the others give coordinates R_{>0}^{(n-1)(n+1)}. Therefore the inclusion R_{>0}^{binom(n,2)} ≅ (E_n)_{>0} -> Gr_{>0}(n-1,2n) ≅ R_{>0}^{(n-1)(n+1)} is a monomial map, in fact a homomorphism of positive real Lie groups. Uniqueness of the conductance representation gives injectivity, and the monomial parametrization gives the smooth embedded-submanifold structure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each i in [2n], define linear operators u_i and d_i on R^{binom(2n,n-1)} with basis e_I, |I|=n-1, by u_i(e_I)=e_{I∪{i+1} minus {i}} if i in I and i+1 not in I, and 0 otherwise; and d_i(e_I)=e_{I∪{i-1} minus {i}} if i in I and i-1 not in I, and 0 otherwise, with indices modulo 2n. For i odd, let kappa(i) be the noncrossing partition of [2n]_odd with two parts {i} and [2n]_odd minus {i}; for i even, let mu(i) be the noncrossing partition with one two-element part {i-1,i+1} and all other odd vertices singleton. Given sigma in NC_n, define sigma'(i) to be the common refinement of sigma and kappa(i) if i is odd, and the common coarsening of sigma and mu(i) if i is even. Then for every i and every sigma in NC_n, (u_i+d_i)(A_sigma)=0 if sigma=sigma'(i), and (u_i+d_i)(A_sigma)=A_{sigma'(i)} otherwise.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper says this combinatorial lemma is essentially Lam18, Proposition 5.15, and can be verified directly. The direct verification tracks how replacing i by i+1 or i-1 in a concordant subset I changes the condition that each part of sigma and its dual tilde sigma has exactly one omitted element. If the refinement/coarsening operation leaves sigma unchanged, the allowed shifts cancel or do not occur, giving 0. Otherwise, the shifted concordant subsets are exactly the subsets concordant with sigma'(i), giving A_{sigma'(i)}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let tau be the cyclic-shift operator from Section 3 acting on Gr(n-1,2n), and let E_n be embedded into Gr_{>=0}(n-1,2n) as X_n∩Gr_{>=0}(n-1,2n). For every X in E_n and every t>0, exp(t tau)·X lies in (E_n)_{>0}=X_n∩Gr_{>0}(n-1,2n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Corollary 3.8 already implies exp(t tau)·X lies in Gr_{>0}(n-1,2n) for t>0, since X lies in Gr_{>=0}. It remains to prove that the flow preserves the slice H defining X_n. Identify Gr(n-1,2n) with its Pluecker image in projective space.
