The infinitesimal action of tau on Pluecker coordinates is the operator Phi=sum_{i=1}^{2n}(u_i+d_i) from Lemma 6.3, so exp(t tau)·X = X + t Phi(X) + O(t^2) in projective coordinates. Lemma 6.3 shows that Phi maps each spanning vector A_sigma of H to a linear combination of spanning vectors A_{sigma'} (indeed to sums of such basis vectors or zero), so the corresponding vector field is tangent to H. Therefore an integral curve starting in X_n stays in H for all real t. Combining preservation of H with ambient strict positivity proves the lemma.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E_n be Lam's compactification of the space of planar electrical networks with n boundary vertices. Then E_n is homeomorphic to a closed ball of dimension binom(n,2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Embed E_n into Gr_{>=0}(n-1,2n) using the recalled identification E_n=X_n∩Gr_{>=0}(n-1,2n). Proposition 6.2 identifies (E_n)_{>0}=X_n∩Gr_{>0}(n-1,2n) as a smooth embedded submanifold of Gr_{>0}(n-1,2n) of dimension binom(n,2). Next embed Gr_{>0}(n-1,2n) into the matrix space Mat(n-1,n+1) using the phi^{-1} coordinates from Proposition 3.4. Set Q=phi^{-1}((E_n)_{>0}); then Q is a smooth embedded submanifold of Mat(n-1,n+1) of dimension binom(n,2). Since (E_n)_{>0} is dense in E_n, the closure cl(Q) is phi^{-1}(E_n), which is compact and hence bounded. The diagonal flow f(t,A)_{i,j}=exp(t(lambda_{n-1+j}-lambda_i))A_{i,j} is contractive by the eigenvalue gap from Lemma 3.1. Lemma 6.5 gives f(t,cl(Q))⊂Q for every t>0. Applying Lemma 2.3 yields that cl(Q), and hence E_n, is a closed binom(n,2)-ball.

proof_techniques.name: Contractive-flow ball criterion
proof_techniques.description: The paper's main topological device is Lemma 2.3. Instead of analyzing a CW decomposition cell by cell, one embeds the desired space as the compact closure cl(Q) of a smooth open submanifold Q in a normed vector space and constructs a flow that strictly decreases a norm along every nonzero orbit. The crucial extra condition is not merely forward invariance of Q, but the stronger inward condition f(t,cl(Q))⊂Q for all t>0: every boundary point is immediately pushed into the smooth interior. The proof then parametrizes cl(Q) by flow lines between the first time an orbit enters cl(Q) and its unique intersection with a small sphere around the attracting point.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, one possible strategy is to realize the cubical matching complex as cl(Q) for some geometric Q and to construct a crossing-smoothing or weight-scaling flow that decreases a norm and sends all lower-crossing/boundary configurations into the top stratum. If such a realization exists, Lemma 2.3 would give contractibility and in fact a closed-ball structure.

proof_techniques.name: Instant positivity from a strongly connected coordinate graph
proof_techniques.description: In the Grassmannian proof, the cyclic shift generator S has a nonnegative transition formula on Pluecker coordinates. Preservation of the nonnegative cone follows from an explicit formula for Delta_I((1+tS)X) as a sum of nonnegative multiples of nearby Pluecker coordinates. Strict positivity for positive time is proved using a directed graph on k-subsets: an edge increases one element cyclically, and this graph is strongly connected. If a boundary point has some zero coordinate and some positive coordinate, a path from zero to positive contains an edge where the derivative of the zero coordinate is positive, so a small negative time would leave the nonnegative cone; this contradiction forces positive-time flow to lie in the interior.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A matching complex may admit coordinates indexed by matchings or partial resolutions, with local resolution moves defining a directed graph. If the relevant move graph is strongly connected and the infinitesimal flow has nonnegative transition coefficients, the same argument could show that positive time moves every boundary stratum into an interior stratum.

proof_techniques.name: Global Euclidean coordinates from a positive attracting point
proof_techniques.description: The paper constructs a distinguished totally positive point X_0 in Gr(k,n) from the top eigenspace of the cyclic operator tau=S+S^T. Positivity of X_0 is proved by a Vandermonde/sine-product formula. The coordinates centered at X_0 are the big-cell coordinates with respect to the eigenbasis of tau. Proposition 3.4 then shows that all of Gr_{>=0}(k,n) lies in this one coordinate chart, using Cauchy-Binet and the fact that both X_0 and a totally nonnegative point have maximal minors of one sign. In these coordinates, the tau-flow is diagonal and contractive because of a spectral gap.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If F_w can be embedded into a positive Grassmannian or another positive variety, finding a canonical positive point and a global chart around it could turn the topological question into a bounded subset of Euclidean space with a diagonal contraction.

proof_techniques.name: Preserving a combinatorial linear slice by checking the infinitesimal generator
proof_techniques.description: For electrical networks, E_n is realized as the intersection of Gr_{>=0}(n-1,2n) with a projective linear subspace H spanned by vectors A_sigma indexed by noncrossing partitions. The ambient cyclic flow is already known to improve nonnegativity to positivity. To show it restricts to E_n, the paper computes the infinitesimal generator on Pluecker coordinates as Phi=sum_i(u_i+d_i), then proves combinatorially that each u_i+d_i sends a spanning vector A_sigma either to 0 or to another spanning vector A_{sigma'(i)}. Thus the vector field is tangent to H, and the ambient flow preserves the slice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If F_w corresponds to a subspace, quotient, or face cut out by word/matching constraints, it may be enough to verify that a natural local-move generator preserves the span of the allowed combinatorial basis elements. This can avoid having to build a separate flow from scratch.

proof_techniques.name: Electrical networks as positive Grassmannian slices
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses Lam's compactification E_n and its embedding E_n ≅ X_n∩Gr_{>=0}(n-1,2n), where X_n is a projective linear slice described using noncrossing partitions and their Kreweras complements. The positive part (E_n)_{>0} is parametrized by positive conductances on a well-connected circular planar network with binom(n,2) edges, and the inclusion into the positive Grassmannian is monomial after passing through a bipartite-network model.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is formulated in terms of matchings and crossing resolutions, which are close to the combinatorics appearing in planar electrical networks and uncrossing posets. If F_w can be identified with a cell closure, link, or subcomplex in Lam's electrical-network compactification, Theorem 1.3 and the slice-flow proof become directly relevant.

proof_techniques.name: Projection inherits a contractive flow
proof_techniques.description: In the cyclically symmetric amplituhedron proof, the target space is not a subspace of the Grassmannian but an image of Gr_{>=0}(k,n). The authors choose coordinates in which the amplituhedron map is simply projection from Mat(k,n-k) to Mat(k,m). The ambient diagonal contractive flow commutes with this projection, and the projected positive image is open with compact closure. This lets Lemma 2.3 apply to the image even though it is not itself a cell closure in the original Grassmannian.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If F_w is more naturally a quotient or image of a larger matching/network/positive space, it may still be possible to prove contractibility by finding a contractive flow upstairs that descends through the quotient or projection.

proof_techniques.name: Cone-over-link reduction
proof_techniques.description: For the totally nonnegative unipotent group U_{>=0}, the paper first proves that a compact affine slice V_{>=0} is a ball. A dilation action multiplying the (j-i)-th superdiagonal by t^{j-i} identifies U_{>=0} with the open cone over V_{>=0}, with the identity matrix as cone point. This converts the ball result for the link into a closed-half-space result for the noncompact cone.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If F_w arises as a link or cross-section of a conical space of weighted diagrams, proving a ball result for the link or using a cone decomposition could simplify the contractibility question.

other_useful_info: The paper emphasizes that Theorem 1.3 proves only that the closure of the unique top-dimensional cell in Lam's electrical-network compactification E_n is a ball. It recalls that E_n has a natural cell decomposition extending Curtis-Ingerman-Morrow and Colin de Verdiere-Gitler-Vertigan, with face poset studied by Kenyon; Lam proved this face poset is Eulerian in Lam15, and Hersh-Kenyon proved it shellable, so by Bjorner it is the face poset of some regular CW ball. The authors expect E_n itself to be such a regular CW complex, so that every cell closure in E_n is a closed ball, but they state that this remains open in the paper. The reference Lam15 is titled The uncrossing partial order on matchings is Eulerian, suggesting a direct combinatorial bridge between electrical networks, uncrossing, and matching posets. For the totally nonnegative Grassmannian, the paper states that Postnikov's full regular-CW conjecture for all cell closures was open at the time but notes that it was later verified by the authors in GKL19a using different methods.
why_relevant: Proves that Lam's compactification of the space of electrical networks is homeomorphic to a closed ball. This is highly relevant if F_w can be embedded into or compared with the electrical-network compactification or its face stratification.

title: k-Foldability of Words
overall_summary: The paper studies finite words over an alphabet with complementary pairs A_i and bar A_i, where a folding is a noncrossing perfect matching pairing complementary letters, equivalently a valid labeling of the half-edges of a rooted plane tree. It recalls a greedy folding theorem: the graph of valid plane trees under elementary local moves has a unique source, the greedy folding, and every valid tree is reachable from it. The main new structural tool is a bijection between foldings of alternating-bar words and edge-colored rooted plane trees; under this bijection local moves are exactly repeated colors on incident edges, giving characterizations of 1-foldable and 2-foldable words. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the identification of reducible free-group words with noncrossing foldings, the connectivity and canonical orientation of the local-move graph on 0-cells, and the edge-colored-tree model for understanding local changes among noncrossing matchings.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Basic equivalence used throughout Section 2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix an alphabet {A_1, bar A_1, ..., A_m, bar A_m} with A_i complementary to bar A_i. For a word w=w[1]...w[2n], a folding of w is a noncrossing perfect matching of the positions {1,...,2n} such that matched positions carry complementary letters. A rooted plane tree T with n edges is w-valid if, when the 2n half-edges of T are read counterclockwise starting at the root and labeled by w[1],...,w[2n], the two half-edges of every edge receive complementary letters. The paper uses the standard bijection between foldings of w and w-valid rooted plane trees. It also states that, viewing the alphabet as generators and inverses of the free group on m generators, the foldable words are precisely the words that reduce to the identity element.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A rooted plane tree gives a noncrossing matching by pairing the two half-edges of each edge; noncrossing follows from the planar embedding and perimeter order. Conversely, a noncrossing perfect matching on ordered boundary points has a nesting/dual rooted plane tree whose edges correspond to matched pairs. For the free-group equivalence, if a word admits a noncrossing complementary matching, an innermost matched pair is adjacent and can be freely cancelled; induction cancels the whole word. Conversely, if a word freely reduces to the identity, a nonempty freely trivial word has some adjacent inverse pair in its current reduced sequence; cancel such a pair and induct, drawing each cancellation as a noncrossing arc in the original word.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.3, quoted from Black-Drellich-Tymoczko
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let w be a foldable word, and let V(w) be the set of w-valid rooted plane trees. Define a directed graph G_w whose vertices are the elements of V(w), and in which there is a directed edge T_1 to T_2 when T_2 is obtained from T_1 by a Type 1 local move, i.e. by re-pairing a pair of edges in the local configuration shown in the paper's Figure 2; the reverse re-pairing is called a Type 2 move. Let T_0 be the greedy folding obtained by scanning w from left to right and matching w[i] to the largest earlier unmatched index j<i such that w[j] is complementary to w[i], leaving w[i] temporarily unmatched if no such j exists. If w is foldable, then T_0 is the unique source of G_w, and for every T in V(w) there is a directed path in G_w from T_0 to T.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This theorem is cited in the paper rather than reproved. The constructions needed for its use are explicit: the greedy algorithm gives a distinguished valid tree, and Type 1 moves orient the local-move graph. The theorem supplies two structural facts: no valid tree other than the greedy one has indegree zero, and repeated Type 1 moves from the greedy tree reach every valid tree. Thus the undirected local-move graph on foldings is connected, with a canonical directed spanning reachability structure rooted at the greedy folding.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2, parity doubling of the alphabet
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For positive integers n and m, let S(n,m) be the set of length-2n words over m complementary pairs, and let P(n,m) be the subset of foldable words. Let hat S(n,q) be the set of length-2n words over q complementary pairs in which every odd-indexed position is unbarred and every even-indexed position is barred, and set hat P(n,q)=hat S(n,q)∩P(n,q). There is a bijection S(n,m) to hat S(n,2m), and it restricts to a bijection P(n,m) to hat P(n,2m). Explicitly, for an original letter A_i or bar A_i, keep odd A_i as A_i and even bar A_i as bar A_i; replace odd bar A_i by A_{m+i}; and replace even A_i by bar A_{m+i}. Thus every image word alternates unbarred, barred, unbarred, barred, ... .
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In any noncrossing folding, if positions i<j are matched, then the subword between them must itself have a perfect matching and hence has even length; therefore i and j have opposite parity. Thus the only possible complementary pairings in a folding of the original word are odd A_i with even bar A_i, or odd bar A_i with even A_i. The doubling construction assigns these two parity-orientations to two distinct complementary pairs, indexed i and m+i, while preserving the order of the word. Collapsing indices i and m+i back to i, with the parity rule reversed, gives the inverse map. Since all possible matched pairs have opposite parity, the construction preserves and reflects valid foldings.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3.2, Theorem 3.3 and Corollaries 3.4–3.5, closed walks on regular trees
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let m≥2 and let T_m be the infinite unrooted m-regular tree with the m incident edge labels at every vertex identified with A_1,...,A_m. Fix a vertex v. Walks of length 2n starting at v are in bijection with words in hat S(n,m): record the edge label on each step, unbarred on odd steps and barred on even steps. Such a walk is closed if and only if the corresponding word is foldable. If a_n is the number of length-2n closed walks in T_m starting at v, then the generating function is f_m(x)=∑_{n≥0} a_n x^n = 2(m−1)/(m−2+m√(1−4(m−1)x)) = (2−m(1−√(1−4(m−1)x)))/(2(1−m²x)). Equivalently, for n≥1, a_n=m^{2n}−∑_{i=1}^n m^{1+2(n−i)}(m−1)^i binom(2i,i)/(4i−2). For fixed m≥3, a_n=(4m−4)^n n^{−3/2}(m(m−1)/(√π(m−2)^2)+O(n^{−1/2})). Consequently, for fixed original alphabet size m≥2, the number of foldable words of length 2n over m complementary pairs is Θ(n^{−3/2}(8m−4)^n), using the doubling bijection and applying the closed-walk count with 2m labels.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The bijection between walks and words follows because from any vertex of T_m there is a unique incident edge of each label. The walk is closed exactly when the label sequence can be recursively paired as returns along the same tree edges; because the word alternates unbarred and barred by parity, such returns give complementary noncrossing pairs. Conversely, a noncrossing folding recursively decomposes the walk into excursions that leave a vertex along an edge and later return along the same edge, so the total walk ends at v. The generating function is quoted from Quenell. The coefficient formula is obtained by expanding √(1−4(m−1)x) via Newton's generalized binomial theorem and substituting into f_m(x). The asymptotic is obtained from the singular behavior of the generating function, or equivalently by the saddle point method as noted in the paper.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.6, bijection between foldings and edge-colored plane trees
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For integers n≥0 and m≥1, let T(n,m) be the set of pairs (w,T) where w is in hat P(n,m), i.e. w is a foldable length-2n word with unbarred letters in odd positions and barred letters in even positions, and T is a w-valid rooted plane tree. Let C(n,m) be the set of pairs (c,T) where T is a rooted plane tree with n edges and c:E(T)→{1,...,m} is an arbitrary edge-coloring, not necessarily proper. Then there is a bijection T(n,m) to C(n,m).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Given (w,T), color an edge e of T by i if its two half-edges are labeled A_i and bar A_i. This gives an element (c,T) of C(n,m). Conversely, given an edge-colored rooted plane tree (c,T), traverse the half-edges counterclockwise from the root. For any edge e, its two half-edges occur with opposite parity in this traversal, because between the first and second occurrence lie all half-edges of the subtree below e, an even number. If c(e)=i, label the odd half-edge by A_i and the even half-edge by bar A_i. Reading these labels around the tree gives a word w in hat P(n,m), and T is w-valid by construction. The two constructions plainly invert each other.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.7, classification of 1-foldable words
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S_1(n,m) be the set of length-2n words over m complementary pairs that have exactly one valid rooted plane tree, equivalently exactly one noncrossing complementary perfect matching. Then the words in S_1(n,m) are in bijection with proper edge-colorings of rooted plane trees with n edges using 2m colors, where proper means that incident edges receive distinct colors.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First apply the doubling bijection S(n,m) to hat S(n,2m), so it suffices to work with alternating-bar words over 2m complementary pairs. Under Theorem 3.6, a folding corresponds to an edge-colored rooted plane tree. In this model, an elementary local move exists exactly when two incident edges have the same color: the equality of colors is precisely the condition that the re-paired half-edges still carry complementary labels. If the coloring is proper, there is no local move from the corresponding valid tree. Since Theorem 2.3 says the local-move graph of all valid trees for a fixed foldable word is connected, absence of local moves implies that the word has only this one valid tree. Conversely, if two incident edges have the same color, performing the local move gives a distinct valid tree, so the word is not 1-foldable. Because a 1-foldable word determines its unique tree, the bijection is with proper 2m-edge-colored plane trees.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 3.8–3.10 and Theorem 3.11, enumeration of 1-foldable words
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let a rooted plane tree T have degree multiset {1^{α_1},2^{α_2},...}, meaning α_i vertices have degree i. Lemma 3.8 says that the number of proper k-edge-colorings of this fixed tree is k ∏_{i≥1}(binom(k−1,i−1)(i−1)!)^{α_i}, with 0^0 interpreted as 1. Lemma 3.9, due to Mallows and Wacher, says that the number of rooted plane trees with degree multiset {1^{α_1},2^{α_2},...} is (2/α_1) multinomial(1+α_2+2α_3+3α_4+...; α_1−1, α_2, α_3, ...), and this number is nonzero exactly when α_1=2+∑_{i≥2}(i−2)α_i. Lemma 3.10 says that such a tree has n edges and is properly edge-colorable with 2m colors if and only if α_i=0 for i>2m and ∑_{i=1}^{2m} i α_i=2n. Combining these facts, Theorem 3.11 gives the number of 1-foldable words of length 2n over m complementary pairs as the sum, over all nonnegative sequences (α_1,...,α_{2m}) satisfying ∑_{i=1}^{2m} i α_i=2n and α_1=2+∑_{i=2}^{2m}(i−2)α_i, of (2/α_1) multinomial(n; α_1−1, α_2, ..., α_{2m}) · (2m) · ∏_{i=1}^{2m}(binom(2m−1,i−1)(i−1)!)^{α_i}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 3.8, choose a leaf edge and color it in k ways. Then visit vertices in breadth-first order away from that starting edge. At a vertex of degree i, one incident edge has already been colored, and the remaining i−1 incident edges must receive distinct colors chosen from the other k−1 colors and assigned to the ordered incident positions; this gives binom(k−1,i−1)(i−1)! choices. Multiplying over all vertices gives the formula. Lemma 3.9 is imported from Mallows and Wacher; the displayed condition on α_1 is the usual degree-sum/tree condition. Lemma 3.10 uses χ′(T)=Δ(T) for a tree, so a proper 2m-edge-coloring exists exactly when no degree exceeds 2m, and ∑ iα_i=2|E(T)|=2n is the condition of having n edges. Theorem 3.11 then counts 1-foldable words via Theorem 3.7: for each allowed degree multiset, multiply the number of rooted plane trees of that degree multiset by the number of proper 2m-edge-colorings, use 1+α_2+2α_3+...+(2m−1)α_{2m}=n to simplify the Mallows-Wacher multinomial numerator, and sum over all allowed degree multisets.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.15, characterization of 2-foldable words by A-decomposition
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Write A^{-1} for the complementary letter to A; A itself may be barred or unbarred. A word w has an A-decomposition if there are words u_1,u_2,u_3,v_1,v_2 and a letter A such that: (1) w=u_1 A v_1 A^{-1} u_2 A v_2 A^{-1} u_3; (2) the words u_1u_3, u_2, v_1, and v_2 are foldable; and (3) the words u_1u_2 A A^{-1} u_3 and A^{-1} v_1v_2 A are 1-foldable. The theorem states that a word w is 2-foldable, i.e. has exactly two valid rooted plane trees, if and only if it has an A-decomposition.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If w has an A-decomposition, the four distinguished letters A, A^{-1}, A, A^{-1} support the two possible noncrossing re-pairings shown in Figure 4 of the paper. Condition (2) supplies foldings of the four regions/subwords needed to build these two valid trees. Conditions (2) and (3) together imply that the substructures attached to the two local pictures are 1-foldable, so no local move is available except the move that re-pairs the two distinguished same-colored edges. Thus the two displayed foldings are connected by one local move and have no other outgoing local moves; since the valid-tree graph is connected by Theorem 2.3, these are the only two foldings. Conversely, if w is 2-foldable, the connected graph of valid trees has exactly two vertices, so they must be adjacent by a single local move. Reading the word around the local configuration of that move yields the decomposition w=u_1 A v_1 A^{-1} u_2 A v_2 A^{-1} u_3. The existence of the two foldings gives the foldability requirements in (2), and the absence of any further valid trees forces the auxiliary words in (3) to be 1-foldable.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.5, Catalan-product superset for R(n,1)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C_s=(1/(s+1))binom(2s,s) be the s-th Catalan number. Let R(n,1) be the set of integers k≥0 for which some length-2n word over one complementary pair has exactly k foldings. For every k in R(n,1), there is an integer t with 0≤t≤⌊n/2⌋ and an integer h with 0≤h≤C_t such that k=∑_{r=1}^h p_r, where each p_r has the form p_r=∏_{i=1}^{j_r} C_{s_{r,i}} for some 1≤j_r≤t+1 and positive or zero integers s_{r,i} satisfying ∑_{i=1}^{j_r} s_{r,i}=n−t. The case h=0 represents k=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the doubling bijection to work with foldable words in hat P(n,2), written with two complementary types A and B and alternating bars. Choose A to be a type occurring t times among the n unbarred positions, with t≤n/2 after swapping A and B if necessary. A noncrossing matching on the A-type letters is a partial matching, and there are at most C_t such A-matchings. Fix one such A-matching. The maximal B-subwords of the word are grouped into regions by the A-arcs. Each group has even total length 2s_i, because the subword enclosed between complementary A-type letters must have even length; after concatenation it is an alternating pure B-word of the form (B bar B)^{s_i} or its barred analogue. Such a group has C_{s_i} possible noncrossing foldings. Since different groups fold independently, the fixed A-matching extends to ∏ C_{s_i} full foldings, with at most t+1 groups and ∑ s_i=n−t. Summing this product over the actual A-matchings gives the total number k of foldings.

proof_techniques.name: Greedy folding as canonical source
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses a left-to-right greedy matching: when processing a letter, match it to the latest previous unmatched complementary letter if one exists. For foldable words this produces a valid noncrossing folding. The cited theorem of Black-Drellich-Tymoczko orients the local-move graph so that this greedy folding is the unique source and every other valid tree is reachable from it by directed Type 1 moves.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original complex, the 0-cells are noncrossing complementary matchings. If the 1-cells of F_w realize the same local re-pairings, this theorem gives a canonical base 0-cell and connectedness of the 0-skeleton, and may provide an orientation for a contraction or discrete Morse argument.

proof_techniques.name: Parity doubling of complementary letters
proof_techniques.description: In any noncrossing perfect matching of a linear word, matched positions have opposite parity because the interval between them contains an even number of letters. The paper exploits this by replacing each original complementary pair with two complementary pairs that encode the parity-orientation of a possible match, thereby converting arbitrary words to words whose odd positions are unbarred and even positions are barred without changing the set of foldings.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For arguments involving 0-cells of F_w, one can try to impose the same parity-split coloring on letters or arcs. This removes orientation ambiguities in pairings and makes the edge-colored tree model available even when the original word does not alternate bars.

proof_techniques.name: Foldings as edge-colored rooted plane trees
proof_techniques.description: For alternating-bar words, the paper constructs a bijection from a folding on a rooted plane tree to an edge-coloring of the same tree: each edge receives the color i of the complementary pair A_i, bar A_i labeling its two half-edges. Conversely, an edge-colored plane tree determines a unique alternating-bar word by putting the unbarred letter on the odd half-edge occurrence and the barred letter on the even occurrence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This can translate noncrossing matchings for a fixed reducible word into colored plane-tree data. In that language, local changes among 0-cells become tree rotations/re-pairings, which may be easier to compare with the cubical cells of F_w.

proof_techniques.name: Local moves detected by repeated incident colors
proof_techniques.description: Under the edge-colored tree bijection, a local move exists exactly at a pair of incident edges with the same color. Therefore a folding is isolated in the local-move graph exactly when the corresponding edge-coloring is proper. Combined with connectedness, this yields the classification of 1-foldable words.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a clean local criterion for when a noncrossing matching admits an adjacent noncrossing resolution through a one-crossing configuration. It may help identify links of 0-cells in F_w or handle base cases where the 0-skeleton has only one vertex.

proof_techniques.name: A-decomposition around one local move
proof_techniques.description: For words with exactly two foldings, the paper cuts the word into five subwords around the four distinguished letters involved in the unique local move: u_1 A v_1 A^{-1} u_2 A v_2 A^{-1} u_3. Foldability and 1-foldability conditions on the pieces ensure that the two re-pairings of the four distinguished letters are the only two global foldings.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a template for analyzing a one-dimensional part of F_w: a single crossing/resolution move naturally decomposes the word into regions. Similar decompositions could be useful for understanding boundaries of 1-cells or small subcomplexes.

proof_techniques.name: Partial matching decomposition and Catalan products
proof_techniques.description: In the rank-one counting section, after doubling to two letter types A and B, the paper fixes a noncrossing partial matching of the A-type letters. The A-arcs partition maximal B-subwords into independent groups, each a pure alternating B-word with a Catalan number of foldings; the total contribution of the fixed A-matching is a product of Catalan numbers, and the total number of foldings is a sum over A-matchings.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests an induction or stratification by first fixing all arcs of one chosen generator type, then analyzing independent regions for the remaining letters. Such a decomposition may help break F_w into simpler pieces or construct covers indexed by partial matchings.

proof_techniques.name: Closed-walk model on regular trees
proof_techniques.description: Alternating-bar foldable words over m complementary pairs are identified with closed walks of even length in the infinite m-regular edge-labeled tree. This gives an external tree-walk representation of free reduction and supplies enumerative information through known closed-walk generating functions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Since the original problem starts from a word representing the identity in a free group, the closed-walk viewpoint can be used to locate return/backtracking structure and may support inductive decompositions of the word before studying matchings.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly notes that S(n,m), the set of length-2n words over m complementary pairs, can be viewed as length-2n words in the free group on m generators, and that foldable words are precisely those reducing to the identity. Local moves on valid plane trees are attributed to Heitsch, Condon, and Hoos [2], while the greedy-source theorem is from Black, Drellich, and Tymoczko [1]. Section 4 contains additional counting facts that may be useful for examples: Corollary 4.6 and Proposition 4.7 describe large gaps near the maximum possible number of foldings; Proposition 4.8 realizes the largest five values in that range for rank one; Proposition 4.9 constructs words with (j+1)C_l foldings under the stated inequality 2j≤n−l; and Proposition 4.10 proves that for n≥4 every integer k with 0≤k≤n occurs as the number of foldings of some length-2n word over one complementary pair.
why_relevant: Studies words over complementary alphabets and the number of noncrossing perfect matchings/foldings. Its characterizations and greedy folding ideas may help analyze the set of 0-cells of F_w for a fixed reducible word.

title: Shellability of face posets of electrical networks and the CW poset property
overall_summary: Hersh and Kenyon prove Thomas Lam's conjecture that the uncrossing posets P_n of matchings or wire diagrams are shellable, more precisely that their duals admit an explicit EC-labeling. Combined with Lam's earlier theorem that P_n is Eulerian, this implies P_n is a CW poset, hence the face poset of a regular CW complex. The proof encodes wire diagrams by endpoint words, filters intervals by a start set, identifies fixed-start intervals with type A Bruhat intervals carrying Dyer's reflection-order EL-labeling, and then handles the remaining intervals by a detailed EC-labeling argument based on local rank-two uncrossing moves. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the paper's explicit treatment of cell/poset structures generated by resolving crossings of matchings, especially the shellability and CW-poset criteria, the Bruhat-interval reduction, and the local uncrossing calculus that may transfer to subcomplexes of matchings satisfying additional label constraints.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Construction 1.1 and Remark 1.2: the uncrossing poset P_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For an integer n≥2, the uncrossing poset P_n is defined as follows. Its non-artificial elements are wire diagrams obtained by placing 2n labeled boundary nodes around a disk and connecting them in pairs by n wires; equivalently these are fixed-point-free involutions on 2n letters. An artificial minimum element hat0 is adjoined. There is a unique maximum hat1, namely the diagram in which every pair of wires crosses; after cyclically labeling boundary nodes, it corresponds to the involution exchanging i with n+i for 1≤i≤n. In P_n, a cover relation u≺v occurs when u is obtained from v by locally uncrossing one crossing pair of wires in one of the two possible ways and this operation decreases the minimal number of crossing pairs by exactly one, equivalently it does not introduce any double crossing of a pair of wires. The poset is graded by rank(hat0)=0 and rank(D)=1 plus the number of crossing pairs in a crossing-minimal drawing of the wire diagram D.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a definition plus the grading observation. The cover relation is set up so that each cover changes the crossing number by exactly one. Starting from any non-artificial diagram D and moving downward by covers repeatedly decreases the crossing number until a crossing-free diagram is reached; all maximal chains from hat0 to D therefore have length one plus the crossing number of D. The artificial element hat0 sits below all crossing-free diagrams, giving those diagrams rank 1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.12: Björner's characterization of CW posets
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A finite graded poset P is called a CW poset if it has a unique minimum element hat0, if P minus hat0 is nonempty, and if for every u≠hat0 the order complex Δ_P(hat0,u) of the open interval (hat0,u) is homeomorphic to a sphere of dimension rank(u)-2. Theorem 2.12 states that a finite poset P is a CW poset if and only if there exists a regular CW complex whose face poset is P, with hat0 representing the empty cell.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this theorem from Björner. The forward direction constructs a regular CW complex inductively from the poset by attaching one cell for each poset element u≠hat0 along the sphere Δ(hat0,u), using the fact that every lower open interval has the required spherical topology. The reverse direction uses the face poset of a regular CW complex: for each cell u, the lower interval (hat0,u) is the face poset of the boundary of the closure of u, and regularity identifies that boundary with a sphere of dimension rank(u)-2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.13: thin shellable graded posets are CW posets
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a finite graded poset with a unique minimum element hat0 and at least one element other than hat0. If P is thin, meaning every rank-two closed interval has exactly four elements, and if P is shellable, meaning the order complex of P has a shelling in the standard poset sense, then P is a CW poset.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this result as a consequence of work of Björner and Danaraj-Klee. Shellability gives strong control over the topology of intervals: shellable pure complexes are homotopy equivalent to wedges of spheres, and shellings restrict to links and hence to intervals. Thinness upgrades the interval topology from a wedge-of-spheres situation to the pseudomanifold sphere situation required in the definition of CW poset. Therefore every lower interval (hat0,u) has order complex homeomorphic to the required sphere, and Theorem 2.12 applies.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.17 and Corollary 2.18: Dyer's reflection-order EL-labeling for Bruhat order
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (W,S) be a Coxeter system, let T be its set of reflections, and let Bruhat order on W have covers u≺v when v=tu for some reflection t∈T and the Coxeter length increases by one. A reflection order is a total order on the positive real roots, equivalently on T, such that whenever α, β, and cα+dβ with c,d positive are positive roots, the middle root cα+dβ lies between α and β in the total order. Theorem 2.17, due to Dyer, says that any reflection order induces an EL-labeling of Bruhat order by labeling a cover u≺v with the reflection vu^{-1}. In type A, for W=S_n, Corollary 2.18 gives an explicit EL-labeling by ordering transpositions as (1,2)<(1,3)<...<(1,n)<(2,3)<...<(2,n)<...<(n-1,n) and labeling a Bruhat cover u≺v by vu^{-1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes Dyer's theorem. The key point is that a reflection order imposes a compatible total order on the reflections appearing along Bruhat covers. Dyer proves that in every Bruhat interval there is a unique saturated chain whose reflection labels are weakly increasing and that this chain is lexicographically first. In type A, the positive roots correspond to transpositions (i,j) with i<j, and the displayed lexicographic order on transpositions is the reflection order obtained by ordering the simple reflections s_1<s_2<...<s_{n-1} and then lexicographically ordering normalized simple-root coordinate vectors.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.19: cover criterion for type A Bruhat order
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let π∈S_n be written in one-line notation, and let 1≤i<k≤n. In type A Bruhat order on S_n, there is a cover relation π≺(i,k)π if and only if two conditions hold: first, π(i)<π(k); second, for every j with i<j<k, either π(j)<π(i) or π(k)<π(j). Equivalently, no value π(j) with i<j<k lies strictly between π(i) and π(k).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Set τ=(i,k)π. Since π(i)<π(k), the pair (i,k) is not an inversion of π but becomes an inversion of τ. For each intermediate index j with i<j<k, if π(j) is outside the interval [π(i),π(k)], then j forms an inversion with exactly one of i,k before the swap and exactly one of i,k after the swap, so the total inversion count changes by zero for that j. All pairs not involving i or k are unchanged, and pairs involving i or k with indices outside [i,k] are also unchanged in net contribution. Thus the inversion number increases by exactly one precisely when no intermediate π(j) lies between π(i) and π(k). If such an intermediate value exists, the inversion number increases by more than one, so the Bruhat relation is not a cover.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.5 and Corollary 3.6: endpoint-word parametrization and count
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a basepoint endpoint in every wire diagram D with n wires and read clockwise around the boundary. When the first endpoint of the i-th newly encountered wire is met, label it i_p; when the other endpoint of that same wire is met, label it i_v. Let w(D) be the resulting word in the alphabet {1_p,...,n_p,1_v,...,n_v}. Proposition 3.5 states that the map D↦w(D) is a bijection from valid wire diagrams with n wires to the permutations of this alphabet satisfying two constraints: i_p appears to the left of j_p whenever i<j, and i_p appears to the left of i_v for every i. Corollary 3.6 states that the total number of elements of P_n is 1+(2n)!/(n!2^n), where the extra 1 is the artificial element hat0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Given a diagram, the construction of w(D) automatically satisfies the two listed constraints: first endpoints are named in the order first encountered, and a first endpoint precedes its own second endpoint. Conversely, any word satisfying these constraints tells one where the two endpoints of each wire lie around the boundary, so connecting equal indices recovers a unique pairing diagram. Counting such words gives (2n)! divided by n! for the forced order of the p-endpoints and divided by 2^n for the forced order i_p before i_v inside each pair; then add the artificial minimum element.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.8: fixed-start intervals are type A Bruhat intervals
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a wire diagram D, define its start set S(D) to be the n-subset of {1,...,2n} consisting of the positions in w(D) occupied by letters with subscript p. Define π(D)∈S_n to be the permutation obtained by restricting w(D) to the letters 1_v,...,n_v and then suppressing subscripts. If D_1<D_2 in the dual poset P_n^* and S(D_1)=S(D_2), then the closed interval [D_1,D_2] in P_n^* is isomorphic to the type A Bruhat interval [π(D_1),π(D_2)] in S_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: When the start set is fixed, the p-endpoint positions never change, so the only possible uncrossing covers in the interval swap two v-endpoints i_v and j_v. A pair of wires i<j crosses exactly when the restriction of w(D) to v-letters has i before j, so crossings correspond to non-inversions of π(D). Uncrossing a crossing pair by swapping i_v and j_v corresponds to left multiplication of π(D) by the transposition (i,j). Lemma 3.16 gives that such an uncrossing is a cover exactly when it does not introduce a double crossing, which is equivalent, via Theorem 2.19, to the corresponding transposition being a Bruhat cover. Therefore the map D↦π(D) preserves and reflects covers throughout the fixed-start interval.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.9 and Corollary 3.10: monotonicity of start sets
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Order n-subsets of {1,...,2n} lexicographically by writing them in increasing order. Proposition 3.9 states that if D_1<D_2 in P_n^*, then S(D_1)≤_lex S(D_2). More explicitly, if S(D_1)={i_1<...<i_n} and S(D_2)={j_1<...<j_n}, then either the two sequences are equal or at the first index s where they differ one has i_s<j_s. Corollary 3.10 states that if D_1<D_2 in P_n^* and S(D_1)=S(D_2), then every saturated chain from D_1 to D_2 consists only of uncrossings that replace a subsequence i,j,i,j in the lower word by i,j,j,i in the upper word, equivalently swap two v-endpoints. If S(D_1)<_lex S(D_2), then every saturated chain from D_1 to D_2 uses at least one uncrossing of the other type, replacing a crossing subsequence i,j,i,j by a noncrossing subsequence i,i,j,j after the required relabeling.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A cover in P_n^* has two possible local forms. If it resolves a crossing by swapping two terminal endpoints i_v and j_v, then all p-endpoint positions are unchanged and the start set is fixed. The other resolution moves an endpoint i_v leftward to a position previously occupied by a first endpoint j_p; after renaming wires so first endpoints are encountered in increasing order, the start set is obtained by replacing the old position of j_p by the larger old position of i_v. Thus every cover weakly increases the start set in lexicographic order, and strict increase can occur only through the second kind of resolution. Iterating along saturated chains gives the corollary.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.11 and Lemma 3.16: noncrossing pair set and cover characterization
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a wire diagram D with endpoint word w(D), define N_1(D) to be the set of ordered pairs (i,j) with i<j such that w(D) has the subsequence i,j,j,i, and define N_2(D) to be the set of ordered pairs (j,i) with i<j such that w(D) has the subsequence i,i,j,j. Let N(D)=N_1(D)∪N_2(D); it records the two possible noncrossing relative positions of a pair of wires, with the crossing case being the subsequence i,j,i,j. Lemma 3.16 states that, for a diagram D with at least one crossing, the downward covers D'≺D in P_n are exactly the wire uncrossings whose ordered label (k,m) is not in N(D), whose reverse (m,k) is also not in N(D), and which satisfy the following further condition. If k<m, then for every l with k<l<m, exactly one of (k,l) and (l,m) lies in N(D). If k>m, then for every l with l<m or k<l, exactly one of (k,l) and (l,m) lies in N(D). Here the ordered label (k,m) refers to the paper's labeling convention for the chosen local resolution of the crossing between wires k and m.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The lemma translates the geometric no-double-crossing requirement into endpoint-word combinatorics. First, (k,m) and (m,k) not lying in N(D) says that the two wires k and m are actually crossing in D. For any third wire l, the local uncrossing of k and m can create a double crossing with l exactly in the cases where the relative order of the six endpoints k,k,l,l,m,m has the wrong pattern. The stated condition that exactly one of the two relevant noncrossing pairs belongs to N(D) is precisely the condition that l crosses one of the two wires k,m in the required way and not the other, so that after the selected resolution no pair of wires crosses twice. The paper verifies this by a straightforward case check over the allowable endpoint subsequences, separating k<m from k>m.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.13, Definition 3.14, and Lemma 3.17: the edge labeling λ and its Bruhat restriction
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Work in the dual poset P_n^*, writing its maximal artificial element as hat1. For a cover D≺D' in P_n^*, suppose w(D) has a crossing subsequence k,m,k,m with k<m. If D' resolves this crossing so that w(D') has subsequence k,m,m,k, set λ(D,D')=(k,m). If D' resolves it so that w(D') has subsequence k,k,m,m, set λ(D,D')=(m,k). Covers D≺hat1 from crossing-free diagrams D to the maximal artificial element are labeled by a special label L. The label order <_λ is: first all pairs (i,j) with i<j in lexicographic order (1,2)<(1,3)<...<(1,n)<(2,3)<...<(n-1,n); then L; then all pairs (r,s) with r>s ordered by decreasing second coordinate and, for equal second coordinate, decreasing first coordinate, so (n,n-1)<(n,n-2)<(n-1,n-2)<...<(2,1). Lemma 3.17 states that if D_1<D_2 in P_n^* with S(D_1)=S(D_2), then under the isomorphism of Theorem 3.8 the restriction of λ to [D_1,D_2] is exactly Dyer's type A Bruhat EL-labeling for the lexicographic reflection order on transpositions.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For covers preserving the start set, the only operation is swapping two v-endpoints i_v and j_v, and the label assigned by Definition 3.13 is exactly the transposition (i,j) with i<j. Theorem 3.8 identifies the interval with a Bruhat interval in S_n, and the label order on pairs (i,j) with i<j is precisely the reflection order in Corollary 2.18. Hence the restricted labeling is Dyer's EL-labeling. The labels L and the pairs (r,s) with r>s do not occur in fixed-start intervals below the artificial top.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.18: P_n^* is EC-shellable
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every n≥2, the dual uncrossing poset P_n^* is EC-shellable with respect to the edge labeling λ of Definition 3.13 and the label order <_λ of Definition 3.14. Here a rank-two subchain x≺y≺z is a topological ascent if its two-label word is lexicographically smaller than the two-label word for every other saturated chain x≺y'≺z, and an EC-labeling means that every interval has a unique saturated chain all of whose consecutive rank-two subchains are topological ascents. Consequently P_n is shellable, since P_n and P_n^* have the same order complex.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is assembled from Section 4. For intervals [u,v] with v below the artificial maximum hat1 of P_n^*, Lemma 4.1 proves that every ordinary descent in λ-labels is a topological descent, and Lemma 4.2 proves that there is a unique saturated chain from u to v with no topological descents. For intervals [u,hat1], Lemma 4.5 proves that the lexicographically first saturated chain has weakly ascending labels, while Lemma 4.6 proves that every other saturated chain has a topological descent. Lemma 4.7 supplies the simple observation that any chain to hat1 using a label (j,i) with j>i must descend before the final label L. Finally, if the only descent occurs at the final step to hat1, it is topological because the diagram immediately below has a single crossing whose two resolutions have labels (i,j) and (j,i) with (i,j)<(j,i). Thus every interval has exactly one topologically ascending chain, which is the EC condition. The standard theory of EC-labelings then gives a lexicographic shelling, and dualizing does not change the order complex.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.19: P_n is a CW poset
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every n≥2, the uncrossing poset P_n is a CW poset: it is finite and graded with a unique minimum, and for every element u≠hat0 the order complex of the open interval (hat0,u) is homeomorphic to a sphere of dimension rank(u)-2. Equivalently, by Theorem 2.12, P_n is the face poset of a regular CW complex.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 3.18 proves shellability of P_n. Lam had previously proved that P_n is Eulerian, and Eulerian graded posets are thin. Therefore P_n is finite, graded, thin, shellable, and has a unique minimum. The criterion in Theorem 2.13 applies and gives that P_n is a CW poset.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.1: ordinary descents below the artificial top are topological descents
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let u<v<hat1 in P_n^*, where hat1 denotes the maximal artificial element of P_n^*. For any saturated chain from u to v, if a consecutive rank-two segment x≺y≺z has λ(x,y)>_λ λ(y,z), then x≺y≺z is a topological descent. Equivalently, there exists another element y' with x≺y'≺z such that the label word λ(x,y'),λ(y',z) is lexicographically smaller than λ(x,y),λ(y,z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof splits according to whether the two consecutive uncrossings involve four distinct wires or only three wires. With four distinct wires, the two uncrossings commute: performing them in the opposite order gives x≺y'≺z. The paper checks the possible label-order cases. If both labels are of form r>s, the descent forces the second coordinate of the first label to be smaller, and after commuting the first label becomes smaller than the original first label. If the first label has r>s and the second has p<q, the same conclusion follows depending on whether p is smaller or larger than the second coordinate. If both labels have increasing coordinates, the descent forces the second uncrossing to have smaller first coordinate, so commuting again gives a lexicographically smaller first label. For three wires, the restriction of the diagram to those wires gives a rank-two situation in P_3^*. The paper checks directly from the displayed labeled Hasse diagram of P_3 that all descents in P_3^* are topological descents. It then observes that restriction to the three wires and the inverse inclusion back into P_n^* preserve the relative order of the two possible first labels: whether a label has increasing or decreasing coordinates is preserved, the ordering of the smaller participating wire is preserved, and ties are preserved. Thus a lexicographically smaller alternative in P_3^* lifts to one in P_n^*.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.2: uniqueness of the topologically ascending chain below the artificial top
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any u<v<hat1 in P_n^*, there is a unique saturated chain from u to v with no topological descents with respect to λ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take a saturated chain C from u to v with no topological descents. By Lemma 4.1, C cannot have any ordinary descents, so its λ-labels are weakly increasing. Therefore all labels of form (i,j) with i<j occur first in the prescribed lexicographic order, followed by labels of form (j,i) with j>i in the prescribed reverse order. If S(u)=S(v), the interval is a Bruhat interval by Theorem 3.8 and the restricted labeling is Dyer's EL-labeling by Lemma 3.17, so the weakly increasing chain is unique. If S(u)≠S(v), it remains to prove uniqueness of the part of C carrying labels (j,i) with j>i, since the initial fixed-start part is then Bruhat-unique. The proof uses two observations: along any cover, p-endpoints never move left, so any p-position common to w(u) and w(v) is fixed throughout the chain; and if a cover labeled (j,l) with j>l moves l_v leftward past some k_v, then k>l, otherwise a double crossing would be created. The last cover v_r≺v in C must have label (j,l) with j>l where l is the smallest possible v-letter that either occupies a position of S(u) in w(v) or has beneath it a nested v-letter occupying such a position. If l were not smallest, a larger label with second coordinate l would have had to appear lower in C, forcing a descent. Once l is known, j is determined because w(v_r) agrees with w(v) to the left of the position where l_v lands. The only remaining ambiguity would be two different predecessors v_r and v'_r of v with the same label (j,l). The paper shows this forces w(v) to contain one of two three-wire subsequences, l,l,j,j,t,t or l,l,j,t,j,t with l<j<t. In either case any topologically ascending chain through one predecessor would require a lower label (t,l); if that label is adjacent, Proposition 4.3 gives a topological descent, and if not adjacent, an intervening label forces an ordinary descent and hence a topological descent by Lemma 4.1. Thus the last cover is unique, and induction downward gives uniqueness of all of C.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.3: three-wire rank-two analysis for labels (k,i),(j,i)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let u≺v≺w be a rank-two saturated chain in P_n^* whose two uncrossings involve exactly three wires and whose labels are λ(u,v)=(k,i) and λ(v,w)=(j,i) for i<j<k. Then there is another saturated chain u≺v'≺w. Its labels have one of two forms: either λ(u,v')=(j,i) and λ(v',w)=(j',k') for some j'<k', in which case u≺v≺w is a topological ascent; or λ(u,v')=(j,k) and λ(v',w)=(j,i), in which case u≺v≺w is a topological descent.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The label (k,i) forces w(u) to have the crossing subsequence i,k,i,k. Since i<j<k and first endpoints occur in increasing order, the restriction of w(u) to the letters i,j,k must be one of four forms: i,j,k,i,k,j; i,j,k,i,j,k; i,j,k,j,i,k; or i,j,j,k,i,k. Lam's Eulerian theorem implies thinness, so the rank-two interval [u,w] has exactly two middle elements v and v'. In the first case, wire i crosses both j and k while j and k do not cross; the two crossings can be uncrossed in either order, and the alternative chain begins with label (j,i), which is lexicographically smaller, making the original chain the topological ascent as described. In the second and third cases, wire k crosses both i and j; the alternative first label is (j,k) with j<k, which is smaller than (k,i) because all increasing-coordinate labels precede decreasing-coordinate labels, so the original chain is a topological descent. The fourth endpoint pattern cannot actually occur in the stated situation, because after the first uncrossing the wires i and j are nested rather than crossing, contradicting the existence of the second cover labeled (j,i).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.5: the lexicographically first chain to the artificial top is weakly ascending
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every D<hat1 in P_n^*, the lexicographically first saturated chain from D to the maximal artificial element hat1 has weakly ascending λ-labels.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If D has no crossings, the interval has one cover to hat1 and there is nothing to prove. Otherwise, construct the lexicographically first chain greedily. Choose the smallest i for which wire i crosses some wire k>i, and among those choose the smallest j crossing i. Lemma 3.16 ensures that there is an upward cover resolving wires i and j with label (i,j). The crucial point is that after this resolution no smaller label becomes available. The only possible danger would come from renaming effects that turn an old crossing involving j into a new smaller crossing label (i,l) with i<l<j. But that would force the old word to have a subsequence i,l,j,l,i,j; after swapping i_v and j_v, l_v still lies before i_v, so wires i and l do not cross and the label (i,l) is not available. Repeating this greedy argument gives weakly increasing labels of form (i,j) with i<j. The final cover to hat1 has label L, and every increasing-coordinate pair is <_λ L, so the entire lexicographically first chain is weakly ascending.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.6 and Lemma 4.7: non-lex-first chains to the artificial top have topological descents
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Lemma 4.7 states that any saturated chain from D<hat1 to hat1 in P_n^* that uses a label (j,i) with j>i has an ordinary descent, because all such labels are larger than L and every chain to hat1 has final label L. Lemma 4.6 states that for every D<hat1 in P_n^*, every saturated chain from D to hat1 that is not lexicographically first has a topological descent.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 4.7, the final edge of any chain to the artificial maximum has label L, while every decreasing-coordinate label (j,i) with j>i is ordered after L, so at or before the final step an ordinary descent occurs. For Lemma 4.6, consider a non-lex-first chain N and let u≺v be the first place where N fails to choose the lexicographically smallest available cover; choose u≺v' with λ(u,v')<λ(u,v). By induction on the remaining rank, the part of N above v may be assumed lexicographically first, hence by Lemma 4.5 and Lemma 4.7 it uses only increasing-coordinate labels before the final L. Thus N has a descent at v unless λ(u,v) itself has increasing coordinates. If v and v' have a common upper bound z<hat1, then the interval [u,z] lies below the artificial top, and Lemmas 4.2 and 4.1 force a topological descent. If no such common upper bound exists, then v and v' must come from resolving the same crossing pair in the two different ways; their labels are (p,q) and (q,p) with p<q. This contradicts the situation where λ(u,v) has increasing coordinates and λ(u,v')<λ(u,v). Hence every non-lex-first chain has a topological descent.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.6: degree-three trees are minors of the well-connected graphs G_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T be a tree with n leaves such that every non-leaf vertex has degree 3. Let G_n be the well-connected planar graph displayed in the paper, with n distinguished top boundary vertices. Lemma 5.6 states that T can be obtained from G_n by a sequence of edge contractions followed by a sequence of safe edge deletions, where a safe edge deletion is one that does not create an unlabeled degree-two vertex.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof constructs a spanning tree inside G_n that contracts to T. It proceeds by induction on the number of leaves. Choose a leaf l_1 of T and map it to the leftmost boundary vertex of G_n. If n=2, map the unique edge to the bottom horizontal edge and finish. For n>2, let v be the degree-three neighbor of l_1, and let the two other branches at v be subtrees T_1 and T_2 with r and n-r-1 leaves, choosing T_1 so r≤n-r-1. Map the path from l_1 to v to a horizontal path of r edges in G_n. From the image of v, send one outgoing edge upward to a subgraph isomorphic, after reflection and deletion of one endpoint, to G_r, and send the other rightward to a subgraph isomorphic to G_{n-r-1}. By induction embed T_1 and T_2 in those two subgraphs, then add vertical paths so their leaves reach the distinguished top boundary vertices. The constructed spanning tree uses all vertices needed and contracts to T. The remaining edges of G_n may then be deleted; because leaves carry labels and every internal vertex of T has degree 3, these deletions are safe.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.5: intervals in Tuffley posets are dual EC-shellable via uncrossing posets
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T(n) be the Tuffley poset, the face poset for the edge product space of phylogenetic trees with leaf label set {1,...,n}. Its elements are combinatorial equivalence classes of labeled forests obtained from degree-three labeled trees by edge contractions followed by safe edge deletions, with an artificial minimum adjoined. Corollary 5.5 states that each interval in T(n) is dual EC-shellable by way of a poset isomorphism to an interval in an uncrossing poset P_n. As a consequence, combined with known results of Gill, Linusson, Moulton, and Steel, the CW decomposition of Moulton and Steel for the edge product space of phylogenetic trees is a regular CW decomposition.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses the medial graph correspondence between wire diagrams and planar graphs. A crossing of two wires corresponds to an edge in the associated medial graph. The two possible uncrossings of that crossing correspond to deletion and contraction of the corresponding graph edge. Starting from a tree T with n labeled leaves, Lemma 5.6 embeds T as a minor of the well-connected graph G_n, which is the medial graph of a fully crossed wire diagram. Thus a lower interval in the Tuffley poset can be matched with a lower interval in an uncrossing poset. The key compatibility check is that, for trees and forests, an edge contraction never creates a non-reduced medial graph, while an edge deletion creates a non-reduced graph exactly when it is unsafe, i.e. when it creates an unlabeled degree-two vertex. This matches precisely the cover relations allowed in the Tuffley poset with the cover relations in the uncrossing poset. The EC-labeling of P_n^* restricts to intervals, so the corresponding Tuffley intervals inherit dual EC-shellings. The cited GLMS result then converts shellability of all intervals into regularity of the Moulton-Steel CW decomposition.

proof_techniques.name: Endpoint-word encoding of wire diagrams
proof_techniques.description: The paper replaces geometric wire diagrams by words w(D) in symbols i_p and i_v. The first endpoint of each newly encountered wire is labeled i_p, the second endpoint i_v, and the word is read clockwise from a fixed basepoint. This encoding is bijective after imposing only the constraints that p-endpoints appear in increasing order and that i_p precedes i_v. It turns local crossing and noncrossing conditions into subsequence patterns such as i,j,i,j for crossing, i,j,j,i for one noncrossing nesting, and i,i,j,j for the other.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the complex F_w, one can try to encode each admissible matching by a similar endpoint word augmented with the boundary letters w_i. The inverse-letter condition on region-boundary arcs may then become a combinatorial constraint on allowed pairings and on which local resolutions remain inside the complex.

proof_techniques.name: Start-set filtration and Bruhat intervals
proof_techniques.description: The start set S(D) records the positions of p-endpoints in w(D). Along the dual uncrossing order P_n^*, start sets are lexicographically nondecreasing. On intervals where the start set is fixed, only v-endpoints move, and the interval is exactly a type A Bruhat interval under the map D↦π(D), where π(D) is the v-subword. This lets the authors import Dyer's reflection-order EL-shelling for Bruhat order on a large class of intervals.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If admissible matchings for F_w admit a comparable invariant that is monotone under resolution, then fixed-invariant strata may reduce to known shellable posets such as Bruhat intervals. This is especially promising for subfamilies where resolving crossings only permutes second endpoints of matched pairs.

proof_techniques.name: Noncrossing pairs as an inversion calculus
proof_techniques.description: The set N(D)=N_1(D)∪N_2(D) records, as ordered pairs, which pairs of wires are noncrossing and in which of the two possible relative configurations. Lemma 3.16 uses N(D) to characterize exactly when a proposed uncrossing is a cover, by checking for every relevant third wire that exactly one of two associated noncrossing relations holds. This is an analogue of using inversion sets to characterize Bruhat covers.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, a similar inversion-like invariant could track which pairs of intervals are already resolved and which resolutions preserve admissibility. Such a tool would help prove that a chosen set of local moves defines a graded poset or a regular cell structure.

proof_techniques.name: EC-labeling instead of EL-labeling
proof_techniques.description: The paper's global labeling is not an EL-labeling: some rank-two intervals have more than one weakly ascending label sequence. The authors use the more flexible notion of EC-labeling, where a rank-two step is judged by whether its two-label word is lexicographically first among all alternatives in that rank-two interval. This topological ascent/descent notion is sufficient to produce a lexicographic shelling while tolerating local label behavior that would violate EL-shellability.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a natural labeling of resolutions in F_w fails to be EL because different resolutions can commute or produce multiple ascending chains, an EC-labeling may still prove shellability. This is a useful relaxation for labeled or constrained matching subcomplexes.

proof_techniques.name: Local rank-two commutation plus three-wire reduction
proof_techniques.description: To prove descents are topological descents, the paper analyzes rank-two intervals. If two consecutive uncrossings involve four distinct wires, they commute, and reversing their order gives the lexicographically smaller chain. If they involve three wires, the situation restricts to P_3^*, where all cases are checked explicitly; relative label order is then shown to be preserved when including that three-wire configuration into P_n^*.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, local confluence or shellability may be reducible to checking independent-crossing commutations and a finite list of three-arc configurations. This can localize the effect of the quasi-reduced word hypothesis and the inverse-letter constraints.

proof_techniques.name: Greedy lexicographically first resolution chain
proof_techniques.description: For intervals ending at the artificial top of P_n^*, the authors construct the lexicographically first chain greedily: choose the smallest wire i crossing a larger wire and then the smallest such j, resolve with label (i,j), and repeat. A careful renaming analysis shows that this greedy choice cannot create a new smaller available label, so the lex-first chain is weakly ascending.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A comparable greedy resolution rule for F_w could provide canonical normal forms or a discrete Morse matching. The main condition to verify would be that performing the greedy admissible resolution never creates a smaller admissible resolution that would disrupt monotonicity.

proof_techniques.name: CW-poset route to regularity
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses a general pipeline: prove shellability, combine with an Eulerian or thinness theorem, then apply the criterion that finite graded thin shellable posets with a unique minimum are CW posets. This avoids constructing characteristic maps directly; regular CW structure follows from interval topology.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the face poset underlying F_w can be identified and shown to be graded, thin or Eulerian, and shellable, then regularity and strong topological control of F_w may follow abstractly. If the resulting regular CW complex has a single maximal cell or is a shellable ball, this would imply contractibility.

proof_techniques.name: Interval heredity of EC-shellings
proof_techniques.description: An EC-labeling of a whole poset restricts to every interval, and the restricted labeling is again an EC-labeling of that interval. The paper uses this both internally and in the application to Tuffley posets, where intervals are transferred to intervals in uncrossing posets and inherit shellings.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If F_w can be realized as an interval, an order ideal with a top, or a union of intervals stable under the paper's uncrossing labels, shellability may be inherited rather than reproved from scratch.

proof_techniques.name: Medial graph deletion-contraction dictionary
proof_techniques.description: In the Tuffley-poset application, the paper uses the medial graph of a wire diagram: each wire crossing corresponds to an edge of a planar graph, and the two local uncrossings correspond to deleting or contracting that edge. The no-double-crossing condition matches the condition that the resulting graph remain reduced; for trees and forests this is equivalent to allowing contractions and only safe deletions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The cubical cells of F_w are indexed by crossings and have facets given by the two local resolutions, just as deletion-contraction choices index graph-minor intervals. A medial-graph model might convert F_w into a deletion-contraction complex with labels inherited from the word w.

other_useful_info: Lam's earlier paper [La14a] is used for the fact that the uncrossing posets P_n are Eulerian; in this paper that fact is essential for the CW-poset conclusion and for thin rank-two arguments. The paper notes that intervals preserving the start set are type A Bruhat intervals, but it remains open there whether every interval in an uncrossing poset is isomorphic to a type A Bruhat interval. The number of atoms of P_n is the n-th Catalan number, and |P_n|=1+(2n)!/(n!2^n). Uncrossing posets arise from planar electrical-network stratifications; the paper cites later work of Galashin, Karp, and Lam on regularity of related spaces. The Tuffley-poset application shows that some deletion-contraction or phylogenetic-tree face posets can be embedded intervalwise into uncrossing posets, suggesting a general strategy of proving shellability by finding an uncrossing-poset interval model.
why_relevant: Proves shellability and the CW-poset property for uncrossing posets of matchings. This is one of the closest available topological results to the complex F_w, especially if F_w can be realized as an interval, subposet, or labeled subcomplex of an uncrossing poset.

title: Groupoids and the Algebra of Rewriting in Group Presentations
overall_summary: The paper builds and studies a reduced Squier complex Sqρ(P) associated to a group presentation P = ⟨X : R⟩: its vertices are elements of the free group F(X), its edges are applications of defining relations in arbitrary left and right contexts, and its 2-cells commute non-overlapping applications of relations. The main technical point is that the fundamental groupoid of this complex has natural left and right whiskering actions by F(X), making it a regular and in fact monoidal groupoid; consequently the star at the identity is a crossed module over F(X). The paper gives an explicit generating set and presentation for this star group and identifies it with Whitehead's free crossed module associated to the presentation. For the original matching/cancellation problem, the most relevant ingredients are the Squier-complex encoding of independent local reductions, the use of square 2-cells to prove commutation of independent moves, and the normalization of paths to products of elementary moves based at the identity. The introduction also points to Pride's augmented Squier complex for handling overlaps in free reductions, which is especially relevant because quasi-reduced words exclude the elementary three-letter overlap ℓℓ^{-1}ℓ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a group, let R be a set, and let ω : R → Γ be any function. Then there exists a free crossed Γ-module on ω, unique up to isomorphism. Explicitly, take the free group F on the set R × Γ, give it the right Γ-action determined on generators by (r,u)^v = (r,uv), and define δ : F → Γ on generators by δ(r,u) = u^{-1}ω(r)u. Let K be the normal Γ-invariant subgroup generated by all Peiffer elements (r,u)^{-1}(s,v)^{-1}(r,u)(s, v u^{-1}ω(r)u), where r,s ∈ R and u,v ∈ Γ. Then δ induces a crossed module ∂ : F/K → Γ, and the map r ↦ class of (r,1) is the universal basis over ω.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction starts with the free group on all formal Γ-translates of the proposed basis elements. The map δ is forced by equivariance of a crossed module boundary: the translate (r,u) must have boundary u^{-1}ω(r)u. The listed Peiffer elements are precisely the relations needed to impose the crossed-module Peiffer identity t^{u∂} = u^{-1}tu in the quotient. The subgroup K is normal, Γ-invariant, and lies in ker δ, so δ descends to F/K and the Γ-action descends as well. The crossed-module axioms then follow directly from the definitions of δ and the imposed Peiffer relations. For the universal property, given any crossed Γ-module T′ with a function σ : R → T′ satisfying ∂′σ(r)=ω(r), send each generator (r,u) to σ(r)^u; the crossed-module axioms in T′ kill all Peiffer elements, so this map factors uniquely through F/K. Uniqueness follows because the Γ-translates of the basis generate F/K.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ∂ : C → Γ be the free crossed Γ-module with basis map ρ : R → C, and let Q = coker ∂. Then the abelianization C^ab is a free Q-module on the image of the composite R → C → C^ab.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Whitehead for this fact. In the explicit construction of the free crossed module, C is generated by formal translates of basis elements by Γ. After abelianization, the Peiffer relations identify the effect of translating by elements of im ∂ with the trivial action, so the Γ-action factors through Q = Γ/im ∂. No further linear relations among the images of the basis elements are introduced. Thus C^ab is the free module over the quotient group Q generated by the images of R.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P = ⟨X : R⟩ be a group presentation. Put F = F(X), let bρ : R → F send a relation pair (l,r) to l^{-1}r in F, and let N be the normal closure in F of the image of bρ, so that G = F/N is the presented group. Let ∂ : C(P) → F be the free crossed F-module on bρ, and let π(P)=ker ∂. Then C(P) is isomorphic as a group to π(P) × N. Its abelianization C(P)^ab is a free G-module, and the natural map π(P) → C(P)^ab is injective. Consequently there is a short exact sequence of G-modules 0 → π(P) → C(P)^ab → N^ab → 0, where N^ab is the relation module of the presentation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The image of ∂ is N, giving an exact sequence 1 → π(P) → C(P) → N → 1. Since N is a subgroup of the free group F, it is itself free, so this extension splits. The kernel π(P) of a crossed module is central in C(P), hence the split extension is a direct product π(P) × N. Abelianizing then keeps π(P) injectively, because centrality gives C(P)^ab ≅ π(P) × N^ab at the level relevant to the kernel inclusion. Proposition 1.3 identifies C(P)^ab as a free module over F/N = G. The induced boundary map on abelianizations has image N^ab and kernel π(P), yielding the displayed exact sequence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a semiregular groupoid: its vertex set V(G) is a monoid with identity e, and V(G) acts on morphisms from the left and right, written x⊲α and α⊳x, compatibly with domains, ranges, identities, and groupoid composition. For morphisms α,β ∈ G define two everywhere-defined products α∗β = (α⊳βd) ∘ (αr⊲β) and α⊛β = (αd⊲β) ∘ (α⊳βr), where d and r denote domain and range. Then each of ∗ and ⊛ makes G into a monoid with identity 1_e. The operation ∗ makes G into a strict monoidal groupoid with object monoid V(G) if and only if ∗ and ⊛ coincide. If V(G) is a group, so G is regular, then ∗ and ⊛ are group operations on G. In the regular case, the inverse of α with respect to ∗ is αr^{-1}⊲α°⊳αd^{-1}, where α° is the groupoid inverse, and the range map r : (G,∗) → V(G) is a group homomorphism; also star_e(G), the set of morphisms with domain e, is a subgroup of (G,∗).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For α∗β, the morphism α⊳βd has range (αr)(βd), while αr⊲β has domain (αr)(βd), so the groupoid composite is defined; similarly for α⊛β. Associativity is checked by expanding both (α∗β)∗γ and α∗(β∗γ) and repeatedly using the axioms that left and right actions distribute over groupoid composition and associate with multiplication in V(G). The identity calculation is direct: 1_e∗α = 1_{αd}∘α and α∗1_e = α∘1_{αr}; the same argument works for ⊛. The equality ∗=⊛ is exactly the interchange condition needed for the product on morphisms and the monoid product on vertices to define a strict monoidal groupoid. When V(G) is a group, the displayed formula for the ∗-inverse is verified by composing α with the whiskered groupoid inverse and using inverse elements in V(G); the formula for ⊛ is analogous. Finally, r(α∗β)=r(α)r(β), so r is a homomorphism, and if αd=βd=e then (α∗β)d=e, so star_e(G) is closed under ∗ and inverses.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a regular groupoid. The group V(G) acts on the group (G,∗) by automorphisms via α^q = q^{-1}⊲α⊳q for q ∈ V(G). With this action, the restricted range map r : star_e(G) → V(G) is a crossed module if and only if G is monoidal, meaning that the two products ∗ and ⊛ on G coincide.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as the standard connection between regular groupoids and crossed modules. The action formula preserves domains equal to e, because q^{-1}e q=e, and sends ranges by conjugation: r(α^q)=q^{-1}r(α)q, giving the equivariance crossed-module axiom. The remaining crossed-module Peiffer identity compares conjugation inside the ∗-group star_e(G) with the action by the range r(β). Expanding β^{-1}∗α∗β using the inverse formula from Proposition 2.1 and simplifying via the whiskering axioms shows that this identity is equivalent to the ability to interchange the two possible products α∗β and α⊛β. Conversely, if the Peiffer identity holds on the star, arbitrary morphisms can be whiskered into the star and the same calculation forces ∗=⊛ throughout G.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.1 / Reduced Squier complex construction
