key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a group presentation P = ⟨X : R⟩, where relation pairs (l,r) ∈ R are words over X∪X^{-1} and products are interpreted in the free group F(X), the reduced Squier complex Sqρ(P) is the following 2-complex. Its vertices are the elements of F(X). Its directed edges are tuples (p,l,r,q), with p,q ∈ F(X) and (l,r) ∈ R, starting at plq and ending at prq. Its 2-cells are attached for every pair of non-overlapping relation applications: for p,q,p′,q′ ∈ F(X) and (l,r),(l′,r′) ∈ R, the two paths (p,l,r, q p′ l′ q′) followed by (p r q p′, l′, r′, q′), and (p l q p′, l′, r′, q′) followed by (p,l,r, q p′ r′ q′), have the same endpoints and bound a 2-cell.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a definition rather than a theorem. The point of the construction is that an edge records applying one defining relation in a specified left and right context, while a 2-cell records the fact that two relation applications in disjoint subwords can be performed in either order. Because vertices are already elements of the free group F(X), the construction suppresses explicit free-reduction bookkeeping.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any group presentation P = ⟨X : R⟩, the fundamental groupoid π(Sqρ(P), F(X)) of the reduced Squier complex, with object set F(X), is a regular groupoid. The vertex group is F(X), and the left and right actions of F(X) on single edges are u⊲(p,l,r,q) = (up,l,r,q) and (p,l,r,q)⊳v = (p,l,r,qv), extended to edge paths and then to homotopy classes.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Multiplying the left context of an edge by u changes its initial and terminal vertices from plq and prq to uplq and uprq, so it defines a valid left action. Multiplying the right context by v similarly changes the initial and terminal vertices to plqv and prqv, defining a right action. These actions extend edgewise to paths because adjacent endpoints remain adjacent after the same context multiplication. They preserve the defining 2-cells of Sqρ(P), since whiskering a square for two non-overlapping relation applications by an element of F(X) gives another square of the same form. The compatibility axioms in the definition of a regular groupoid are then immediate from associativity and identity in F(X).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every group presentation P = ⟨X : R⟩, the regular groupoid π(Sqρ(P), F(X)) is monoidal: for all homotopy classes of paths α and β, the two products α∗β = (α⊳βd) ∘ (αr⊲β) and α⊛β = (αd⊲β) ∘ (α⊳βr) are equal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First consider single-edge paths α=(p,l,r,q) and β=(p′,l′,r′,q′). Then α∗β is the path (p,l,r, q p′ l′ q′) followed by (p r q p′, l′, r′, q′), while α⊛β is the path (p l q p′, l′, r′, q′) followed by (p,l,r, q p′ r′ q′). These are precisely the two sides of one defining 2-cell of Sqρ(P), so they are homotopic. For longer paths, the proof uses induction. Fix β to be one edge and induct on the length of α: split α into a prefix and its final edge, commute the final edge past β using the single-edge case, and then use the induction hypothesis for the prefix. Then induct on the length of β: split β into a prefix and its final edge, apply the induction hypothesis to commute α past the prefix, and use the already-proved single-edge-in-β case for the last edge. The whiskering actions distribute over path concatenation, so the algebraic expansions match the definitions of ∗ and ⊛ at each induction step.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P = ⟨X : R⟩ be a group presentation. The set star_1(π(Sqρ(P),F(X))) of homotopy classes of paths in Sqρ(P) whose domain is the identity element 1 ∈ F(X) is a group under the product ∗. The range map r : star_1(π(Sqρ(P),F(X))) → F(X) is a crossed module, using the action α^q = q^{-1}⊲α⊳q of F(X).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 3.1 the fundamental groupoid is regular. Proposition 2.1 says that in a regular groupoid, the star at the vertex identity is a subgroup under ∗ and the range map is a group homomorphism. Theorem 3.2 says the groupoid is monoidal, and Proposition 2.2 then gives precisely the crossed-module axioms for the range map on the star.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S1 be the set of edges of Sqρ(P) whose domain is 1 ∈ F(X). Explicitly, S1 = {λ_{l,r,q} : q ∈ F(X), (l,r) ∈ R}, where λ_{l,r,q} = (q^{-1}l^{-1}, l, r, q). If e is any edge in the connected component of 1, define eλ = (ed)^{-1}⊲e ∈ S1. Then every edge path α in star_1(Sqρ(P)) is equal, under the ∗ product, to a product of elements of S1. Hence star_1(Sqρ(P)) is generated by S1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write α as a composable edge path α1∘α2∘...∘αn. For each edge set λ_i=(α_i d)^{-1}⊲α_i, which is based at 1 by construction. Since α starts at 1, λ_1=α_1. Assume by induction that the prefix α1∘...∘α_{n-1} equals λ_1∗...∗λ_{n-1}. The range of this prefix is α_n d, so multiplying by λ_n using ∗ gives prefix ∘ (α_n d⊲λ_n). But α_n d⊲λ_n = α_n by definition of λ_n. Thus the full path equals λ_1∗...∗λ_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If α∘β is an edge path in star_1(Sqρ(P)), and if γλ denotes the ∗-product of based edges associated to a path γ as in Proposition 3.4, then (α∘β)λ = αλ ∗ βλ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The list of single edges in the composite α∘β is the concatenation of the list of single edges in α and the list of single edges in β. Proposition 3.4 constructs γλ by translating each edge of γ individually to an edge based at 1 and then multiplying these translated edges in the original order using ∗. Applying this construction to the concatenated list gives exactly the ∗-product of the construction for α followed by the construction for β.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a group presentation P = ⟨X : R⟩, the group star_1(Sqρ(P)) under ∗ has generating set S1 = {λ_{l,r,q} : q ∈ F(X), (l,r) ∈ R}, with λ_{l,r,q}=(q^{-1}l^{-1},l,r,q), and defining relations λ_{l,r, v s u} ∗ λ_{s,d,u} = λ_{s,d,u} ∗ λ_{l,r, v d u} for all relation pairs (l,r),(s,d) ∈ R and all u,v ∈ F(X). Here products such as v s u and v d u are computed in F(X), using the paper's convention that words in relation pairs are interpreted in the free group.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Proposition 3.4 gives the generators. The displayed relation is exactly the normalized form of the boundary of a Squier 2-cell for two non-overlapping applications of relations (l,r) and (s,d). More generally, a 1-homotopy that inserts or deletes a pair α∘α° of inverse edges does not change the normalized ∗-product: the translated pair becomes ((αd)^{-1}⊲α)∗((αr)^{-1}⊲α°), which is the constant path after expanding the definition of ∗. For a 2-cell with boundary paths α∘β and γ∘δ, normalization gives αλ∗βλ on one side and γλ∗δλ on the other. Writing the general 2-cell in the form used in Definition 3.1 and setting v equal to the combined middle context, these four normalized edges are λ_{l,r, v s u}, λ_{s,d,u}, λ_{s,d,u}, and λ_{l,r, v d u}. Therefore every 2-homotopy imposes one of the displayed relations, and conversely each displayed relation is realized by such a 2-cell. Thus these relations are complete.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P = ⟨X : R⟩ be a group presentation, let F=F(X), and let C → F be the free crossed F-module on the function R → F sending (l,r) to l^{-1}r. Then the crossed F-module r : star_1(Sqρ(P)) → F obtained from the reduced Squier complex is isomorphic to C → F. Under this isomorphism, the standard generator (l,r,u) of the free crossed module, corresponding to the relation (l,r) translated by u ∈ F, is sent to λ_{l,r,u}=(u^{-1}l^{-1},l,r,u).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define a basis map R → star_1(Sqρ(P)) by (l,r) ↦ λ_{l,r,1}=(l^{-1},l,r,1). Its range under r is l^{-1}r, the same boundary as the basis element in the free crossed module. By the universal property of the free crossed module, there is a crossed-module morphism φ : C → star_1(Sqρ(P)), and equivariance forces φ(l,r,u)=λ_{l,r,u}. This map is bijective on the chosen generating sets. To construct the inverse, send λ_{l,r,u} to (l,r,u). Proposition 3.6 says it suffices to check the defining relations. The relation λ_{l,r, v s u}∗λ_{s,d,u}=λ_{s,d,u}∗λ_{l,r, v d u} maps to (l,r,v s u)(s,d,u)=(s,d,u)(l,r,v d u). In the crossed module C, conjugating (l,r,v s u) by (s,d,u) changes its context by multiplication by ∂(s,d,u)=u^{-1}s^{-1}d u, giving (s,d,u)^{-1}(l,r,v s u)(s,d,u)=(l,r,v d u). Thus the relation holds in C, so the inverse homomorphism is well defined. The two maps are inverse on generators and respect the crossed-module boundary and action.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P = ⟨X : R⟩ be a presentation of a group G, let θ : F(X) → G be the presentation map, and let N=ker θ. Then there is a short exact sequence of G-modules 0 → π1(Sqρ(P),1) → ⊕_{rel∈R} ZG → N^ab → 0, where the middle term has one free ZG-summand for each defining relation pair in R.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The local fundamental group π1(Sqρ(P),1) is the kernel of the range crossed module r : star_1(Sqρ(P)) → F(X). By Theorem 3.7 this crossed module is the free crossed module C(P) → F(X), so the kernel is π(P)=ker ∂ in the notation of Proposition 1.4. Proposition 1.4 supplies the exact sequence 0 → π(P) → C(P)^ab → N^ab → 0 of G-modules. Proposition 1.3 identifies C(P)^ab as the free G-module with basis the relation set R. Substituting π1(Sqρ(P),1) for π(P) gives the stated sequence.

proof_techniques.name: Encoding reductions as a Squier 2-complex
proof_techniques.description: The paper encodes applications of presentation relations as directed edges in a complex whose vertices are group elements. An edge (p,l,r,q) means that the relation l=r is applied in the context p _ q, moving from plq to prq. A square 2-cell is attached whenever two relation applications occur in non-overlapping contexts; the square records that the two possible orders of applying them are homotopic.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A matching or cancellation scheme for a word in a free group may be modeled by local cancellation moves in contexts. The quasi-reduced hypothesis excludes the elementary overlap ℓℓ^{-1}ℓ, so a Squier-style complex with cells for independent moves may be especially close to the combinatorics of the matching complex F_w.

proof_techniques.name: Whiskering by left and right contexts
proof_techniques.description: The fundamental groupoid of the reduced Squier complex carries left and right actions by the free group F(X): one multiplies the left or right context of every edge in a path. This turns the groupoid into a regular groupoid and makes it possible to define global products of paths even when their endpoints do not match in the ordinary concatenation sense.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, one can try to label subarcs or partial reductions by surrounding word contexts and use whiskering to compare local moves made in different positions. This can reduce dependence on absolute location in the word and isolate the algebraic effect of a cancellation pair.

proof_techniques.name: Commuting independent moves via the equality ∗=⊛
proof_techniques.description: The two products α∗β and α⊛β represent the two possible orders in which one performs path α and path β after inserting the appropriate contexts. The proof of Theorem 3.2 shows first that they agree for single relation applications because of a defining Squier square, then uses induction to extend the equality to arbitrary paths.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Crossing resolutions in F_w have two local outcomes and higher cells are built from independent choices. The induction proving that elementary commutations imply arbitrary path commutations may transfer to show that different sequences of resolving or uncrossing operations produce homotopic paths.

proof_techniques.name: Normalizing every path to based elementary generators
proof_techniques.description: Given an edge e, the paper translates it by the inverse of its domain to obtain a based edge eλ in S1, the set of edges starting at the identity. Any path beginning at the identity can then be written as a ∗-product of these based elementary edges. This converts path homotopy questions in the full groupoid into algebraic questions in the star group at the identity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a base object can be chosen for cancellation matchings or reduction diagrams, each local move might be normalized to a standard based move. This could give a presentation of the fundamental group of F_w or of a related reduction complex.

proof_techniques.name: Deriving generators and relations from homotopies
proof_techniques.description: After normalizing paths, the paper analyzes how elementary homotopies affect the resulting products. Insertion or deletion of an inverse edge pair contributes nothing, while passing across a Squier 2-cell gives exactly one Peiffer-type relation among based generators. This yields a complete group presentation for star_1(Sqρ(P)).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A route to contractibility of F_w is to extract a combinatorial presentation for its loops from codimension-one/codimension-two local changes and then prove the presentation is trivial. The paper gives a template for doing this systematically.

proof_techniques.name: Crossed-module identification by universal property
proof_techniques.description: The star group of the Squier complex is not merely presented ad hoc; it is identified with the free crossed module associated to the group presentation. The proof defines the map on basis relation edges, obtains a morphism by freeness, and then checks the Squier relations are exactly Peiffer relations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the matching complex can be related to a rewriting presentation for free reductions, a crossed-module description may turn topological questions about loops into algebraic questions about kernels of boundary maps.

proof_techniques.name: Using relation-module exact sequences as homotopy detectors
proof_techniques.description: The paper identifies π1(Sqρ(P),1) with the kernel in a free crossed module and then embeds it into a free G-module mapping onto the relation module N^ab. This gives an algebraic invariant of the fundamental group of the Squier complex.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For an attempted proof that a complex like F_w is contractible, an analogous exact sequence could either prove simple connectivity by showing the kernel vanishes or reveal obstructions by producing nonzero algebraic classes.

proof_techniques.name: Separating non-overlap and overlap phenomena
proof_techniques.description: The reduced Squier complex in the paper uses only 2-cells for non-overlapping relation applications. In the introduction, the authors recall Pride's augmented Squier complex K+, where extra 2-cells are added to handle overlaps in free-reduction relations xx^{-1}=1=x^{-1}x, so that freely equivalent words have homotopic free-reduction paths.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original word is assumed quasi-reduced, precisely excluding subwords ℓℓ^{-1}ℓ where two length-two free cancellations overlap. This suggests that the non-overlap Squier mechanism may suffice locally, while Pride-type overlap cells identify what would need extra handling without the quasi-reduced hypothesis.

other_useful_info: The introduction recalls prior work of Pride, Cremanns-Otto, and Gilbert: for monoid presentations of groups, adding extra 2-cells to the Squier complex to form the Pride complex K+ accounts for possible overlaps in free reductions xx^{-1}=1=x^{-1}x, and in K+ any two edge paths representing the same free equivalence are fixed-endpoint homotopic. Example 3.9 computes the reduced Squier complex for P = ⟨x : xx^{-1}=1⟩, which presents the infinite cyclic group: the vertices are ⟨x⟩, every edge is a loop, and π1(Sqρ(P),1) is a free abelian group of countably infinite rank with generators λ_q indexed by q ∈ Z and action λ_q^x = λ_{q+1}. Corollary 3.10 states that for a finite presentation P of G, π1(Sqρ(P),1) is finitely generated as a G-module if and only if G is of type FP3. Corollary 3.11 reformulates Dyer's L-Cockcroft condition for a subgroup L ≤ G as the vanishing of the induced map π1(Sqρ(P),1) ⊗_L Z → ⊕_{rel∈R} Z(G/L).
why_relevant: Builds Squier-type complexes for group presentations and rewriting systems. This is relevant because the word w is reducible in a free group, and F_w may be interpretable as a space of derivations between reductions.

title: Regularity theorem for totally nonnegative flag varieties
overall_summary: The paper proves that Lusztig and Rietsch's cell decomposition of the totally nonnegative part of any partial flag variety G/P is a regular CW complex, so every closed cell is homeomorphic to a closed ball. The proof is split into an abstract topological criterion and a geometric construction: a shellable totally nonnegative space equipped with a Fomin-Shapiro atlas has conical product neighborhoods of all cells, and hence its links and closed cells are balls by induction, shellability, and the generalized Poincare conjecture. The atlas for G/P is built by embedding local affine charts of projected Richardson strata into affine flag varieties, where Bruhat/Gaussian decomposition gives product coordinates and a torus action gives radial contraction. For the original matching-complex problem, the most useful contribution is the abstract link-induction package: if the matching complex can be given a shellable/thin face poset and local star charts of the form cell times cone on link, the paper gives a robust route to regularity, ball closures, and contractibility.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a semisimple algebraic group split over R, let P be a parabolic subgroup, and let (G/P)_{≥0} be Lusztig's totally nonnegative part of the partial flag variety G/P. Rietsch's decomposition of (G/P)_{≥0} into totally positive cells is a regular CW complex. Consequently, the closure of each cell is homeomorphic to a closed ball.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The theorem is obtained by combining the abstract regularity criterion, Theorem 2.4, with the geometric verification in Theorem 2.5. The known results of Rietsch, Williams, and Knutson-Lam-Speyer show that (G/P, (G/P)_{≥0}, Q_J) is a shellable totally nonnegative space: the closure poset Q_J is graded, thin, and shellable; the strata are smooth projected Richardson varieties; positive pieces are open balls; and positive closure relations match the poset. The main new geometric work constructs a Fomin-Shapiro atlas for this space by embedding suitable affine charts in G/P into affine flag varieties. Once this atlas exists, the purely topological link-induction argument proves regularity and ball closures.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the Grassmannian Gr(k,n), the totally nonnegative Grassmannian Gr_{≥0}(k,n), stratified by Postnikov's positroid cells according to which Plucker coordinates vanish and which are positive, is a regular CW complex. The closure of every positroid cell is homeomorphic to a closed ball.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the special case of Theorem 1.1 in which G/P is Gr(k,n). The totally nonnegative part in Lusztig's sense agrees with Postnikov's Gr_{≥0}(k,n), and Rietsch's cell decomposition agrees with the positroid cell decomposition. Thus the regularity and ball-closure conclusion follows directly from Theorem 1.1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.4 (abstract regularity criterion)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (Y, Y_{≥0}, Q) be a shellable totally nonnegative space in the following precise sense. The finite poset Q, after adjoining a minimum element 0-hat, is graded, thin, shellable, and has a unique maximal element 1-hat; Y is a smooth manifold stratified as Y = disjoint union over g in Q of smooth embedded strata Y_g^o, with dim Y_g^o equal to the rank dim(g) and closure Y_h = union over g ⪯ h of Y_g^o; Y_{≥0} is compact; for each g, Y_g^{>0} = Y_g^o ∩ Y_{≥0} is a connected component of Y_g^o diffeomorphic to R_{>0}^{dim(g)}; and the closure of Y_h^{>0} inside Y is Y_h^{≥0} = union over g ⪯ h of Y_g^{>0}. Assume further that (Y, Y_{≥0}, Q) admits a Fomin-Shapiro atlas: for every g in Q, with Star_g = union over h ⪰ g of Y_h^o, there is an open set O_g ⊂ Star_g, a smooth cone (Z_g, θ_g) with Z_g a closed embedded submanifold of R^{N_g} and θ_g an R_{>0}-action increasing Euclidean norm away from 0, and a diffeomorphism ν_g: O_g → (Y_g^o ∩ O_g) × Z_g such that strata become products, the stratum Y_g is sent to (Y_g^o ∩ O_g) × {0}, the cone action preserves the pieces of Z_g, and the positive star Star_g^{≥0} is contained in O_g. Then the decomposition Y_{≥0} = disjoint union over h in Q of Y_h^{>0} is a regular CW complex. In particular, every Y_h^{≥0} is homeomorphic to a closed ball of dimension dim(h).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on cell dimension, relying on a nested induction for links. For each pair g ≺ h, Section 3 defines the link Lk_{g,h}^{≥0} as the intersection of the normal positive piece Z_{g,h}^{≥0} with the unit sphere in R^{N_g}. Lemma 3.2 proves that link strata are contractible smooth manifolds and that the closed links are compact. Proposition 3.5 identifies the positive star Star_{g,h}^{≥0} with Y_g^{>0} × Cone(Lk_{g,h}^{≥0}). Lemma 3.6 gives local models for points in lower-dimensional link strata in terms of smaller links. Theorem 3.12 then proves by induction that every link Lk_{g,h}^{≥0} is a closed ball. Returning to Y_h^{≥0}, a point in the interior cell Y_h^{>0} has Euclidean local coordinates, while a point in a lower cell Y_g^{>0}, g ≺ h, has a neighborhood Y_g^{>0} × Cone(Lk_{g,h}^{≥0}), hence a half-space local model because the link is a ball. Thus Y_h^{≥0} is a compact topological manifold with boundary equal to the union of lower positive cells. Its interior Y_h^{>0} is contractible, so by Brown collaring the whole manifold is contractible. By induction the boundary is a regular CW complex with face poset the open interval (0-hat,h); shellability and thinness imply this boundary is a sphere by Theorem 3.9. The generalized Poincare consequence Theorem 3.10 then implies Y_h^{≥0} is a closed ball.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every partial flag variety G/P of a simple simply connected algebraic group G split over R, the triple ((G/P)_R, (G/P)_{≥0}, Q_J), where Q_J is Rietsch's closure poset of projected Richardson cells, is a shellable totally nonnegative space and admits a Fomin-Shapiro atlas.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The shellable totally nonnegative axioms are verified using known results: Williams proves that the poset Q_J with an added minimum is graded, thin, and shellable; Knutson-Lam-Speyer and related Richardson-variety theory give smooth projected Richardson strata and their closure relations; Rietsch proves the positive cells, their positivity components, and their closure relations; compactness is immediate from compactness of the real partial flag variety. For the Fomin-Shapiro atlas, fix a minimal element f = (u,u) below g in Q_J. The paper constructs an affine Bruhat atlas map φ_u from an affine chart C_u^{(J)} in G/P to the affine flag variety G/B. The map sends projected Richardson strata in the star of f isomorphically to affine Richardson strata. The affine flag variety has Gaussian/Bruhat product projections ν_g around the big cell C_{ψ(g)}, and a dominant affine torus action supplies the smooth cone structure. The open set O_g is the preimage of C_{ψ(g)} under φ_u; Theorem 7.3, whose positivity part relies on the subtraction-free analysis of Sections 5 and 6, proves that O_g contains the whole positive star Star_g^{≥0}. These data satisfy all Fomin-Shapiro axioms.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume (Y, Y_{≥0}, Q) is a totally nonnegative space with a Fomin-Shapiro atlas. Fix g ≺ h in Q. Write ν_g = (ν_{g,1}, ν_{g,2}). Define Star_{g,h}^{≥0} = Y_h^{≥0} ∩ Star_g^{≥0}; define Z_g^{≥0} = ν_{g,2}(Star_g^{≥0}), Z_{g,h}^{≥0} = ν_{g,2}(Star_{g,h}^{≥0}), Z_{g,h}^{>0} = Z_g^{≥0} ∩ Z_{g,h}^o, where Z_{g,h}^o is the stratum in Z_g corresponding to h; let S_g be the unit sphere in R^{N_g}; and define Lk_{g,h}^{≥0} = Z_{g,h}^{≥0} ∩ S_g and Lk_{g,h}^{>0} = Z_{g,h}^{>0} ∩ S_g. Then: (i) for x in O_g, x lies in Y_h^{>0} if and only if ν_g(x) lies in Y_g^{>0} × Z_{g,h}^{>0}; (ii) Z_{g,h}^{>0} is an embedded submanifold of Z_g of dimension dim(h) - dim(g), intersects S_g transversely, and is preserved by the cone action θ_g(t,−) for all t > 0; (iii) Lk_{g,h}^{>0} is a contractible smooth manifold of dimension dim(h) - dim(g) - 1; and (iv) Lk_{g,h}^{≥0} is compact.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (i), the positive star Star_g^{≥0} is connected because it contains the dense top positive cell. Its image under ν_{g,1} is connected, lies in Y_g^o ∩ O_g, and contains Y_g^{>0}; since Y_g^{>0} is a connected component, the image is Y_g^{>0}. Applying the product-stratification axiom shows that ν_g(Y_h^{>0}) is contained in Y_g^{>0} × Z_{g,h}^{>0}. The set Z_{g,h}^{>0} is then a connected subset of the corresponding Z-stratum; taking the connected component C containing it, ν_g^{-1}(Y_g^{>0} × C) is a connected subset of Y_h^o ∩ O_g containing Y_h^{>0}, so by the positive-component axiom it equals Y_h^{>0}. For (ii), since Y_h^{>0} is an embedded submanifold and ν_g(Y_h^{>0}) = Y_g^{>0} × Z_{g,h}^{>0}, Lemma 3.3 implies Z_{g,h}^{>0} is embedded, with the dimension difference. The cone action preserves the ambient stratum and hence the connected component Z_{g,h}^{>0}. Strict increase of norm along action orbits implies transversality with the unit sphere. For (iii), the product Y_g^{>0} × Z_{g,h}^{>0} is contractible because it is homeomorphic to Y_h^{>0}; hence Z_{g,h}^{>0} is contractible as a retract. The cone action radially retracts Z_{g,h}^{>0} onto its unit-sphere slice Lk_{g,h}^{>0}. For (iv), Star_{g,h}^{≥0} = Y_h^{≥0} ∩ O_g is closed in O_g, so its image is closed in Y_g^{>0} × Z_g; projecting gives Z_{g,h}^{≥0} closed in Z_g. Intersecting with the unit sphere in finite-dimensional R^{N_g} gives compactness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A and B be smooth manifolds. If B' is a subset of B such that A × B' is an embedded submanifold of A × B, then B' is an embedded submanifold of B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose any point a in A. The projection A × B' → A has a as a regular value, so {a} × B' is an embedded submanifold of A × B'. Since A × B' is embedded in A × B, the slice {a} × B' is embedded in {a} × B, which identifies B' as an embedded submanifold of B.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let θ: R_{>0} × R^N → R^N be a smooth map giving an R_{>0}-action on R^N such that ∂/∂t ||θ(t,x)|| > 0 for every t > 0 and every nonzero x. Then: (i) θ(t,0) = 0 for all t > 0; (ii) lim_{t→0+} θ(t,x) = 0 for all x in R^N; and (iii) for every nonzero x there is a unique t_1(x) > 0 such that ||θ(t_1(x),x)|| = 1, and the function t_1: R^N \ {0} → R_{>0} is continuous.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper reduces this to properties of contractive flows from earlier work by setting f(s,x) = θ(e^{-s},x). The strict monotonicity of norm along θ-orbits becomes strict contraction for f as s increases. Such flows fix the origin and contract every point to the origin in the limit t → 0+. Strict monotonicity gives uniqueness of the time at which an orbit meets the unit sphere, and standard implicit/flow-continuity arguments give continuity of t_1. Compactness of the unit sphere is used later to obtain uniform convergence near the cone point.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In a totally nonnegative space with a Fomin-Shapiro atlas, for any g ≺ h in Q: (i) the set Z_{g,h}^{≥0} is homeomorphic to the open cone Cone(Lk_{g,h}^{≥0}), with 0 mapping to the cone point and each stratum Z_{g,g'}^{>0}, g ≺ g' ⪯ h, mapping to Lk_{g,g'}^{>0} × R_{>0}; and (ii) the positive star Star_{g,h}^{≥0} is homeomorphic to Y_g^{>0} × Cone(Lk_{g,h}^{≥0}), with Y_g^{>0} mapping to Y_g^{>0} times the cone point.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For nonzero x in Z_{g,h}^{≥0}, let t_1(x) be the unique scaling time from Lemma 3.4 such that ||θ_g(t_1(x),x)|| = 1. Define ξ(0) to be the cone point and ξ(x) = (θ_g(t_1(x),x), 1/t_1(x)) for x ≠ 0. The inverse sends the cone point to 0 and sends (y,t) in Lk_{g,h}^{≥0} × R_{>0} to θ_g(t,y). Continuity away from the cone point is immediate. Continuity at 0 follows because x_n → 0 forces t_1(x_n) → ∞, otherwise monotonicity would keep θ_g(R,x_n) at norm at least 1 for a bounded R and contradict θ_g(R,0)=0. Continuity of the inverse at the cone point follows from uniform convergence θ_g(t,y) → 0 for y on the compact unit sphere as t → 0+. Part (ii) combines part (i) with Lemma 3.2(i), which identifies Star_{g,h}^{≥0} under ν_g with Y_g^{>0} × Z_{g,h}^{≥0}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume a totally nonnegative space with a Fomin-Shapiro atlas. Let g ≺ p ⪯ h in Q, let x_p be a point of the link stratum Lk_{g,p}^{>0}, and put d = dim(p) - dim(g) - 1. Then there is a local homeomorphism from (Lk_{g,h}^{≥0}, x_p) to (Z_{p,h}^{≥0} × R^d, (0,0)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a point x_g in Y_g^{>0}. On the open subset H_p of Z_g consisting of points x such that ν_g^{-1}(x_g,x) lies in O_p, define θ_{g,p}(x) = ν_{p,2}(ν_g^{-1}(x_g,x)) for x in H_p ∩ S_g. The point x_p lies in H_p and maps to 0. The key is to show x_p is a regular point of θ_{g,p}. The differential of ν_{p,2} has kernel the tangent space to Y_p^o, and the slice ν_g^{-1}({x_g} × (H_p ∩ S_g)) meets Y_p^{>0} transversely because Lemma 3.2 gives transversality of Z_{g,p}^{>0} with S_g. The submersion theorem then gives local coordinates in which θ_{g,p} is projection onto the Z_p-coordinate, with an R^d fiber. Finally, by comparing positivity under the two product charts, a nearby point x lies in Lk_{g,r}^{>0} exactly when θ_{g,p}(x) lies in Z_{p,r}^{>0}. Taking the union over p ⪯ r ⪯ h identifies the local piece of Lk_{g,h}^{≥0} with (local piece of Z_{p,h}^{≥0}) × R^d.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If a graded finite poset is shellable, then every interval in that poset is shellable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Bjorner's result. Shellability of the order complex restricts to intervals because the order complex of an interval is a link or induced subcomplex controlled by the shelling order. This is used repeatedly so that every boundary face poset appearing in the induction is shellable, not only the global poset.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a regular CW complex with face poset Q. If Q with an added minimum element 0-hat and maximum element 1-hat is graded, thin, and shellable, then X is homeomorphic to a sphere of dimension rank(Q) - 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites results of Lundell-Weingram, Danaraj-Klee, and Bjorner. Thinness and shellability imply that the order complex of the proper part of the augmented face poset is a shellable pseudomanifold with sphere topology. For regular CW complexes, the barycentric subdivision is precisely the order complex of the face poset, so the topology of X is recovered from the poset. This theorem is the mechanism converting shellable boundary posets into actual topological spheres in the link induction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C be a compact contractible n-dimensional topological manifold with boundary. If its boundary ∂C is homeomorphic to an (n-1)-sphere, then C is homeomorphic to an n-dimensional closed ball.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is stated as a standard consequence of the generalized Poincare conjecture of Smale, Freedman, and Perelman. The proof sketch given in the paper is: Brown's collar theorem gives a collar of ∂C in C. Attach an n-ball along the collared boundary sphere to form a closed topological manifold C'. Van Kampen gives simple connectivity, and Mayer-Vietoris gives that C' is a homology sphere. The generalized Poincare conjecture implies C' is a sphere. Brown's Schoenflies theorem then implies that the original C is a closed ball.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If C is a topological manifold with boundary ∂C, then C is homotopy equivalent to its interior C \ ∂C.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is another consequence of Brown's collar theorem. A collar identifies a neighborhood of ∂C with ∂C × [0,1), and pushing the boundary slightly into the collar gives a deformation of C onto the open manifold obtained by removing the boundary. In the paper this is used to prove a compact manifold closure is contractible once its interior cell is known to be contractible.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.12 (link induction)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (Y, Y_{≥0}, Q) be a shellable totally nonnegative space with a Fomin-Shapiro atlas. For every g ≺ h in Q, the link Lk_{g,h}^{≥0} = disjoint union over g ≺ g' ⪯ h of Lk_{g,g'}^{>0} is a regular CW complex homeomorphic to a closed ball of dimension dim(h) - dim(g) - 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on d = dim(h) - dim(g) - 1. For d = 0, Lemma 3.2 says the only link stratum is a contractible 0-manifold, hence a point. For d > 0, first prove Lk_{g,h}^{≥0} is a topological manifold with boundary equal to the union of lower link strata Lk_{g,g'}^{>0} for g ≺ g' ≺ h. At a point in the top stratum Lk_{g,h}^{>0}, Lemma 3.2 gives ordinary manifold coordinates. At a point in a lower stratum Lk_{g,g'}^{>0}, Lemma 3.6 gives a local model Z_{g',h}^{≥0} × R^{d'}, and Proposition 3.5 plus the induction hypothesis identify Z_{g',h}^{≥0} with a cone on a ball, hence a half-space. Thus the stated boundary formula holds. Lemma 3.2 gives compactness. The interior Lk_{g,h}^{>0} is contractible by Lemma 3.2, and Proposition 3.11 implies the compact manifold is contractible. By induction, the boundary is a regular CW complex whose face poset is the open interval (g,h). The interval [g,h] is graded, thin, and shellable because the global augmented poset is and intervals inherit shellability, so Theorem 3.9 identifies the boundary with a sphere. Theorem 3.10 then identifies the link with a closed ball. The CW axioms follow from the boundary decomposition and induction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proof of Theorem 2.4 (cell-closure induction)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses of Theorem 2.4, for each h in Q the closed positive cell Y_h^{≥0} = union over g ⪯ h of Y_g^{>0} is a compact topological manifold with boundary union over g ≺ h of Y_g^{>0}, and it is a closed ball.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Proceed by induction on dim(h). If dim(h)=0, Y_h^{>0} is a point. For dim(h)>0, let x lie in Y_h^{≥0}. If x is in the open cell Y_h^{>0}, then the positive cell is locally Euclidean. If x lies in a lower cell Y_g^{>0}, then Star_g^{≥0} is open in Y_{≥0}; Proposition 3.5 gives a neighborhood Star_{g,h}^{≥0} homeomorphic to Y_g^{>0} × Cone(Lk_{g,h}^{≥0}); Theorem 3.12 says the link is a closed ball, so this is a half-space local model. Therefore Y_h^{≥0} is a compact manifold with boundary the lower-cell union. Its interior is Y_h^{>0}, which is contractible, and Proposition 3.11 gives contractibility of the closure. The boundary is regular by induction, and its face poset is the interval (0-hat,h) in the augmented poset, hence graded, thin, and shellable. Theorem 3.9 makes the boundary a sphere, and Theorem 3.10 makes Y_h^{≥0} a closed ball.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 8.2 (Bruhat product projection)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the affine flag variety G/B with Iwahori subgroups B and B_- and unipotent radicals U and U_-, fix an affine Weyl group element g. Let C_g = dot{g} B_- B/B, U_1(g) = dot{g} U_- dot{g}^{-1} ∩ U, and U_2(g) = dot{g} U_- dot{g}^{-1} ∩ U_-. Then: (i) for every xB in C_g there are unique y_1 in U_1(g) and y_2 in U_2(g) such that y_1 xB lies in the opposite cell through g and y_2 xB lies in the Schubert cell through g; (ii) the resulting map ν_tilde_g: C_g → opposite cell at g × Schubert cell at g is a biregular isomorphism; and (iii) for h ≤ g ≤ f in the affine Bruhat order, ν_tilde_g restricts to a biregular isomorphism C_g ∩ R_h^f → R_g^f × R_h^g between the corresponding open Richardson pieces.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key algebraic input is that multiplication gives two biregular factorizations U_1(g) × U_2(g) → dot{g} U_- dot{g}^{-1} and U_2(g) × U_1(g) → dot{g} U_- dot{g}^{-1}. Given x in dot{g} U_- dot{g}^{-1}, one can solve y_1 x in U_2(g) and y_2 x in U_1(g) uniquely by comparing the two factorizations. The inverse map is explicit using Gaussian decomposition: for x_1 in U_1(g) and x_2 in U_2(g), find y_1,y_2 with y_1 x_2 = y_2 x_1 by setting y_2 = [x_1 x_2^{-1}]_-^{-1} and y_1 = [x_1 x_2^{-1}]_+ in the big cell decomposition. Regularity follows from regularity of Gaussian factors. Finally, multiplying by U_1(g) does not change the upper Bruhat index and multiplying by U_2(g) does not change the lower Bruhat index in the relevant way; thus if xB lies in the Richardson stratum R_h^f, the two projected factors lie in R_g^f and R_h^g, and the product decomposition gives the stated isomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7.3 (affine Bruhat atlas for projected Richardson strata)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G/P be a partial flag variety, let Q_J be the projected Richardson closure poset, and choose an antidominant coweight λ whose stabilizer in the Weyl group is W_J. Define ψ: Q_J → affine Weyl group by ψ(v,w) = v τ_λ w^{-1}. For u in W^J, let C_u^{(J)} be the standard affine chart in G/P around uP, and let φ_bar_u: C_u^{(J)} → G/B be the map constructed from the two Bruhat projections of the chart coordinates. Then: (1) φ_bar_u is a biregular isomorphism from C_u^{(J)} onto X_{τ_λ^J} ∩ Schubert cell τ_{uλ}, and for every (v,w) ⪰ (u,u) in Q_J it restricts to a biregular isomorphism C_u^{(J)} ∩ Π_{v,w}^o → affine Richardson cell R_{vτ_λw^{-1}}^{τ_{uλ}}; (2) if (u,u) ⪯ (v,w) ⪯ (v',w') in Q_J, then φ_bar_u sends the positive cell Π_{v',w'}^{>0} into the affine big cell C_{vτ_λw^{-1}}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1), write a point in C_u^{(J)} using the group dot{u} U_-^{(J)} dot{u}^{-1}. A finite-dimensional Bruhat projection splits this element into U_1^{(J)} and U_2^{(J)} parts, and φ_bar_u is represented by g_1^{(J)} dot{τ}_{uλ} (g_2^{(J)})^{-1}. Conjugation identities for dot{τ}_λ show this lies in the affine Schubert cell through τ_{uλ} and in the correct opposite Schubert varieties. The image is shown to be a closed subvariety of the affine Schubert cell of the same dimension as X_{τ_λ^J} ∩ Schubert cell τ_{uλ}; irreducibility of Richardson varieties then gives equality. The stratum-level statement follows from checking that projected Richardson strata map into the corresponding affine Richardson strata and using dimension/isomorphism. For (2), one must prove the positive star is contained in the domain C_{ψ(v,w)}. This is the technically hardest part of the paper: Sections 5 and 6 build subtraction-free Marsh-Rietsch parametrizations and prove nonvanishing of certain generalized minors for positive points. Theorem 6.4 shows that the required Gaussian decomposition exists on all positive cells above (v,w), and translating this condition through the formula for φ_bar_u gives membership in C_{vτ_λw^{-1}}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (u,u) ⪯ (v,w) ⪯ (v',w') in Q_J, and let x be an element of G such that xB lies in the totally positive Richardson cell R_{v',w'}^{>0}. The paper defines, on a suitable domain G_{u,v}^{(J)}, a regular map ζ_{u,v}^{(J)}: G_{u,v}^{(J)} → G using Bruhat projection κ, parabolic Gaussian projection π_{dot{u}P_-}, and a Levi factor η. Then x lies in G_{u,v}^{(J)}, and ζ_{u,v}^{(J)}(x) lies in B B_- dot{w}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The theorem is proved via a subtraction-free argument. First, Section 5 develops subtraction-free versions of Marsh-Rietsch parametrizations of positive Richardson cells, proving that certain coordinate changes and Bruhat collision moves preserve subtraction-free rationality. These imply that generalized minors Δ_i^{±} evaluated on relevant expressions are nonnegative subtraction-free functions and are strictly positive when forced by Bruhat order. Section 6 applies this to the special element ζ_{u,v}^{(J)}(x). One first proves the result on the top subtraction-free cell R_{id,w_0}^{sf}, where Theorem 6.14 shows Δ_i^{±}(ζ_{u,v}^{(J)}(x) dot{w}^{-1}) is subtraction-free. Then one specializes boundary variables to zero using a continuity lemma for subtraction-free functions, showing the same expressions remain subtraction-free on R_{v',w'}^{>0}. A contradiction using Zariski density and direct evaluation on R_{v,w}^{>0} rules out identically zero minors, so the relevant bottom-right principal generalized minors are strictly positive. By the Gaussian-decomposition criterion, this is exactly ζ_{u,v}^{(J)}(x) dot{w}^{-1} in B B_-, equivalently ζ_{u,v}^{(J)}(x) in B B_- dot{w}.

proof_techniques.name: Fomin-Shapiro atlas as an abstract local normal form
proof_techniques.description: The paper isolates a general structure sufficient for regularity: around every stratum Y_g there is an open star O_g and a diffeomorphism O_g ≅ (Y_g^o ∩ O_g) × Z_g. The first factor is the stratum direction and the second is a normal slice stratified by all h ⪰ g. The positive part of the whole star must lie inside the chart, and the chart must identify each larger stratum with the product of the base stratum and a corresponding normal stratum. A smooth R_{>0}-action on Z_g supplies radial structure and allows links to be defined as unit-sphere sections.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the matching complex F_w, one could try to define O_g as the set of matchings containing a fixed partial resolution pattern and Z_g as the space of remaining crossing-resolution parameters. If every star of a cell is shown to split as cell × cone(link), the abstract theorem can replace ad hoc contraction arguments.

proof_techniques.name: Radialization by a norm-increasing cone action
proof_techniques.description: Instead of requiring an a priori PL cone, the paper assumes a smooth R_{>0}-action θ on a normal slice Z such that ||θ(t,x)|| strictly increases with t for x ≠ 0. This minimal analytic condition implies that every nonzero orbit meets the unit sphere exactly once and continuously. It yields an explicit homeomorphism Z_{≥0} ≅ Cone(Z_{≥0} ∩ S) by x ↦ (normalized point, radial parameter).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If crossing parameters in a matching cell can be scaled simultaneously or by a height/energy flow so that crossings move monotonically away from a base resolution, the same normalization could turn local deformation spaces into cones over links.

proof_techniques.name: Link induction through smaller links
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 3.12 uses a recursive local model: a point in a lower stratum of the link Lk_{g,h} has a neighborhood homeomorphic to Z_{p,h}^{≥0} × R^d, and Z_{p,h}^{≥0} is a cone on the smaller link Lk_{p,h}. Thus knowing smaller links are balls gives half-space neighborhoods in the current link. This proves the current link is a manifold with boundary; shellability identifies the boundary as a sphere; contractibility plus Poincare gives a ball.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is highly transferable to F_w if links of cells can be described in terms of links of larger cells after resolving one additional local feature. It suggests proving contractibility by a double induction on dimension of cells/links rather than by constructing a global deformation retraction.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary sphere from thin shellable face poset
proof_techniques.description: At each induction step, the boundary of a closed cell or link is already a regular CW complex by induction. Its face poset is an interval in the global closure poset. Because graded thin shellable posets have spherical order complexes, Theorem 3.9 turns this combinatorial boundary into an actual topological sphere. This avoids constructing explicit sphere homeomorphisms.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, a natural strategy is to identify the face poset of a cell closure or link, prove its intervals are thin and shellable, and then invoke the same boundary-sphere mechanism. The crossing-resolution poset may have cubical or oriented-matroid-like shellings worth testing.

proof_techniques.name: Compact contractible manifold with spherical boundary implies ball
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly upgrades a compact contractible manifold with boundary to a closed ball using the generalized Poincare conjecture and Brown's Schoenflies theorem. Contractibility is obtained cheaply: once the space is a manifold with boundary whose interior is a contractible open cell, Brown collaring implies the whole manifold is homotopy equivalent to its interior.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If closures in F_w can be shown to be compact manifolds with boundary and their interiors are open balls or cubes, then one need not explicitly build ball coordinates. It suffices to prove the boundary is a sphere, likely by poset shellability.

proof_techniques.name: Local product projections from Gaussian or Bruhat decomposition
proof_techniques.description: In the affine flag variety, a big cell C_g admits two compatible factorizations of dot{g}U_-dot{g}^{-1} into U_1(g) and U_2(g). Comparing these factorizations gives a canonical isomorphism C_g ≅ opposite cell at g × Schubert cell at g, and this restricts to Richardson strata as R_h^f ∩ C_g ≅ R_g^f × R_h^g. This is the geometric origin of the Fomin-Shapiro product charts.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: One could look for an analogous factorization of crossing matchings into a part before a chosen cell and a residual part after it. The attaching-by-resolution construction already has a product-cube flavor, so a canonical projection to base cell and normal crossing data may be possible.

proof_techniques.name: Embedding into a larger stratified space to import charts
proof_techniques.description: The paper does not construct all local product charts directly inside G/P. Instead, it embeds an affine chart of G/P into an affine flag variety where Bruhat product charts are available. The map sends projected Richardson strata exactly to affine Richardson strata, so product structure in the target pulls back to the original space.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, it may be fruitful to realize matchings or their resolution states inside a better-understood stratified object, such as a cubical complex, arc complex, noncrossing matching lattice, or rewriting/van Kampen diagram complex, where stars have standard product charts.

proof_techniques.name: Positivity or monotonicity to ensure charts contain full positive stars
proof_techniques.description: A key obstacle is that product charts may be defined only on open dense subsets. The paper proves the needed positive stars lie inside those domains using subtraction-free parametrizations: relevant functions are nonnegative on positive cells and cannot vanish identically on required strata, hence are strictly positive where needed. This verifies the Fomin-Shapiro axiom that Star_g^{≥0} is contained in O_g.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In F_w, local charts may fail on degenerate configurations such as tangencies or forbidden cancellations. An analogue would be to find monotone or sign-free invariants of quasi-reduced words/matchings that stay nonzero on all allowable cells in a star.

proof_techniques.name: Regular CW definition includes closure being a union of cells
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses a version of regular CW complex requiring both that every closed cell is a closed ball whose open cell is its interior and that the closure of every cell is a union of cells. The second condition is essential for applying Bjorner's theorem identifying topology from the face poset.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, the attaching maps are defined cubically by crossing resolutions, but one still needs to verify that each cell closure is exactly a union of lower-dimensional matching cells and that attaching maps are embeddings if aiming for regularity rather than only contractibility.

other_useful_info: The paper emphasizes that link induction is a known method, citing GLMS08 and Hersh14a, but that the difficult point is proving closed cells are topological manifolds; the Fomin-Shapiro atlas is designed precisely to overcome this. It also uses Bjorner's philosophy that, for regular CW complexes with suitable shellable face posets, topology is determined combinatorially. Remark 3.13 notes that the same machinery recovers Hersh's regularity theorem for links of the identity in totally nonnegative unipotent cells. Section 10 lists other settings where the authors expect similar regular CW structures, including Kac-Moody totally nonnegative flag varieties, double Bruhat cells, compactified electrical networks, Ising boundary correlation spaces, and amplituhedra/Grassmann polytopes; these are useful pointers for analogous conical-stratified regularity arguments.
why_relevant: Proves regular CW and ball-closure results for totally nonnegative cell decompositions. Its techniques may help show that crossing-resolution cell complexes have contractible cell closures or regular CW structures.

title: An introduction to diagram groups
overall_summary: This survey explains diagram groups through semigroup presentations, Squier square and cube complexes, and planar semigroup diagrams modulo dipole reduction. The central geometric construction is Farley's CAT(0) cubical model: for a semigroup presentation P and baseword w, the graph M(P,w) of reduced diagrams with top word w is median, and its cube-completion is the universal cover of the Squier cube complex S+(P,w), hence contractible. The key technical mechanisms are encoding paths by diagrams, identifying homotopies with square flips and dipole cancellations, and proving medianity either by maximal common prefixes of diagrams or by the CAT(0) cube link criterion. For the original matching complex, the most useful route suggested by the paper is to recast crossing resolutions as a Squier-type cubical state complex or as a universal cover of one; contractibility would then follow from the median/CAT(0) machinery. The sections on picture groups and monoid presentations are also relevant because the original problem involves embedded arcs with crossings and free-group cancellations, which are close to diagrammatic and picture-theoretic models but may require care when empty-word reductions are involved.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definitions 2.1 and 2.2 (Squier square complex and diagram group)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P=<Σ | R> be a semigroup presentation, where Σ is an alphabet and R is a set of relations u=v between nonempty positive words over Σ. The Squier square complex S(P) has one vertex for every positive word over Σ; an edge [a,u=v,b] joining aub to avb whenever u=v is a relation of P and a,b are positive words or empty contexts; and a square [a,u=v,b,p=q,c] filling the 4-cycle obtained by applying the relations u=v and p=q in the two possible orders to the disjoint subwords in aubpc. For a baseword w in Σ+, S(P,w) denotes the connected component containing w, and the diagram group D(P,w) is pi_1(S(P,w),w).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a construction rather than a theorem. The vertices record words in the presented semigroup. Edges record a single use of a defining relation inside a context. Squares are inserted exactly when two relation applications occur in disjoint subwords, so that changing the order of these two independent applications is declared homotopic. Thus loops in S(P,w) are derivations from w back to w modulo the elementary commutations of independent steps, and their fundamental group measures the non-asphericity of the semigroup presentation at the baseword w.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a semigroup presentation. Given two edge paths in the Squier square complex S(P) with the same initial and terminal vertices, encode each path by the planar semigroup diagram obtained by attaching one 2-cell for each relation application. Then the two paths are homotopy equivalent rel endpoints in S(P) if and only if the corresponding diagrams have the same reduction, where reduction means repeatedly deleting dipoles, i.e. adjacent pairs of cells labelled by a relation and its inverse sharing the bottom path of the first and the top path of the second.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In any square complex, homotopy of edge paths with fixed endpoints is generated by two elementary operations: flipping across a square and inserting or deleting a backtrack. In the Squier complex, flipping a square just changes the order in which two disjoint relation cells are attached; the resulting planar diagram is the same. Inserting a backtrack applies a relation and immediately applies its inverse in the same context, which creates a dipole in the associated diagram; deleting a backtrack removes such a dipole. Conversely, if two diagrams have the same reduced form, one passes from each original diagram to the common reduced diagram by deleting dipoles, and the different possible orders of building a fixed diagram are accounted for by square flips. Hence equality after dipole reduction is exactly the homotopy relation on paths.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a semigroup presentation. The map sending an edge path in S(P) to its semigroup diagram induces an isomorphism from the fundamental groupoid of the Squier square complex S(P) to the diagram groupoid D(P), whose elements are diagrams over P modulo dipole reduction and whose partially defined product is vertical concatenation of diagrams with matching bottom and top labels. In particular, for a baseword w, the group D(P,w) can be identified with reduced spherical (w,w)-diagrams over P under concatenation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The map from paths to diagrams respects concatenation because concatenating paths corresponds to gluing the associated diagrams vertically. Proposition 2.4 says precisely that two paths with the same endpoints define the same element of the fundamental groupoid if and only if their diagrams become equal after dipole reduction. The identity morphisms correspond to diagrams with no 2-cells, and inverses correspond to reflecting a diagram across a horizontal axis, so the path groupoid and diagram groupoid are isomorphic.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every semigroup presentation P, let X(P) be the directed 2-complex with one vertex, one directed edge for each generator of P, and two directed 2-cells for each relation u=v, with top and bottom paths labelled u,v and v,u. Then the map that sends a 2-path in X(P) to the semigroup diagram encoding it induces a groupoid isomorphism Π2(X(P)) -> D(P), where Π2(X(P)) is the second fundamental groupoid of directed 2-complexes and D(P) is the diagram groupoid over P. Conversely, every directed 2-complex X can be converted into the directed 2-complex associated to some semigroup presentation P so that Π2(X) and D(P) are isomorphic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A 2-path in a directed 2-complex is a sequence of directed 1-paths where each step pushes a subpath across one directed 2-cell. For X(P), such a step is exactly an application of a relation of P inside a context, so a 2-path is encoded by stacking the corresponding semigroup cells, i.e. by a diagram over P. The elementary homotopies of 2-paths say that pushing across a cell and then across its inverse does nothing, and that two pushes supported on disjoint subpaths commute. These are exactly dipole cancellation and square-flip commutation for semigroup diagrams. Thus quotienting 2-paths by homotopy gives the same groupoid as quotienting diagrams by dipole reduction. The converse direction is obtained by replacing the directed 2-complex data by a semigroup presentation whose letters encode directed edges and whose relations encode the top and bottom boundary paths of 2-cells.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.8 (Squier cube complex)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P=<Σ | R> be a semigroup presentation. The Squier cube complex S+(P) has vertices the positive words over Σ; oriented edges (a,u→v,b) from aub to avb whenever u=v or v=u belongs to R; and, for each n≥1, an n-cube (a1,u1→v1,...,an,un→vn,an+1) whose vertices are the 2^n words a1 w1 a2 w2 ... an wn an+1 with wi equal to ui or vi. For a baseword w, S+(P,w) is the connected component containing w. Its 2-skeleton is the Squier square complex S(P,w), and therefore D(P,w)=pi_1(S+(P,w),w).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The n-cube records n relation applications supported in pairwise disjoint consecutive subwords; each coordinate chooses whether the corresponding subword is kept as ui or replaced by vi. The 1- and 2-dimensional parts recover the edges and commuting squares of the Squier square complex. Adding cubes of dimension at least 3 to a square complex does not change its fundamental group, so the fundamental group of S+(P,w) is the same diagram group D(P,w).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a median graph, and define a hyperplane to be an equivalence class of edges under the transitive closure of the relation that identifies opposite edges of a 4-cycle. Then each hyperplane J separates X into exactly two connected components after removing all edges dual to J, and these two halfspaces are convex. A path in X is a geodesic if and only if it crosses each hyperplane at most once. Consequently, the graph distance d(x,y) between two vertices x,y equals the number of hyperplanes separating x and y.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Median graphs are partial-cube-like objects whose edge equivalence classes behave as wall hyperplanes. Convexity of halfspaces follows from the median property: if two vertices lie on the same side of a hyperplane, the intervals between them cannot be forced to cross the hyperplane. If a path crosses the same hyperplane twice, the segment between the two crossings can be shortened using convexity of the halfspaces, so a geodesic crosses each hyperplane at most once. Conversely, any path from x to y must cross every hyperplane separating x from y, so a path crossing each such hyperplane exactly once has minimal possible length. This gives the distance formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.5 and Proposition 4.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a graph X, its cube-completion X^□ is the cube complex obtained by filling every subgraph isomorphic to the 1-skeleton of a cube with the corresponding cube. A graph X is median if and only if X^□ is simply connected and nonpositively curved, where nonpositive curvature for a cube complex means that the link of every vertex is a flag simplicial complex. Moreover, cube-completions of median graphs are contractible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence between median graphs and simply connected nonpositively curved cube completions is due to Gerasimov, Chepoi, and Roller. In one direction, the median property forces compatible cubes to fill all finite collections of pairwise transverse hyperplanes, and the local compatibility condition is exactly the flag condition on vertex links; simple connectedness follows from filling all square and higher cubical relations. In the other direction, the 1-skeleton of a CAT(0) cube complex has unique median points obtained by majority vote over halfspaces. Once X^□ is simply connected and nonpositively curved, the CAT(0) cube complex criterion gives a CAT(0) metric, and CAT(0) spaces are contractible.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a semigroup presentation, w a baseword, and M(P,w) the graph whose vertices are diagrams over P with top label w modulo dipole reduction, with edges given by right-concatenation by an atomic diagram, i.e. a diagram with one 2-cell. Let Φ and Ψ be vertices of M(P,w). A path of the form Φ, Φ∘Δ1, Φ∘Δ1∘Δ2, ..., Φ∘Δ1∘...∘Δn=Ψ, where each Δi is atomic, is a geodesic if and only if the concatenated diagram Δ1∘...∘Δn is reduced. Consequently, the distance between Φ and Ψ in M(P,w) equals #(Φ^{-1}Ψ), the number of 2-cells in the reduced diagram representing Φ^{-1}Ψ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Any path from Φ to Ψ labelled by a product Γ of atomic diagrams represents, after reducing dipoles, the diagram Φ^{-1}Ψ. Therefore the length of the path is at least the number of cells in the reduced representative of Φ^{-1}Ψ. If Δ1∘...∘Δn is already reduced, then it has exactly n cells and equals the reduced representative of Φ^{-1}Ψ, so the path has minimal length. If the concatenation contains a dipole, deleting it produces a shorter atomic factorization representing the same endpoint, so the original path cannot be geodesic.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a semigroup presentation and w a positive baseword. Let M(P,w) be the connected component containing top word w in the graph M(P) whose vertices are diagrams over P modulo dipole reduction and whose edges connect two diagrams when one is obtained from the other by right-concatenating an atomic diagram. Then M(P,w) is a median graph, locally of finite cubical dimension. The diagram group D(P,w) acts freely on M(P,w) by left multiplication. The action is cocompact if and only if the set [w]_P of positive words equal to w modulo P is finite.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: One proof of medianity uses prefixes of diagrams. Given three vertices Δ0, Δ1, Δ2, left-multiply by Δ0^{-1} so that Δ0 is the trivial diagram. Let Δ be the largest common prefix of the reduced diagrams Δ1 and Δ2. Because Δ is a prefix of each, Lemma 4.8 puts Δ on geodesics from the trivial diagram to Δ1 and to Δ2. Writing Δ1=Δ∘Γ1 and Δ2=Δ∘Γ2, maximality of Δ implies Γ1∘Γ2^{-1} is reduced, so Δ also lies on a geodesic between Δ1 and Δ2. Thus Δ is a median point. If Ξ is any other median point, then Ξ must be a common prefix of Δ1 and Δ2 and hence a prefix of Δ; the condition that Ξ lies on a geodesic between Δ1 and Δ2 forces the complementary prefix Δ beyond Ξ to have no cells, so Ξ=Δ. A second proof identifies cubes in the cube-completion M^□(P,w): from a diagram Δ and a thin diagram Ψ, meaning a sum of atomic diagrams, the prefixes of Ψ span a cube. The link at a vertex with bottom word m has vertices corresponding to subwords of m that are sides of relations, and simplices correspond to pairwise disjoint such subwords; this link is flag. Simple connectedness is shown by taking any finite set of vertices, closing it under prefixes, and repeatedly removing a vertex of maximal cell number by retracting the cube determined by the maximal thin suffix of that diagram. Thus M^□(P,w) is CAT(0), and M(P,w) is median. The action of D(P,w) by left multiplication is free by cancellation in the diagram groupoid. Vertex orbits are determined by the bottom word, so there are finitely many orbits exactly when [w]_P is finite.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a semigroup presentation. The map that sends a semigroup diagram over P to the label of its bottom path induces a universal covering map M^□(P) -> S+(P), where M^□(P) is the cube-completion of the diagram graph M(P) and S+(P) is the Squier cube complex.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A vertex of M^□(P) is a reduced diagram, and its image is the positive word labelling its bottom path. Right-concatenating an atomic diagram changes the bottom word by one relation application, so edges map to edges of S+(P); similarly, a thin diagram made of independent atomic cells maps to the corresponding cube of S+(P). Around any vertex, possible outgoing atomic cells are exactly possible relation applications in the bottom word, so the induced map on links is an isomorphism. Hence the map is a covering map. The domain M^□(P) is simply connected because its components are CAT(0) cube complexes by Theorem 4.9, so the covering is universal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a semigroup presentation, w a baseword, and X(P) the directed 2-complex associated to P with distinguished 1-path labelled w. Let tilde X(P) be its universal cover as a rooted directed 2-complex, and let Sq(tilde X(P)) be the cube complex whose vertices are directed 1-paths in tilde X(P), with cubes generated by collections of independent elementary transformations. Then M^□(P,w) is naturally isomorphic to Sq(tilde X(P)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The universal rooted 2-tree tilde X(P) can be described by taking all reduced diagrams over P with top label w, gluing their top paths together, and identifying common prefixes whenever two diagrams share one. Every directed 1-path α in this rooted 2-tree, together with the distinguished root path, bounds a unique reduced diagram Ω(α). Elementary transformations of 1-paths correspond to adding or deleting one bottom cell of the corresponding diagram, and independent transformations correspond to thin suffixes, hence to cubes. Therefore α -> Ω(α) gives an isomorphism between the cube complex Sq(tilde X(P)) and the cube-completion M^□(P,w).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.16 (Bestvina-Brady Morse criterion as stated in the survey)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be an n-connected polyhedral complex and f:X -> R a Morse function, meaning f is affine on each polyhedron, nonconstant on every positive-dimensional cell, and f(X^0) is discrete in R. Suppose there is h0 in R such that the descending link of every vertex of height at least h0 is n-connected. Then the sublevel set f^{-1}((−∞,h0]) is n-connected.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The idea is to push maps into X down through high vertices. For n=1, take a loop in the sublevel and a filling disc in X, which exists because X is simply connected. If the disc has a vertex x of maximal height above h0, then a small loop around x in the disc lies in the descending link of x. Since that descending link is simply connected, replace the portion of the disc near x by a disc lying in the descending link, thereby lowering the maximum height. Iterating eliminates all vertices above h0 and produces a filling in the sublevel. Higher connectivity is handled by the same surgery on maps of balls, using n-connectivity of the descending links to fill the links of high vertices.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.25 and Corollary 4.26
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P=<Σ | R> be a semigroup presentation and W a baseword. A hyperplane in S+(P,W) may be represented by data [a,u→v,b], where aub and avb are words in the component of W and u=v is a relation of P. The Squier cube complex S+(P,W) is conspicial, meaning all hyperplanes are two-sided and there are no self-intersections, self-osculations, or inter-osculations, if and only if both of the following conditions hold: first, there are no words a,b,p with W=ab, a=ap, and b=pb modulo P and [p]_P not equal to {p}; second, there are no words a,u,v,t,b,p,q,ξ with W=auvtb, au=au(vξ), and tb=(ξv)tb modulo P, and with uv=p and vt=q relations in R. In particular, if [W]_P is finite, then S+(P,W) is conspicial.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses explicit combinatorial descriptions of hyperplanes in Squier cube complexes. Two oriented hyperplanes [a,u→v,b] and [c,p→q,d] coincide precisely when a=c and b=d modulo P and the relation data match. Two hyperplanes are transverse precisely when their representatives can be placed in disjoint contexts, e.g. c=auy and b=ypd modulo P, or symmetrically. The possible pathologies of specialness translate into the two listed word patterns: the first pattern detects one-sidedness or self-osculation arising from a nontrivial word p that can be shifted from the right context to the left context around W; the second detects inter-osculation, where two relation applications can be both transverse and tangent because of an overlap controlled by ξ. If [W]_P is finite, failure of the first condition produces infinitely many words ap^n b equal to W, and failure of the second produces infinitely many words au(vξ)^m v(ξv)^n tb equal to W. Thus finiteness of [W]_P rules out both obstructions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For annular and symmetric diagram groups over a semigroup presentation P and a baseword w, Farley constructs median graphs Ma(P,w) and Ms(P,w), respectively, locally of finite cubical dimension, on which the annular diagram group Da(P,w) and the symmetric diagram group Ds(P,w) act properly.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The planar diagram formalism can be dualized into pictures made of wires and transistors. For annular pictures, the pictures live on annuli; for symmetric pictures, wires may be permuted. One forms a graph whose vertices are reduced pictures modulo the appropriate equivalence and whose edges add a single transistor at the bottom, analogously to the graph M(P,w) for planar diagrams. The same cubical construction, with cubes corresponding to independent transistor additions, yields a median graph. Unlike planar diagram groups, annular and symmetric groups may have finite vertex stabilizers coming from permutations of finitely many wires, but these stabilizers are controlled permutation subgroups, giving proper actions.

proof_techniques.name: Encode paths by diagrams and homotopies by dipoles
proof_techniques.description: In the Squier complex of a semigroup presentation, an edge path is not treated merely as a sequence of words; it is thickened into a planar diagram whose cells record the successive relation applications. The two elementary homotopies of edge paths have direct diagrammatic meanings: square flips commute independent cells without changing the diagram, while backtrack insertion or deletion adds or removes a dipole. Thus the homotopy class of a path is captured by the reduced diagram obtained after cancelling all dipoles.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a matching and its crossing resolutions can be encoded as a derivation or diagram, then contractibility questions may reduce to uniqueness of a reduced diagram or to showing that all homotopies are generated by local square flips and dipole-like cancellations.

proof_techniques.name: Squier cubulation from independent local moves
proof_techniques.description: The Squier cube complex S+(P) is built by adding an n-cube whenever n relation applications are supported on disjoint subwords and hence can be performed independently. Each coordinate of the cube records one binary choice, and the facets correspond to resolving or omitting one local move. This is a general cubical state-complex construction: local commuting operations become cubes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The cells of F_w are explicitly products of intervals indexed by crossings, and facets resolve one crossing. This strongly suggests trying to realize F_w as a Squier-type cubical state complex where crossings are independent local moves, or at least to prove CAT(0)-type properties using the same independence-cube principle.

proof_techniques.name: Median point as maximal common prefix
proof_techniques.description: For the diagram graph M(P,w), medianity can be proved without invoking global CAT(0) theory. After translating one of three vertices to the trivial diagram, take the largest common prefix of the other two reduced diagrams. This prefix lies on geodesics between all three vertices, and maximality plus dipole-reduced geodesic criteria force uniqueness. The argument relies on a prefix partial order and a normal form by reduced diagrams.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A parallel strategy for F_w would be to define a prefix or common-initial-cancellation structure on matchings or on diagrams dual to matchings. If triples of objects have unique maximal common prefixes that lie on all pairwise geodesics in the 1-skeleton, then the cube-completion is median and hence contractible.

proof_techniques.name: CAT(0) cube criterion via flag links and simple connectedness
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the equivalence between median graphs and simply connected nonpositively curved cube complexes. Nonpositive curvature is checked locally by showing every vertex link is flag; for Squier-type complexes, the link at a word has vertices corresponding to relation-applicable subwords and simplices corresponding to pairwise disjoint choices, making flagness transparent. Simple connectedness can be proved by prefix-closure and cubical retractions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, one can try to verify directly that the cubical attaching maps define a genuine cube complex, compute vertex links in terms of simultaneously resolvable crossings, prove those links are flag, and then establish simple connectedness. This would imply contractibility by CAT(0).

proof_techniques.name: Universal cover by reduced diagrams
proof_techniques.description: The Squier cube complex S+(P,w) is generally a quotient object whose fundamental group is the diagram group. Its universal cover is the cube-completion M^□(P,w) of the graph of reduced diagrams with fixed top word. The covering map sends a diagram to its bottom word and is locally an isomorphism because atomic extensions of a diagram correspond exactly to relation applications in the bottom word.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If F_w is not itself obviously CAT(0), it may be identifiable as a universal cover of a smaller quotient complex of words, pairings, or cancellation states. Showing that its cells are the lifted independent-move cubes would give contractibility from the universal-cover CAT(0) model.

proof_techniques.name: Hyperplane and halfspace analysis
proof_techniques.description: Median graphs and CAT(0) cube complexes can be analyzed through hyperplanes: hyperplanes separate into convex halfspaces, geodesics cross each hyperplane at most once, and distance is the number of separating hyperplanes. For Squier complexes, hyperplanes have explicit representatives [a,u→v,b], and crossing or pathological behavior can be translated into word equations in the semigroup.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The crossings of intervals in F_w may themselves define natural hyperplanes corresponding to choosing one of the two local resolutions. Proving that these hyperplanes separate and that paths cross them in a controlled way could give a median or CAT(0) proof of contractibility.

proof_techniques.name: Combinatorial Morse theory on cubical complexes
proof_techniques.description: The survey recalls the Bestvina-Brady method: choose a Morse height function on a cube complex, analyze descending links of high vertices, and conclude that sufficiently low sublevel sets inherit connectivity from the ambient complex. In Farley's use for diagram groups, heights often count a word length or diagram complexity, and descending links encode allowable simplifying moves.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A possible approach to F_w is to use the number of crossings, nesting complexity, or another cancellation complexity as a height function. If every high-complexity matching has contractible or highly connected descending link of simplifications, then F_w may be collapsed or proved contractible by Morse theory.

proof_techniques.name: Picture-group viewpoint for diagrams with wires
proof_techniques.description: The paper explains that planar semigroup diagrams have a dual formulation as pictures made of labelled wires and transistors, and that annular or symmetric variants allow wires to wrap or permute. Farley constructs CAT(0)/median complexes for these picture groups by taking vertices to be reduced pictures and edges to be additions of one transistor.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The objects in F_w are embedded intervals in the upper half-plane with transverse crossings, so a dual picture or wire-diagram model may be natural. The picture-group framework suggests looking for a reduced-picture graph and treating crossing resolutions as local cubical operations.

other_useful_info: The main diagram-group theory in the paper uses semigroup presentations, whose relation sides are nonempty positive words. Section 5.3 notes that allowing monoid relations u=1 can change the behavior substantially: reduced pictures may not be unique, equivalence modulo dipole reduction can be undecidable, and the Squier cube complex over a monoid presentation may fail to be nonpositively curved; the paper gives the example <x | x=1>, where the link at the empty word has a loop. This matters for attempts to encode free-group cancellation directly by relations like ℓℓ^{-1}=1. Also, for applying the CAT(0) results one should distinguish the Squier cube complex S+(P,w), whose fundamental group is D(P,w), from its universal cover M^□(P,w), which is the contractible CAT(0) cube complex. Relevant cited sources inside the survey include Farley [Far03] for CAT(0) properties of diagram groups, Guba-Sapir [GS97] for foundational diagram-group theory, Genevois [Gen18] for hyperplanes of Squier cube complexes, and Farley [Far05a] for picture-group actions on CAT(0) cubical complexes.
why_relevant: Surveys Squier complexes, diagram groups, CAT(0) cube complexes, and hyperplanes. It is useful for identifying whether F_w is a Squier-type cube complex or can be studied by diagram-group methods.

title: Generalized Recursive Atom Ordering and Equivalence to CL-Shellability
overall_summary: The paper introduces generalized recursive atom orderings, a relaxation of Björner-Wachs recursive atom orderings for finite bounded posets, and proves that a finite bounded poset admits a generalized recursive atom ordering if and only if it admits an ordinary recursive atom ordering, equivalently if and only if it is CL-shellable. The main technical tool is an atom reordering process that transforms any generalized recursive atom ordering into a recursive atom ordering by locally moving atoms that lie above earlier atoms while preserving the crucial recursive conditions. The paper also clarifies the relationship among CL-shellability, CC-shellability, EC-shellability, TCL-shellability, and new conditions called self-consistency and the unique earliest property.
