A particularly relevant application is that the uncrossing posets of complete matchings on a circle, previously known to be dual EC-shellable, are actually dual CL-shellable because their cover labels determine the uncrossing uniquely. For the original matching/CW-complex problem, the most useful content is the package of shellability criteria for finite posets built from local resolving or uncrossing moves, especially the strategy of proving a self-consistent CC/EC-labeling or a generalized recursive atom/coatom ordering and then upgrading it to CL-shellability.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.15 (Björner-Wachs, quoted)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a finite bounded poset with least element ˆ0 and greatest element ˆ1. A recursive atom ordering of P is an ordering a1, ..., at of the atoms of P such that either P has length 1, or recursively each interval [aj, ˆ1] admits a recursive atom ordering whose first atoms, for j > 1, are exactly those lying above some earlier atom ak of P with k < j, and such that whenever i < j and y is an element with ai < y and aj < y, there exist k < j and z with aj ⋖ z and ak < z ≤ y. A CL-labeling is a chain-edge labeling of P such that in every rooted interval [x, y]r there is a unique ascending maximal chain and its label sequence is lexicographically first among all maximal chains of [x, y]r. Then P admits a recursive atom ordering if and only if P is CL-shellable, i.e. admits a CL-labeling.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The theorem is quoted from Björner-Wachs, with the paper recalling the main idea of the direction from a recursive atom ordering to a CL-labeling. Given a recursive atom ordering a1, ..., at of P, label the covers ˆ0 ⋖ ai by i. Inductively, each interval [ai, ˆ1] has its own recursive atom ordering and hence labels on covers above ai. The key adjustment is to shift the label values above ai so that atoms of [ai, ˆ1] lying above some earlier atom of P receive labels that create a descent from ˆ0 ⋖ ai, while the remaining atoms receive labels creating ascents, all while preserving the relative order of the labels coming from the recursive atom ordering of [ai, ˆ1]. This uses the sets Fr(u) of atoms above earlier atoms and Gr(u) of the remaining atoms. The recursive atom ordering condition ensures that in every rooted interval the unique all-ascent chain is lexicographically first. The converse construction from a CL-labeling to a recursive atom ordering is part of the cited Björner-Wachs theorem rather than reproved in this paper.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.20
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a finite bounded poset equipped with a recursive atom ordering. Then the recursive atom ordering gives a CC-labeling of P. Consequently every finite bounded CL-shellable poset is CC-shellable. Here a CC-labeling is a chain-edge labeling such that every rooted interval has a unique saturated chain consisting entirely of topological ascents, and saturated chains in each rooted interval have distinct label sequences with no label sequence a prefix of another.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Given a recursive atom ordering Λ, label each cover u ⋖ a'i in a rooted interval [u, ˆ1]r by the position i of the atom a'i in the recursive atom ordering of [u, ˆ1]r. Distinct atom positions force distinct label sequences for saturated chains, and no chain label sequence can be a prefix of another in a bounded interval. To prove the unique topologically ascending chain property, argue by induction on the length of the longest chain in a rooted interval [u, v]r. Let c = u ⋖ u1 ⋖ ... ⋖ v be the lexicographically first maximal chain; by definition it is topologically ascending. Suppose c' = u ⋖ u'1 ⋖ u'2 ⋖ ... ⋖ v is another topologically ascending chain. Since c is lexicographically first, u1 comes earlier than u'1 in the recursive atom ordering of [u, ˆ1]r. The recursive atom ordering axiom then gives an atom a earlier than u'1 and an element z with u'1 ⋖ z, a < z ≤ v. If z = u'2, the first two covers of c' cannot form a topological ascent, since there is a lexicographically earlier route through a. If z ≠ u'2, then the tail from u'1 to v is not lexicographically first in [u'1, v]r∪u'1, so by induction it is not topologically ascending. Both cases contradict the assumption on c', proving uniqueness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a finite bounded poset with a generalized recursive atom ordering Γ. In this paper, a generalized recursive atom ordering is a chain-atom ordering satisfying the recursive conditions that each upper rooted interval [a, ˆ1] has a generalized recursive atom ordering, each interval [a, w] either has first atom above an earlier atom or has no atom above an earlier atom, and whenever two atoms ai, aj with ai earlier than aj both lie below y, there is an earlier atom ak and z ≤ y with aj ⋖ z and ak < z. Then for every u < v in P and every root r from ˆ0 to u, the restriction of Γ to the rooted interval [u, v]r is again a generalized recursive atom ordering of [u, v]r.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on the length of the longest saturated chain from u to ˆ1. The base cases of maximum length 1 or 2 are immediate because every chain-atom ordering on such a short bounded poset satisfies the generalized recursive conditions. For the inductive step, if ai is an atom of [u, v]r, the interval [ai, v]r∪ai has smaller maximum length upward, so the induced ordering on it is a generalized recursive atom ordering by induction. This gives condition (i)(a). Conditions (i)(b) and (ii) of the generalized recursive atom ordering definition are inherited by restriction from [u, ˆ1]r to [u, v]r, because they quantify only over elements and atoms lying in the smaller interval.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a finite bounded poset, let Λ be a generalized recursive atom ordering of P, and let a1, ..., at be the ordering of the atoms of P induced by Λ. For each atom aj of P and each v with aj < v, the restriction Λ|[aj, v] is a generalized recursive atom ordering of [aj, v] with the following property: either the first atom of [aj, v] is greater than some atom ai of P with i < j, or no atom of [aj, v] is greater than any atom ai of P with i < j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 3.8 first gives that Λ restricts to a generalized recursive atom ordering on [aj, v]. Let x be the first atom of [aj, v]. Suppose some atom y of [aj, v] lies above an earlier atom ai of P with i < j. If y is not x, condition (ii) of a generalized recursive atom ordering, applied inside [aj, v] to x and y, gives an atom y1 of [aj, v] coming before y and an element w1 with y ⋖ w1 ≤ v and y1 < w1. By condition (i)(b), the first atom in [aj, w1] must itself be above some earlier atom of P; replace y1 by this first atom. If y1 is still not x, repeat. This produces a strictly earlier sequence y, y1, y2, ... of atoms of [aj, v], each above an earlier atom of P. Finiteness forces termination, and the only possible terminal atom is x. Thus x is also above an earlier atom.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a chain-atom ordering of a finite bounded poset P. For a rooted interval [u, v]r, define FΓr(u, v) to be the set of atoms a of [u, v]r that lie above some atom a' of the immediately lower rooted interval [u−, v]r− which comes earlier than u in Γ, where u− is the element of the root r covered by u and r− is r with u deleted; define GΓr(u, v) as the complementary set of atoms of [u, v]r. Suppose ai and ai+1 are consecutive atoms of [u, ˆ1]r in Γ and that for every rooted interval [u, w]r containing both ai and ai+1, neither ai nor ai+1 is the first atom of [u, w]r. Let Λ be obtained from Γ by swapping ai and ai+1 in [u, ˆ1]r and changing nothing else. Then for every rooted interval [u', v']r' in P, FΓr'(u', v') = FΛr'(u', v') and GΓr'(u', v') = GΛr'(u', v').
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Only rooted intervals whose definition of F uses the atom order in [u, ˆ1]r can be affected, namely those with u ⋖ u' and root r ∪ {u'}. If u' is neither ai nor ai+1, then because ai and ai+1 are consecutive, they are either both before u' or both after u' in every relevant restricted interval, so swapping them does not change which atoms are earlier than u'. If u' equals ai or ai+1, one proves set containment in one direction and then repeats the same argument after swapping back. For example, if an atom a' belongs to F before the swap, choose the earliest atom of the interval [u, a']r witnessing this. The hypothesis that neither ai nor ai+1 can be the first atom of any interval containing both ensures this earliest atom is not changed by the swap. Hence a' remains in F. Symmetry gives equality, and equality of G follows by complementarity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a generalized recursive atom ordering of a finite bounded poset P. Suppose ai and ai+1 are consecutive atoms in the induced ordering on a rooted interval [u, ˆ1]r, and suppose that in every rooted interval [u, w]r containing both ai and ai+1, neither is the earliest atom. Let Λ be obtained from Γ by swapping ai and ai+1 in [u, ˆ1]r and otherwise leaving Γ unchanged. Then Λ satisfies condition (i)(b) in the definition of a generalized recursive atom ordering: for every atom aj of P and every interval [aj, v] with aj ⋖ x ⋖ v for some x, either the first atom of [aj, v] is above an earlier atom of P than aj, or no atom of [aj, v] is above an earlier atom of P than aj.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof splits according to the position of u. If u is neither ˆ0 nor an atom of P, the swap cannot affect the order of atoms of P or of any [aj, v] with aj an atom of P, so condition (i)(b) is unchanged. If u is an atom of P, intervals [aj, v] with aj ≠ u are unaffected. For intervals [u, v], if at most one of ai, ai+1 lies in [u, v], the restriction is unchanged; if both lie in [u, v], the first atom is unaffected by the hypothesis, and the existence or nonexistence of atoms above earlier atoms is unaffected. If u = ˆ0, one checks intervals [aj, v] according to whether aj is one of the swapped atoms and whether both swapped atoms lie in [ˆ0, v]. When both lie in [ˆ0, v] and aj is one of them, the hypothesis gives an atom earlier than both; using condition (ii) and then condition (i)(b) for Γ shows the first atom above aj remains above an earlier atom after the swap. The other subcases are unchanged because the relevant relative order of earlier atoms is unchanged.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a generalized recursive atom ordering of a finite bounded poset P. Suppose ai and ai+1 are consecutive atoms in the induced ordering on [u, ˆ1]r and that every rooted interval [u, w]r containing both ai and ai+1 has neither ai nor ai+1 as its earliest atom. Let Λ be obtained from Γ by swapping ai and ai+1 in [u, ˆ1]r. Then Λ satisfies condition (ii) in the definition of a generalized recursive atom ordering: whenever two atoms al, am of P satisfy al earlier than am and al < y, am < y, there exist an atom ak earlier than am and an element z ≤ y with am ⋖ z and ak < z.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If u ≠ ˆ0, the swap does not change the ordering of the atoms of P, so condition (ii) is inherited from Γ. If u = ˆ0, fix an interval [ˆ0, y]. When both swapped atoms lie in [ˆ0, y], the first atom of [ˆ0, y] is unchanged because neither swapped atom is allowed to be first. Given al earlier than am after the swap, am is not first before the swap either. Condition (ii) for Γ gives ak and z with am ⋖ z and ak < z. The only possible problem is that ak = ai and am = ai+1, so after swapping ak might no longer be earlier than am. But then both ai and ai+1 lie in [ˆ0, z], and the hypothesis says neither is first there, so some atom earlier than both is also below z and can replace ak. If [ˆ0, y] does not contain both swapped atoms, the restricted atom order is unchanged.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a generalized recursive atom ordering of a finite bounded poset P. Suppose ai and ai+1 are consecutive atoms in the induced generalized recursive atom ordering of a rooted interval [u, ˆ1]r, and suppose that for every rooted interval [u, w]r containing both ai and ai+1, neither ai nor ai+1 is the earliest atom. If Λ is obtained from Γ by swapping ai and ai+1 in [u, ˆ1]r and leaving all other atom orders unchanged, then Λ is again a generalized recursive atom ordering of P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on the maximum length of a saturated chain in P. To verify recursive condition (i)(a), restrict to each interval [a, ˆ1] above an atom a of P. If u lies in [a, ˆ1], the restriction of Λ is obtained from the generalized recursive atom ordering Γ|[a, ˆ1] by the same kind of safe swap, so the induction hypothesis applies. If u does not lie in [a, ˆ1], the restriction is unchanged. Lemma 3.11 verifies condition (i)(b) for Λ, and Lemma 3.12 verifies condition (ii). Thus all defining conditions of a generalized recursive atom ordering survive the swap.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Algorithm 4.3 and Proposition 4.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Given a finite bounded poset P with a chain-atom ordering Λ, the atom reordering process constructs a new chain-atom ordering Λre as follows. Choose a linear extension u0, u1, ..., un of P. Starting at ˆ0 and then proceeding through the linear extension, for every rooted interval [ui, ˆ1]r, compute the set F of atoms of [ui, ˆ1]r that lie above atoms earlier than ui in the already partially reordered order on the immediately lower rooted interval, and the complementary set G. Define the new order on atoms of [ui, ˆ1]r by listing all atoms of F in their original relative order from Λ, followed by all atoms of G in their original relative order from Λ. Proposition 4.5 states that for every v in P, restricting Λre to [ˆ0, v] gives the same chain-atom ordering as first restricting Λ to [ˆ0, v] and then applying the atom reordering process inside [ˆ0, v].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key point is that the sets F and G are local to intervals. For a root r = ˆ0 ⋖ t1 ⋖ ... ⋖ u and an upper bound v, one proves by induction on the root length that FΛre_r(u, ˆ1) ∩ [u, v] equals FΛre_r(u, v), and similarly for G. If an atom a of [u, v] lies in F in P, it lies above an earlier atom a' of the lower rooted interval; since a ≤ v, the witness a' also lies in [ˆ0, v], and the induction hypothesis says it remains earlier in the reordered restriction. Conversely, if no such witness exists in P, none exists in the subinterval. Because F and G partition the atoms, the two reorderings agree on every rooted interval of [ˆ0, v].

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a finite bounded poset with a generalized recursive atom ordering. Let t ⋖ u < v be elements of P and let r be a root for [t, v]. Then either the first atom of the rooted interval [u, v]r∪u is above an atom of [t, v]r that comes earlier than u, or else u is the first atom of [t, v]r.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 3.9, applied in the rooted interval [t, v]r, either the first atom of [u, v]r∪u is above an earlier atom than u, or no atom of [u, v]r∪u is above an earlier atom than u. In the second case, suppose for contradiction that u is not first in [t, v]r, and let u' be the first atom. Since u and u' both lie below v, condition (ii) of the generalized recursive atom ordering gives an atom u'' earlier than u and an element x' with u ⋖ x' ≤ v and u'' < x'. This says an atom of [u, v]r∪u is above an earlier atom than u, contradicting the second case.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a finite bounded poset with a generalized recursive atom ordering Λ. For any u < v in P and any root r for [u, v], the first atom of [u, v]r with respect to Λ is also the first atom of [u, v]r with respect to the atom reordering Λre obtained by Algorithm 4.3.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use Proposition 4.5 to reduce to the interval [ˆ0, v], and argue by induction on the length of the root r = ˆ0 ⋖ t1 ⋖ ... ⋖ tn ⋖ u. Let x be the first atom of [u, v]r in Λ. If x is in the F-set at u, then when the reordering process reaches [u, v]r it is placed in the initial F-block and remains first because relative order inside F is preserved. If x is not in F, Proposition 4.6 implies that u is first in the immediately lower interval [tn, v]r−, and by induction u remains first after reordering; hence no atom of [u, v]r is in F, so the whole atom order of [u, v]r is unchanged. In the general inductive proof, if x were moved behind another atom, then x would have to be in G after partial reordering. If x was above an earlier atom before reordering, take the first atom u1 of [tn, x]r−; induction says u1 remains before u, contradicting x ∈ G. If no atom of [u, v]r was above an earlier atom, Proposition 4.6 and induction again force all atoms to stay in G, so x cannot move.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Σ be a chain-atom ordering on a finite bounded poset P, and fix a rooted interval [u, ˆ1]r. Let Σre be the atom reordering of Σ. Then Σre restricted to [u, ˆ1]r can be obtained by first applying to the atoms of [u, ˆ1]r a permutation π that performs the reordering step at u, and then applying the atom reordering process inside [u, ˆ1]r. If Σ is a generalized recursive atom ordering, then π can be written as a product of adjacent transpositions such that each adjacent transposition preserves the first atom of every rooted interval [u, v]r. Consequently, if Σ is a generalized recursive atom ordering, applying π only to the atoms of [u, ˆ1]r and changing nothing else still yields a generalized recursive atom ordering of P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For part (a), observe that the first time the global reordering process affects [u, ˆ1]r, it only permutes the atoms of [u, ˆ1]r by moving F before G while preserving relative order within each block; all later changes occur entirely inside intervals above those atoms. Induction on the distance from u shows that this is the same as first applying that permutation π and then reordering internally. For part (b), Lemma 4.7 says π preserves the first atom of every [u, v]r when Σ is a generalized recursive atom ordering. Choose a reduced expression for π as adjacent transpositions. The paper uses the standard weak-order fact for permutations that if π(l) < π(m), then along a reduced expression applied from right to left, l remains before m at every intermediate stage. Applying this to the first atom a of each [u, v]r and any other atom a' shows that every adjacent transposition in the reduced expression preserves the first atom of every [u, v]r. Part (c) then follows by applying Theorem 3.13 successively to these safe adjacent transpositions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If Λ is a generalized recursive atom ordering of a finite bounded poset P, then its atom reordering Λre, constructed by Algorithm 4.3, is again a generalized recursive atom ordering of P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses an enriched view of Algorithm 4.3 in which the partially reordered object is always a full chain-atom ordering, initialized as Λ and gradually modified until it becomes Λre. For each rooted interval [u, ˆ1]r, Lemma 4.8 expresses the relevant reordering of atoms as a product of adjacent transpositions, each of which preserves the first atom in every rooted interval [u, v]r. Theorem 3.13 says that each such adjacent transposition transforms a generalized recursive atom ordering into another generalized recursive atom ordering. Since the entire atom reordering process is a finite sequence of these safe swaps, the final chain-atom ordering Λre is a generalized recursive atom ordering.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a finite bounded poset with a generalized recursive atom ordering Λ. For every rooted interval [u, v]r, the atom reordering process preserves the F- and G-sets: FΛ_r(u, v) = FΛre_r(u, v) and GΛ_r(u, v) = GΛre_r(u, v), where F consists of atoms of [u, v]r lying above atoms earlier than u in the immediately lower rooted interval and G is its complement.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 4.9, the atom reordering process can be decomposed into a sequence of safe adjacent swaps of the type treated in Lemma 3.10. Lemma 3.10 says each such swap preserves all F- and G-sets. Therefore the entire finite sequence preserves them.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A finite bounded poset P admits a generalized recursive atom ordering if and only if it admits a recursive atom ordering. Moreover, every recursive atom ordering is a generalized recursive atom ordering, and every generalized recursive atom ordering can be transformed into a recursive atom ordering by the atom reordering process of Algorithm 4.3.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The easy direction is Lemma 4.1: a recursive atom ordering satisfies the generalized recursive conditions because recursive atom orderings already put all atoms lying above earlier atoms first, and the remaining generalized condition is the same as the second recursive atom ordering axiom. For the converse, let Λ be a generalized recursive atom ordering and use induction on the length of the longest saturated chain in P. For each atom aj of P, Lemma 4.8 says Λre restricted to [aj, ˆ1] is the atom reordering of a generalized recursive atom ordering on [aj, ˆ1]; by induction this restriction is a recursive atom ordering, giving recursive atom ordering condition (i)(a). The definition of the atom reordering process places every atom of [aj, ˆ1] above an earlier atom of P before every atom not above an earlier atom, giving condition (i)(b). Finally Lemma 4.9 says Λre remains a generalized recursive atom ordering, and condition (ii) for generalized recursive atom orderings is exactly the condition (ii) required of recursive atom orderings. Hence Λre is a recursive atom ordering.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let λ be a CC-labeling of a finite bounded poset P. Suppose λ has the unique earliest property, meaning that for every rooted interval [u, v]r, among all cover relations u ⋖ a with a an atom of [u, v]r, the smallest label occurs on exactly one such cover. Then λ is self-consistent: if a is the atom in the lexicographically first saturated chain of [u, v]r and b is any other atom of [u, v]r, then for every v' with a, b ∈ [u, v']r, every saturated chain of [u, v']r containing a is lexicographically earlier than every saturated chain containing b.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In each rooted interval [u, v]r, the unique atom a whose cover u ⋖ a has smallest label must be the first atom of the lexicographically earliest saturated chain. For any other atom b and any upper bound v' containing both a and b, the first label of every saturated chain through a is λ(u, a), while the first label of every saturated chain through b is λ(u, b), and λ(u, a) < λ(u, b). Thus all chains through a are lexicographically earlier than all chains through b.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every CL-labeling of a finite bounded poset has the unique earliest property: in every rooted interval [u, v]r, the smallest label among the covers u ⋖ a with a an atom of [u, v]r occurs on exactly one such cover.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Suppose two distinct atoms a and a' of [u, v]r both have the same smallest first label. Let M = u ⋖ a ⋖ x2 ⋖ ... ⋖ v be the lexicographically first saturated chain in [u, v]r, chosen to start with a, and let M' = u ⋖ a' ⋖ y2 ⋖ ... ⋖ v be the lexicographically first saturated chain through a'. Since M is the unique ascending chain in the CL-labeling, λ(u, a) < λ(a, x2). Since M and M' have the same first label and M is lexicographically earlier, λ(a, x2) ≤ λ(a', y2). Hence λ(u, a') = λ(u, a) < λ(a', y2), so M' has an ascent at a'. The tail of M' from a' to v is ascending because it is lexicographically first in the rooted interval [a', v]r∪a'. Thus M' is another ascending chain in [u, v]r, contradicting uniqueness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every CC-labeling of a finite bounded poset is a TCL-labeling. A TCL-labeling is a chain-edge labeling such that each rooted interval has a unique saturated chain with lexicographically smallest label sequence and every other saturated chain has at least one topological descent. However, not every TCL-labeling is a CC-labeling.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a CC-labeling, distinct label sequences in each rooted interval give uniqueness of the lexicographically earliest label sequence. The lexicographically earliest chain cannot have a topological descent, while the CC-labeling axiom says there is only one saturated chain consisting entirely of topological ascents; therefore every other saturated chain has a topological descent, so the labeling is TCL. The converse fails by an explicit rank-two bounded poset with atoms x1, x2, x3 and labels λ(ˆ0, x1) = λ(x2, ˆ1) = λ(x3, ˆ1) = 1 and λ(ˆ0, x2) = λ(ˆ0, x3) = λ(x1, ˆ1) = 2. This is a CL-labeling and hence TCL, but two saturated chains have the same label sequence, violating the CC requirement of distinct label sequences.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If a finite bounded poset P admits a TCL-labeling, then every linear extension of the lexicographic partial order on the maximal chains of P is a shelling order for the corresponding facets of the order complex Δ(P). Thus a TCL-labeling induces one or more lexicographic shellings of Δ(P).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is described as the Björner-Wachs proof that CL-labelings give lexicographic shellings, with ordinary ascents and descents replaced everywhere by topological ascents and topological descents. The unique lexicographically earliest topologically ascending chain in every rooted interval supplies the restriction face needed for a shelling step, and the condition that every other saturated chain has a topological descent ensures that each later facet intersects the union of earlier facets in a pure codimension-one subcomplex. No extra argument beyond this substitution is required in the paper.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If a finite bounded poset P admits a self-consistent TCL-labeling λ, then P admits a generalized recursive atom ordering.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Construct a chain-atom ordering Ω from λ. For each rooted interval [u, ˆ1]r, choose the first atom a1 to be the atom appearing in the lexicographically first saturated chain of [u, ˆ1]r. Having chosen a1, ..., ai, choose ai+1 as the atom in a lexicographically earliest saturated chain of [u, ˆ1]r that avoids the previously chosen atoms. Self-consistency ensures compatibility: the earliest atom selected for any subinterval [u, v]r is the atom in the lexicographically first chain of that subinterval. Prove by induction on longest chain length that Ω is a generalized recursive atom ordering. Condition (i)(a) follows by induction on intervals above atoms. For condition (i)(b), suppose an atom x' of [aj, w] is above an earlier atom ai of P, but the first atom x1 of [aj, w] is not. The lexicographically first chain from aj to w through x1 is topologically ascending, and since x1 is not above an earlier atom, the pair ˆ0 ⋖ aj ⋖ x1 is also a topological ascent. This yields a topologically ascending chain from ˆ0 to w through aj. But because ai < w and ai is earlier than aj, the lexicographically first chain from ˆ0 to w starts with a different atom and is also topologically ascending, contradicting uniqueness in the TCL-labeling. For condition (ii), if ai < y and aj < y with ai earlier than aj, take the lexicographically first chain M in [aj, y], whose first step is aj ⋖ x. Since the first atom of [ˆ0, y] is not aj, the full chain through aj cannot be the unique topologically ascending chain from ˆ0 to y; thus the first pair ˆ0 ⋖ aj ⋖ x is a topological descent. Hence there is a lexicographically earlier chain from ˆ0 to x through some atom ak, and self-consistency forces ak earlier than aj. Then z = x witnesses condition (ii).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a finite bounded poset P, the following eight conditions are equivalent: (1) P admits a recursive atom ordering; (2) P admits a generalized recursive atom ordering; (3) P admits a CL-labeling; (4) P admits a CL-labeling with the unique earliest property; (5) P admits a self-consistent CC-labeling; (6) P admits a CC-labeling with the unique earliest property; (7) P admits a self-consistent TCL-labeling; and (8) P admits a TCL-labeling with the unique earliest property. Moreover, the implications are constructive: each proof either builds the latter object from the former or proves the former object already has the latter property.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence of (1) and (2) is Theorem 4.11. The equivalence of (1) and (3) is the Björner-Wachs theorem. Lemma 5.5 shows every CL-labeling has the unique earliest property, giving (3) iff (4). To connect generalized recursive atom orderings and self-consistent CC-labelings, Theorem 5.8 turns CC into TCL, Theorem 6.3 turns self-consistent TCL into a generalized recursive atom ordering, and Theorem 6.1 or Theorem 8.6 constructs a self-consistent CC-labeling from a generalized recursive atom ordering. A CC-labeling with the unique earliest property is self-consistent by Lemma 5.3, and conversely a generalized recursive atom ordering directly gives a CC-labeling with the unique earliest property by assigning atom-position labels in each rooted interval. The equivalence with TCL versions follows from Theorem 5.8, Lemma 5.3, and the fact that CL-labelings are TCL-labelings. Constructivity follows because all cited implications give explicit labelings, atom orderings, or reorderings.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 6.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a finite bounded poset with a self-consistent EC-labeling λ. Then λ can be transformed algorithmically into a CL-labeling for P. Here an EC-labeling is the edge-labeling analogue of a CC-labeling.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Every EC-labeling is a CC-labeling, because it is a CC-labeling with no dependence on roots. A self-consistent EC-labeling is therefore a self-consistent CC-labeling. By Theorem 6.4, P admits a generalized recursive atom ordering and hence a recursive atom ordering; the constructive proofs provide the algorithmic transformation to a CL-labeling.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 6.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A finite bounded poset P admits a generalized recursive coatom ordering if and only if it admits a recursive coatom ordering.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Theorem 4.11 to the dual poset P*. Atoms of P* are coatoms of P, and recursive atom orderings in P* are recursive coatom orderings in P. The same applies to generalized recursive atom orderings.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Pn be the uncrossing poset whose nonzero elements are complete matchings of 2n labeled boundary nodes placed around a circle, represented by strand diagrams, with u ⋖ v when u is obtained from v by uncrossing one crossing of two strands and ranks determined by minimal crossing number plus one, together with a unique minimum ˆ0 below all noncrossing matchings. Then Pn is dual CL-shellable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof starts from a dual EC-labeling of Pn constructed in Hersh-Kenyon. Fix a base boundary node and encode each strand diagram by numbering strands in the order first encountered clockwise and reading the strand numbers around the circle. A cover u ⋖ v corresponds to replacing a crossing pattern ijij, with i < j, either by ijji or by iijj; the label is (i, j) in the first case and (j, i) in the second, while covers from noncrossing diagrams down to ˆ0 are labeled L, with a specified total order on labels. Hersh-Kenyon proved this is a dual EC-labeling. To use Corollary 6.5, the paper proves the unique earliest property for the dual labeling: for any fixed v and any downward cover label K, the label identifies both the pair of strands being uncrossed and which of the two possible uncrossings is performed, hence it determines the unique lower element u with λ(u, v) = K. Therefore labels on all covers downward from v are distinct, so in the dual poset the smallest upward label occurs uniquely in each rooted interval. Lemma 5.3 gives self-consistency, and Corollary 6.5 upgrades the self-consistent dual EC-labeling to a dual CL-labeling.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Any generalized recursive atom ordering Γ of a finite bounded poset P gives rise directly to a self-consistent CC-labeling that orders the maximal chains of P consistently with Γ, without first applying the atom reordering process.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define a chain-edge labeling λ by labeling a cover u ⋖ ai, with root r, by the position i of the atom ai in the Γ-ordering of the atoms of [u, ˆ1]r. This immediately gives the unique earliest property and distinct label sequences for saturated chains. It remains to prove the CC condition that every non-lexicographically-first saturated chain has a topological descent. Suppose not, and choose a counterexample rooted interval [u, v]r of minimal maximum chain length, with a saturated chain M = u ⋖ u1 ⋖ u2 ⋖ ... ⋖ v that is not lexicographically first but has no topological descent. Minimality implies u1 is not the first atom of [u, v]r. If u2 is the first atom of [u1, v]r∪u1, condition (ii) of the generalized recursive atom ordering gives an earlier atom u'1 and a cover u1 ⋖ z below v, and Lemma 3.9 ensures u2 lies above some atom earlier than u1; therefore the pair u ⋖ u1 ⋖ u2 is a topological descent, contradiction. If u2 is not the first atom of [u1, v]r∪u1, then by minimality the tail u1 ⋖ u2 ⋖ ... ⋖ v has a topological descent, also a contradiction. Thus λ is a CC-labeling; its unique earliest property makes it self-consistent by Lemma 5.3, and the construction makes its lexicographic chain order agree with Γ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 8.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Δ be a d-complex, meaning a polyhedral complex whose maximal faces all have dimension d, and let L(Δ) be its face lattice with a bottom element and, if needed, an added top element. Then shellability of Δ is equivalent to L(Δ) admitting a self-consistent dual CC-labeling. It is also equivalent to L(Δ) admitting a self-consistent dual TCL-labeling.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof combines Björner's result that a d-complex Δ is shellable if and only if its face lattice L(Δ) is dual CL-shellable with Theorem 6.4 applied to the dual poset L(Δ)*. Theorem 6.4 identifies CL-shellability with self-consistent CC-shellability and self-consistent TCL-shellability. Translating back through duality gives the stated equivalences.

proof_techniques.name: Prove topology through a finite bounded face poset
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically treats topological questions about complexes by passing to finite bounded posets and studying their order complexes. A poset is called shellable when its order complex is shellable, and lexicographic labelings of maximal chains are used to produce explicit shelling orders. Proposition 8.8 also records a face-lattice transfer: for d-complexes, shellability of the complex is equivalent to dual CL-shellability of its face lattice, and hence to the existence of self-consistent dual CC or TCL labelings.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the cells of F_w form a regular CW complex with a finite face poset or with finite bounded intervals, one can try to encode face relations by resolving crossings and prove shellability or contractibility via a labeling of that poset. If F_w is not finite globally, the interval-local nature of the paper suggests proving shellability on finite subposets or skeleta and then passing to a colimit only after controlling topology.

proof_techniques.name: Recursive atom and coatom orderings
proof_techniques.description: Recursive atom orderings build a shelling by recursively ordering atoms of every upper interval so that atoms lying above earlier atoms are forced to appear first, and so that any two atoms below a common element can be connected through a cover involving an earlier atom. The dual version, recursive coatom ordering, is obtained by applying the same idea to the dual poset. Theorem 2.15 makes this equivalent to CL-shellability, and Corollary 6.6 gives the same equivalence for the generalized coatom version.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a complex built from crossing resolutions, a coatom ordering may be more natural than an atom ordering if covers correspond to resolving or deleting one crossing. The recursive condition suggests looking for a canonical first crossing or first reducible pair whose resolution dominates all competing choices.

proof_techniques.name: Generalized recursive atom ordering as a relaxed target
proof_techniques.description: The paper introduces generalized recursive atom orderings to relax the hardest part of recursive atom orderings. Instead of requiring all atoms above earlier atoms to be placed first in each upper interval, it only requires that in each smaller interval either the first atom is above an earlier atom or no atom is. This relaxation is stable under interval restriction and easier to construct, but Theorem 4.11 proves that it is just as powerful because it can always be reordered into an ordinary recursive atom ordering.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When constructing a shellability proof for F_w-like resolution posets, it may be easier to verify the weaker first-atom condition than to globally order all atoms exactly. The atom reordering theorem means such a weaker construction can still be enough to obtain CL-shellability.

proof_techniques.name: Atom reordering by F/G blocks
proof_techniques.description: Algorithm 4.3 is a local reordering procedure. For each rooted interval [u, ˆ1]r, atoms are split into F, those lying above atoms earlier than u in the immediately lower rooted interval, and G, the complement. The algorithm replaces the atom order by all F-atoms followed by all G-atoms, preserving the original relative order within each block, and proceeds upward through a linear extension of the poset. The key lemmas show that for generalized recursive atom orderings this preserves first atoms of all intervals, preserves the GRAO property, and ultimately yields a recursive atom ordering.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a candidate ordering of local crossing resolutions almost works but fails to put all overlap-producing resolutions first, an F/G-style reordering may repair it. The construction is especially relevant when F corresponds to resolutions compatible with some earlier local simplification.

proof_techniques.name: Safe adjacent swaps preserving first atoms
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves that if two consecutive atoms ai and ai+1 of a rooted interval are never first in any subinterval containing both, then swapping them preserves the generalized recursive atom ordering. This is proved through detailed control of condition (i)(b), condition (ii), and the F/G sets. Lemma 4.8 then decomposes atom reordering permutations into sequences of such safe adjacent swaps.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This offers a way to modify an ordering of resolutions without reproving all shelling conditions from scratch. In a matching problem, two noncanonical resolutions that are never the first resolution in any subproblem could potentially be swapped freely to simplify an inductive construction.

proof_techniques.name: Unique earliest property as a shortcut to self-consistency
proof_techniques.description: The unique earliest property says that in every rooted interval, the smallest label on covers upward from the bottom occurs exactly once. Lemma 5.3 proves this implies self-consistency, meaning that once an atom is first in one interval it beats every competing atom in all larger intervals containing both. Theorem 6.4 then shows that CC or TCL labelings with this property are equivalent in strength to CL-labelings.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a poset of crossing resolutions, a practical route is to label each cover by data identifying the local move and then check that the minimal available move is unique. This can bypass the harder direct construction of a CL-labeling.

proof_techniques.name: Topological ascents and TCL-labelings
proof_techniques.description: CC- and TCL-shellability replace ordinary increasing label sequences by topological ascents: a two-step segment is a topological ascent if its two-label sequence is lexicographically first among all saturated chains with the same endpoints and root. A TCL-labeling only requires a unique lexicographically earliest chain and a topological descent in every other chain. Theorem 5.11 shows this still gives a lexicographic shelling by the same mechanism as CL-shellability.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If ordinary label monotonicity fails for F_w because different crossing resolutions have label ties or nonlocal interactions, topological ascents may be easier to verify: one only compares alternative local two-step resolutions with the same endpoints.

proof_techniques.name: Uncrossing labels that determine the cover
proof_techniques.description: In the uncrossing poset Pn, a cover is labeled by the pair of strands being uncrossed and by which of the two uncrossings is performed. The proof of Theorem 7.1 hinges on the observation that, for a fixed upper strand diagram v, this label determines the unique lower diagram u. Therefore labels on covers downward from v are distinct, giving the unique earliest property in the dual.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original F_w cells are also governed by local crossing resolutions. A promising labeling is by the crossing or paired arcs being resolved together with the chosen resolution; if such a label determines the face uniquely from the cell, the unique earliest property may be accessible.

proof_techniques.name: Interval restriction and locality
proof_techniques.description: Several results, especially Lemma 3.8 and Proposition 4.5, emphasize that the relevant orderings and reorderings restrict well to intervals. This locality is essential because shellability criteria are checked on every rooted interval and because the atom reordering process must be compatible with subintervals.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, any proof by resolving crossings will likely need to analyze subcomplexes obtained after fixing some resolutions. The paper suggests formulating the induction so that every such residual subproblem inherits the same kind of ordering or labeling.

proof_techniques.name: Upgrade self-consistent EC-labelings to CL-labelings
proof_techniques.description: Corollary 6.5 states that a self-consistent EC-labeling can be transformed algorithmically into a CL-labeling. This was used in Section 7 to strengthen a known dual EC-shelling of the uncrossing order to a dual CL-shelling.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If an edge labeling of a matching-resolution poset is easier to construct than a chain-edge labeling, it may suffice to prove it is EC and self-consistent, or prove the unique earliest property, then invoke this upgrade mechanism.

other_useful_info: The paper repeatedly notes that virtually all statements have dual versions because a poset and its dual have the same order complex; this is important when covers in an application are more naturally described by resolving crossings downward rather than building objects upward. The uncrossing posets Pn in Section 7 are face posets for stratified spaces of planar electrical networks; Lam proved these posets are Eulerian, and Hersh-Kenyon previously proved dual EC-shellability before this paper upgraded it to dual CL-shellability. The paper cites Björner-Wachs for CL-shellability and recursive atom orderings, Kozlov for CC/EC-shellability, and Hersh-Kenyon for the EC-labeling of uncrossing orders. Section 8 records that it remains open whether self-consistent CC-shellability is equivalent to CL-shellability in the more general setting of balanced simplicial complexes, beyond posets.
why_relevant: Develops shellability tools and applies them to uncrossing posets. These methods are natural candidates for proving contractibility of labeled subcomplexes via recursive atom orderings or CL-shellings.

title: Stallings Foldings and Subgroups of Free Groups
overall_summary: The paper gives a combinatorial account of Stallings foldings for subgroups of finitely generated free groups. Its central construction represents subgroups of F(X) by based X-labeled directed graphs, folds pairs of equally labeled edges with the same initial vertex, and extracts a canonical folded core graph whose reduced loop labels are exactly the subgroup. The most relevant content for the original problem is the rigorous treatment of free cancellation as a sequence of local, complexity-decreasing foldings that preserve the represented free-group element or subgroup, together with deterministic path-reading in folded graphs. The spanning-tree and Nielsen-reduced-basis arguments also provide concrete cancellation estimates and normal forms for reduced loops, which may be useful if matchings are encoded as cancellation histories or graph quotients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a finite alphabet, let Γ be an X-labeled directed graph, and let ^hatΓ denote the graph obtained by adjoining to every directed edge e a formal inverse edge e^{-1} with inverse label. Suppose Γ1 is obtained from Γ by one Stallings folding: two distinct edges f1 and f2 of ^hatΓ with the same origin and the same label x in X ∪ X^{-1} are identified, and their terminal vertices are identified, producing a new X-digraph Γ1. If v is a vertex of Γ and v1 is its image in Γ1, then: (1) if Γ is connected, Γ1 is connected; (2) every path p in Γ from v to v with label w maps edgewise to a path in Γ1 from v1 to v1 with the same label w; and (3) if Γ is finite, a folding decreases the number of positive edges of Γ by one.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The folding is a quotient operation on the underlying graph: it identifies the two chosen equally labeled directed edges and identifies the corresponding terminal vertices. A quotient of a connected graph is connected, giving (1). Since the quotient map sends edges to edges with the same labels and preserves origins and termini after the identifications, the edgewise image of any loop based at v is a loop based at v1 with exactly the same sequence of labels, giving (2). In the positive edge set of the original X-digraph, the two positive representatives of the folded pair are replaced by one new positive edge, while all other positive edges remain; hence the number of positive edges drops by one, giving (3).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a folded X-digraph, meaning that for every vertex q of Γ and every letter x in X ∪ X^{-1}, there is at most one edge of ^hatΓ with origin q and label x. If p is a reduced path in Γ, meaning p has no consecutive subpath of the form e,e^{-1}, then the label of p is a freely reduced word in X ∪ X^{-1}. Consequently, for any base vertex v, every word in the language L(Γ,v), defined as the set of labels of reduced loops at v, is freely reduced.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If the label of a reduced path contained a free cancellation xx^{-1}, then for some consecutive edges e_i,e_{i+1} of the path, μ(e_i)=x and μ(e_{i+1})=x^{-1}. At the common vertex t(e_i)=o(e_{i+1}), the two edges e_i^{-1} and e_{i+1} both have origin t(e_i) and label x^{-1}. Since Γ is folded, they must be the same edge, so e_{i+1}=e_i^{-1}. This contradicts that the path is reduced. Thus no adjacent inverse labels occur.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1 and Lemma 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be an X-digraph and v a base vertex. Let L(Γ,v) be the set of labels of reduced paths in Γ from v to v, where labels are words in X ∪ X^{-1}. Let overline{L(Γ,v)} be the set of freely reduced forms of all words in L(Γ,v), viewed as elements of the free group F(X). Then overline{L(Γ,v)} is a subgroup of F(X). If Γ is folded, then every word in L(Γ,v) is already freely reduced, and L(Γ,v)=overline{L(Γ,v)} is a subgroup of F(X).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For closure under multiplication, take two reduced loops p1,p2 at v with labels w1,w2. Their concatenation p1p2 is a loop at v, possibly not path-reduced. Perform all possible path reductions to obtain a reduced loop p. The label of p freely reduces to the product of the freely reduced labels of p1 and p2, so the product lies in overline{L(Γ,v)}. For inverses, reverse a reduced loop p and replace each edge by its formal inverse; the resulting reduced loop has label representing the inverse element. The empty path gives the identity. If Γ is folded, Lemma 2.9 says labels of reduced loops are freely reduced, so no passage to freely reduced forms is needed.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ′ be obtained from an X-digraph Γ by a single Stallings folding, and let v′ be the image of a base vertex v. Then the subgroup of F(X) represented by reduced loop labels at v is the same as the subgroup represented by reduced loop labels at v′. Equivalently, after freely reducing labels of reduced loops, folding preserves the based graph language.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For one inclusion, take a reduced loop p at v in Γ. Its edgewise image p′ in Γ′ is a loop at v′ with the same label as a word before free reduction. The image path may fail to be reduced only where the two folded edges create an immediate backtrack, so reducing p′ as a path gives a reduced loop at v′ whose label represents the same element of F(X). Thus every represented element before folding is represented after folding. Conversely, suppose p′ is a reduced loop at v′ in Γ′. Let e be the new folded edge, obtained from preimages e1,e2 in Γ. Decompose p′ into subpaths p0 f0 p1 f1 ... fk pk+1, where each fi is e or e^{-1} and the pi do not use e. The subpaths pi can be regarded in Γ away from the folded edge. For each occurrence fi=e or e^{-1}, choose the corresponding preimage di among e1,e2 or e1^{-1},e2^{-1} that connects the terminal vertex of pi to the initial vertex of p_{i+1} in Γ. Replacing each fi by di yields a path p in Γ from v to v with the same label as p′. The construction preserves reducedness because p′ was reduced and the only possible new cancellations would correspond to forbidden adjacent inverse occurrences at the folded edge. Hence every represented element after folding was already represented before folding.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For an X-digraph Γ and a vertex v, define Core(Γ,v) to be the union of all reduced paths in Γ from v to v. Then Core(Γ,v) is a connected subgraph containing v; it has no degree-one vertices except possibly v; and L(Core(Γ,v),v)=L(Γ,v), where L denotes the set of labels of reduced loops at the base vertex.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The core is a union of loops all based at v, so it is connected and contains v. Since every edge in the core lies on a reduced loop based at v, a non-base vertex of degree one cannot occur: a reduced loop entering such a vertex would have to leave immediately along the inverse edge, contradicting reducedness. The equality of languages is immediate from the definition: every reduced loop at v in Γ is included in the core, and every reduced loop in the core is a reduced loop in Γ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a finitely generated subgroup of the free group F(X), where X is finite. Then there exists a finite based X-digraph (Γ,v) such that: Γ is folded and connected; every vertex except possibly v has degree greater than one; every vertex has degree at most 2|X|; Γ is a core graph with respect to v; and L(Γ,v)=H, where L(Γ,v) is the subgroup of F(X) represented by reduced loop labels at v. If H is trivial, Γ may be the single vertex graph.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose freely reduced generators h1,...,hm of H. Start with a wedge of m oriented circles at a common vertex v1, subdividing the i-th circle into |hi| edges and labeling it so that reading around it gives hi. The reduced loop labels in this initial graph represent exactly the subgroup generated by the hi. If the graph is not folded, perform a Stallings folding. By Lemma 2.5, connectedness is preserved and the number of edges decreases; by Lemma 3.4, the represented subgroup is preserved. Since the initial graph is finite, the folding process terminates in a finite folded graph Γ. Foldedness gives the degree bound 2|X|. To see Γ is a core graph, take any final edge e and trace it back to an original edge e1 in the wedge of circles. The original edge e1 lies on a generator loop with freely reduced label. Its image in the final graph is a loop through e with the same freely reduced label. If the final graph had a non-base degree-one vertex u incident to e, any loop through e would have to traverse e and immediately traverse e^{-1}, forcing a non-free-reduced label, a contradiction. Thus no non-base degree-one vertices occur, and the graph is core with respect to the base.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a connected folded X-digraph that is a core graph with respect to a base vertex v, and let H=L(Γ,v) ≤ F(X). Then: (1) for every initial segment w of a freely reduced word h in H, there is a unique reduced path in Γ with origin v and label w; (2) for every reduced path p in Γ with origin v and label w, the word w occurs as an initial segment, hence as a subword, of some freely reduced word h in H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1), since h is accepted by Γ, there is a reduced loop at v with label h; its initial segment of length |w| gives the required path. Uniqueness follows from foldedness: at each vertex there is at most one outgoing edge of any given label. For (2), let p end at u. If u=v, then w itself represents an element of H. If u≠v, the core property implies u has degree at least two. Choose an edge e starting at u that is not the inverse of the last edge of p. Since Γ is connected, choose a reduced path p′ from t(e) to v. Then p e p′ is a reduced loop at v whose label is freely reduced because Γ is folded; this label lies in H and has w as an initial segment.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ and Δ be connected X-digraphs, and assume Δ is folded. Fix vertices v in Γ and u in Δ. Then there is at most one morphism of X-digraphs f:Γ→Δ such that f(v)=u, where a morphism sends vertices to vertices, directed edges to directed edges, and preserves origins, termini, directions, and labels.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For any vertex v′ of Γ, choose a reduced path p from v to v′ and let w be its label. If f and j are two morphisms Γ→Δ sending v to u, then f(p) and j(p) are paths in Δ starting at u with the same label w. Because Δ is folded, there is at most one such path, so f(v′)=j(v′). Thus the two morphisms agree on vertices. Since labels and endpoints determine the image of each edge in a folded target, they agree on edges as well.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K≤H≤F(X). Suppose (Γ1,v1) and (Γ2,v2) are connected folded based X-digraphs, each a core graph with respect to its base vertex, such that L(Γ1,v1)=K and L(Γ2,v2)=H. Then there exists a unique morphism of X-digraphs π:Γ1→Γ2 with π(v1)=v2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Uniqueness is Lemma 4.2. For existence, define π on vertices by path labels. For a vertex q of Γ1, choose a reduced path pq from v1 to q and let w=μ(pq). By Lemma 3.9, w is an initial segment of a freely reduced word in K, hence of a word in H. Since Γ2 is folded and core with language H, Lemma 3.9 gives a unique path in Γ2 from v2 with label w; define π(q) to be its endpoint. This does not depend on the chosen path pq: if p′q has label w′, then pq(p′q)^{-1} is a loop at v1 whose label represents an element of K≤H, and following the corresponding loop in Γ2 shows the endpoints obtained from w and w′ coincide; foldedness supplies uniqueness. For an edge e of Γ1 with label x, the core property puts e on a reduced loop at v1 whose label lies in K≤H. Reading the same loop in Γ2 forces an edge with label x from π(o(e)) to π(t(e)); set π(e) equal to that edge. This yields a label-preserving graph morphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f:Γ→Δ be an epimorphism of X-digraphs. Then f factors as f=f2∘f1, where f1:Γ→Γ′ is injective on the edge set of Γ and f2:Γ′→Δ is a bijection on vertex sets. Informally, f1 only identifies vertices of Γ that f identifies, while f2 then identifies edges with the same image in Δ. If Γ is finite, the second step may be viewed as a finite sequence of generalized foldings.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Construct Γ′ by collapsing each fiber of the vertex map VΓ→VΔ to a single vertex, without yet identifying edges. The induced map f1 is injective on the original edge set, because edges are still distinct. After this vertex identification, any two edges of Γ′ that have the same image edge in Δ have the same label and the same origin and terminus as far as Δ is concerned. The map f2 identifies exactly these edge fibers. Since the vertices have already been collapsed to the correct fibers, f2 is a bijection on vertex sets. The composite is the original epimorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorems 5.1 and 5.2; Definition 5.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be any subgroup of the free group F(X), with X finite. There exists a based X-digraph (Γ,1_H) such that Γ is connected, folded, a core graph with respect to 1_H, and L(Γ,1_H)=H. Moreover, if (Γ1,v1) and (Γ2,v2) are two connected folded based X-digraphs, each core with respect to its base, and both have language H, then there is a unique base-preserving isomorphism Γ1→Γ2. The unique based graph is denoted Γ(H) and called the subgroup graph of H with respect to X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For existence, build the coset X-digraph Δ of H in F(X). Its vertices are left cosets Hf, and for x in X there is an x-labeled directed edge from Hf to Hg exactly when Hfx=Hg. The base vertex is H. The graph is connected because a freely reduced word f=x1...xn gives a path H,Hx1,Hx1x2,...,Hf. It is folded because from a coset Hf and a letter x there is only one possible coset Hfx, so at most one outgoing x-edge. A freely reduced word labels a loop at H exactly when Hf=H, i.e. f∈H; hence L(Δ,H)=H. Taking Γ=Core(Δ,H) preserves the language and gives the required core graph. For uniqueness, apply Proposition 4.3 to get a base-preserving morphism Γ1→Γ2 and another Γ2→Γ1. Their composites are base-preserving morphisms from a connected graph to itself; by Lemma 4.2 each composite must be the identity. Thus the morphisms are inverse isomorphisms.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a folded connected X-digraph with base vertex v, and let T be a spanning tree of Γ. For each positive edge e of Γ not in T, define p_e to be the reduced path [v,o(e)]_T e [t(e),v]_T, where [a,b]_T is the unique reduced path in the tree T from a to b. Let [e]=μ(p_e), and set Y_T={[e] : e is a positive edge of Γ outside T}. Then Y_T is a free basis of the subgroup H=L(Γ,v)≤F(X).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Extend the notation to all oriented edges of ^hatΓ by p_e=[v,o(e)]_T e [t(e),v]_T and [e]=μ(p_e); then [e^{-1}]=[e]^{-1}. If e lies in the tree T, the path p_e reduces to the trivial path, so [e]=1. For generation, take any reduced loop p=e1...ek at v. Insert the tree paths from v to each intermediate vertex and back; after path reductions, p is obtained from the concatenation p_{e1}...p_{ek}. Thus its label is the product [e1]...[ek], and tree edges contribute only the identity, so H is generated by Y_T. For freeness, consider a nonempty freely reduced word [e1]...[ek] in symbols from Y_T^{±1}, with ei not equal to e_{i+1}^{-1}. The corresponding path reduces to [v,o(e1)]_T e1 [t(e1),o(e2)]_T e2 ... ek [t(ek),v]_T. This path is reduced: the non-tree edges ei cannot cancel with tree paths, and adjacent non-tree edges would cancel only if ei=e_{i+1}^{-1}, which is excluded. Since Γ is folded, its label is a nontrivial freely reduced word in F(X). Hence no nontrivial reduced word in Y_T represents the identity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.2 and Corollary 6.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a connected folded X-digraph, let Γ′ be a connected subgraph containing a vertex v, and set G=L(Γ,v), H=L(Γ′,v). Then H is a free factor of G. If Γ′ does not contain Core(Γ,v), then H≠G. In particular, if both Γ and Γ′ are core graphs with respect to v and Γ′ is a proper subgraph of Γ, then L(Γ′,v) is a proper free factor of L(Γ,v).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a spanning tree T′ of Γ′ and extend it to a spanning tree T of Γ. Lemma 6.1 gives a free basis Y_T for G from positive edges outside T. This basis splits as the disjoint union of the basis Y_{T′} coming from positive edges of Γ′ outside T′ and a set Z coming from positive edges outside Γ′. Therefore G=F(Y_{T′}) * F(Z), while H=F(Y_{T′}), so H is a free factor. If Γ′ does not contain Core(Γ,v), then there is a positive edge of Γ outside Γ′ that lies on a reduced loop based at v; such an edge cannot be contained entirely in the tree part attached outside Γ′. Thus Z is nonempty, and the free product decomposition is proper.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.6, Proposition 6.7, and Corollary 6.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a connected graph with base vertex v. A spanning tree T is geodesic relative to v if, for every vertex u, the tree path [v,u]_T has the smallest possible length among all paths in Γ from v to u. Such a geodesic spanning tree exists. If Γ is a folded X-digraph that is a core graph with respect to v, H=L(Γ,v), and T is a geodesic spanning tree, then the free basis Y_T from Lemma 6.1 is Nielsen-reduced with respect to X: for u,w in Y_T ∪ Y_T^{-1} with u≠w^{-1}, the freely reduced product u·w has length at least both |u|_X and |w|_X; and for u,w,z in Y_T ∪ Y_T^{-1} with u≠w^{-1} and z≠w^{-1}, the product u·w·z has length greater than |u|_X+|z|_X−|w|_X. Consequently every subgroup of F(X) is free, and it has a Nielsen-reduced free basis.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For existence of T, build it by breadth-first layers. Let T0 be the base vertex v. Having built a geodesic tree on the ball of radius n−1, attach to every vertex at distance n one edge from a predecessor at distance n−1. The union over all n is a geodesic spanning tree. For Nielsen reducedness, write for an oriented non-tree edge e the loop p_e=A_e e B_e, where A_e=[v,o(e)]_T and B_e=[t(e),v]_T. Since T is geodesic and o(e),t(e) are adjacent in Γ, the lengths of A_e and B_e differ by at most one, and each of A_e, B_e, A_e e, and e B_e is controlled by approximately one half of |p_e|. For a product [e][f] with e≠f^{-1}, the path p_e p_f reduces to A_e e [t(e),o(f)]_T f B_f; the non-tree edges survive. Geodesicity and triangle inequalities compare the middle tree segment with the omitted tree segments and show the remaining path has length at least |p_e| and at least |p_f|, giving the first Nielsen condition. For a triple [e][f][g] with e≠f^{-1} and g≠f^{-1}, the reduced path is A_e e [t(e),o(f)]_T f [t(f),o(g)]_T g B_g. When p_e p_f p_g is reduced to this path, at most |A_f| letters can cancel from the end of p_e and at most |B_f| letters from the beginning of p_g, while the central edge f survives. Since |p_f|=|A_f|+1+|B_f|, the final length is at least 1+|p_e|+|p_g|−|A_f|−|B_f|, which is strictly greater than |p_e|+|p_g|−|p_f|. Lemma 6.1 supplies freeness, and applying the construction to the subgroup graph Γ(H) gives a Nielsen-reduced basis for any subgroup H≤F(X).

proof_techniques.name: Language-preserving Stallings fold
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the local operation of folding two equally labeled edges with the same initial vertex. This operation is purely combinatorial, decreases the number of edges in a finite graph, and preserves the subgroup represented by based reduced loops. The preservation proof has two directions: images of loops remain loops after possible path reduction, and reduced loops in the folded graph can be lifted by choosing appropriate preimages of each folded edge.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A reducible word with a matching can potentially be encoded as a cancellation diagram or graph; a local folding move could serve as a canonical complexity-reducing move on such diagrams. If the cells of F_w can be interpreted as choices among local resolutions of folds or cancellations, this technique suggests a route to a deformation retraction or discrete Morse matching.

proof_techniques.name: Fold until deterministic
proof_techniques.description: Starting from a wedge of loops labeled by subgroup generators, the paper folds all non-deterministic pairs of edges until the graph is folded. Finiteness guarantees termination because each fold reduces the positive edge count. The terminal folded graph has deterministic path-reading: from any vertex and label there is at most one path with that label.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Different matchings of the same reducible word may be viewed as different cancellation histories. A proof of contractibility might try to show that all histories flow to a common deterministic normal form, analogously to folding all graphs representing the same subgroup to the same folded core.

proof_techniques.name: Core extraction
proof_techniques.description: The core Core(Γ,v) is the union of all reduced loops based at v. Removing everything outside the core does not change the based language, and the resulting graph has no non-base degree-one vertices. This separates essential cancellation data from hanging tree-like data that carries no closed reduced loop labels.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a matching diagram, arcs or regions that do not contribute to a closed cancellation pattern might be removable without changing the represented free-group triviality. The no-degree-one feature is a useful analogue for ruling out degenerate local configurations.

proof_techniques.name: Deterministic path-reading and canonical morphisms
proof_techniques.description: Folded graphs behave like deterministic automata: a starting vertex and a word determine at most one path. This gives uniqueness of graph morphisms into folded graphs, and subgroup inclusion K≤H yields a canonical base-preserving morphism Γ(K)→Γ(H) by sending a vertex to the endpoint of the same path label in Γ(H).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one can associate to a partial matching a folded or deterministic state, prefixes of the fixed word w may determine canonical endpoints or canonical next moves. Such uniqueness can reduce choices and help prove that a space of choices is contractible.

proof_techniques.name: Morphism factorization into vertex identifications and folds
proof_techniques.description: Any epimorphism of X-digraphs factors first by identifying vertices and then by identifying edges over the same image; in finite cases the second stage is a sequence of generalized foldings. Thus a global quotient map can be decomposed into elementary local moves.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The cell attachments in F_w are defined by resolving one crossing at a time. If matchings can be encoded as quotients of a graph associated to w, this factorization suggests comparing global changes of matchings to sequences of elementary local resolutions or folds.

proof_techniques.name: Spanning-tree normal form for loop labels
proof_techniques.description: Given a folded connected based graph and a spanning tree, each non-tree positive edge determines a loop based at the base vertex. The labels of these loops form a free basis for the subgroup represented by the graph. The proof expresses any reduced loop as a product of these edge loops and proves no nontrivial relation by observing that a reduced word in non-tree edges yields a genuinely reduced path with nontrivial label.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A matching diagram may have an underlying graph whose independent cycles correspond to independent cancellation choices. A spanning-tree decomposition could isolate independent parameters and help analyze cubical coordinates or prove that certain subcomplexes are products or collapsible.

proof_techniques.name: Geodesic-tree cancellation estimates
proof_techniques.description: Using a geodesic spanning tree, the paper obtains Nielsen-reduced bases and explicit bounds on how much cancellation can occur in products of basis elements. The central estimates compare tree-path lengths on the two sides of a non-tree edge and show that in products and triples, specified non-tree edges survive while cancellation is bounded by adjacent geodesic segments.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The quasi-reduced hypothesis in the original problem forbids the local pattern ℓℓ^{-1}ℓ, which is a constraint on overlapping cancellations. The Nielsen estimates provide a template for proving that a chosen local cancellation survives or that excessive overlap would force a forbidden short pattern.

other_useful_info: Convention 2.2 is important: every positive X-labeled edge is accompanied by a formal inverse edge labeled by X^{-1}, so paths naturally carry words in X ∪ X^{-1}. Remark 2.8 identifies folded based X-digraphs with deterministic automata over X ∪ X^{-1}; for finite folded graphs, the accepted freely reduced language is regular because freely reduced words form a regular language and regular languages are closed under intersection. The paper points to Stallings, Topology of finite graphs [43], as the original source for the folding method, and Nielsen's work [36,38] for subgroup bases in free groups.
why_relevant: Gives a detailed combinatorial treatment of folding methods in free groups. Although graph foldings differ from boundary matchings, the paper provides standard free-group folding and cancellation techniques that may inform a proof.

title: Diagram groups and directed 2-complexes: homotopy and homology
overall_summary: The paper reformulates diagram groups in terms of directed 2-complexes, where 2-paths are directed homotopies between directed 1-paths and diagrams are planar representatives modulo dipole cancellation. It constructs a multidimensional Squier semi-cubical complex Sq(K), identifies it with a space of positive paths in K, and proves that its fundamental groups are exactly the diagram groups. The most relevant contractibility result is that the universal cover of every connected component Sq(K,p) is contractible; equivalently, Sq(K,p) is a K(D(K,p),1). The proof uses universal 2-covers, which are rooted 2-trees, and a direct deformation retraction of their positive path spaces along the natural rooted-tree filtration. The paper also develops a collapsing-scheme/discrete-Morse method for complete directed 2-complexes, producing smaller K(G,1) complexes with cells indexed by essential cubes; this may be useful if the matching complex in the original problem can be encoded as a Squier-type cubical complex or as a universal cover/component thereof.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a directed 2-complex. An atomic 2-path is a triple (p,f,q), where p and q are 1-paths in K and f is a 2-cell, with top 1-path p⌈f⌉q and bottom 1-path p⌊f⌋q. The isotopy relation on 2-paths is the smallest equivalence relation that is invariant under concatenation and contains all pairs obtained by interchanging two independent atomic 2-paths: for 2-cells f1 and f2 and 1-paths p,q,r for which all concatenations are defined, the 2-path (p,f1,q⌈f2⌉r) ◦ (p⌊f1⌋q,f2,r) is isotopic to (p⌈f1⌉q,f2,r) ◦ (p,f1,q⌊f2⌋r).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Each atomic 2-path is represented by a one-cell planar diagram. Concatenating 2-paths corresponds to gluing the bottom path of one diagram to the top path of the next. If two atomic replacements occur in disjoint subpaths, the same two-cell planar diagram can be read in two vertical orders, giving exactly the displayed pair of 2-paths. Conversely, any isotopy of a diagram preserves the planar cell structure but may change the order in which cells are read from top to bottom. The possible changes of reading order are generated by adjacent swaps of cells that are incomparable in the planar dependence order, and such cells are precisely independent atomic 2-paths. Closure under concatenation accounts for performing these swaps inside longer 2-paths.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.5 and Theorem 2.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a directed 2-complex. For 1-paths u and v in K, u is homotopic to v by a 2-path if and only if there exists a planar (u,v)-diagram over K. A dipole in a diagram is a pair of cells π1,π2 such that the bottom path of π1 coincides with the top path of π2 and the labels of π1 and π2 are mutually inverse; reducing a dipole removes the two cells and identifies the remaining boundary paths. Every equivalence class of diagrams over K contains exactly one reduced diagram, meaning a diagram with no dipoles. Equivalently, every 2-path is homotopic to a reduced 2-path, and two homotopic reduced 2-paths have equal diagrams and the same number of atomic factors. Thus morphisms in the diagram groupoid D(K) can be represented by reduced diagrams, with multiplication given by concatenation followed by dipole reduction.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Given a 2-path, glue the atomic diagrams of its atomic factors along matching top and bottom 1-paths; this produces a planar diagram with top u and bottom v. Conversely, a planar diagram satisfying the directed conditions can be peeled or read cell by cell, yielding a sequence of atomic replacements, hence a 2-path. Dipole cancellation corresponds exactly to deleting a subword δδ^{-1} in a 2-path. The number of cells strictly decreases under dipole cancellation, so the process terminates. The uniqueness of the reduced form is the Kilibarda normal-form lemma: different possible dipole reductions are compatible, because disjoint cancellations commute and the planar structure prevents incompatible overlap from producing distinct reduced outcomes. Therefore two diagrams are equivalent precisely when their reduced forms coincide.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a directed 2-complex with a chosen orientation of its 2-cells, and let p be a 1-path in K. Define the Squier semi-cubical complex Sq(K) as follows: its 0-cubes are 1-paths in K, and its n-cubes for n ≥ 1 are thin diagrams ε(u0)+f1+ε(u1)+...+fn+ε(un), where u0,...,un are 1-paths and f1,...,fn are negative 2-cells of K. The face maps replace the i-th cell fi by its top or bottom path. Let Sq(K,p) be the connected component containing p. Then the diagram group D(K,p), the local group of reduced spherical (p,p)-diagrams over K, is isomorphic to the fundamental group π1(Sq(K),p), equivalently π1(Sq(K,p),p).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The 1-skeleton of Sq(K) has directed edges corresponding to atomic 2-paths (u,f,v), and an edge path in Sq(K,p) therefore determines a 2-path in K starting at p. A loop based at p determines a spherical (p,p)-diagram, giving a homomorphism π1(Sq(K,p),p) to D(K,p). The 2-cells of Sq(K) correspond to pairs of independent atomic 2-paths, and their boundaries encode exactly the commutation relation from Lemma 2.2, so the map respects homotopy of loops. Surjectivity follows because every spherical diagram can be read as a sequence of atomic 2-paths, hence as an edge loop. Injectivity follows because if the resulting diagram is trivial in D(K,p), then the sequence of atomic moves can be transformed to the trivial sequence by swaps of independent moves and cancellations δδ^{-1}; the swaps are filled by Squier 2-cells and the cancellations give backtracking in the 1-skeleton. Higher-dimensional cubes do not change π1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a directed 2-complex, and let p and q be homotopic 1-paths in K. Then the diagram groups D(K,p) and D(K,q) are isomorphic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If p and q are homotopic, they lie in the same connected component of Sq(K). By Theorem 3.4, D(K,p) and D(K,q) are the fundamental groups of this connected component with different basepoints, and fundamental groups of a path-connected space with different basepoints are isomorphic after choosing a path between the basepoints.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a directed 2-complex. A positive path in K is a finite concatenation of ordinary 1-paths and paths ft through 2-cells f, where t ∈ [0,1] parametrizes the horizontal slice of the cell and f0 = ⌈f⌉, f1 = ⌊f⌋ for a positive cell f, with the convention (f^{-1})t = f1−t. Let Ω+(K) be the set of all positive paths. Every positive path has a unique normal form u0 f1,t1 u1 ... fm,tm um with fi negative 2-cells and ti ∈ (0,1). The map ξ sending this normal form to the point with coordinates (t1,...,tm) in the cube ε(u0)+f1+ε(u1)+...+fm+ε(um) is a bijection from Ω+(K) to the geometric realization of Sq(K). More generally, if some ti are 0 or 1, the same coordinate rule agrees with the face identifications of Sq(K).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Each point in the realization of Sq(K) lies in the interior of a unique cube, and its cube coordinates determine a unique positive path in normal form, giving an inverse to ξ. If a positive path is written with some parameter ti equal to 0 or 1, replacing fi,0 by ⌈fi⌉ or fi,1 by ⌊fi⌋ passes to a face of the corresponding cube. The definition of the Squier face maps identifies precisely that face with the cube obtained after this replacement. Therefore the coordinate assignment is independent of the chosen non-normal expression and gives a well-defined bijection.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1 and Lemma 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a directed 2-complex. First, if u is a nonempty 1-path in K and K′ is obtained from K by adding a new edge e with the same endpoints as u and a new 2-cell f of the form u = e, then for every 1-path w in K the diagram groups D(K,w) and D(K′,w) are isomorphic.
