Second, suppose K is the union of directed 2-complexes K1 and K2 that have the same vertices and edges as K, and suppose that for each positive 2-cell f of K1, the paths ⌈f⌉ and ⌊f⌋ are homotopic in K2 to paths uf and vf, respectively. If K′ is obtained by keeping K2 and replacing each such cell f by a cell uf = vf, then D(K,w) is isomorphic to D(K′,w) for every 1-path w. Consequently, if K′ is a subdivision of K obtained by cutting 2-cells by adding intermediate edges and cells, then D(K,w) ≅ D(K′,w) for every nonempty 1-path w in K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the edge-adjunction move, the inclusion K ⊂ K′ gives a natural homomorphism D(K,w) to D(K′,w). A reduced (w,w)-diagram over K′ whose boundary labels lie in K cannot contain the new edge e: any occurrence of e is internal and adjacent to two cells, and the only cells containing e are f and f^{-1}, so the two adjacent cells form a dipole. Thus every reduced spherical diagram over K′ with boundary w is already over K, proving surjectivity and injectivity. For the replacement move, choose diagrams Γf over K2 from ⌈f⌉ to uf and Δf over K2 from ⌊f⌋ to vf. Define a map from diagrams over K to diagrams over K′ by replacing each cell f by Γf followed by the new cell uf = vf followed by Δf^{-1}; define the inverse map by replacing the new cell by Γf^{-1}, then the old cell f, then Δf. Compositions produce diagrams differing from the identity by subdiagrams Γf Γf^{-1} or Δf Δf^{-1}, which reduce by dipole cancellation, so the induced diagram-group homomorphisms are mutually inverse. A subdivision is obtained by combining these moves: add an intermediate edge and one half-cell, then replace the old cell by the other half-cell.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let φ: K → K′ be a morphism of directed 2-complexes, meaning that vertices map to vertices, edges map to nonempty 1-paths, and 2-cells map to 2-paths compatibly with top, bottom, initial, terminal, and inverse operations. For a 1-path p in K, φ induces a homomorphism φp: D(K,p) → D(K′,φ(p)). The following hold. (1) If φ(δ) is reduced for every reduced 2-path δ in K and φ(f) is nonempty for every 2-cell f of K, then φ is nonsingular, i.e. φp is injective for every p. (2) If φ is injective on the set of 2-cells and φ(f) is a single 2-cell for every 2-cell f of K, then φ is nonsingular. (3) If K′ is obtained from K by adding 2-cells, then each D(K,p) naturally embeds in D(K′,p). (4) If K′ is obtained from K by adding 2-cells and for every new 2-cell f the paths ⌈f⌉ and ⌊f⌋ are homotopic in K, then D(K,p) is a retract of D(K′,p) for every 1-path p in K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1), if a nontrivial element of D(K,p) were in the kernel, choose its unique reduced diagram or reduced 2-path δ. By hypothesis φ(δ) is reduced and nonempty, so by uniqueness of reduced diagrams it cannot represent the identity in D(K′,φ(p)). For (2), replacing each cell of a reduced diagram by a distinct single cell cannot create a dipole, because a dipole in the image would come from a dipole in the original diagram with inverse labels; hence (1) applies. Part (3) is the special case of (2) for the inclusion map. For (4), choose for every new cell f a fixed diagram Γf over K connecting ⌈f⌉ to ⌊f⌋, with inverse diagrams assigned to inverse cells. Replacing every new cell in a diagram over K′ by Γf defines a homomorphism D(K′,p) → D(K,p) that restricts to the identity on D(K,p), hence a retraction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a complete directed 2-complex, meaning that it is Noetherian with respect to positive 2-path reductions and confluent. A left forest in Sq(K) is a spanning forest T such that whenever an edge (p,f,q) lies in T, the 1-path p is irreducible, and then every edge (p,f,q′) also lies in T. A right forest is defined symmetrically using the right context q. Then Sq(K) has both a left forest and a right forest.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In each connected component choose the unique irreducible vertex, which exists by Noetherianity and confluence. For every non-irreducible 1-path p, choose its shortest non-irreducible initial segment. This segment has the form u⌊f⌋ for some negative 2-cell f with u irreducible, so p = u⌊f⌋v; assign to p the edge (u,f,v). Let T contain all vertices and all assigned edges together with their inverses. Following assigned edges moves a vertex toward its irreducible normal form, so no cycle can occur and every vertex is connected to the chosen irreducible vertex in its component. The construction gives condition F1 by the choice of u, and condition F2 because the assignment depends only on the left irreducible prefix and the cell, not on the remaining right context. The right forest is obtained by the analogous construction using shortest non-irreducible terminal segments.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.1 and Theorem 7.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A rooted 2-tree with root 1-path w is a directed 2-complex K given as an ascending union K0 ⊂ K1 ⊂ K2 ⊂ ... where K0 is the simple directed path w, and Kn+1 is obtained from Kn by adding positive 2-cells f whose top paths ⌈f⌉ lie in Kn and whose bottom paths ⌊f⌋ are new simple arcs meeting Kn only at endpoints, with distinct new bottom arcs disjoint except possibly at endpoints. For a directed 2-complex K with distinguished 1-path w, consider: T1, every vertex of K lies on some 1-path from ι(w) to τ(w); T2, any two 1-paths in K with the same endpoints are homotopic in K; T3, the diagram group D(K,w) is trivial. Lemma 7.1 states that every rooted 2-tree with root w satisfies T1, T2, and T3. Theorem 7.2 states conversely that if K with distinguished path w satisfies T1, T2, and T3, then K is a rooted 2-tree with root w.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 7.1, argue by induction over the filtration. T1 is preserved because a new vertex lies on the new bottom arc of some cell f, and the endpoints of that arc already lie on root-to-root paths in the previous stage. For T2, induct on the number of new edges from Kn+1 − Kn appearing in two paths with the same endpoints. If one path contains a new edge, then because new arcs meet the old complex only at endpoints, the path contains the whole bottom arc of a newly attached cell; replacing that bottom arc by the top path reduces the number of new edges and preserves homotopy. For T3, a reduced nontrivial spherical diagram over Kn+1 with boundary w must contain a cell from Kn+1 − Kn. The bottom path of such a cell is a new simple arc, so it must be adjacent to an inverse cell, forming a dipole, contradiction. For Theorem 7.2, define K0 to be the path w and let Kn+1 be obtained by adjoining all 2-cells of K whose top paths lie in Kn. Conditions T1 and T2 ensure that the union of the Kn is all of K. The key claim is that if M is already a rooted 2-tree with root w and f is a 2-cell outside M with top in M, then no internal vertex of ⌊f⌋ lies in M. If such a vertex o existed, split ⌊f⌋ at o. Using T1 inside M and T2 in K, build diagrams connecting the split pieces to paths in M, then combine them with the cell f and diagrams in M to obtain a nontrivial spherical (w,w)-diagram containing f but no f^{-1}. After reducing dipoles, a nontrivial reduced spherical diagram remains, contradicting T3. Hence each new bottom path is a fresh simple arc, so the filtration is exactly a rooted 2-tree filtration.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a directed 2-complex and p a 1-path in K. A directed 2-complex M over K has a labelling map φ: M → K sending edges to edges and 2-cells to 2-cells. For a 1-path r in M, the 2-star of r is the set of atomic 2-paths in M with top r, and φ induces a local map from the 2-star of r to the 2-star of φ(r). A universal 2-cover of K with base p is a directed 2-complex M over K satisfying U1, M is a rooted 2-tree with root path p̃ labelled by p, and U2, for every 1-path q̃ in M from ι(p̃) to τ(p̃), the local map at q̃ is bijective. For every K and p, a universal 2-cover exists and is unique up to label-preserving isomorphism.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Existence is by induction. Start with M0 equal to a simple path p̃ labelled by p. Given Mn, for every pair (r̃,f) where r̃ is a 1-path in Mn, f is a 2-cell of K, and ⌈f⌉ = φ(r̃), if Mn does not already contain a 2-cell labelled f with top r̃, attach a new 2-cell f̃ with top r̃ and bottom a fresh simple arc labelled ⌊f⌋. Do this for all such pairs, with new arcs disjoint except at endpoints, to obtain Mn+1. The union M is a rooted 2-tree. The construction gives property U2′: for every 1-path r̃ in M and every 2-cell f of K with ⌈f⌉ = φ(r̃), there is exactly one 2-cell of M labelled f with top r̃. Surjectivity of the local map at q̃ follows by decomposing any atomic 2-path in K with top φ(q̃) as (u,f,v) and applying U2′ to the corresponding subpath of q̃; injectivity follows because two preimages would give two cells with the same top and label. For uniqueness, first show that any universal 2-cover satisfying U1 and U2 also satisfies U2′ by extending r̃ to a root-to-root path and applying local bijectivity. Then compare the natural rooted-tree filtrations of two universal 2-covers by induction: the roots are label-preservingly isomorphic, and U2′ gives a unique corresponding new cell with the same top and label at each next stage.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let eKp be the universal 2-cover of a directed 2-complex K with base 1-path p, with root p̃ and labelling map φ: eKp → K. For every 2-path δ in K with top ⌈δ⌉ = p, there exists a unique 2-path δ̃ in eKp with top ⌈δ̃⌉ = p̃ and φ(δ̃) = δ. Consequently, for every (p,q)-diagram Δ over K there exists a unique 1-path q̃ in eKp and a unique (p̃,q̃)-diagram Δ̃ over eKp such that φ(Δ̃) = Δ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lift δ atom by atom. Suppose an initial segment has been lifted and ends at a lifted 1-path r̃. The next atomic 2-path in δ lies in the 2-star of φ(r̃), and U2 gives a unique preimage in the 2-star of r̃. This proves existence and uniqueness of the lifted 2-path. A diagram Δ corresponds to a 2-path δ, so lifting δ gives a diagram Δ̃ over eKp. If a different 2-path represents the same diagram, it differs by isotopies generated by swaps of independent atomic 2-paths; local maps preserve independence, so the lifted 2-paths are isotopic and have the same diagram. Thus the lift of the diagram is unique.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a directed 2-complex, p a 1-path in K, and eKp the universal 2-cover of K with root p̃. Then the connected component Sq(eKp,p̃) of the Squier complex of eKp is homeomorphic to the universal cover of the connected component Sq(K,p) of the Squier complex of K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the positive-path realization of Squier complexes. A positive path q̃ in the component of p̃ in Ω+(eKp) has a unique expression q̃ = u0 f1,t1 u1 ... fm,tm um, with fi 2-cells in eKp and ti ∈ (0,1). Let r̃ = u0⌈f1⌉u1...⌈fm⌉um and let Ψ̃ = ε(u0)+f1+ε(u1)+...+fm+ε(um) be the corresponding thin diagram. Since eKp is a rooted 2-tree, properties T2 and T3 give a unique reduced diagram Δ̃ from p̃ to r̃. Applying the labelling map φ gives a diagram Δ over K from p to r and a thin diagram Ψ over K such that Δ ◦ Ψ has no dipoles. Farley's model of the universal cover of Sq(K,p) has cubes represented by such pairs (Δ,Ψ); the point corresponding to q̃ is the point with coordinates (t1,...,tm) in that cube. The inverse map is obtained by uniquely lifting diagrams and thin diagrams from K to eKp using Lemma 8.3. Face identifications match because setting ti to 0 or 1 replaces fi by its top or bottom path on both sides.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8.5 and Remark 8.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every directed 2-complex K and every 1-path p in K, the universal cover of Sq(K,p) is contractible. Hence Sq(K,p) is a K(G,1) CW complex for the diagram group G = D(K,p).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The theorem is stated as Farley's contractibility theorem, and the paper also gives a direct proof using Theorem 8.4. By Theorem 8.4, the universal cover of Sq(K,p) is Sq(eKp,p̃), equivalently the component of p̃ in Ω+(eKp). Let K0 ⊂ K1 ⊂ ... be the natural rooted-tree filtration of eKp and let Ωn be the subspace of positive paths in the component of p̃ that lie in Kn. Then Ω0 is the single point p̃. For h ∈ Ωn+1, write h uniquely as h = v0 g1,s1 v1 ... gk,sk vk, where the vi are positive paths in Kn, the gi are positive 2-cells in Kn+1 − Kn in the natural orientation, and si ∈ (0,1]. Define hs = v0 g1,ss1 v1 ... gk,ssk vk for s ∈ [0,1]. At s=1 this is h, at s=0 each new-cell segment is replaced by its top path in Kn, so h0 ∈ Ωn, and if h already lies in Ωn then hs = h for all s. This gives a deformation retraction Ωn+1 → Ωn for every n. Since the component is the ascending union of these Ωn and Ω0 is a point, it is contractible. The K(G,1) conclusion follows from Theorem 3.4, which identifies π1(Sq(K,p),p) with D(K,p).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 9.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a semi-cubical complex. Suppose the cubes of X are partitioned into essential, collapsible, and redundant cubes; suppose there is a strict partial order ≻ on redundant cubes satisfying the descending chain condition; and suppose there is a bijection c ↦ ĉ from redundant n-cubes to collapsible (n+1)-cubes for all n such that c occurs exactly once among the n-faces of ĉ and every other redundant n-face c′ of ĉ satisfies c ≻ c′. Then X is homotopy equivalent to a CW complex Y whose n-cells are in one-to-one correspondence with the essential n-cubes of X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a cubical collapsing-scheme or discrete-Morse argument. For each redundant cube c, its paired collapsible cube ĉ has c as a free face after all smaller redundant faces have been dealt with. The descending chain condition ensures that the required elementary collapses can be ordered so that no infinite descending obstruction occurs. Performing these collapses dimension by dimension removes every collapsible cube together with its redundant free face and preserves homotopy type at each elementary step. The remaining cells are exactly the essential cubes, and their attaching maps define the desired CW complex Y.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 9.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a complete directed 2-complex, let w be a nonempty 1-path in K, and let G = D(K,w). Fix a left forest Tl in Sq(K). For an n-cube c = ε(u0)+f1+...+fn+ε(un) of Sq(K), where the fi are negative 2-cells, call a term ui special if ui is not irreducible, and call a term fi special if the edge (ui−1,fi,*) belongs to Tl. Then there exists a K(G,1) CW complex Yw whose n-cells are in one-to-one correspondence with the thin diagrams c = ε(u0)+f1+...+fn+ε(un) such that all ui are irreducible, no edge (ui−1,fi,*) lies in Tl, and the top path ⌈c⌉ is homotopic to w in K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Lemma 9.1 to Sq(K,w). Classify a cube as essential if it has no special term. If it has a special term, inspect the leftmost special term: if it is some ui, call the cube redundant; if it is some fi, call it collapsible. Define a partial order on redundant cubes by c ≻ c′ if ⌊c⌋ reduces positively to ⌊c′⌋, and if the bottom paths are equal, by moving cells to the right in the thin diagram. Noetherianity of K and a lexicographic length decrease for right-moving cells give the descending chain condition. For a redundant cube c whose leftmost special term is ui, let (p,f,q) be the left-forest edge assigned to ui, so ui = p⌊f⌋q. Insert the cell f at that position to get a collapsible cube ĉ. Its face obtained by replacing f by its bottom path is exactly c; all other redundant faces either have more reduced bottom path or are obtained by moving a cell to the right, hence are smaller. This gives the required collapsing scheme. Since Sq(K,w) is already a K(G,1) by Theorem 8.5, the homotopy equivalent complex Yw is also a K(G,1), with cells indexed by the essential cubes described.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 9.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a complete directed 2-complex, let w be a nonempty 1-path in K, and let G = D(K,w). With the same notion of essential cubes in Sq(K,w) determined by a left forest as in Theorem 9.2, the integer homology group Hn(G; Z) is free Abelian for every n ≥ 0, and a free basis for Hn(G; Z) is given by the essential n-cubes of Sq(K,w).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the cubical cellular chain complex Pn freely generated by n-cubes of Sq(K,w), with boundary ∂c = Σi (−1)^i(⌈c⌉i − ⌊c⌋i). The collapsing scheme from Theorem 9.2 defines a chain-equivalent complex Q generated by essential cubes via an endomorphism φ: P → P: φ is the identity on essential cubes, zero on collapsible cubes, and on a redundant cube c is defined recursively from the relation ∂ĉ = ±c + lower terms for its paired collapsible cube ĉ. The key lemma in the proof states that if c = ε(u0)+f1+...+fn+ε(un) and cbar is obtained by replacing every ui by its irreducible form, then φ(c) = cbar if cbar is essential and φ(c) = 0 if cbar is collapsible. This is proved by Noetherian induction, using the ordering from the collapsing scheme and the fact that opposite faces of a thin cube have the same irreducible replacement. It follows that φ(⌈c⌉i) = φ(⌊c⌋i) for every face pair, so the induced differential δ = φ∂ on Q is identically zero. Therefore Hn(G; Z) is Qn itself, the free Abelian group on essential n-cubes.

proof_techniques.name: Encode local rewrites as a semi-cubical Squier complex
proof_techniques.description: The paper builds Sq(K) from local directed homotopies in a directed 2-complex K. Vertices are 1-paths; edges are atomic replacements of a subpath ⌈f⌉ by ⌊f⌋; higher cubes are thin diagrams consisting of several mutually separated 2-cells, and each facet is obtained by resolving one of those cells to its top or bottom path. The attaching maps are canonical because replacing one independent cell does not affect the coordinates of the others. This construction is precisely designed to turn a system of local moves with commuting independent moves into a cubical or semi-cubical complex.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The complex F_w also has k-cells indexed by objects with k independent local binary resolutions, namely crossings of a matching. A useful route is to identify matchings and crossing resolutions with thin diagrams or simultaneous local rewrites, so that F_w becomes a Squier-type complex or a subcomplex/cover of one.

proof_techniques.name: Use planar diagrams and dipole normal forms
proof_techniques.description: Diagram groups in the paper are controlled by planar diagrams over directed 2-complexes. Concatenation glues diagrams, and the only cancellation allowed is removal of dipoles, pairs of adjacent inverse cells. The Kilibarda normal-form theorem gives a unique reduced representative in every equivalence class. This makes it possible to prove injectivity, identify fundamental groups, and show that certain constructions do not introduce ambiguity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a matching can be represented as a planar diagram recording pairings or reductions, uniqueness after canceling obvious inverse local features could provide a canonical model for cells of F_w and prevent distinct resolution sequences from producing unwanted identifications.

proof_techniques.name: Recognize the desired complex as a universal cover of a Squier complex
proof_techniques.description: The contractibility theorem is not that every Squier complex is contractible, but that its universal cover is. The universal cover is described concretely as Sq(eKp,p̃), where eKp is the universal 2-cover of K. The lifting lemma says that every 2-path or diagram over K with top p lifts uniquely to eKp, and the rooted 2-tree property of eKp kills all spherical diagrams at the root.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If F_w parametrizes reduction diagrams with a fixed boundary word and no monodromy, it may correspond more naturally to a lifted Squier component than to the base Squier complex. Establishing a unique lifting/canonical boundary condition for matchings would allow contractibility to follow from Theorem 8.5.

proof_techniques.name: Rooted 2-tree deformation retraction
proof_techniques.description: A rooted 2-tree is built by repeatedly attaching 2-cells along old top paths and new bottom arcs. Its positive-path space is filtered by the finite stages of this construction. The key deformation retract sends a positive path using newly attached cells to the path obtained by moving all new-cell parameters continuously to 0, thereby replacing each new-cell slice by its old top path. This retracts Ωn+1 onto Ωn and ultimately onto the root path.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A direct proof of contractibility of F_w might mimic this filtration: find a hierarchy of matchings or arcs in which the newest local features can be continuously/cubically retracted to older ones, fixing the previous stage.

proof_techniques.name: Collapsing scheme via the leftmost reducible or special term
proof_techniques.description: For complete directed 2-complexes, the paper constructs a discrete Morse matching on Sq(K). Each cube is scanned from left to right; if no term is special it is essential, if the first special term is an irreducible-context failure the cube is redundant, and if the first special term is a forest edge the cube is collapsible. A well-founded order based on positive reduction and moving cells to the right guarantees that each redundant cube is a free face of a unique collapsible cube after lower collapses. This yields a homotopy equivalent CW complex with only essential cells.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If F_w admits a canonical choice of a first bad crossing, first reducible submatching, or first noncanonical pairing, the same redundant/collapsible pairing could collapse F_w. Contractibility would follow if the only essential cell is a single 0-cell or if the essential subcomplex is visibly contractible.

proof_techniques.name: Tietze and subdivision invariance for directed 2-complexes
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves that adding an edge identified with an existing path, replacing cells by cells whose top and bottom are homotopic through a subcomplex, and subdividing 2-cells preserve the relevant diagram groups. The proofs work by replacing cells with fixed diagrams and by observing that reduced diagrams cannot contain newly introduced edges except inside dipoles.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When encoding free reductions or matching moves, the most convenient directed 2-complex may not initially match the combinatorics of F_w. These invariance moves justify changing the presentation or subdividing local moves to align cubes with individual crossings/resolutions.

proof_techniques.name: Positive-path realization of cubes
proof_techniques.description: Instead of viewing Sq(K) only combinatorially, the paper realizes it as Ω+(K), the space of paths that may pass through 2-cells at heights t ∈ [0,1]. A thin n-cube is literally parametrized by the heights of n cell-crossings in a positive path, and setting a parameter to 0 or 1 is the same as taking the corresponding top or bottom face.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The cells of F_w are defined by local resolutions of crossings. A positive-path model may help turn these binary choices into actual cubical coordinates and verify that the given attaching maps agree with standard Squier face maps.

other_useful_info: Remark 3.2 states that the realization of Sq(K) does not depend on the chosen orientation of the 2-cells; changing orientation only changes cube reference points. This is useful when relations are symmetric. Remark 8.7 records Farley's stronger result that, for finite K, the connected components of the universal cover of Sq(K) are CAT(0) cubical complexes, giving another source of contractibility. The paper emphasizes that empty paths give isolated points in Sq(K), so nonempty base paths are the substantive case. Theorem 4.3 shows that diagram groups over arbitrary directed 2-complexes are the same class as diagram groups over semigroup presentations after suitable subdivisions, so one can freely move between geometric directed-complex language and rewriting-system language. The main cited precursor for CAT(0) and contractibility of Squier universal covers is Farley [14], while the diagram normal-form and presentation machinery comes from Guba-Sapir [16].
why_relevant: Develops Squier complexes and proves contractibility results for their universal covers. F_w resembles a cubical rewriting/resolution complex for free reductions, so diagram-group techniques are a plausible route.

title: Subword Complexes in Coxeter Groups
overall_summary: The paper studies subword complexes Δ(Q,π) attached to a word Q in the simple reflections of a Coxeter system and an element π of the Coxeter group. Its central topological result is that every subword complex is vertex-decomposable and hence shellable, and in fact is homeomorphic to a ball or a sphere. The proof combines a deletion-link recursion on the first letter of Q, the Coxeter exchange axiom, and the Demazure product, which detects when an arbitrary word contains a reduced expression for a given element. For the original problem, the most relevant transferable pattern is: encode choices of reductions or resolutions as a combinatorial complex, prove a recursive shelling, control codimension-one incidences by an exchange-type lemma, and then conclude ball/contractibility once the sphere case is excluded.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.1 and Lemma 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (Π,Σ) be a Coxeter system with Coxeter length ℓ, let Q=(σ1,...,σm) be an ordered word in simple reflections σi∈Σ, and let π∈Π. A subword P⊆Q means an ordered subsequence with its positions remembered. A subword P represents π if its ordered product is a reduced expression for π, and P contains π if some subword of P represents π. The subword complex Δ(Q,π) is the simplicial complex whose faces are complements Q∖P such that P contains π. Lemma 2.2 says that Δ(Q,π) is pure and that its facets are exactly the complements Q∖P where P⊆Q represents π. Consequently every facet has m−ℓ(π) vertices, so Δ(Q,π) has dimension m−ℓ(π)−1 when nonvoid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If Q∖P is a face, then P contains a subword D representing π. Since D⊆P, the complement Q∖D contains Q∖P as a larger face. Thus every face lies in a face whose complement is a reduced expression for π. Conversely, if P itself represents π, then no proper subword of P can still contain π, because all reduced expressions for π have length ℓ(π). Hence Q∖P is maximal among faces. This identifies the facets and proves purity, since all complements of reduced expressions for π have the same cardinality m−ℓ(π).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every Coxeter system (Π,Σ), every word Q in Σ, and every π∈Π, the subword complex Δ(Q,π) is vertex-decomposable; therefore it is shellable. Here a pure simplicial complex is vertex-decomposable if it is {∅} or if it has a vertex v such that both the deletion del(v,Δ), consisting of faces not containing v, and the link link(v,Δ), consisting of faces G disjoint from v with G∪{v}∈Δ, are vertex-decomposable. The implication vertex-decomposable implies shellable is the standard Provan-Billera result used by the paper.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Induct on the length of the word Q. Write Q=(σ,σ2,...,σm)=(σ,Q′), where σ is the first simple reflection and Q′ is the remaining word. Consider the vertex of Δ(Q,π) corresponding to the first position σ. The link of this vertex consists of faces G supported on Q′ such that G∪{σ} is a face of Δ(Q,π). In complement language this means that Q′∖G contains π, so the link is naturally Δ(Q′,π). For the deletion, a face G supported on Q′ lies in del(σ,Δ(Q,π)) precisely when the complement σ together with Q′∖G contains π. There are two cases. If ℓ(σπ)>ℓ(π), then no reduced expression for π can begin with σ, so any reduced subword witnessing containment of π avoids the first σ; hence the deletion equals Δ(Q′,π), the same as the link. If ℓ(σπ)<ℓ(π), then using the first σ to build π is equivalent to having the tail contain σπ, and the deletion is Δ(Q′,σπ). In both cases the link and deletion are subword complexes on the shorter word Q′, so the induction applies.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.1 and Lemma 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (Π,Σ) be a Coxeter system. For a word P=(σ1,...,σm) in Σ, its Demazure product δ(P) is defined by the multiplication rule eτ eσ = eτσ if ℓ(τσ)>ℓ(τ), and eτ eσ = eτ if ℓ(τσ)<ℓ(τ), for τ∈Π and σ∈Σ; δ(P) is the element satisfying eσ1...eσm=eδ(P). Let ≥ denote Bruhat order, where τ≥π means that a reduced word for τ contains a reduced word for π. Lemma 3.4 states: (1) δ(P)≥π if and only if P contains π. (2) If δ(P)=π, then every subword P′⊆P that contains π has δ(P′)=π. (3) If δ(P)>π, then P contains a subword T representing some element τ with τ>π and |T|=ℓ(τ)=ℓ(π)+1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For part (1), first suppose δ(P)=π′≥π. Scan P from left to right and omit exactly those reflections that do not increase length in the Demazure multiplication. The retained subword is a reduced expression for π′. Since π′≥π in Bruhat order, this reduced expression contains a reduced expression for π, so P contains π. Conversely, suppose a subword T⊆P represents π. The proof is by induction on |P|. Let σ be the last reflection of P. By definition of Demazure product, δ(P∖σ) is either δ(P) or δ(P)σ, and δ(P)σ<δ(P). If πσ>π, then a reduced expression for π cannot end by using the last σ, so T⊆P∖σ, and induction gives π≤δ(P∖σ)≤δ(P). If πσ<π, then a subword T′ of T represents πσ, and T′⊆P∖σ; induction gives πσ≤δ(P∖σ), and the Bruhat compatibility with multiplying by σ yields π≤δ(P). For part (2), if P′⊆P contains π, then by part (1) δ(P′)≥π; also P contains δ(P′), so part (1) gives δ(P)≥δ(P′). Since δ(P)=π, this forces δ(P′)=π. For part (3), choose an element τ on a saturated Bruhat chain from π to δ(P), so π<τ≤δ(P) and ℓ(τ)=ℓ(π)+1. By part (1), P contains a reduced expression T for τ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T be a word in the simple reflections of a Coxeter system, and let π be a Coxeter group element such that |T|=ℓ(π)+1. Then: (1) there are at most two positions σ in T such that T∖σ represents π; (2) if δ(T)=π, then there are two distinct positions σ in T such that T∖σ represents π; and (3) if T itself is a reduced expression representing an element τ with τ>π, then there is exactly one position σ in T such that T∖σ represents π.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For part (1), assume toward contradiction that three positions σ1,σ2,σ3 in increasing order can each be omitted to obtain a reduced expression for π. Write T=T1 σ1 T2 σ2 T3 σ3 T4. Equality of the two products obtained by omitting σ1 and σ2 gives T1 T2 σ2 T3 σ3 T4 = T1 σ1 T2 T3 σ3 T4 in the group. Cancelling the common left factor T1 and right factor T3 σ3 T4 gives T2 σ2 = σ1 T2. Then the product T1 σ1 T2 σ2 T3 T4 equals T1 σ1 σ1 T2 T3 T4 = T1 T2 T3 T4. This expresses π by a word of length |T|−2, contradicting ℓ(π)=|T|−1. For part (2), δ(T)=π and |T|=ℓ(π)+1 imply that in the Demazure scan exactly one reflection σ is skipped because it decreases length, and omitting this σ gives a reduced expression for π. More explicitly, write T=T1 σ T2, where T1T2 represents π and, if T1 represents τ1, then τ1σ<τ1. By the Coxeter exchange axiom, omitting some reflection σ′ from T1 gives a reduced expression for τ1σ. It follows that both T∖σ and T∖σ′ represent π, giving two distinct omissions. Part (3) is the Coxeter exchange condition in the case of a reduced word T for τ and a Bruhat-covering subelement π of length one less.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Δ be a shellable simplicial complex such that every codimension-one face of Δ is contained in at most two facets. Then Δ is a topological manifold with boundary and is homeomorphic to either a ball or a sphere. Moreover, the facets of the topological boundary of Δ are exactly the codimension-one faces of Δ that are contained in exactly one facet of Δ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Björner-Las Vergnas-Sturmfels-White-Ziegler, Oriented Matroids, Proposition 4.7.22. The invoked result says that a shellable pure complex with no codimension-one face contained in more than two facets is a shellable pseudomanifold with possible boundary, and shellability forces such a complex to have the topology of a ball when the boundary is nonempty and of a sphere when the boundary is empty. The description of boundary facets follows from the same proposition: in a manifold-with-boundary triangulation, the boundary ridges are precisely those incident to only one top-dimensional facet.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (Π,Σ) be a Coxeter system, let Q be a word in Σ, and let π∈Π. The subword complex Δ(Q,π) is either a ball or a sphere. More precisely, for any face Q∖P of Δ(Q,π), meaning P⊆Q contains π, the face Q∖P lies in the topological boundary of Δ(Q,π) if and only if the Demazure product δ(P) is not equal to π.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First consider a codimension-one face Q∖T of Δ(Q,π). Since facets are complements of reduced expressions for π, such a T has length ℓ(π)+1 and contains π. The facets containing Q∖T correspond exactly to positions σ in T such that T∖σ represents π. Lemma 3.5(1) says there are at most two such positions. Since Δ(Q,π) is shellable by Theorem 2.5, Lemma 3.6 applies and gives the ball-or-sphere conclusion. For the boundary criterion, let Q∖P be a face. If δ(P)≠π, then because P contains π, Lemma 3.4 gives δ(P)>π. Lemma 3.4(3) provides a subword T⊆P representing an element τ>π with |T|=ℓ(π)+1. Then Q∖T is a codimension-one face containing Q∖P. Since T is reduced for τ>π, Lemma 3.5(3) says Q∖T lies in exactly one facet, hence it is a boundary ridge by Lemma 3.6; therefore its subface Q∖P is also in the boundary. Conversely, suppose δ(P)=π. Let Q∖T be any codimension-one face containing Q∖P. Then T⊆P, T contains π, and |T|=ℓ(π)+1. Lemma 3.4(2) gives δ(T)=π, and Lemma 3.5(2) says Q∖T is contained in two facets. By Lemma 3.6 all such codimension-one faces are interior ridges, and hence Q∖P is an interior face rather than a boundary face.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a Coxeter system (Π,Σ), a word Q in Σ, and π∈Π, the subword complex Δ(Q,π) is a sphere if δ(Q)=π and is a ball otherwise.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Theorem 3.7 to the empty face, whose complement is the whole word Q. If δ(Q)=π, then the empty face is not in the boundary, so the boundary is empty and the ball-or-sphere complex must be a sphere. If δ(Q)≠π, then the empty face lies in the boundary, so the boundary is nonempty and Lemma 3.6 gives that the complex is a ball.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Topological consequence used in Lemma 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Δ=Δ(Q,π) be a subword complex and let F=Q∖P be a face. Identify the vertices not in F with the positions of P. Then link(F,Δ) is naturally the subword complex Δ(P,π). Therefore, if ℓ=ℓ(π), the link of F is a sphere of dimension |P|−ℓ−1 when δ(P)=π, and is contractible, indeed a ball, when δ(P)≠π.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A face H lies in link(Q∖P,Δ) exactly when H is disjoint from Q∖P, so H⊆P, and (Q∖P)∪H is a face of Δ(Q,π). The complement of (Q∖P)∪H inside Q is P∖H, so this condition says exactly that P∖H contains π. This is precisely the definition of H being a face of Δ(P,π), after viewing H as the complement in P of P∖H. Now apply Corollary 3.8 to the word P: if δ(P)=π the link is spherical of dimension |P|−ℓ(π)−1; if F is a face and δ(P)≠π, then δ(P)>π by Lemma 3.4, so Corollary 3.8 says Δ(P,π) is a ball, hence contractible.

proof_techniques.name: Complement-of-certificates complex
proof_techniques.description: The paper encodes reduced expressions indirectly: facets of Δ(Q,π) are not the reduced subwords P themselves, but their complements Q∖P. More generally, a face is the complement of any subword P that contains some reduced expression for π. This reversal of containment makes the complex pure automatically, because all minimal certificates for containing π have the same size ℓ(π), and it turns enlargement of a face into deletion of unnecessary letters from a certificate.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, one possible route is to encode a matching or reduction certificate by the complement of the data that certifies reducibility, so that maximal cells or facets correspond to minimal reduction patterns. This may turn the topology of matchings into a shellable complex controlled by minimal cancellation certificates.

proof_techniques.name: Deletion-link recursion on the first generator
proof_techniques.description: The vertex-decomposition proof always peels off the first letter σ of the word Q. The link of that vertex is Δ(Q′,π), where Q′ is Q with σ removed. The deletion is either the same complex Δ(Q′,π), when σ cannot start a reduced expression for π, or Δ(Q′,σπ), when σ is a left descent of π. Thus both local pieces are subword complexes of strictly shorter words, giving a clean induction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A comparable induction for F_w would peel off a distinguished local feature, such as the first boundary point or a forced inverse match, and identify the link and deletion or analogous local subcomplexes with the same construction for shorter words. The quasi-reduced hypothesis may be useful precisely for making such local cases well behaved.

proof_techniques.name: Demazure product as a monotone closure invariant
proof_techniques.description: The Demazure product δ(P) is a length-nonincreasing modification of the usual product: when multiplying by a simple reflection that would decrease Coxeter length, the product stays fixed. Lemma 3.4 shows that this invariant exactly detects containment: δ(P)≥π in Bruhat order if and only if P contains a reduced expression for π. It also distinguishes interior from boundary data: δ(P)=π means no excess beyond π, while δ(P)>π means P contains a one-step larger reduced subword.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For free-group matching complexes, an analogous closure or normal-form invariant could track whether a partial configuration still certifies that the word reduces to the identity and whether it contains excess reducibility. Such an invariant could replace ad hoc cancellation arguments with a boundary criterion.

proof_techniques.name: Exchange axiom controls codimension-one incidence
proof_techniques.description: The proof of the ball-or-sphere theorem reduces codimension-one topology to a length ℓ(π)+1 word T. Facets containing the ridge Q∖T correspond to one-letter deletions T∖σ that represent π. Lemma 3.5, derived from the Coxeter exchange axiom, says there are at most two such deletions; there are two in the Demazure-product-equal case and exactly one when T is reduced for a Bruhat-larger element. This is the local combinatorial fact that makes the complex a pseudomanifold with boundary.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, the analogous target would be a local exchange lemma for matchings or cancellations: a codimension-one partial configuration should have at most two completions/resolutions, with a criterion for when it has one versus two. Proving such a lemma would make the global topological argument much closer to the subword-complex proof.

proof_techniques.name: Shellable pseudomanifold implies ball or sphere
proof_techniques.description: After shellability is established, the paper does not compute homotopy groups directly. Instead it verifies that each codimension-one face is in at most two facets, invokes the standard shellable pseudomanifold criterion, and obtains that the whole complex is homeomorphic to a ball or sphere. The boundary is then identified combinatorially as the set of ridges incident to only one facet.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a barycentric subdivision or face poset model of F_w can be shown shellable and to have controlled codimension-one incidences, contractibility would follow once one proves that the spherical case cannot occur or that the resulting object has nonempty boundary.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary and links from smaller ambient words
proof_techniques.description: For any face Q∖P of a subword complex, its link is naturally Δ(P,π), the same construction with the ambient word replaced by the complement P. Corollary 3.8 then classifies every link as a sphere or ball according to δ(P)=π or δ(P)≠π. This gives a local-to-global mechanism: the same invariant that classifies the whole complex also classifies all links.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, it would be useful to show that the link of a cell is again a matching complex for a smaller or residual word. Then an inductive classification of links as contractible or spherical could support a proof that the whole CW complex is a ball or otherwise contractible.

proof_techniques.name: Canonical simplification and acyclic facet orientation
proof_techniques.description: In Section 4 and Remark 4.5, the paper considers simplifying a subword P to the lexicographically first reduced subword D with the same Demazure product. Across an interior ridge, the facet adjacency graph is oriented toward the facet determined by this simplification. The paper notes that this directed graph is acyclic because the ridge relation is a subrelation of lexicographic order.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, a canonical way to simplify a crossing matching to a preferred resolved or reduced matching could orient the adjacency graph of cells and potentially yield a discrete Morse function or shelling order.

other_useful_info: The paper uses the convention that {∅} is a sphere of dimension −1 with reduced homology Z in dimension −1; this matters for base cases where the ambient word itself is a reduced expression. The introduction points to Björner-Wachs on shellability of Bruhat intervals and Björner on weak-order intervals being homotopy equivalent to balls or spheres as related Coxeter-theoretic topology. The paper cites Provan-Billera for vertex-decomposable implies shellable and Björner-Las Vergnas-Sturmfels-White-Ziegler, Oriented Matroids, Proposition 4.7.22, for the shellable pseudomanifold ball-or-sphere criterion. Remark 4.5 states that the lexicographic Demazure-simplification orientation of the facet adjacency graph is acyclic, but the stronger claim that every linear extension gives a shelling is presented only as a belief, not as a proved theorem.
why_relevant: Subword complexes are shellable balls or spheres built from choices of resolving/deleting letters in words. Since uncrossing posets are related to Bruhat order, these results may provide an alternative shellability or contractibility framework for F_w.

title: Crossings and Nestings of Matchings and Partitions
overall_summary: The paper proves that, for set partitions and in particular for complete matchings, the maximum size of a crossing family and the maximum size of a nesting family have a symmetric joint distribution, even after fixing the sets of minimal and maximal block elements. The core technique is a bijection between set partitions and vacillating tableaux built from RSK row insertion and deletion; under this bijection, the crossing number is the maximum number of rows of a shape encountered and the nesting number is the maximum number of columns. Conjugating every Young diagram in the vacillating tableau gives the symmetry. For complete matchings the construction specializes to oscillating tableaux and to lattice walks in Weyl chambers, giving explicit models and enumerations for k-noncrossing matchings. For the original problem, the most useful content is the dynamic tableau encoding of matchings/partitions, the way crossing information is converted into subsequence/order information, and the fixed-endpoint/canonical noncrossing constructions that may help organize induction or discrete Morse collapses on crossing resolutions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2 bijection ψ: vacillating tableaux and decorated set partitions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let [n] = {1,...,n}. A vacillating tableau of shape λ and length 2n is a sequence λ_0, λ_1, ..., λ_{2n} of integer partitions such that λ_0 = ∅, λ_{2n} = λ, λ_{2i+1} is obtained from λ_{2i} by doing nothing or deleting one square, and λ_{2i} is obtained from λ_{2i−1} by doing nothing or adding one square. There is a bijection ψ from vacillating tableaux of shape λ and length 2n to pairs (P,T), where P is a set partition of [n] and T is a standard Young tableau of shape λ whose entries form a subset of max(P), the set of maximal elements of the blocks of P. In particular, when λ = ∅, this gives a bijection between set partitions of [n] and vacillating tableaux of empty shape and length 2n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Given V = (∅ = λ_0, λ_1, ..., λ_{2n} = λ), recursively construct pairs (P_i,T_i), where P_i is a set of ordered pairs from [n] and T_i is an SYT of shape λ_i. Start with P_0 empty and T_0 empty. If λ_i = λ_{i−1}, set (P_i,T_i) = (P_{i−1},T_{i−1}). If λ_i strictly contains λ_{i−1}, then i = 2k for some k, set P_i = P_{i−1}, and obtain T_i from T_{i−1} by placing the entry k in the newly added square. If λ_i is strictly contained in λ_{i−1}, then i = 2k−1 for some k; use inverse row insertion to write T_{i−1} as T_i ← j for a uniquely determined integer j, and set P_i = P_{i−1} ∪ {(j,k)}. The construction ensures that each integer occurs at most once as a first coordinate and at most once as a second coordinate, and all edges increase, so P_{2n} is the standard representation of a set partition P into directed paths. Any entry left in T_{2n} cannot have an outgoing edge, hence is maximal in its block, giving content(T_{2n}) ⊆ max(P). Conversely, given (P,T), set T_{2n}=T and reconstruct backwards for k=n,n−1,...,1: delete k from T_{2k} if k appears to obtain T_{2k−1}, otherwise do nothing; then, if the standard representation of P has an edge (i,k), obtain T_{2k−2} by row-inserting i into T_{2k−1}, otherwise do nothing. These two procedures are inverse.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5: enumeration of vacillating tableaux
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let λ be an integer partition of k, let g_λ(n) be the number of vacillating tableaux of shape λ and length 2n, let f^λ be the number of standard Young tableaux of shape λ with content [k], and let B(n,k) be the number of set partitions of [n] with k distinguished blocks. Then g_λ(n) = B(n,k) f^λ. Moreover the exponential generating function of B(n,k) is Σ_{n≥0} B(n,k) x^n/n! = (1/k!)(e^x−1)^k exp(e^x−1).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply the bijection ψ. For a fixed set partition P, choosing the content of T is exactly choosing k maximal block elements, equivalently choosing k distinguished blocks of P; once those k contents are chosen, the number of SYT of shape λ on that ordered set is f^λ, because only the relative order matters. This gives g_λ(n)=B(n,k)f^λ. For the generating function, a partition with k distinguished blocks is obtained by splitting the label set into the union of elements lying in the distinguished blocks and elements lying in ordinary blocks, partitioning the first part into exactly k nonempty blocks and marking them, and partitioning the second part arbitrarily. The exponential generating function is therefore the product of the EGF for k unordered nonempty blocks, (e^x−1)^k/k!, and the Bell-number EGF exp(e^x−1).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3 map φ: inverse construction from partitions to vacillating tableaux
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a set partition P of [n] with standard representation by arcs joining consecutive elements in each block, the inverse φ(P) of ψ can be constructed directly as follows. Set T_{2n}=∅ and scan j from n down to 1, defining T_{2j−1} and T_{2j−2}. If j is a right endpoint of an arc (i,j) but not a left endpoint, first do nothing and then row-insert i. If j is a left endpoint of an arc (j,k) but not a right endpoint, first delete j and then do nothing. If j is isolated, do nothing twice. If j is both a right endpoint of (i,j) and a left endpoint of (j,k), first delete j and then row-insert i. The sequence of shapes of the tableaux T_0,T_1,...,T_{2n} is the vacillating tableau φ(P).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the inverse procedure in the special case λ=∅ of the bijection ψ, written in a way that uses the four possible local statuses of j in the standard representation: right endpoint only, left endpoint only, isolated point, or internal point of a block. Scanning from right to left means that when a right endpoint j is encountered, the corresponding left endpoint i becomes active and is inserted into the tableau; when a left endpoint j is encountered, its active entry is removed. The RSK inverse/forward insertion rules ensure that this recovers exactly the tableaux that ψ would have produced.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6: crossing and nesting numbers from vacillating tableaux
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a set partition of [n], with standard representation consisting of arcs (i,j) between consecutive elements of each block. A k-crossing of P is a set of arcs (i_1,j_1),...,(i_k,j_k) such that i_1 < i_2 < ... < i_k < j_1 < j_2 < ... < j_k. A k-nesting is a set of arcs (i_1,j_1),...,(i_k,j_k) such that i_1 < i_2 < ... < i_k < j_k < ... < j_2 < j_1. Let cr(P) be the maximum k for which P has a k-crossing, and let ne(P) be the maximum k for which P has a k-nesting. If φ(P) = (∅ = λ_0, λ_1, ..., λ_{2n} = ∅), then cr(P) is the maximum, over all i, of the number of rows of λ_i, and ne(P) is the maximum, over all i, of the number of columns of λ_i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let T(P) = (T_0,...,T_{2n}) be the sequence of SYT constructed by φ. An arc (i,j) of P is characterized by the fact that the entry i appears in exactly the tableaux T_{2i}, T_{2i+1}, ..., T_{2j−2}; the paper says that i is added at step i and leaves at step j. Thus arcs (i_1,j_1),...,(i_k,j_k) form a k-crossing exactly when at some common time the entries i_1<...<i_k are all present and they leave in increasing order of their values; they form a k-nesting exactly when at some common time they are all present and leave in decreasing order. For each tableau T_i, define a word σ_i recursively backwards: σ_{2n} is empty; if T_i=T_{i−1}, set σ_{i−1}=σ_i; if T_{i−1} is obtained from T_i by row-inserting j, set σ_{i−1}=σ_i j; and if T_i is obtained from T_{i−1} by adding entry i/2, delete i/2 from σ_i to obtain σ_{i−1}. Then σ_i is a permutation of content(T_i), and if σ_i=w_1...w_r, the entries leave T(P) in the order w_r,...,w_1. The key claim is that the RSK insertion tableau of σ_i is exactly T_i. This is proved by backward induction. The only nontrivial case is deleting the largest entry j from σ_i: writing σ_i = u_1...u_s j v_1...v_t with j larger than every other letter, one proves by induction on t that removing j from the word removes exactly the square containing j from the insertion tableau. The insertion-path analysis splits into whether j lies on the insertion path of v_t; if it does, j, being largest, is bumped only into the next row as a terminal entry, and if it does not, the same insertion path occurs in the tableau with j removed. Finally apply Schensted's theorem: for a word with distinct letters and insertion shape λ, the longest increasing subsequence has length equal to the number of columns of λ, and the longest decreasing subsequence has length equal to the number of rows of λ. Since crossings correspond to decreasing subsequences of some σ_i and nestings correspond to increasing subsequences of some σ_i, the result follows.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1: fixed-minimum/fixed-maximum symmetry of crossings and nestings
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For subsets S,T of [n] with |S|=|T|, let P_n(S,T) be the set of set partitions P of [n] such that min(P)=S and max(P)=T, where min(P) and max(P) are respectively the sets of minimal and maximal elements of blocks of P. Let f_{n,S,T}(i,j) be the number of P in P_n(S,T) with cr(P)=i and ne(P)=j, where cr and ne are the maximum crossing and nesting sizes in the standard representation. Then f_{n,S,T}(i,j) = f_{n,S,T}(j,i) for all i,j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the bijection φ from P_n(S,T) to vacillating tableaux of empty shape. By Theorem 6, cr(P) is the maximum number of rows among all shapes in φ(P), and ne(P) is the maximum number of columns. Define an involution τ on vacillating tableaux by replacing every Young diagram λ_i with its conjugate λ_i′. This preserves the allowed vacillating moves, because adding, deleting, or not changing one square remains an allowed move after conjugation. It swaps the maximum number of rows and the maximum number of columns. The local move pattern around the pair of times 2i−2, 2i−1, 2i determines whether i is a left endpoint only, right endpoint only, isolated point, or internal point of a block; conjugation preserves whether the step is an addition, deletion, or equality, hence preserves min(P) and max(P). Therefore τ induces an involution of P_n(S,T) that interchanges cr and ne.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollaries 2, 3, and 4: consequences for partitions and complete matchings
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let NCN_{k,l}(n) be the number of set partitions of [n] that have no k-crossing and no l-nesting, equivalently cr(P)<k and ne(P)<l. Then NCN_{k,l}(n)=NCN_{l,k}(n). In particular, the number NC_k(n) of k-noncrossing partitions of [n] equals the number NN_k(n) of k-nonnesting partitions of [n]. For complete matchings on [2n], viewed as set partitions into n blocks of size 2, the same symmetry holds: for every pair of disjoint subsets S,T of [2n] with |S|=|T|=n, cr and ne have symmetric joint distribution on P_{2n}(S,T); summing over such S,T, the number of matchings with cr=i and ne=j equals the number with cr=j and ne=i; and the number of k-noncrossing matchings equals the number of k-nonnesting matchings.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For set partitions, sum the equality f_{n,S,T}(i,j)=f_{n,S,T}(j,i) from Theorem 1 over all S,T and over the relevant ranges 1≤i<k and 1≤j<l. For complete matchings, observe that a partition of [2n] is a complete matching exactly when |min(P)|=|max(P)|=n and min(P)∩max(P)=∅, equivalently the chosen S and T are disjoint n-element subsets. Restrict Theorem 1 to those S,T and then sum.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 10: lattice-walk model for k-noncrossing partitions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let k≥2 and let V_k = {(a_1,...,a_{k−1}) in Z^{k−1}: a_1 ≥ a_2 ≥ ... ≥ a_{k−1} ≥ 0}. The number of k-noncrossing set partitions of [n], meaning partitions P with cr(P)<k, equals the number of closed lattice walks of length 2n in V_k from the origin to itself with steps in {±ε_1,...,±ε_{k−1},0}, where ε_i is the ith unit coordinate vector, subject to the parity rule that after an even number of steps the walk either moves by −ε_i or stays still, and after an odd number of steps the walk either moves by +ε_i or stays still.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Theorem 6, a partition is k-noncrossing exactly when every shape λ_i in φ(P) has at most k−1 rows. Record λ_i by its row-length vector (a_1,...,a_{k−1}), padding with zeroes. The Young-diagram condition is exactly a_1≥...≥a_{k−1}≥0. A vacillating deletion or addition changes exactly one coordinate by −1 or +1, and doing nothing gives the zero step. The definition of vacillating tableaux imposes the parity rule: odd-index intermediate shapes arise from doing nothing or deleting after an even time, and even-index shapes arise from doing nothing or adding after an odd time. The empty initial and final shapes give a closed walk from the origin to itself, and the construction is reversible.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Fixed endpoint Motzkin-path characterization and canonical 2-noncrossing/2-nonnesting partitions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For subsets S,T of [n] with |S|=|T|, the set P_n(S,T) of partitions with min(P)=S and max(P)=T is nonempty if and only if |S∩[i]| ≥ |T∩[i]| for every i in [n]. Equivalently, define a lattice path L(S,T) from (0,0) to (2n,0) by reading i=1,...,n and taking two steps as follows: if i is in S∩T take (1,0),(1,0); if i is in S−T take (1,0),(1,1); if i is in T−S take (1,−1),(1,0); if i is in neither S nor T take (1,−1),(1,1). Then P_n(S,T) is nonempty if and only if L(S,T) is a Motzkin path, i.e. never goes below the x-axis. When L(S,T) is Motzkin, the paper gives explicit recovery of the unique noncrossing partition and the unique nonnesting partition with these min and max sets: write the path heights as a_0,...,a_{2n}, let A=[n]−T={i_1<...<i_t} and B=[n]−S={j_1<...<j_t}. The noncrossing standard representation pairs each j_r with max{i_s in A: i_s<j_r and a_{2i_s}=a_{2j_r−2}}, while the nonnesting standard representation pairs j_r with i_r for every r.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The prefix inequality counts open blocks: among the first i labels, the number of block minima already seen must be at least the number of block maxima already seen. The path L(S,T) is just a height encoding of this open-block count with two half-steps for each label, so the same condition is that its height never becomes negative. Conversely, the displayed pairing rules construct standard representations with the prescribed left and right endpoint sets. Pairing a right endpoint to the most recent compatible left endpoint at the same height gives the stack-like noncrossing construction; pairing the rth available left endpoint to the rth right endpoint gives the queue-like nonnesting construction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 8 and Corollary 9: nestings as decreasing subsequences and Greene sums
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a set partition whose standard representation has arcs (i_1,j_1),...,(i_t,j_t), ordered so that i_r<j_r for each r and j_1<j_2<...<j_t. Let α(P)=i_1 i_2 ... i_t be the word of left endpoints in increasing order of right endpoints. Then there is a one-to-one correspondence between nestings of P and decreasing subsequences of α(P). Define the nesting graph Ne(P) to have these arcs as vertices, with two vertices adjacent when the corresponding arcs are nested. Let ne_r(P) be the maximum number of vertices contained in a union of r cliques of Ne(P), equivalently the maximum number of arcs contained in a union of r nestings. If λ is the RSK shape of α(P) and λ′ is its conjugate, then ne_r(P)=λ′_1+...+λ′_r.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In the tableau process T(P), the word α(P) records the order in which entries leave, by increasing right endpoint. Let σ be the reverse word i_t...i_1. In the proof of Theorem 6, nestings are represented by increasing subsequences of the auxiliary words σ_i associated to tableaux T_i. Since each σ_i is a subsequence of σ, every nesting gives an increasing subsequence of σ, equivalently a decreasing subsequence of α(P). Conversely, if i_{r_1}<...<i_{r_m} is an increasing subsequence of σ, then, because the terms leave in reverse order, all of them are simultaneously present just before the first of them leaves; hence they form a nesting. The corollary then applies Greene's extension of Schensted's theorem: for a word of RSK shape λ, the largest size of a union of r decreasing subsequences is λ′_1+...+λ′_r.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 11: enhanced crossing/nesting symmetry via hesitating tableaux
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a partition P of [n], its enhanced representation is the standard representation together with a loop (i,i) at each isolated point i. An enhanced k-crossing is a set of enhanced edges (i_1,j_1),...,(i_k,j_k) such that i_1<...<i_k≤j_1<...<j_k; an enhanced k-nesting is a set such that i_1<...<i_k≤j_k<...<j_1. Let cr_en(P) and ne_en(P) be the maximum enhanced crossing and nesting sizes. A hesitating tableau of empty shape and length 2n is a path in Young's lattice from ∅ to ∅ whose paired moves are one of: do nothing then add a square, delete a square then do nothing, or add a square then delete a square. For disjoint subsets S,T of [n] with |S|=|T|, let Pbar_n(S,T) be the set of partitions P with min(P)−max(P)=S and max(P)−min(P)=T. If fbar_{n,S,T}(i,j) counts P in Pbar_n(S,T) with cr_en(P)=i and ne_en(P)=j, then fbar_{n,S,T}(i,j)=fbar_{n,S,T}(j,i).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper modifies φ to a bijection φbar from partitions to hesitating tableaux. As before, scan j from n down to 1 and use RSK insertion/deletion. If j is a left endpoint only or right endpoint only, use the same rules as φ. If j is isolated, first insert j and then delete j. If j is both a right endpoint of (i,j) and a left endpoint of (j,k), first insert i and then delete j. This changes the tableau model so that isolated points and adjacent arcs are counted in the enhanced inequalities. The same Schensted argument identifies cr_en with the maximum number of rows and ne_en with the maximum number of columns among the hesitating-tableau shapes. Conjugating all shapes swaps rows and columns. In the hesitating setting, conjugation preserves the statuses left endpoint only and right endpoint only, hence preserves min(P)−max(P) and max(P)−min(P), but it need not distinguish isolated points from internal points; this is why the theorem fixes S and T in that form.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 5 specialization: complete matchings and oscillating tableaux; Corollaries 12 and 13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a complete matching on [2m], applying φ and deleting all do-nothing steps yields an oscillating tableau of empty shape and length 2m, namely a sequence ∅=λ_0,λ_1,...,λ_{2m}=∅ in which consecutive shapes differ by adding or deleting exactly one square. This is a bijection between complete matchings on [2m] and oscillating tableaux of empty shape and length 2m. Consequently, for k≥2, the number of k-noncrossing matchings on [2m] equals the number of closed lattice walks of length 2m in V_k = {(a_1,...,a_{k−1}) in Z^{k−1}: a_1≥...≥a_{k−1}≥0} from the origin to itself with unit steps ±ε_i. In particular, 2-noncrossing matchings are in bijection with Dyck paths of length 2m, and 3-noncrossing matchings are in bijection with pairs of noncrossing Dyck paths with common endpoints.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In a complete matching every vertex is either a left endpoint only or a right endpoint only, so each two-step vacillating move has exactly one nontrivial add/delete operation. Removing the trivial operations gives an oscillating tableau, and the construction is reversible by reinserting the forced do-nothing moves according to endpoint type. The row-length encoding used in Theorem 10 now has no zero steps and no parity restriction, giving walks in V_k with steps ±ε_i. For k=2 this is a one-dimensional nonnegative walk from 0 to 0 with up/down unit steps, i.e. a Dyck path. For k=3, a walk (x_i,y_i) with x_i≥y_i≥0 is sent to two Dyck paths P_i=x_i+y_i and Q_i=x_i−y_i; the inequalities ensure P stays weakly above Q, and the construction is invertible.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 5 enumeration formulas for k-noncrossing and bounded crossing/nesting matchings
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f_k(m) be the number of k-noncrossing complete matchings on [2m], and define the exponential generating function F_k(x)=Σ_m f_k(m) x^{2m}/(2m)!. Then F_k(x)=det[I_{i−j}(2x)−I_{i+j}(2x)]_{i,j=1}^{k−1}, where I_m(2x)=Σ_{j≥0} x^{m+2j}/(j!(m+j)!) is the hyperbolic Bessel function of the first kind. In particular F_2(x)=Σ_{j≥0} C_j x^{2j}/(2j)!, where C_j is the jth Catalan number, and f_3(m)=3!(2m+2)!/(m!(m+1)!(m+2)!(m+3)!)=C_m C_{m+2}−C_{m+1}^2. More generally, if g_{k,j}(m) is the number of complete matchings on [2m] with no (k+1)-crossing and no (j+1)-nesting, then g_{k,j}(m) is the number of length 2m walks from ∅ to ∅ in the Hasse diagram of L(k,j), the poset of Young diagrams fitting in a k by j rectangle. Its ordinary generating function G_{k,j}(x)=Σ_m g_{k,j}(m)x^{2m} satisfies G_{k,j}(x)=det(I−x A_{k,j}(0))/det(I−x A_{k,j}), where A_{k,j} is the adjacency matrix of the Hasse diagram of L(k,j), and A_{k,j}(0) is obtained by deleting the row and column corresponding to ∅.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The determinant formula for F_k is obtained by combining the oscillating-tableau/lattice-walk correspondence with the Grabiner-Magyar determinant for walks in Weyl chambers, specialized to walks from the origin back to itself in the chamber V_k. The k=2 and k=3 evaluations are algebraic simplifications of that determinant and match the Catalan and noncrossing-Dyck-pair counts. For simultaneous crossing and nesting bounds, Theorem 6 says that all shapes must remain inside a k by j rectangle. The transfer matrix method for walks on the finite graph given by the Hasse diagram of L(k,j) gives the stated ratio of determinants for the return-walk generating function.

proof_techniques.name: Right-to-left RSK encoding of open arcs
proof_techniques.description: The paper encodes a set partition by scanning labels from right to left and maintaining an SYT of currently open left endpoints. Encountering a right endpoint (i,j) inserts i; encountering a left endpoint j deletes j; isolated and internal points are handled by the corresponding combination of do-nothing, deletion, and insertion. This turns a static arc diagram into a time sequence of tableaux, with the shape recording a controlled amount of global crossing/nesting information.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original matching complex, one can try to encode a matching or a partial resolution by the set/order of arcs crossing a moving vertical line. The RSK tableau may supply a complexity measure for induction or a canonical choice of crossing to resolve.

proof_techniques.name: Crossings and nestings as departure-order subsequences
proof_techniques.description: A central observation is that a family of arcs forms a crossing exactly when their left endpoints are simultaneously active and leave in increasing order, while it forms a nesting when they are simultaneously active and leave in decreasing order. The auxiliary words σ_i record active entries in reverse departure order, so crossings become decreasing subsequences and nestings become increasing subsequences. Schensted's theorem then converts these order conditions into row and column bounds on Young diagrams.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests analyzing crossings not only geometrically but as inversions in the order in which paired endpoints become inactive. Resolving a crossing may be interpretable as altering a departure order, which could support a monotone induction or discrete Morse matching.

proof_techniques.name: Shape conjugation involution
proof_techniques.description: After translating partitions into vacillating tableaux, conjugating every Young diagram preserves the valid move sequence and the endpoint-status pattern, while swapping rows and columns. This gives an involution preserving fixed min/max endpoint data and exchanging crossing and nesting statistics.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original word constraints can be expressed as fixed endpoint types or colors, a similar shape-level involution or symmetry might preserve admissibility while exchanging two complementary complexity measures. Even if not directly used, it indicates that endpoint-preserving transformations can be easier after passing to tableau paths.

proof_techniques.name: Weyl-chamber lattice-walk model
proof_techniques.description: Bounding the crossing number translates into bounding the number of rows of every tableau shape. Recording row lengths gives a walk in the chamber a_1≥...≥a_{k−1}≥0, with allowed steps determined by add/delete/stay moves. For complete matchings, this simplifies to oscillating tableaux and walks with unit coordinate steps.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Subfamilies of matchings with controlled crossing behavior may be modeled as walks in a finite or locally finite chamber. Such a model can make induction on first exit, maximal height, or return structure more systematic.

proof_techniques.name: Fixed-endpoint Motzkin paths and canonical noncrossing/nonnesting objects
proof_techniques.description: For fixed min and max sets S,T, nonemptiness is equivalent to a Motzkin prefix condition. When nonempty, the paper gives explicit stack-like and queue-like pairings that recover the unique noncrossing and unique nonnesting partitions with those endpoint sets.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a fixed reducible word, admissible pairings of inverse letters may have analogous prefix conditions and canonical noncrossing representatives. Such representatives are natural candidates for deformation targets or base vertices in a contractibility proof.

proof_techniques.name: Enhanced crossings via hesitating tableaux
proof_techniques.description: The paper modifies the vacillating-tableau construction to include loops at isolated points and to count enhanced crossings/nestings, where inequalities allow i_k≤j_1 or i_k≤j_k. The resulting hesitating tableaux use paired moves of types stay-add, delete-stay, and add-delete, and the same RSK/Schensted/conjugation strategy applies with slightly different preserved endpoint data.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original quasi-reduced condition is a local constraint on adjacent inverse patterns; enhanced crossing ideas may be useful if local cancellations or shared-endpoint analogues need to be represented in the combinatorics of resolutions.

proof_techniques.name: Finite transfer-matrix reduction under simultaneous row and column bounds
proof_techniques.description: When both crossing and nesting sizes are bounded, all tableau shapes lie in a fixed rectangle, so the problem becomes counting closed walks in a finite Hasse diagram. The paper applies the transfer matrix method to express return-walk generating functions as determinant ratios.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proof reduces to bounded subcomplexes or finite intervals in a poset of resolutions, the same finite-state viewpoint may help compute examples, identify recurrences, or verify collapses in small cases.

other_useful_info: Notation caveat: in this paper a k-crossing means a family of k mutually crossing arcs with endpoints i_1<...<i_k<j_1<...<j_k, and cr is the maximum size of such a family; it is not the total number of intersection points in a drawn matching. The paper notes that usual noncrossing partitions are exactly partitions with no 2-crossing in the standard representation, and usual nonnesting partitions are exactly those with no 2-nesting. It also notes that its notion of k-noncrossing partitions differs from stronger notions such as Klazar's for k≥3. The authors explicitly state that they do not have a Greene-theorem analogue for crossing graphs comparable to their nesting result, because an increasing subsequence of the left-endpoint word gives a crossing only under the extra condition that the last left endpoint lies before the first right endpoint. Related cited tools include Sundaram's oscillating-tableau correspondence, Grabiner-Magyar Weyl-chamber walk enumeration, and Baik-Rains/Goulden determinant and asymptotic results for matchings and fixed-point-free involutions.
why_relevant: Provides detailed combinatorics of crossing and nesting statistics for matchings. These techniques can help organize induction on crossing number and understand how resolving crossings changes matching structure.

title: Triangulations of Surfaces
overall_summary: Hatcher proves that the arc complex A(S,V) of a compact surface with a finite vertex set is contractible except for two boundary-only exceptional families, where it is a sphere, and also proves contractibility of a larger arc-and-circle complex C(S,V) when V has an interior point. The core argument is an explicit surgery deformation: fix an essential arc β, put every arc system in minimal position with β, and successively slide intersections with β to one endpoint, discarding inessential arcs, to deformation retract the complex onto the star of β. The paper also proves that adding a boundary vertex suspends the arc complex, and uses the identification of links with arc complexes of cut-open surfaces to prove connectivity of triangulation flip graphs. For the original matching-complex problem, the most relevant material is the surgery-to-a-star paradigm, the use of weighted arc systems to make surgery continuous on cells, and link/cutting arguments for inductive connectivity or contractibility proofs.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Main Theorem: contractibility and exceptional spheres for arc and curve complexes
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S be a compact connected surface, possibly with boundary, and let V be a nonempty finite subset of S. An essential arc in (S,V) is an embedded arc in S whose endpoints lie in V, whose interior is disjoint from ∂S ∪ V, whose endpoints may coincide, and which does not separate S into two components one of which is a disk meeting V only in the endpoints of the arc. An essential circle in (S,V) is an embedded circle in S disjoint from ∂S ∪ V which does not separate S into two components one of which is either a disk meeting V in at most one point or an annulus disjoint from V. A curve system is a finite collection of essential arcs and essential circles, pairwise disjoint except that arcs may share endpoints, with no two curves ambient isotopic rel V. Let C(S,V) be the simplicial complex whose k-simplices are ambient isotopy classes rel V of curve systems with k+1 curves, with faces given by subcollections, and let A(S,V) be the subcomplex spanned by arc systems only. Excluding the trivial cases where these complexes are empty: (a) A(S,V) is contractible except when S is a disk with V contained in ∂S, or S is an annulus with V contained in one component of ∂S; in these exceptional cases A(S,V) is homeomorphic to a sphere. (b) If V contains at least one point in the interior of S, then C(S,V) is contractible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For part (a), Hatcher first treats the case that each boundary component of S contains at most one point of V. Choose an essential arc β. For every simplex [α0,...,αk] of A(S,V), isotope the arc system α0 ∪ ... ∪ αk into minimal position with β. A point of the simplex is viewed as a weighted arc system P = Σ ti αi, with the arcs replaced by parallel families of total thickness ti. Near intersections with β these parallel families are pinched into a single band of total thickness. Starting at a chosen endpoint of β, cut through this band and redirect the two cut ends to that endpoint of β. In combinatorial terms, if x1,...,xm are the intersections with β ordered along β, slide x1 to the chosen endpoint to obtain a new arc system c2 from c1 = α0 ∪ ... ∪ αk, discarding arcs that become parallel duplicates or boundary-parallel; then repeat with x2, and so on. Each elementary slide takes place inside a simplex spanned by the old and new arc systems, and the motion is linear on that simplex. The resulting flow is continuous on every simplex, compatible with passage to faces, fixes the star of [β], and ends in the star of [β]. Since a star is contractible, this gives contractibility in the special case. For general boundary vertex sets in part (a), Hatcher proves separately that adding a new boundary vertex to a boundary component already containing a vertex suspends the arc complex. Iterating this suspension result reduces to the special case except for the disk and annulus families. The exceptional sphere cases arise from the base cases where A(S,V) is S^0: a disk with four boundary vertices and an annulus with two vertices in one boundary component; repeated suspensions give spheres. For part (b), the same surgery method is applied to C(S,V), choosing β to be an essential arc with both endpoints at a point of V lying in the interior of S.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Surgery deformation retraction onto the star of a fixed arc
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume S is a compact connected surface and V is a finite nonempty subset with at most one point of V in each boundary component of S. Let β be an essential arc in (S,V). In the arc complex A(S,V), the star of the vertex [β] consists of all simplices represented by arc systems disjoint from β, allowing one of the arcs to be isotopic to β. Hatcher constructs a deformation retraction of A(S,V) onto this star; the deformation is a flow which is linear on the simplices through which it passes.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take a simplex [α0,...,αk] and choose representatives in minimal position with β. For a point P = Σ ti αi in the simplex, interpret the coefficients ti geometrically by replacing each αi by a thin family of nearby parallel arcs of total thickness ti, with endpoints pinched at vertices. Where the families cross β, pinch them into one band crossing β. The deformation starts at a chosen endpoint of β. It cuts partially through this band from that endpoint and redirects the two free ends of the cut band to the endpoint of β. As the cut depth increases, this removes the first intersection of the arc system with β. If x1,...,xm are the intersections in order along β, then sliding x1 to the endpoint converts the old arc system c1 into a new arc system c2, after discarding arcs that have become boundary-parallel or parallel to another arc. The union c1 ∪ c2 is again a curve system and hence spans a simplex σ1 containing the original simplex as a face; on the initial part of the deformation, points move linearly along fibers of a linear projection of σ1 to the face corresponding to c2. The procedure then repeats for x2, then x3, and so on, producing a finite sequence of simplices σ1,...,σm. At the end the resulting arc system is disjoint from β, so it lies in the star of [β]. If the original system was already disjoint from β, no intersections are present and the flow is the identity. Compatibility with faces follows because restricting the construction to a face simply deletes some weighted arcs and performs the same ordered surgeries on the remaining system. This gives a continuous global deformation retraction onto the star.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Minimal-position isotopy fact used in the surgery construction
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let [α0,...,αk] be a simplex of A(S,V) and let β be an essential arc. Hatcher uses the standard fact that if the arc system α0 ∪ ... ∪ αk has been isotoped to intersect β minimally, equivalently so that there is no disk D in S with boundary consisting of an arc in β and an arc in some αi, with ∂D not equal to αi ∪ β and with the interior of D disjoint from V, then any two such minimal-position representatives are isotopic through a family of minimum-position curve systems.
