key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Given an isotopy F from one minimal-position representative of the arc system to another, view F as a map from the disjoint union of the αi times an interval into S. Perturb F to be transverse to β. Then F^{-1}(β) is a one-dimensional set in the source cylinder(s), and one studies the projection F^{-1}(β) → I to the isotopy parameter. By gradually simplifying F^{-1}(β) and cancelling critical points of this projection, one removes non-minimal creation or cancellation of intersections. The result is an isotopy connecting the two representatives while staying within the class of representatives having minimal intersection with β. This fact ensures that the surgery flow is well defined on isotopy classes, not on arbitrary chosen representatives.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition: adding a boundary vertex suspends the arc complex
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V′ be obtained from V by adding one new vertex v′ in a component of ∂S that already contains at least one vertex of V. Then A(S,V′) is homeomorphic, in fact piecewise linearly homeomorphic, to the suspension ΣA(S,V). Here the suspension is the quotient of A(S,V) × [−1,1] obtained by collapsing A(S,V) × {−1} to one point and A(S,V) × {1} to another point.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let v be a vertex of V adjacent to the new vertex v′ on the relevant boundary component. Define two special boundary-parallel essential arcs β and β′: β joins v′ to the next vertex of V′ on the other side of v, and β′ is defined similarly with the roles of v and v′ interchanged. Let X be the subcomplex of A(S,V′) consisting of arc systems containing neither β nor β′. The full complex A(S,V′) is obtained from X by attaching the stars St(β) and St(β′) along their links, which lie in X. Hatcher embeds X into ΣA(S,V) as follows. Regard a point x of X as a weighted arc system. Collapse the boundary segment between v and v′ to identify v and v′, obtaining a weighted arc system x̄ in A(S,V). Let θ(x) be the total weight of arcs of x incident at v and θ′(x) the total weight incident at v′. Send x to (x̄, (θ(x) − θ′(x))/2) in the suspension coordinate. Since θ and θ′ lie in [0,1], the height lies in [−1/2,1/2]. For fixed collapsed weighted system x̄, the possible height values form a symmetric interval about 0 whose length is θ(x)+θ′(x), varying continuously with x̄. The map X → A(S,V), x ↦ x̄, is surjective. The complement of the image of X in the suspension consists of two open cones, corresponding exactly to the links of β and β′; their closures correspond to the stars St(β) and St(β′). Thus A(S,V′) and ΣA(S,V) are decomposed into the same three pieces, X plus the two stars/cones, with the same intersection pattern, yielding a PL homeomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Link-after-cutting identification
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let σ = [α0,...,αk] be a simplex of A(S,V). Let (S′,V′) be obtained from (S,V) by cutting S open along the arcs α0,...,αk, with the induced vertex set on the cut surface. Then the link of σ in A(S,V) can be identified with A(S′,V′). If S′ is disconnected, Hatcher treats A(S′,V′) as the join of the arc complexes of its connected components.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A simplex in the link of σ is represented by an arc system disjoint from α0 ∪ ... ∪ αk and not containing arcs isotopic to the αi. Cutting S open along the αi turns every such disjoint arc system into an arc system in the cut surface (S′,V′). Conversely, any essential arc system in (S′,V′) descends to an arc system in S disjoint from the αi and hence determines a simplex in the link. Disjointness, isotopy rel vertices, and passage to faces are preserved by the cutting and gluing operations, giving the simplicial identification. In the proof of the triangulation corollary, this identification is combined with the theorem: if σ has codimension at least two in A(S,V), then the corresponding A(S′,V′) has dimension at least one, hence is connected; disconnected S′ causes no difficulty because a join of the component complexes is used.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary: triangulations are connected by elementary moves
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Define a triangulation of (S,V) to be a maximal simplex of A(S,V): when V meets each component of ∂S, such a maximal arc system decomposes S into triangles with vertex set V, allowing triangles with coinciding vertices or edges; in the general case, maximal arc systems decompose S into triangles together with punctured monogons corresponding to boundary components disjoint from V. An elementary move changes one edge of a triangulation in a quadrilateral or in a punctured digon, as shown in the paper. Then any two isotopy classes of triangulations of (S,V) are related by a finite sequence of elementary moves.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Triangulations correspond to maximal simplices of A(S,V), and all maximal simplices have the same dimension by Euler characteristic considerations. A codimension-one face is obtained by deleting one edge from a triangulation. In the two elementary-move configurations, namely a quadrilateral or a punctured digon, this codimension-one face lies in exactly two maximal simplices, corresponding to the two possible choices of the missing edge. The only other case is that the deleted edge occurs twice in the same triangle; then the codimension-one face lies in only one maximal simplex. Thus it suffices to show that any two maximal simplices can be connected by a path passing through open codimension-one faces, except for the zero-dimensional exceptional cases, which are precisely the quadrilateral and punctured-digon cases and already consist of two vertices related by one elementary move. The theorem implies that A(S,V) is connected whenever it has dimension greater than zero. Choose any polygonal path in A(S,V) between the two maximal simplices. Push the path off all simplices of codimension greater than one by induction on the minimum dimension of simplices met by the path. This pushing is possible because the link of any codimension-at-least-two simplex σ is identified with A(S′,V′) for the surface cut open along σ, and this link has dimension at least one and is connected. Hence the path can be adjusted to cross only codimension-one faces, giving the required finite sequence of elementary moves.

proof_techniques.name: Weighted arc-system surgery flow
proof_techniques.description: A point of a simplex [α0,...,αk] is represented not just formally by barycentric coordinates Σ ti αi, but geometrically as a weighted arc system: each αi is replaced by a family of nearby parallel arcs of total thickness ti. Near a fixed arc β, these parallel families are pinched into a band, and the deformation cuts through the band from one endpoint of β, redirecting cut strands to that endpoint. In combinatorial terms, intersections with β are ordered along β and removed one at a time; the transition from the old arc system to the new one lies in a simplex spanned by their union, so the deformation is linear on that simplex.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the matching complex F_w, cells are already products of intervals corresponding to local crossing resolutions. This technique suggests trying to order intersections or crossings relative to a fixed reference arc/matching and define a cellular flow that resolves or slides one crossing at a time while remaining inside a cell or a controlled adjacent cell.

proof_techniques.name: Contraction onto the star of a fixed object
proof_techniques.description: Rather than proving global contractibility directly, Hatcher fixes an essential arc β and constructs a deformation retraction onto the star of the vertex [β]. The star consists of all arc systems compatible with β, meaning disjoint from β up to isotopy, and is contractible because it is a cone-like subcomplex. The whole proof is organized around making every arc system compatible with β by successive surgeries.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A parallel strategy for F_w would be to identify a canonical interval, pair, or noncrossing submatching that can serve as β, then show every matching can be flowed to the subcomplex of matchings compatible with it. If that subcomplex is a star or cone, contractibility would follow.

proof_techniques.name: Minimal-position and bigon-removal control
proof_techniques.description: Before performing surgery, arc systems are put in minimal position with respect to β. The criterion used is the absence of a disk whose boundary is an arc in β together with an arc in one of the system arcs, with interior disjoint from V. Hatcher also invokes the standard fact that any two minimal-position representatives are isotopic through minimal-position representatives, proved by studying the inverse image of β under an isotopy and cancelling critical points of its projection to the isotopy parameter.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Because F_w is built from isotopy classes of immersed intervals, any surgery or crossing-resolution operation needs to be independent of chosen representatives. A matching analogue of minimal position with respect to a reference object could make local operations well defined on isotopy classes.

proof_techniques.name: Discarding inessential or duplicate arcs during surgery
proof_techniques.description: The surgery operation can create arcs that are boundary-parallel or parallel to arcs already present. Hatcher simply discards such arcs so that the result remains an allowed arc system in A(S,V). In the special case with at most one vertex on each boundary component, he notes that not all of the weighted system is discarded.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In the word-matching setting, resolving or sliding arcs may create trivial components or local cancellations. The paper’s method suggests allowing a normalization step after each local move, while proving that the hypotheses prevent the entire object from disappearing or leaving the allowed class.

proof_techniques.name: Continuity by simplex-by-simplex linearity and face compatibility
proof_techniques.description: The surgery process is shown to be continuous by decomposing each path into finitely many linear segments through simplices σ1,...,σm, where each σj is spanned by the arc systems before and after one elementary surgery. The construction respects faces because deleting some arcs from the initial weighted system commutes with the same ordered surgery procedure on the remaining arcs. These two points give a well-defined global deformation of the whole simplicial complex.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, a prospective deformation must respect cubical attaching maps. The analogous check would be that local crossing-resolution flows are linear or product-linear on each cube and commute with passing to facets obtained by resolving crossings.

proof_techniques.name: Links as arc complexes of cut-open surfaces
proof_techniques.description: The link of a simplex represented by arcs α0,...,αk is identified with the arc complex of the surface obtained by cutting open along those arcs. This converts local combinatorics near a simplex into the same type of global object on a simpler or decomposed surface. If the cut surface is disconnected, the relevant complex is the join of the complexes of the components.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Fixing part of a matching or resolving some crossings in F_w may decompose the upper half-plane or disk into independent regions with subwords on their boundaries. A link analysis modeled on Hatcher’s cutting argument could reduce local contractibility or connectivity questions to smaller matching problems and join/product decompositions.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary-vertex suspension induction
proof_techniques.description: When a new vertex is inserted on a boundary component that already contains a vertex, Hatcher decomposes A(S,V′) into a subcomplex X avoiding two special arcs plus the two stars of those arcs. He embeds X into the suspension of A(S,V) by collapsing the boundary segment between the old and new vertices and recording a height equal to half the difference of the total weights incident at the two vertices. The two missing open cones in the suspension are exactly the two stars, giving a PL homeomorphism A(S,V′) ≅ ΣA(S,V).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the matching complex changes predictably when adjacent boundary positions are inserted, split, or identified, this suspension-style decomposition could provide an induction on word length or on the number of boundary points.

proof_techniques.name: Pushing paths off high-codimension strata using connected links
proof_techniques.description: To prove connectivity of the triangulation flip graph, Hatcher starts with an arbitrary polygonal path in the connected arc complex and then pushes it away from simplices of codimension greater than one. The induction works because the link of each such simplex is connected, so a path entering a small neighborhood of the simplex can be rerouted through its link. This turns general connectivity of the complex into connectivity through codimension-one adjacencies.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, if one first proves the complex is connected or highly connected and can identify links of cells, this technique may help promote coarse connectivity to connectivity of a move graph or support inductive proofs of higher connectivity.

other_useful_info: Maximal simplices of A(S,V) all have the same dimension by Euler characteristic considerations. The exceptional sphere cases in the main theorem are generated from two S^0 base cases, namely a disk with four boundary vertices and an annulus with two vertices in one boundary component, by repeated use of the boundary-vertex suspension proposition. The paper notes that when V is contained in ∂S, A(S,V) is Harer’s complex AZ(Δ) from [H1]; Hatcher’s proof is an elementary alternative to Harer’s lamination-space proof. Hatcher also notes that [H2] constructs a stronger contractible cell complex whose vertices are triangulations and whose edges are elementary moves when V meets each boundary component, and cites Mosher [M] for another elementary proof of flip connectivity.
why_relevant: Contains fundamental contractibility arguments for arc and triangulation complexes of surfaces. Noncrossing matchings of boundary points are arc systems in a disk, so Hatcher-style surgery and link arguments are likely useful.

title: The cancellation norm and the geometry of bi-invariant word metrics
overall_summary: The paper studies the cancellation length of words in a finitely normally generated group and proves that the induced cancellation norm on group elements is exactly the bi-invariant word norm associated to the generating set. Its most relevant free-group contribution is a recursive first-letter formula for the cancellation norm: in a free group, an optimal trivialisation of xw either deletes the initial x or pairs it with an inverse occurrence x^{-1} in w, splitting the problem into the two intervening subwords. This yields an O(N^3)-time, O(N^2)-memory dynamic programming algorithm for the cancellation norm of a word of ordinary length N in a free group. For the original matching-complex problem, the paper is useful mainly as an algebraic source of cancellation pairings and interval-splitting induction: in the norm-zero case of a freely trivial word, the recursion forces an inverse-letter pair whose removal separates the word into two freely trivial subwords.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a group finitely normally generated by a symmetric set S. For a word w = s1 ... sn in the alphabet S, define its cancellation length |w|× to be the least k such that deleting k letters from w leaves a word representing the identity in G. For g in G, define the cancellation norm |g|× to be the minimum of |w|× over all words w representing g. Let ∥g∥ be the bi-invariant word norm associated to S, equivalently the least k such that g is a product of k conjugates of elements of S. Then |g|× = ∥g∥ for every g in G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the inequality |g|× ≤ ∥g∥, write g as a product of k conjugates, g = product_{i=1}^k wi^{-1} si wi with si in S. In the concatenated word representing this product, deleting the distinguished letters si leaves a product of conjugating words and their inverses, hence a word trivial in G; thus k deletions suffice. For the reverse inequality, suppose a word representing g has a trivialising sequence of k deleted letters. Write the word in the form u0 s1 u1 ... sk uk, where deleting the letters s1,...,sk leaves u0 u1 ... uk = 1 in G. Set Wi = ui ui+1 ... uk for i = 1,...,k. Since u0 = (u1 ... uk)^{-1}, a telescoping calculation gives g = product_{i=1}^k Wi^{-1} si Wi. Thus g is a product of k conjugates of generators, so ∥g∥ ≤ k. Minimising over trivialising sequences gives ∥g∥ ≤ |g|×.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G = ⟨S | R⟩ be a group presentation. A defining relation v = w in R is called balanced if, whenever a word v̄ is obtained from v by deleting k letters, there are k letters in w whose deletion gives a word w̄ with v̄ = w̄ in G. If v = w is a balanced relation, then for any words x and y in the alphabet S, the cancellation lengths satisfy |x v y|× = |x w y|×.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The idea is to transfer any deletion pattern through the balanced relation without changing the number of deleted letters. Given a trivialising sequence for x v y, restrict attention to how many letters it deletes from the subword v. By balancedness, delete the same number of letters from w so that the remaining subword represents the same group element as the remaining part of v. Keeping the deletion choices in x and y unchanged produces a trivialising sequence of the same length for x w y. Applying the same argument in the reverse direction for the same equality gives equality of cancellation lengths.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G = ⟨S | R⟩ be a presentation all of whose defining relations are balanced. If u and v are two words in the alphabet S representing the same element g in G, then |u|× = |v|×. Consequently, for such a presentation, the cancellation norm |g|× is equal to the cancellation length of any word representing g.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It is enough to show that applying one defining relation inside an arbitrary context preserves cancellation length. Suppose one word has the form x y and the other has the form x r^{-1} t y, where r = t is a defining relation. The paper computes |x r^{-1} t y|× = |x r^{-1} r y|× by Lemma 2.2, because t may be replaced by r without changing cancellation length. The word r^{-1} r is freely trivial, so |x r^{-1} r y|× = |x y|×. Any two words representing the same group element are connected by a finite sequence of such applications of defining relations in contexts, so cancellation length is constant along the sequence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example 2.3 and Theorem 1.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Coxeter groups and right-angled Artin groups admit presentations in which all defining relations are balanced. In particular, if G is either a Coxeter group or a right-angled Artin group, then the cancellation norm of an element of G does not depend on the chosen representing word.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a right-angled Artin group, use the standard presentation with commutation relations st = ts for the commuting generators; deleting any number of letters from one side can be matched on the other side because the two sides contain the same letters in the opposite order. For a Coxeter group, the paper notes a presentation with relations s = s^{-1} and braid-type alternating relations st ... s = ts ... t, equivalently (st)^n = (ts)^n in the relevant cases; these are balanced for the same reason, because corresponding sides contain compatible alternating multisets of generators. Proposition 2.5 then implies that cancellation length is invariant under changing the representative word, and hence the cancellation norm is realised by every representative.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let x be a generator of a free group F_m, and let w be a word in F_m. With ∥·∥ denoting the conjugation-invariant word norm, equivalently the cancellation norm by Proposition 2.1, one has ∥x w∥ = min(1 + ∥w∥, min{∥u∥ + ∥v∥ : w = u x^{-1} v}), where the inner minimum ranges over all decompositions of the word w around an occurrence of the letter x^{-1}. If no occurrence of x^{-1} appears in w, the inner minimum is absent.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the upper bound, there are two constructions. First, delete the initial x and then use an optimal trivialising sequence for w; this gives length 1 + ∥w∥. Second, if w = u x^{-1} v, delete optimal trivialising sequences in u and in v. The remaining word is x x^{-1}, which freely cancels, so ∥xw∥ ≤ ∥u∥ + ∥v∥ for every such decomposition. For the lower bound, take a minimal trivialising sequence for xw. If the initial x is among the deleted letters, then the remaining deleted letters must trivialise w, giving length at least 1 + ∥w∥. If the initial x is not deleted, then in the freely trivial word left after deleting the minimal sequence, that initial x must cancel with some undeleted occurrence of x^{-1} in w. Write w = u x^{-1} v at that occurrence. Because the group is free, no relation can mix letters across this cancellation except by first reducing the prefix u to the identity and independently reducing the suffix v to the identity. Thus the deleted letters before that x^{-1} form a minimal trivialising sequence for u, and the deleted letters after it form a minimal trivialising sequence for v, giving length at least ∥u∥ + ∥v∥. This proves the displayed recurrence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.5 / Section 2.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a word w of ordinary length N in a nonabelian finitely generated free group, there is an algorithm computing the conjugation-invariant word length, equivalently the cancellation norm, of w. The algorithm runs in O(N^3) time and uses O(N^2) memory.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The algorithm is dynamic programming on connected subwords of a reduced input word. Suppose v is a reduced connected subword of ordinary length k and the norms of all proper connected subwords of v are already known. Apply Lemma 2.10 to the first letter of v: scan v from left to right to find all occurrences of the inverse of the first letter, and for each such occurrence use the stored values of the two resulting shorter connected subwords. Taking the minimum over these possibilities and over deleting the first letter computes ∥v∥ in time O(k). Now compute all subwords in increasing order of length: lengths 1 and 2 are initialised directly for reduced words, then length 3, length 4, and so on up to N. There are at most N connected subwords of each length k, so the total time is bounded by a constant times sum_{k=1}^N N k = O(N^3). The algorithm stores only the norm values for connected subwords, of which there are O(N^2), giving the stated memory bound.

proof_techniques.name: Trivialising sequences as deletion certificates
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically replaces geometric or metric questions about the bi-invariant word norm by combinatorics of deleting letters from a word. A trivialising sequence is a sequence of indices whose deletion leaves a word equal to the identity. Proposition 2.1 shows that these deletion certificates are exactly equivalent to products of conjugates of generators: deleting the marked letters gives one inequality, and conversely the subwords between deleted letters telescope to express the original element as a product of conjugates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A matching of inverse letters can be treated as a strengthened deletion/cancellation certificate: instead of merely deleting letters, one records which inverse letters cancel. This suggests converting questions about the existence or structure of matchings into inductive statements about trivialising sequences in a free group.

proof_techniques.name: Balanced-relation invariance
proof_techniques.description: The balanced-relation framework gives a robust way to show that cancellation length is unchanged under local substitutions in a presentation. If a relation v = w is balanced, then every deletion pattern on one side can be transferred to a deletion pattern of the same size on the other side. Lemma 2.2 and Proposition 2.5 use this to prove that, in presentations all of whose relations are balanced, cancellation length depends only on the represented group element and not on the representative word.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For free groups, where reductions are governed only by inverse cancellation, this supports simplifying representatives or subwords while preserving cancellation data. If a proof of contractibility needs local word modifications before constructing or comparing matchings, the balanced-relation argument is the paper’s mechanism for justifying that such modifications do not change the relevant cancellation length.

proof_techniques.name: First-letter dichotomy and splitting over an inverse occurrence
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 2.10 is the central free-group technique. In a minimal trivialisation of xw, either the initial letter x is deleted, or it survives and must cancel with a specific surviving inverse occurrence x^{-1} inside w. In the latter case, freeness forces the word between x and that x^{-1} to be trivialised independently, and also forces the suffix after that occurrence to be trivialised independently. This converts a global cancellation problem into two smaller interval problems.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a reducible word of norm zero, the delete-the-first-letter alternative cannot realise the zero minimum, so some inverse occurrence gives a decomposition w = u x^{-1} v with both u and v reducible. Iterating this gives a nested noncrossing inverse-letter cancellation pairing, which is a natural base object for inductive or discrete-Morse approaches to the matching complex F_w.

proof_techniques.name: Dynamic programming over connected subwords
proof_techniques.description: The algorithm in Section 2.9 organises the first-letter recurrence by connected subwords. Norms of all shorter intervals are computed first and stored; the value for a longer interval is obtained by scanning for inverse occurrences of its first letter and reading off the two smaller interval values. Thus all needed information is local to intervals, and the recurrence has an explicitly triangular dependence order.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The cells of F_w are built from pairings of positions in a linear word, and resolving or choosing a pair often separates the word into intervals. The paper’s interval-recursion suggests trying an inductive cover, filtration, or acyclic matching on F_w whose pieces are indexed by subwords and whose attaching data decomposes as products or joins over smaller intervals.

proof_techniques.name: Forced cancellation by cost barriers
proof_techniques.description: In the proof of Theorem 3.6, the paper uses a quantitative free-group cancellation argument to force a particular letter to appear in every minimal trivialising sequence. The constructed word contains a long block b^{4k} a^{-1} b^{-4k}; if a^{-1} is not deleted, then pairing it with an earlier a would require deleting more than 2k intervening b-letters, which is more expensive than simply deleting a^{-1}. This kind of estimate shows that sufficiently long separator blocks can make a local cancellation choice unavoidable.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original problem fixes an arbitrary word rather than allowing auxiliary long blocks, the same pattern may help prove that certain local resolutions or pair choices are forced under additional filtrations or auxiliary constructions. It is a method for converting free-group reduced-length estimates into rigidity statements about cancellation patterns.

other_useful_info: The paper’s term trivialising sequence means a sequence of indices, or equivalently the corresponding letters, whose deletion makes the word trivial. In the free-group algorithm, the proof works with reduced connected subwords and stores only their norm values. Remark 2.11 notes that outside special settings there is no obvious general algorithm for the conjugation-invariant norm; for groups with balanced presentations and solvable word problem one can in principle compute by checking subsequences, but this is exponential in time. A direct consequence of Lemma 2.10, not separately named in the paper, is that any nonempty freely trivial word xw admits a decomposition w = u x^{-1} v with both u and v freely trivial; iterating this is the standard nested cancellation-pairing construction for trivial words in a free group.
why_relevant: Develops foldings and cancellation pairings for words in free groups, including algorithms based on matching inverse letters. This is useful for translating reducible words into structured cancellation diagrams.

title: The cd-indices of intervals in the uncrossing partial order on matchings
overall_summary: The paper studies the uncrossing poset P_n of complete matchings on 2n labeled points, ordered by resolving crossings, and gives recursive formulas for cd-indices and ab-indices of its intervals. Its main structural tool is an explicit order-reversing bijection from P_n to a certain induced subposet MP_n of affine Bruhat order, whose elements are modular palindromic affine permutations with a no-long-consecutive-subword condition. Using Reading's framework for Bruhat intervals, the paper proves that certain intervals in MP_n are obtained from a product with a two-element chain by a controlled sequence of zipping operations, and converts this into cd-index recursions. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the poset-level model for crossing matchings and their resolutions, the interval decomposition by products and zippings, and the possibility of treating cubical cell attachments coming from resolving crossings via uncrossing-poset intervals or subposets.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3 basic facts about the uncrossing poset P_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P_n be the set of complete matchings of [2n], i.e. partitions of [2n] into n two-element blocks. If a matching has blocks (i_r,j_r) and (i_s,j_s), written with i_r<j_r and i_s<j_s, these two blocks form a crossing when i_r<i_s<j_r<j_s. Resolving such a crossing means replacing the two crossing blocks by either (i_r,i_s),(j_r,j_s) or by (i_r,j_s),(i_s,j_r). The partial order P_n is generated by declaring a matching obtained by resolving a crossing of τ to lie below τ. If c(τ) denotes the number of crossings of τ, then P_n is graded of rank binom(n,2) with rank function c. It has a unique maximum element {(1,n+1),(2,n+2),...,(n,2n)}, and its minimal elements are the noncrossing matchings, counted by the Catalan number (1/(n+1)) binom(2n,n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states these as basic properties of the uncrossing poset. The rank function is the crossing number: each cover relation is a local uncrossing and lowers c by one, while the displayed maximum has every pair of its n wires crossing, hence binom(n,2) crossings. The minimal elements are precisely the matchings with no crossings left to resolve, which are the usual noncrossing matchings on 2n points, counted by the Catalan number.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1, after Lam
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let hat P_n be the poset obtained from P_n by adjoining a unique minimum element hat0 below all matchings, with c(hat0)=-1. Then hat P_n is Eulerian: for every nontrivial interval in hat P_n, the number of elements of even rank equals the number of elements of odd rank.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This theorem is cited from Lam and is not reproved in this paper. The paper summarizes Lam's proof as follows. Lam uses a map τ ↦ g_τ from matchings to affine permutations of type (n,2n). First, for intervals [τ,η] contained in P_n, he proves equality of the numbers of odd-rank and even-rank elements by descending induction on c(τ)+c(η). Then he handles intervals [hat0,η] by constructing an involution σ ↦ s_i·σ on the set {σ ∈ (hat0,η] : s_i·σ ≠ σ}, which pairs elements of opposite parity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let tilde S_{2n} be the affine permutation group with simple generators s_0,s_1,...,s_{2n-1}. For τ ∈ P_n, write τ(i) for the point matched with i and define h_τ(i)=τ(i)-n if i<τ(i), and h_τ(i)=τ(i)+n if i>τ(i). Let φ(τ)=[h_τ(1),h_τ(2),...,h_τ(2n)] in window notation. An affine permutation w ∈ tilde S_{2n} is modular palindromic if it has a reduced decomposition w=s_{i_1}s_{i_2}...s_{i_{2k}} such that |i_{2k+1-r}-i_r|=n for every r∈[k]. Let MP_n be the set of modular palindromic affine permutations for which no reduced word contains, as a not-necessarily-adjacent subword, n consecutive generator indices. Then φ(P_n)⊆MP_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof has two parts. First, modular palindromicity is proved by decreasing induction on the rank of τ. The maximum matching hat1={(1,n+1),...,(n,2n)} maps to the identity, which is modular palindromic. Suppose τ is already known to map to MP_n and choose a crossing between wires (a,τ(a)) and (b,τ(b)) with a<b<τ(a)<τ(b). If the crossing is resolved to (a,τ(b)),(b,τ(a)), then in window notation φ(τ′) is obtained from φ(τ) by swapping the entries in positions a and b and also swapping the values a+n and b+n; equivalently φ(τ′)=t_{a+n,b+n} φ(τ) t_{a,b}, where t_{p,q} is the periodic transposition. The other resolution is handled similarly using the paired transpositions t_{b-n,τ(a)-n} and t_{b,τ(a)}. These paired operations preserve the modular palindromic form. Second, to prove the no-consecutive-subword condition, the paper argues by contradiction. If some reduced word for φ(τ) contained a subword of n consecutive indices, take a maximal such subword, say s_{a+1}s_{a+2}...s_{a+m} with m≥n, after possibly reversing orientation and interpreting indices modulo 2n. Then φ(τ)(a+m+1)=a+1. But directly from the definition of h_τ one has i-n<φ(τ)(i)<i+n for every i∈[2n], contradicting m≥n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The map φ:P_n→MP_n is order-reversing. Equivalently, if τ′≤τ in the uncrossing poset P_n, then φ(τ′)≥φ(τ) in Bruhat order on MP_n, viewed as an induced subposet of tilde S_{2n}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It suffices to check a cover relation, where τ′ is obtained by resolving one crossing of τ. In the proof of Lemma 11, resolving a crossing changes φ(τ) by multiplying on the two sides by paired periodic transpositions, so a reduced word for φ(τ) appears as a subword of a reduced word for φ(τ′). By the subword characterization of Bruhat order, φ(τ)≤φ(τ′). Taking transitive closure gives the result.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let p,q∈MP_n satisfy q=s_a p s_{a+n} and ℓ(q)=ℓ(p)+2, where ℓ is affine Coxeter length and indices are read modulo 2n. Let x,y∈[2n] be such that p(x)≡a mod 2n and p(y)≡a+1 mod 2n. Then: (1) the four numbers x, y, a+n, and a+n+1 are distinct; (2) q(i)=p(i) for every i∈[2n] outside {a+n,a+n+1,x,y}; and (3) q(a+n)=p(a+n+1), q(a+n+1)=p(a+n), q(x)≡a+1 mod 2n, and q(y)≡a mod 2n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The inequalities i-n<p(i)<i+n for elements of MP_n imply immediately that x≠y, a+n≠a+n+1, a+n≠x, and a+n+1≠y. If y=a+n, then p(a+n)≡a+1, forcing p to contain a subword s_{a+1}s_{a+2}...s_{a+n-1}; multiplying to form q=s_a p s_{a+n} would then create a forbidden subword of n consecutive indices. The case x=a+n+1 is analogous, using the decreasing consecutive subword s_{a+2n+1}s_{a+2n}...s_{a+n+1}. This proves distinctness. For i outside the four exceptional positions, right multiplication by s_{a+n} does not move i and left multiplication by s_a does not change p(i), so q(i)=p(i). The displayed formulas in (3) follow directly from the fact that right multiplication by s_{a+n} swaps the positions a+n and a+n+1, while left multiplication by s_a swaps the values congruent to a and a+1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The map φ:P_n→MP_n defined by φ(τ)=[h_τ(1),...,h_τ(2n)], with h_τ(i)=τ(i)-n for i<τ(i) and h_τ(i)=τ(i)+n for i>τ(i), is an order-reversing bijection from the uncrossing poset P_n to the induced Bruhat subposet MP_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Injectivity is immediate from the definition: the mate τ(i) is recovered from φ(τ)(i) by shifting by n in the appropriate direction. Lemma 11 shows φ(P_n)⊆MP_n, and Corollary 12 shows the order-reversing property. The main work is surjectivity. Given p∈MP_n, define τ_p(i)=p(i)+n if p(i)≤n, and τ_p(i)=p(i)-n if p(i)≥n+1. The displacement inequality i-n<p(i)<i+n implies τ_p(i)≠i. The paper then proves τ_p is an involution by induction on ℓ(p)/2. One rewrites τ_p^2(i) as the congruence τ_p^2(i)≡p(p(i)+n)+n mod 2n, with the representative in [2n]. For p=e this gives τ_e^2(i)=i. For the induction step, write q=s_a p s_{a+n} with ℓ(q)=ℓ(p)+2. Let x,y be the preimages of a and a+1 modulo 2n under p. Lemma 13 says q differs from p only at x,y,a+n,a+n+1 and describes the differences. For all other i, q(q(i)+n)+n≡s_a(p(p(i)+n))+n≡s_a(i+n)+n≡i+2n, and the four exceptional cases are checked directly. Thus τ_q^2(i)=i for all i. Hence τ_p is a fixed-point-free involution, i.e. a matching, and by construction φ(τ_p)=p.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 14, Ehrenborg-Readdy
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be an Eulerian poset with unique minimum hat0 and unique maximum hat1, and let Pyr(P) denote the product of P with a two-element chain. If Φ_P(c,d) denotes the cd-index of P, then Φ_Pyr(P)=1/2(Φ_P·c + c·Φ_P + ∑_{x:hat0<x<hat1} Φ_[hat0,x]·d·Φ_[x,hat1]), where the sum ranges over all proper non-extreme elements x of P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this proposition from Ehrenborg and Readdy and uses it as a black box. Its role in the paper is to compute the cd-index of Q_0=[u,w]×[e,ss′], the product of an interval with a two-element chain, before subsequent zipping operations are applied.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 15, Reading zipping theorem
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a poset with unique minimum and maximum, and let x,y,z be a zipper in P: x,y,z are distinct, the strict lower sets of x and y are equal, z=x∨y covers x and y, and z covers no other element. Form P′ by deleting x,y,z, inserting one new element xy, putting a≤b as before among undeleted elements, declaring xy≤a when x≤a or y≤a in P, and declaring a≤xy when a≤x equivalently a≤y in P. Then P′ is a poset. If P is Eulerian, then P′ is Eulerian. Moreover, P has a cd-index if and only if P′ has a cd-index, and Φ_P′=Φ_P−Φ_[hat0,x]·d·Φ_[z,hat1].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this result from Reading and uses it as the mechanism for passing from the product interval [u,w]×[e,ss′] to the interval [u,sws′]. The operation can be visualized as deleting the top z of a diamond-like configuration and identifying its two lower elements x and y. The cd-index changes by exactly the product of the cd-indices of the lower interval up to x, the single d, and the upper interval above z.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 16, Reading structural recursion for Bruhat intervals
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (W,S) be a Coxeter system, let u,w∈W, and let s∈S. Assume ws>w, us>u, and u≤w in Bruhat order. If us∉[u,w], then [u,ws] is isomorphic to [u,w]×[1,s] and [us,ws] is isomorphic to [u,w]. If us∈[u,w], then [u,ws] can be obtained from [u,w]×[1,s] by a sequence of zipping operations.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this theorem from Reading as the prototype for its interval recursion in MP_n. The theorem uses the behavior of right multiplication by a simple reflection and the lifting property in Bruhat order to compare [u,w], [us,ws], and [u,ws]. The product with [1,s] represents adding one binary choice, and the zippings account for collisions where two choices map to the same Bruhat element.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 17, Reading cd-index recursion for Bruhat intervals
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (W,S) be a Coxeter system, let u,w∈W, and let s∈S. Assume u<us, w<ws, and u≤w. For v∈W set σ_s(v)=ℓ(vs)−ℓ(v), so σ_s(v)∈{−1,1}. If us∉[u,w], then Φ_[us,ws]=Φ_[u,w] and Φ_[u,ws]=Φ_Pyr([u,w])=1/2(Φ_[u,w]·c+c·Φ_[u,w]+∑_{v:u<v<w} Φ_[u,v]·d·Φ_[v,w]). If us∈[u,w], then Φ_[u,ws]=Φ_Pyr([u,w])−∑_{v:u<v<w, vs<v} Φ_[u,v]·d·Φ_[v,w]=1/2(Φ_[u,w]·c+c·Φ_[u,w]+∑_{v:u<v<w} σ_s(v)Φ_[u,v]·d·Φ_[v,w]).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is cited from Reading and motivates Theorem 18. The first case is the cd-index of a pyramid/product interval. In the second case, the interval is obtained from the product by zipping exactly those triples corresponding to elements v for which vs<v; applying the zipping formula subtracts Φ_[u,v]·d·Φ_[v,w] for each such v. Combining this with the pyramid formula converts the expression into the signed sum using σ_s(v).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 21, Bruhat lifting property
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (W,S) be a Coxeter system, let u,w∈W, and let s∈S. If w>ws and us>u, then the following are equivalent in Bruhat order: (i) w>u; (ii) ws>u; (iii) w>us.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this standard lifting property from Björner and Brenti. It is used as the Coxeter-theoretic input for proving the analogous lifting property inside MP_n and for checking order preservation of the map η.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 22, lifting property for MP_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let u,w∈MP_n, let s=s_i be a simple generator of tilde S_{2n}, and let s′=s_{i+n}. If w>sws′ and sus′>u in MP_n, then the following are equivalent: (i) w>u; (ii) sws′>u; and (iii) w>sus′.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implications (ii)⇒(i) and (iii)⇒(i) are immediate by transitivity. For (i)⇒(ii),(iii), choose a modular palindromic reduced decomposition sws′=t_1t_2...t_{2q}. Since w>sws′, the word w=s t_1t_2...t_{2q} s′ is also reduced and modular palindromic. If u≤w, then u has a reduced decomposition appearing as a subword of this word. The assumption sus′>u prevents the chosen subword for u from using the initial s and the final s′. Hence the subword already lies in t_1...t_{2q}, giving u≤sws′, and adjoining the initial and final generators gives sus′≤w.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 23
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let u,w∈MP_n, let s=s_i and s′=s_{i+n}, and assume u<sus′ and w<sws′. Define η:[u,w]×[e,ss′]→[u,sws′] by η(v,e)=v, and η(v,ss′)=svs′ if svs′>v, while η(v,ss′)=v if svs′<v. Then η is a well-defined, surjective, order-preserving map.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Well-definedness follows because v∈[u,w] implies v∈[u,sws′], and if svs′>v then u≤v<svs′<sws′ by the MP_n lifting property. For order preservation, compare (v_1,a_1)≤(v_2,a_2) in the product. If a_1=a_2=e, the claim is immediate. If a_1=e and a_2=ss′, then η(v_2,ss′) is either v_2 or sv_2s′, both above v_1. If a_1=a_2=ss′, there are four cases depending on whether sv_js′ is above or below v_j. The two easy cases use transitivity; the mixed case η(v_1,ss′)=sv_1s′ and η(v_2,ss′)=v_2 uses the MP_n lifting property to get sv_1s′≤v_2; and the case where both images are sv_js′ again uses lifting to get sv_1s′≤sv_2s′. The paper refers to Reading's analogous proposition for the proof of surjectivity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 24
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume u<sus′ and w<sws′ in MP_n. Let Z={v∈MP_n : u<v<w and svs′<v}, and list Z as v_1,...,v_k with weakly increasing Coxeter lengths. Let Q_0=[u,w]×[e,ss′]. Recursively, let Q_i be obtained from Q_{i-1} by zipping the triple {(v_i,e),(s v_i s′,ss′),(v_i,ss′)}. Then for every 1≤i≤k, the triple {(v_i,e),(s v_i s′,ss′),(v_i,ss′)} is a zipper in Q_{i-1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First the paper checks that the three elements have not already been removed or identified before stage i. The element (v_i,ss′) has not yet appeared in a lower-rank zipping; the elements (v_i,e) and (s v_i s′,ss′) are not of the form deleted in earlier zippings, except that they will be identified with each other at the current step. Next it proves equality of the lower sets of (v_i,e) and (s v_i s′,ss′). If (x,a)<(v_i,e), then a=e and x<v_i. If x did not satisfy x<sxs′, then a zipper involving x would already have occurred at a smaller length, impossible; hence x<sxs′, and the MP_n lifting property gives x≤s v_i s′, so (x,e)<(s v_i s′,ss′). Conversely, if (x,a)<(s v_i s′,ss′), then either a=e, in which case x≤s v_i s′<v_i, or a=ss′. In the latter case x<s v_i s′ has smaller length, and if it occurred it would likewise force an earlier zipper, so it is ruled out. Thus the strict lower sets coincide. The element (v_i,ss′) plainly covers (v_i,e) and (s v_i s′,ss′). If it covered another (x,a), then either a=e and x=v_i, or a=ss′ and x⋖v_i; in the second case the lifting property and x<sxs′ force x=s v_i s′. Therefore it covers no other elements, so the triple is a zipper.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 26 and Corollary 27
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let u,w∈MP_n, let s=s_i and s′=s_{i+n}, and assume u<sus′, w<sws′, and u≤w. If sus′ is not in the interval [u,w], equivalently sus′ not ≤ w under these hypotheses, then svs′>v for every v∈[u,w], and the map η:[u,w]×[e,ss′]→[u,sws′] from Proposition 23 is an order-preserving bijection. Consequently, θ:[u,w]→[sus′,sws′] defined by θ(v)=svs′ is an order-preserving bijection.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as the MP_n analogue of Reading's Proposition 5.2 and omits the proof. Conceptually, if some v∈[u,w] had svs′<v, then applying the lifting behavior would force sus′≤w, contradicting the hypothesis. Hence η never identifies the e and ss′ layers and becomes bijective. Restricting to the ss′ layer gives the interval isomorphism θ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 28
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let u,w∈MP_n, let s=s_i and s′=s_{i+n}, and assume w<sws′, u<sus′, and u≤w. If sus′∉[u,w], then [u,sws′] is isomorphic to [u,w]×[e,ss′], and [sus′,sws′] is isomorphic to [u,w]. If sus′∈[u,w], then [u,sws′] can be obtained from [u,w]×[e,ss′] by a sequence of zipping operations.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first case follows directly from Proposition 26 and Corollary 27: η gives an order-preserving bijection from the product to [u,sws′], and θ gives the copy [sus′,sws′]≅[u,w]. In the second case, Proposition 23 gives a surjective order-preserving map η from the product to [u,sws′]. The elements where η has nontrivial fibers are precisely the triples indexed by Z={v:u<v<w and svs′<v}. Ordering Z by weakly increasing length and applying Proposition 24, each such fiber is collapsed by a valid zipper. Performing all these zippings gives the target interval.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 18
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let u,w∈MP_n, let s=s_i and s′=s_{i+n}, and assume u<sus′, w<sws′, and u≤w. For v∈MP_n define σ_s(v)=1/2(ℓ(svs′)−ℓ(v)), so σ_s(v)∈{−1,1}. If sus′∉[u,w], then Φ_[sus′,sws′]=Φ_[u,w], and Φ_[u,sws′]=Φ_Pyr([u,w])=1/2(Φ_[u,w]·c+c·Φ_[u,w]+∑_{v∈MP_n, u<v<w} Φ_[u,v]·d·Φ_[v,w]). If sus′∈[u,w], then Φ_[u,sws′]=Φ_Pyr([u,w])−∑_{v∈MP_n, u<v<w, svs′<v} Φ_[u,v]·d·Φ_[v,w]=1/2(Φ_[u,w]·c+c·Φ_[u,w]+∑_{v∈MP_n, u<v<w} σ_s(v)Φ_[u,v]·d·Φ_[v,w]).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first case uses Theorem 28: [u,sws′] is the product of [u,w] with a two-element chain, and [sus′,sws′] is a copy of [u,w]. Applying the Ehrenborg-Readdy pyramid formula gives the displayed expression. For the second case, define the sequence of posets Q_i from Proposition 24, starting with Q_0=[u,w]×[e,ss′] and ending with Q_k=[u,sws′]. For the i-th zipper, Reading's zipping formula gives Φ_{Q_{i-1}}−Φ_{Q_i}=Φ_{[(u,e),(v_i,e)]}·d·Φ_{[(v_i,ss′),(w,ss′)]}, where the intervals are taken inside Q_{i-1}. These intervals are naturally isomorphic to [u,v_i] and [v_i,w]. Summing over all v_i with sv_i s′<v_i yields the subtraction formula. Finally, replacing Φ_{Q_0} by the pyramid formula and observing that σ_s(v)=1 for svs′>v and σ_s(v)=-1 for svs′<v gives the signed half-sum expression.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 29
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a graded poset of rank n with a unique maximum element hat1 and possibly multiple minimal elements. Assume every interval in P is Eulerian. Let hat P be obtained by adjoining a unique minimum element hat0 with rank ρ(hat0)=-1, and assume hat P is Eulerian. Then the ab-index of hat P is Ψ_hatP(a,b)=(a−b)^n + ∑_{i=0}^{n-1} (a−b)^i b ∑_{ρ(x)=i} Ψ_[x,hat1](a,b).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof starts from the chain formula for the ab-index after the substitution Ψ_hatP(a+b,b). A chain hat0<t_1<...<t_{k-1}<hat1 contributes a monomial a^{ρ(hat0,t_1)-1} b a^{ρ(t_1,t_2)-1} b ... b a^{ρ(t_{k-1},hat1)-1}; substituting a−b for a gives the displayed form for Ψ_hatP(a,b). The sum is then grouped by the first element t_1 above hat0. If there is no such t_1, the contribution is (a−b)^n. If ρ(t_1)=i, the initial segment contributes (a−b)^i b and the remaining part of the chain contributes exactly Ψ_[t_1,hat1](a,b). Summing over all t_1 of rank i gives the formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 30
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The ab-index of hat P_n is recursively given by Ψ_hatP_n(a,b)=(a−b)^{binom(n,2)} + ∑_{i=0}^{binom(n,2)-1} (a−b)^i b ∑_{x∈MP_n, ℓ(x)=2 binom(n,2)-2i} Ψ_[e,x](a,b), where ℓ is affine Coxeter length. More generally, if τ∈P_n has c(τ)=k≤binom(n,2) and φ(τ)=w∈MP_n, then the ab-index of the interval [hat0,τ]⊂hat P_n is Ψ_[hat0,τ](a,b)=(a−b)^k + ∑_{i=0}^{k-1} (a−b)^i b ∑_{x∈MP_n, x>w, ℓ(x)=2 binom(n,2)-2i} Ψ_[w,x](a,b).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is an application of Proposition 29 to hat P_n and to intervals [hat0,τ]. Theorem 10 identifies elements below a matching τ with elements x above w=φ(τ) in MP_n, because φ is order-reversing. The rank c(σ) of a matching σ corresponds to affine length ℓ(φ(σ))=2 binom(n,2)-2 c(σ). Substituting this correspondence into the first-element decomposition of Proposition 29 gives both displayed recursions. The intervals [w,x] in MP_n have ab-indices obtainable from the cd-index recursions of Theorem 18 via Ψ(a,b)=Φ(a+b,ab+ba).

proof_techniques.name: Affine-permutation encoding of uncrossing matchings
proof_techniques.description: The paper replaces a matching τ by an affine permutation φ(τ) whose window entries record the mate of each endpoint shifted by ±n according to whether the mate lies to the right or left. The image is characterized intrinsically as MP_n, the modular palindromic affine permutations with no reduced word containing n consecutive generator indices. This turns the combinatorics of resolving crossings into Bruhat-order comparisons, with φ reversing the uncrossing order.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the matchings allowed by a fixed word w form a subcollection of complete matchings stable under local resolution, one can try to view it as a subposet or union of intervals inside P_n and then transfer it through φ to an affine-Bruhat setting. This could give access to rank functions, interval decompositions, and known Bruhat-order tools for studying the face poset of the cubical complex F_w.

proof_techniques.name: Resolving a crossing as adding paired simple reflections
proof_techniques.description: In the φ-model, resolving a crossing in a matching corresponds to multiplying the associated affine permutation by paired transpositions whose simple-reflection words are shifted by n from one another. This is why the relevant affine permutations have modular palindromic reduced words, and why Bruhat length changes in even increments.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The cells of F_w are cubes whose facets are obtained by resolving individual crossings. A useful local model may be to encode one crossing resolution as a paired operation, so that resolving any subset of crossings corresponds to moving in a Bruhat-like interval or in a quotient of such an interval.

proof_techniques.name: Product-with-chain plus zipping decomposition
proof_techniques.description: The core recursive structure is: after choosing a paired generator s,s′, the interval [u,sws′] is compared to [u,w]×[e,ss′]. If no collision occurs, it is exactly this product; if collisions occur, they are resolved by a sequence of zipping operations, each deleting the top of a small diamond and identifying its two lower elements. The zipping sequence is indexed by elements v with svs′<v.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The construction resembles building a cubical complex one crossing at a time: product with a two-element chain corresponds to adding a binary resolution choice, while zipping corresponds to identifying configurations that yield the same resolved matching. This may be a template for proving contractibility by recursive decomposition, quotienting, or controlled collapses.

proof_techniques.name: Lifting property adapted to the matching subposet
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves an MP_n analogue of the Bruhat lifting property: under w>sws′ and sus′>u, the inequalities w>u, sws′>u, and w>sus′ are equivalent. The proof uses modular palindromic reduced decompositions and the subword criterion for Bruhat order.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A lifting property for whatever labeled or word-constrained matching poset underlies F_w would help control whether resolving or adding a chosen crossing keeps one inside the relevant interval. Such control is essential for recursive arguments on cells and their attaching maps.

proof_techniques.name: No-long-consecutive-subword displacement criterion
proof_techniques.description: The condition that an affine permutation in MP_n has no reduced word containing n consecutive generator indices implies a concrete displacement inequality i−n<p(i)<i+n for every window position i. This inequality lets the paper reconstruct a genuine matching from an affine permutation by shifting values by n, and it rules out pathological affine permutations that are modular palindromic but not matchings.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For constrained matchings, a similar intrinsic characterization of valid states could be valuable: instead of tracking embedded arcs directly, one might characterize allowable resolution states by inequalities or forbidden patterns in an algebraic encoding.

proof_techniques.name: Surjectivity by reconstructing an involution
proof_techniques.description: To prove φ is onto MP_n, the paper defines τ_p(i)=p(i)+n or p(i)-n depending on the window value p(i), then proves τ_p is a fixed-point-free involution. The involution property is shown by induction on half the affine length; the induction step isolates exactly four exceptional positions affected by q=s_a p s_{a+n}, using Lemma 13.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a useful pattern for proving that an algebraically defined object really encodes a matching: define candidate partners explicitly, prove no fixed points by a displacement inequality, and prove involutivity by tracking a local move with finitely many exceptional positions.

proof_techniques.name: Flag-enumerative recursion from first chain element
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 29 computes the ab-index of a poset with a new minimum by grouping chains according to their first element above the new minimum. This reduces global flag enumeration to the ab-indices of ordinary intervals above elements of fixed rank.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one attacks F_w via Euler characteristic or flag enumeration of a face poset, this chain-decomposition method may help express global counts in terms of local resolution intervals.

proof_techniques.name: Eulerian parity via involutions
proof_techniques.description: Although Lam's proof is only summarized, the paper records that Eulerianity of hat P_n is proved by pairing odd- and even-rank elements in intervals using affine-permutation operations, after handling intervals inside P_n by descending induction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, a sign-reversing involution on cells or on unmatched resolution states could help compute Euler characteristic or verify local sphere/ball behavior in links, which are common steps in contractibility proofs.

other_useful_info: The paper notes that Lam's earlier map τ↦g_τ identifies P_n with an induced subposet of dual affine Bruhat order of type (n,2n), while Kim's modified φ lands in the full affine permutation group tilde S_{2n} and gives an explicit image MP_n. It also notes that Lam's work on electrical networks gives cell structures described by uncrossing posets, suggesting a broader precedent for topological cell complexes governed by crossing-resolution orders. Remark 19 states without proof an additional recursion for Φ_[sus′,sws′] in the case sus′∈[u,w] using Reading's vertex-shaving operation Shv. The examples in the paper show small intervals in MP_n that are Boolean lattices or isomorphic to the Bruhat order of S_3, illustrating that local uncrossing intervals can have familiar combinatorial types.
why_relevant: Analyzes intervals in the uncrossing order and gives recursive and order-reversing descriptions. Interval structure is likely relevant because cells of F_w are attached by resolving subsets of crossings.

title: A User's Guide to Discrete Morse Theory
why_relevant: Discrete Morse theory is a standard method for proving finite CW or cubical complexes contractible by constructing an acyclic matching. It is a natural tool for collapsing F_w by reducing crossings or choosing canonical cancellations.
